nodes.label: RG-as-info-flow
nodes.full_name: Renormalization Group as Information Flow
nodes.description: Candidate-foundational instance within the information-dynamic family. Articulates Wilsonian RG flow as information-flow under coarse-graining: integrating out high-energy degrees of freedom reduces information content; relevant operators are the slow information modes; irrelevant operators are fast modes. Apenko 2012 and Beny-Osborne 2015 provide explicit information-theoretic articulations. The c-theorem (Zamolodchikov 1986) and a-theorem (Komargodski-Schwimmer 2011) establish information-monotonicity along RG trajectories with substantial discharge. Overall hosting claim is partial; the monotonicity / coarse-graining subclaims are substantial.

hosting_claims.scope_description: Partial overall as a hosting claim for QFT. Substantial for specific subclaims: information-monotonicity along RG flow (c-theorem, a-theorem); coarse-graining as information loss (Apenko 2012, Beny-Osborne 2015). Full QFT hosting via information-theoretic apparatus is partial-to-gestural — the framework articulates RG structure but doesn't yet derive QFT's full content from information-theoretic axioms alone.

nodes.label: NCG

Articulates the Standard Model via Connes-Chamseddine spectral action principle: a noncommutative manifold whose spectral triple encodes the SM gauge group and fermion content. Substantial for SM spectral-action articulation; partial for full QFT hosting (the spectral-action apparatus has reach-termination at certain content); gestural for unification with GR's gravitational sector beyond the Connes-Chamseddine setup.

equations.description: Chamseddine-Connes spectral action principle. loose_ends.description: Why the specific spectral function $f$ rather than another? The form is constrained by the heat-kernel expansion's coefficient structure but the underlying principle that selects $f$ is partial.

equations.description: Connes' reconstruction theorem (2008): a commutative unital spectral triple satisfying suitable axioms is equivalent to a unique compact Riemannian spin manifold. Provides categorical-equivalence between commutative spectral triples and Riemannian spin geometry. Substantial within commutative case.

loose_ends.description: Non-commutative extension (genuinely quantum spectral triples beyond almost-commutative) has classification partial — not a single noncommutative geometry but a category of them; full categorical classification of physically-relevant noncommutative spectral triples remains open.

equations.description: Kasparov's bivariant K-theory provides the natural categorical framework for noncommutative geometry. Operator-algebraic morphisms classified up to homotopy; spectral triples represent elements of K-homology. Substantial for K-theoretic content; partial for full physical content.

loose_ends.description: Relation between K-theoretic / cyclic-cohomology invariants and physically observable content is partial — the framework articulates topological invariants but their direct physical interpretation requires bridging.

nodes.label: Causal sets
nodes.full_name: Causal Set Theory (Sorkin and collaborators)
nodes.description: Candidate-foundational instance within the discrete-causal family. Articulates spacetime as a locally finite partially-ordered set where the partial order encodes causal structure and the discreteness provides the fundamental scale. Partial discharge for spacetime causal order and discreteness via Sorkin and collaborators; gestural- to-partial for matter content (the program articulates causal-set structure but matter-on-causal-set apparatus remains partial).

hosting_claims.scope_description: Partial for spacetime causal order and discreteness — causal-set theory recovers Lorentzian-manifold-like structure in the continuum limit (Sorkin 2003 and subsequent work). Gestural-to- partial for matter content — matter-on-causal-set apparatus (scalar fields, gauge fields, fermions) remains partial. Gestural as universal foundation.
citations: Bombelli-Lee-Meyer-Sorkin 1987 (foundational)
Sorkin 2003 'Causal Sets: Discrete Gravity'

hosting_claims.scope_description: Partial for the geometric/causal aspect of QG via discrete causal structure replacing the continuum. Gestural for matter content within QG. Gestural as universal foundation.

equations.latex: (C, \preceq) \;:\; \text{locally finite poset with } x \preceq y \Leftrightarrow x \text{ causally precedes } y
equations.description: Causal set as a locally finite partially-ordered set encoding causal structure discretely. The Lorentzian metric of GR emerges in the continuum limit from the partial-order structure.

loose_ends.description: Matter content on causal sets: scalar fields, gauge fields, fermions all require additional apparatus that remains partial.

equations.description: Benincasa-Dowker action for causal sets in 4D. Defined from counts $\mathcal{N}_k$ of $k$-element causal subgraphs (order-intervals of specific cardinality). In the continuum limit on a sprinkled spacetime, this discrete action recovers $\frac{1}{2}\int R \sqrt{-g}\, d^4x$ (the Einstein-Hilbert action up to topological terms). Substantial articulation of geometric content without manifolds.

loose_ends.description: Quantum dynamics defined via path-integral over causal sets with weight $e^{iS[\mathcal{C}]/\hbar}$ remains formal — partition function existence and convergence in 4D is not established.
citations: Benincasa-Dowker 2010 Phys Rev Lett 104:181301
Surya 2019 Living Rev Relativ 22:5

loose_ends.description: Higher-dimensional generalizations of the BDG action exist but universality across dimensions is partial; matching to higher-dimensional GR remains under active development.

equations.description: Discrete scalar d'Alembertian on causal sets (Sorkin 2007; Aslanbeigi-Saravani-Sorkin 2014). Acts on scalar fields defined on causal-set elements; the kernel $K_n$ counts intervening links at distance $n$. In the continuum limit on Minkowski-sprinkled causal sets, $B \to \Box$. Substantial articulation of matter content's geometric structure on causal sets.

loose_ends.description: Scalar fields on causal sets are well-defined and recover continuum dynamics; gauge fields and fermions on causal sets remain partial — apparatus for spinor structure without a manifold is under active development.

equations.description: Continuum-limit content. Malament (1977): the causal structure of a future-distinguishing spacetime determines its conformal structure. For causal sets, the analogous statement is the Hauptvermutung — a causal set faithfully embedded into a Lorentzian manifold determines that manifold uniquely (up to conformal class). Substantial in 1+1D; partial in 3+1D.

loose_ends.description: The Hauptvermutung-uniqueness conjecture is partial in 3+1D; explicit construction of continuum manifolds from given causal sets requires Lorentz-invariance preserved by sprinkling — Bombelli-Henson-Sorkin 2009 proved Lorentz invariance for Poisson sprinkling but full Hauptvermutung remains conjectural.

equations.description: A causal set is precisely a small category in which every Hom-set has at most one morphism (a thin category / poset). Causal-set theory thereby sits naturally within category-theoretic framework — causal evolution is composition; events are objects; the causality relation is the morphism structure.

loose_ends.description: Extension to higher-dimensional categories (2-categories for two-element causal subgraphs, ∞-categories for general higher-order causal structure) provides candidate apparatus for matter content but remains gestural at the level of full physical content.

equations.description: Rideout-Sorkin classical sequential growth: causal-set dynamics articulated as Markov chain on the partial order of causal sets ordered by inclusion, with growth probabilities constrained by general covariance and Bell causality. Categorical articulation: dynamics as functor from time category to category of causal sets.

loose_ends.description: Quantum sequential growth (causal-set decoherent histories) remains partial; non-trivial covariance and Bell-causality conditions narrow allowed quantum dynamics but a complete quantum dynamics for causal sets is not yet established.

equations.description: Expected number of causal-set elements in a spacetime region equals density times Lorentz-invariant 4-volume. Provides the bridge between discrete-causal apparatus and continuum-geometric volume. Lorentz invariance of Poisson sprinkling proven (Bombelli-Henson-Sorkin 2009). Substantial articulation of how Lorentzian-geometric content is recovered.

loose_ends.description: Specific finite-$N$ geometric content is not uniquely determined by the causal set alone; the embedding-as-sprinkling articulation is statistical, not deterministic. Bridge to GR-tensorial structure at finite-$N$ remains partial.

nodes.label: AdS/CFT
nodes.full_name: AdS/CFT Correspondence (Maldacena 1998 and developments)
nodes.description: Candidate-foundational instance within the holographic family. Articulates a duality between gravitational theories on Anti-de Sitter (AdS) backgrounds and conformal field theories on the AdS boundary. Substantial for specific dual pairs (canonically: Type IIB string theory on AdS_5 × S^5 dual to N=4 SYM in 4D). Partial as general holographic architecture (extensions to non-AdS backgrounds, to non-conformal field theories, to phenomenologically relevant settings remain partial). Gestural as universal foundation.

hosting_claims.scope_description: Substantial for specific dual pairs (the canonical Maldacena duality and its specific extensions). Partial for general QFT — non-conformal QFTs, phenomenologically relevant QFTs, and dynamics beyond ground states require additional articulation. Gestural as universal foundation for QFT.

hosting_claims.scope_description: Substantial for AdS-background gravitational dynamics in the dual-gauge regime. Partial for non-AdS backgrounds and for phenomenologically relevant cosmological settings (de Sitter, FRW). Gestural as universal foundation for GR.

hosting_claims.scope_description: Substantial for QG content in specific dual pairs (the AdS gravitational theory has emergent quantum-gravitational content via the dual gauge theory). Partial for general QG — extensions to non-AdS backgrounds and to phenomenologically observable QG content remain partial. Gestural as universal foundation.

equations.description: GKP-Witten relation: the CFT generating functional with boundary source $\\phi_0$ equals the bulk gravitational partition function with boundary condition $\\phi|_\\partial = \\phi_0$. The holographic duality articulated as equality of partition functions.

loose_ends.description: Beyond the canonical AdS_5/CFT_4 setting, the GKP-Witten relation requires extensions; non-AdS holographic duals are partial.
citations: Aharony-Gubser-Maldacena-Ooguri-Oz 2000
Gubser-Klebanov-Polyakov 1998

equations.description: Ryu-Takayanagi formula: entanglement entropy of a boundary region equals the area of the bulk minimal surface. Substantial for the holographic articulation of entanglement entropy in dual pairs.

loose_ends.description: Extensions beyond static settings (covariant Hubeny-Rangamani-Takayanagi formula) and quantum corrections remain active research areas.

candidate_targeting.program: covariant HRT and quantum extremal surfaces

equations.latex: \text{Type IIB string on AdS}_5 \times S^5 \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \mathcal{N}{=}4 \text{ SYM in 4D}
equations.description: The canonical Maldacena duality. Type IIB superstring theory on $\mathrm{AdS}_5 \times S^5$ background is dual to $\mathcal{N}{=}4$ super-Yang-Mills theory with gauge group $SU(N)$ on the 4D conformal boundary, with parameters related by $g_{\mathrm{YM}}^2 = g_s$ and $L^4/\alpha'^2 = 4\pi g_s N$. Substantial structural articulation; extensive operational evidence in correlator-by-correlator comparisons.

loose_ends.description: Extension to non-AdS, non-supersymmetric, and phenomenologically-relevant settings (FRW cosmology, dS holography, QCD-like theories) remains partial across dual pairs.

equations.description: Holographic dictionary mapping boundary operators of conformal dimension $\Delta$ to bulk fields with specific asymptotic behavior near the boundary. The leading coefficient $\phi_0$ sources the dual operator; the subleading coefficient $\phi_1$ is its VEV. Substantial articulation for primary operators in canonical setting.

loose_ends.description: The holographic dictionary is substantial for primaries in canonical AdS/CFT setting; multi-trace deformations and gravitational $1/N$-corrections to the dictionary remain partial.

equations.description: Maldacena-Susskind ER=EPR conjecture: entangled qubits in CFT correspond to non-traversable wormhole geometries in the dual bulk. Substantial structural articulation in canonical settings (eternal AdS black hole as thermofield-double); broader operational discharge across general entanglement structures remains partial.

loose_ends.description: Extending ER=EPR beyond canonical maximally-entangled / thermofield-double cases remains partial; non-perturbative tests are open.

equations.description: AdS$_5 \times S^5$ Poincaré-patch metric: the geometric foundation of canonical AdS/CFT, solving type IIB supergravity equations of motion in the near-horizon limit of a stack of $N$ D3-branes. The conformal boundary at $z \to 0$ hosts the dual $\mathcal{N}{=}4$ SYM. Substantial-local.

loose_ends.description: Extension to other supergravity backgrounds gives partial discharge for different dual gauge theories; phenomenologically relevant FRW / de-Sitter backgrounds gestural.

equations.description: Holographic c-theorem (Henningson-Skenderis 1998; Myers-Sinha holographic a-theorem 2010): the bulk AdS radius decreases monotonically along RG flow, providing geometric realization of information-monotonicity. Substantial articulation in canonical setting; gives geometric face of the c-theorem.

loose_ends.description: Extensions to dimension $>4$ and to non-AdS settings are partial; universal discharge of c/a-theorem across dimensions and architectures remains gestural.

equations.description: Quantum extremal surface formula (Engelhardt-Wall 2014; Faulkner-Lewkowycz-Maldacena 2013): the entanglement entropy of a boundary region equals the minimum over extremal surfaces of the area-piece plus the bulk entanglement of the homology region. Substantial refinement of Ryu-Takayanagi with quantum corrections.

loose_ends.description: Full reconstruction of bulk operators from boundary entanglement data (bulk locality from entanglement) remains partial; the entanglement wedge reconstruction is substantial but completeness of bulk-from-boundary reconstruction is open.

loose_ends.description: Bridge from canonical thermofield-double to phenomenologically-relevant cosmological spacetimes remains partial; the entanglement-builds-spacetime articulation is substantial for special states, partial for general.

nodes.label: SM EW
nodes.full_name: Standard Model Electroweak Sector
nodes.description: Electroweak sector of the Standard Model: $SU(2)_L \\times U(1)_Y$ gauge theory with Higgs mechanism producing $W^\\pm$, $Z^0$, photon, and providing fermion mass via Yukawa couplings. The SM EW sector is the most precisely-tested QFT regime content — electroweak precision observables tested to 0.1% accuracy at LEP/SLC, with ATLAS/CMS Higgs measurements continuing to refine. The program's targets this regime specifically; substantial-local discharge with seven framework-boundaries documented in the program literature.
nodes.empirical_content: $W^\\pm$, $Z^0$, photon mass spectrum; weak interaction couplings; Higgs mechanism with $v = 246$ GeV; CKM matrix elements and CP violation; PMNS matrix and neutrino mixing.

equations.description: Electroweak Lagrangian decomposed into gauge, Higgs, fermion kinetic, and Yukawa components. The structural articulation of how the SM EW sector sustains its content within QFT.

loose_ends.description: Yukawa coupling values ($y_e, y_\\mu, y_\\tau$ for charged leptons; $y_u, y_c, y_t$ for up-type quarks; etc.) are external inputs to the SM. The Yukawa structure is determined by the gauge structure but the magnitudes are not.
citations: Pati-Salam 1974
Froggatt-Nielsen 1979

loose_ends.description: Hierarchy problem: why is $v = 246$ GeV so much smaller than the Planck scale? Quadratic divergences in the Higgs mass need fine-tuning or new physics.

Established because EW precision tests at LEP/SLC (0.1% accuracy) and ATLAS/CMS Higgs discovery provide substantial empirical discharge.

nodes.label: QCD
nodes.full_name: Quantum Chromodynamics (Strong Interaction)
nodes.description: Strong-interaction sector of the Standard Model: $SU(3)_c$ gauge theory with quarks and gluons. QCD has substantial discharge in the perturbative regime (high-energy scattering, deep inelastic scattering, jet physics) but remains partial in the non-perturbative regime (confinement, chiral symmetry breaking, hadron spectrum). Lattice QCD provides substantial numerical discharge for non-perturbative content; analytic non-perturbative QCD remains open. The program's apparatus is obstructed for QCD-like regimes (the program literature).
nodes.empirical_content: Hadron spectrum (mesons, baryons); confinement (no free quarks); asymptotic freedom; chiral symmetry breaking; QCD vacuum structure; jet physics in high-energy collisions.

equations.description: QCD Lagrangian: gluon kinetic term plus quark Dirac terms with covariant derivative. The architecture's articulation of strong-interaction content.

loose_ends.description: Confinement: why no free quarks observed? The non-perturbative QCD vacuum structure that produces confinement is not analytically derived; lattice QCD provides numerical evidence.
citations: Wilson 1974 (lattice gauge theory)

loose_ends.description: Strong CP problem: why is $\\bar\\theta_{\\mathrm{QCD}} < 10^{-10}$ when no symmetry forbids large CP violation in QCD? Standard candidate: Peccei-Quinn axion.

nodes.label: Higgs
nodes.full_name: Higgs Sector
nodes.description: Higgs sector providing the mechanism for electroweak symmetry breaking and fermion mass. Discovered at LHC in 2012 with mass $m_H \\approx 125.25$ GeV. Substantial empirical content; the loose ends are at the boundary where Higgs sector connects to fundamental questions (vacuum stability, hierarchy problem, multi-Higgs extensions).
nodes.empirical_content: Higgs boson with $m_H \\approx 125.25$ GeV; $v = 246$ GeV vacuum expectation value; Higgs couplings to fermions and gauge bosons consistent with SM at current LHC precision.

equations.description: Higgs potential with $\\mu^2 > 0$ producing spontaneous symmetry breaking. The mechanism that sustains electroweak symmetry breaking and fermion mass.

loose_ends.description: Higgs self-coupling $\\lambda$ value gives marginal vacuum stability (or metastability) at high scales. Whether the SM vacuum is genuinely stable depends on precision $m_t$ and $\\lambda$ measurements.
citations: Buttazzo et al. 2013 (Higgs vacuum stability)

nodes.label: classical grav
nodes.full_name: Classical Gravity (within GR)
nodes.description: Classical gravitational regime within GR: solar-system tests, gravitational waves, cosmological observations. GR's predictions have been tested with extraordinary precision: solar-system perihelion precession, GPS time corrections, binary pulsar timing (Hulse-Taylor), LIGO/Virgo gravitational-wave detection 2015+, Event Horizon Telescope BH imaging 2019+. Substantial empirical content. The loose ends are at boundaries: singularities, strong- field limits, cosmological-scale dynamics where dark sector parameterizations enter.
nodes.empirical_content: Solar system tests (perihelion precession, light bending); gravitational waves (Hulse-Taylor binary 1974+, LIGO/Virgo 2015+); black hole imaging (EHT 2019+, M87* and Sgr A*); gravitational lensing; FRW cosmological dynamics (with dark sector).

nodes.label: Hilbert/meas
nodes.full_name: Hilbert-space Structure and Measurement Content (QM)
nodes.description: Hilbert-space structure and measurement content within QM-foundational. The empirical content of QM as actually used: state vectors, observables as self-adjoint operators, measurement outcomes following the Born rule, entanglement and non-locality (Bell inequalities, CHSH). Substantial empirical discharge: countless experiments confirm QM predictions to high precision; Bell-inequality violations rule out local hidden-variable theories.
nodes.empirical_content: Bell-inequality violations (Aspect 1982; Hensen 2015 loophole-free); quantum interference (single-photon, electron, neutron, large-molecule); quantum measurement statistics; entanglement-based protocols (teleportation, dense coding, key distribution); decoherence rates in mesoscopic systems.

equations.description: Born rule connecting state vector to measurement probabilities. The architecture's articulation of empirical content.

loose_ends.description: Why the squared-amplitude rule rather than another? Gleason's theorem derives it from Hilbert-space plus non-contextuality, but the foundations remain contested.

equations.latex: |\langle E(a, b) - E(a, b') + E(a', b) + E(a', b') | \rangle \leq 2\sqrt{2} \;\;\text{(Tsirelson bound)}
equations.description: CHSH/Tsirelson bound for quantum correlations, exceeding the classical Bell bound of 2. The architecture's articulation of non-locality. Empirically confirmed to high precision.

nodes.label: equilibrium
nodes.full_name: Equilibrium Statistical Mechanics
nodes.description: Equilibrium content within statistical mechanics: thermodynamic ensembles, phase transitions, critical phenomena, fluctuation- dissipation. The most empirically tested and theoretically discharged regime within stat mech. Jaynesian foundations articulate the regime via MaxEnt with substantial discharge. The program's (an equilibrium component) targets equilibrium-content directly.
nodes.empirical_content: Thermodynamic state functions (T, P, V, E, S, μ); phase transitions (gas-liquid, magnetic, superconducting, superfluid); critical phenomena with universal exponents; Bose-Einstein condensation; Fermi degeneracy pressure; equation-of-state measurements across systems.

equations.latex: Z = \sum_i e^{-\beta E_i}, \;\; F = -k_B T \ln Z

nodes.label: BH thermo
nodes.full_name: Black Hole Thermodynamics
nodes.description: One of the most successful cross-architecture entries in established physics. Bridges GR (horizon geometry) and statistical mechanics (entropy and temperature) jointly. Black holes carry entropy proportional to horizon area, emit thermal Hawking radiation, and satisfy four laws structurally analogous to thermodynamic laws. Jacobson 1995 derives Einstein equations from thermodynamic principles applied to local Rindler horizons — a substantial structural articulation of GR-as-thermodynamics that grounds the program's apparatus.
nodes.empirical_content: Bekenstein-Hawking entropy $S = A/(4 G \\hbar)$; Hawking radiation with Hawking temperature $T_H = \\hbar c^3 / (8\\pi G M k_B)$; four laws of black hole thermodynamics; entropy bounds (Bekenstein bound, Bousso bound).

equations.description: Bekenstein-Hawking entropy: black hole entropy proportional to horizon area. The cross-architecture articulation: GR sustains horizon area; stat mech sustains entropy concept; together they sustain BH entropy.

loose_ends.description: The microscopic origin of BH entropy — what are the microstates being counted? This is the BH information question (related but distinct from the BH info paradox per Page).

nodes.label: Cosmological models

nodes.description: Cross-architecture content jointly hosted by GR (background dynamics via Friedmann equations), statistical mechanics (thermal history), and QFT (early-universe content, vacuum-energy contribution). The FRW + ΛCDM framework is empirically substantial — CMB anisotropy (WMAP 2003+, Planck 2013+), Type Ia supernovae for dark energy (Riess 1998, Perlmutter 1999), large-scale structure surveys (SDSS, DESI), BBN nucleosynthesis. The framework has explicit dark-sector loose ends — dark matter (~26% of energy budget) and dark energy / cosmological constant (~69% of energy budget) — that are empirically robust as parameterizations but architecturally unhosted at fundamental level.
nodes.empirical_content: FRW expansion with $H_0 \\approx 67-73$ km/s/Mpc (Hubble tension); CMB anisotropy with acoustic peaks consistent with ΛCDM at ~0.1% precision; matter density $\\Omega_m \\approx 0.31$; cosmological constant $\\Omega_\\Lambda \\approx 0.69$; baryon density consistent with BBN; large-scale structure consistent with cold dark matter.

nodes.label: QG
nodes.full_name: Quantum Gravity Frontier
nodes.description: The canonical instance of cross-architecture-non-sharing (one of the five structural-reason categories). QFT and GR are not jointly hosted by a single architecture; their organizing structures don't share at the architecture level. The gap is not an apparatus failure of one architecture extended to cross-arch scope — it is the sibling-non-sharing of two architectures whose organizing principles are internally coherent. Closure pathway: candidate-foundational apparatus from which both architectures emerge as different aspects of one deeper organizing structure. Multiple candidate-foundational families target this gap; none has discharged the unification.

architecture_level_edges.description: QFT does not span the QG gap; gauge-theoretic content with renormalization-group structure does not extend to gravitational content via QFT's own organizing principle.
GR does not span the QG gap; pseudo-Riemannian geometric content with general-covariance closure does not extend to quantum gauge content via GR's own organizing principle.

nodes.label: CC
nodes.full_name: Cosmological Constant Problem
nodes.description: Cross-architecture reach-termination involving QFT, GR, and stat mech jointly. Naive QFT vacuum-energy estimation gives ~$10^{120}$ above the empirically-required value $\\Lambda \\approx 10^{-122}$ in Planck units. SUSY cancellation has been largely ruled out by LHC results; anthropic landscape arguments are philosophically contested. The program's vacuum-energy in the program literature is highly discrepant with observation — the gap is documented as framework-boundary in program literature11.9. Multiple architectures contribute to the empirical content; none alone sustains the closure.

nodes.label: BH info
nodes.full_name: Black Hole Information Paradox
nodes.description: Cross-architecture non-sharing with bridge-failure aspects. Semi- classical gravity (QFT on curved background) predicts thermal Hawking radiation; unitary quantum mechanics requires information preservation. The two architectures impose apparently incompatible commitments at the BH evaporation regime. The island formula and replica wormhole results (Almheiri et al. 2019-2020) substantially improve the Page-curve story by deriving the entropy curve, but do not fully close the conceptual loop on microscopic information flow — what specific microscopic mechanism preserves information remains open.

architecture_level_edges.description: QFT-on-curved-spacetime sustains semiclassical Hawking radiation but the unitarity content of full QFT requires information preservation that the semiclassical articulation doesn't supply.
GR sustains BH horizon and singularity content; the semiclassical predictions appear to violate unitarity at the QFT level. The QFT-GR joint commitment is incompatible at this regime.

regime_content_loci.description: Empirical locus where the joint commitment is observable in principle (Hawking radiation spectrum, BH evaporation).
BH formation and evolution are within the classical-gravity regime, with quantum effects entering at the horizon.
Information preservation is articulated in QM-foundational language (unitary evolution); the paradox is at the boundary of QFT and QM-foundational commitments.

nodes.label: turbulence
nodes.full_name: Turbulence in Fluids
nodes.description: Multi-architecture interference content. Hosting architectures: classical mechanics + statistical mechanics + thermodynamics jointly, without any single architecture sustaining the whole. Empirical content is robust (Kolmogorov scaling, energy cascade, intermittency, transition to turbulence at critical Reynolds numbers) but theoretical articulation is partial — the Navier-Stokes equations sustain the dynamics formally, but the regularity/uniqueness of solutions is open (Clay Millennium problem). The proposed connection between physical turbulence and the categorical-mode "turbulence" content within architectures (active networks at $C \\gg 1$ in network-classification literature, multi-scale closure content) is candidate-and-speculative — a proposed structural analogy that has not been discharged.

equations.description: Navier-Stokes equation for incompressible fluid flow. The architecture's articulation; the regularity / uniqueness of solutions is open (Clay Millennium problem).

loose_ends.description: Whether smooth Navier-Stokes solutions exist globally for arbitrary smooth initial data is open. Singularities may form in finite time.

nodes.label: Koide formula
nodes.full_name: Koide Mass-Ratio Formula
nodes.description: Empirically substantial but architecturally unhosted. The Koide formula gives $\\frac{m_e + m_\\mu + m_\\tau}{(\\sqrt{m_e}+ \\sqrt{m_\\mu}+\\sqrt{m_\\tau})^2} = 2/3$ to ~5 decimal places using current PDG charged-lepton masses. The formula is empirically robust but architecturally unhosted — no accepted theoretical framework derives it, and proposed extensions (to quarks, neutrinos) have not closed. Sits at the totality-approach stratum because the formula is *of* the realized content (mass ratios) without belonging to any architecture's organizing structure.

equations.description: Koide formula for charged-lepton masses. Holds to ~5 decimal places using current Particle Data Group values. Empirically substantial; theoretically unhosted.

loose_ends.description: No accepted derivation. Proposed extensions to quarks and neutrinos have not produced equivalent precision.

nodes.label: muon g-2
nodes.full_name: Muon Anomalous Magnetic Moment
nodes.description: Candidate anomaly pending resolution. The muon anomalous magnetic moment $a_\\mu = (g_\\mu - 2)/2$ measured at Fermilab's Muon g-2 experiment shows a discrepancy with the SM theoretical prediction. The 2021 Fermilab result combined with BNL gave a ~4.2σ discrepancy relative to the Theory Initiative 2020 SM prediction (mainly using dispersive HVP). However, lattice-QCD HVP calculations (BMW 2021+) give SM predictions much closer to experiment, narrowing the interpretive space substantially. Post-2025 the picture has substantially reconciled: the Muon g-2 Theory Initiative WP25 update [Aliberti et al. 2025 Phys Rep 1143:1] adopts a lattice-QCD HVP-only consensus average (omitting the dispersive branch pending resolution of the CMD-3 vs earlier $e^+ e^- \\to \\pi^+ \\pi^-$ tension below 1 GeV), and combined with the Fermilab E989 final Run-1–6 measurement [Aguillard et al. 2025 PRL 135:101802] the residual SM-vs-experiment deviation is ≈1σ. The persistent open question has shifted from a measurement-vs-SM-prediction tension to an SM-internal HVP-methodology question about dispersive vs lattice reconciliation; the discrepancy's status remains provisional rather than fully closed pending higher-precision experimental progress (J-PARC E34 systematics-independent cross-check; future g-2 continuations) and SM-side methodology refinement (per-mille lattice HVP; CMD-3 vs earlier-data reconciliation).

equations.description: SM contributions to muon anomalous magnetic moment from QED, EW, hadronic vacuum polarization (HVP), and hadronic light-by-light (HLbL). HVP is the dominant uncertainty contributor.

loose_ends.description: Discrepancy between dispersive-HVP-based SM prediction (Theory Initiative 2020) and lattice-HVP (BMW 2021+). The two methods give inconsistent SM predictions; reconciling them is active investigation.

Aguillard et al. (Muon g-2 Collaboration) 2025 'Measurement of the positive muon anomalous magnetic moment to 127 ppb' Phys Rev Lett 135:101802 (arXiv:2506.03069) — Fermilab E989 final Run-1–6 combined measurement a_μ^exp = 116 592 070.5(14.6) × 10⁻¹¹ at design-completion 127 ppb relative precision; combined with the WP25 SM prediction the residual deviation is Δa_μ / σ_combined = 37.5 / √(62² + 14.6²) ≈ 0.59σ, rounded to 0.6σ as the canonical post-2025 sigma_deviation refresh value. The historical 4.2σ figure cited Abi 2021 PRL 126:141801 + Aoyama 2020 Phys Rep 887:1 (TI WP20 dispersive HVP); both papers remain anchored on this carrier via the if_real_implies entry's condition_citations as the historical-tension reference frame.

The Boccaletti 2024 SM-vs-experiment deviation against the Aguillard 2023 PRL 131:161802 Run-1+2+3 200-ppb measurement was 0.9σ per the paper's abstract; the post-Aguillard-2025-final refresh tightens this to ≈0.6σ against WP25.

nodes.label: ChPT
nodes.full_name: Chiral Perturbation Theory
nodes.description: Multi-architecture interference content as cross-scale bridge exemplar. Effective theory below QCD scale articulating low-energy hadronic physics (pion-pion scattering, kaon decays, hadronic structure) phenomenologically without first-principles QCD derivation. Empirically substantial (chiral perturbation theory predictions match experiment for low-energy QCD observables). Lattice methods provide partial discharge of the bridge from QCD to ChPT; analytic discharge is open. Sits at totality-approach because the EFT operates between QCD's regime-content and the empirical content it predicts, with the bridge from QCD to ChPT being partial.

bridges.bridge_status: partial (lattice QCD provides numerical discharge; analytic discharge open)

equations.description: Leading-order chiral Lagrangian for pseudoscalar mesons. Higher-order terms (Gasser-Leutwyler) systematically expand in $p^2/\\Lambda_\\chi^2$. The architecture's effective articulation; not derived from first-principles QCD.

loose_ends.description: The bridge from first-principles QCD to ChPT effective Lagrangian is partial. Lattice methods provide numerical discharge; analytic derivation remains open.

nodes.label: Hierarchy
nodes.full_name: Hierarchy Problem (electroweak / Planck scale)
nodes.description: Within-architecture frontier within QFT. The electroweak scale $v \\approx 246$ GeV is ~17 orders of magnitude below the Planck scale $M_{\\mathrm{Pl}} \\approx 10^{19}$ GeV. Quadratic divergences in the Higgs mass under loop corrections require fine-tuning to produce the observed $m_H \\approx 125$ GeV unless new physics enters at the TeV scale. SUSY at the TeV scale was the canonical candidate; LHC has now ruled out SUSY at TeV, narrowing the interpretive space. The frontier is structurally apparatus- mechanism-failure — the SM apparatus extrapolates to scales where the structural articulation fails to hold without unnatural cancellations.

architecture_level_edges.description: QFT's apparatus produces quadratic divergences in the Higgs mass requiring fine-tuning at SM scales; the within-architecture frontier.

regime_content_loci.description: The Higgs sector's $v = 246$ GeV scale is the empirical locus where the hierarchy problem is most directly articulated.
Electroweak sector content (W, Z masses, fermion masses) sits at the v scale; their hierarchy with $M_{\mathrm{Pl}}$ is the empirical content.

candidate_targeting.annotation: TeV-scale SUSY ruled out by LHC. SUSY at higher scales remains possible but loses the naturalness motivation. Not a direct candidate-foundational instance in v1's families but historically the canonical hierarchy-candidate.
Higgs as bound state of new strongly-interacting fermions; still active research with parameter constraints from LHC.
Warped extra dimensions can address hierarchy via geometric warp factor; constrained by LHC and precision EW.

If many universes with varying parameters exist (string landscape), anthropic selection produces $v$ value compatible with life. Philosophically contested and empirically untestable in conventional sense.
Relaxion mechanism: scalar field dynamically selects $v$ during inflation. Active research.

nodes.label: Strong CP
nodes.full_name: Strong CP Problem
nodes.description: Within-architecture frontier within QFT (QCD sector). The QCD Lagrangian admits a CP-violating term proportional to $\\bar\\theta_{\\mathrm{QCD}} G \\tilde G$, but experimental bounds from neutron electric dipole moment require $\\bar\\theta_{\\mathrm{QCD}} < 10^{-10}$. No QCD symmetry forbids the term; the smallness is unexplained. The frontier is structurally organizing-structure-failure within QCD — the organizing structure (gauge symmetry) doesn't constrain $\\bar\\theta_{\\mathrm{QCD}}$ to be small. Standard candidate: Peccei-Quinn symmetry producing a pseudo-Goldstone axion that dynamically relaxes $\\bar\\theta_{\\mathrm{QCD}}$ to zero.

architecture_level_edges.description: QFT's organizing structure (gauge symmetry alone) does not constrain $\bar\theta_{\mathrm{QCD}}$.

regime_content_loci.description: QCD sector is the empirical locus where the $G \tilde G$ term and the strong CP smallness are observed.

candidate_targeting.annotation: PQ symmetry provides dynamical relaxation; axion is observable target. Active experimental searches (ADMX, ABRACADABRA, IAXO). Theoretical mechanism substantial; experimental discharge gestural-to-partial pending detection.
Anthropic argument: small $\bar\theta$ required for stable matter. Philosophically contested.

candidate_targeting.program: Vafa-Witten 'No spontaneous CP violation in vector-like theories'
candidate_targeting.annotation: Theoretical constraint from vector-like nature of QCD; doesn't fully discharge the puzzle.

nodes.label: Flavor
nodes.full_name: Flavor Puzzle / Yukawa Hierarchy / CKM and PMNS Structure
nodes.description: Within-architecture frontier within QFT. The Yukawa coupling hierarchy spans ~6 orders of magnitude (electron Yukawa $y_e \\sim 10^{-6}$; top Yukawa $y_t \\sim 1$). The CKM matrix shows hierarchical mixing structure; the PMNS matrix shows different hierarchical structure. Why these specific values? Why these hierarchies? The structural-reason differs across candidate- foundational programs: in standard physics, organizing-structure- failure (QFT's organizing structure doesn't determine Yukawa magnitudes); in an open candidate-foundational program (per the program literature), bridge-failure (the framework's bare-to-observed bridge fails at functional-form level for charged-lepton Yukawa hierarchy). The frontier's structural-reason category depends on which candidate-foundational program is engaged.

architecture_level_edges.description: QFT's organizing structure determines the Yukawa Lagrangian's form but not the coupling magnitudes; bridge from bare to observed values is partially absent.

regime_content_loci.description: Electroweak sector contains the Yukawa structure; charged-lepton, quark, neutrino Yukawa hierarchies are empirical content.

candidate_targeting.annotation: Family symmetries with abelian or non-abelian structure produce Yukawa hierarchies via flavon VEVs.
GUT proposals predict Yukawa relations across quark/lepton sectors at unification scale.
Fermion overlap functions in extra dimensions produce hierarchies.
Statistical-distribution arguments suggest hierarchies as typical in random-Yukawa ensembles.

nodes.label: Measurement
nodes.full_name: Measurement Problem
nodes.description: Within-architecture frontier within QM-foundational. QM's organizing structure (unitary linear evolution between measurements) doesn't articulate why measurement events appear non-unitary (definite outcomes from superpositions). Schrödinger evolution describes physics between measurements; the Born rule articulates probabilities at measurement; but what *makes* measurement events different from other interactions is open. The frontier is structurally organizing-structure-failure: the architecture's organizing structure (unitarity) doesn't constrain measurement events. Multiple candidate programs propose different resolutions, with substantially different ontological commitments.

architecture_level_edges.description: QM's organizing structure (unitarity) doesn't articulate why measurement events appear non-unitary; within-architecture organizing-structure-failure.

regime_content_loci.description: Hilbert/measurement regime is where the Born rule articulates measurement probabilities empirically; the organizing-structure question lives here.

candidate_targeting.annotation: Unitarity intact; measurement is observer-relative branching. Substantial structural articulation; partial discharge of why we observe definite outcomes (decoherence + branching). Active research on quantitative theory.
Additional stochastic dynamics produces collapse. Empirically constrained; testable predictions. Partial discharge with phenomenological parameters.
Environmentally-induced superselection produces effective classicality. Substantial subclaim discharge; full ontological resolution partial.
Hidden-variable theory with non-local pilot wave. Substantial structural articulation; non-relativistic version well-developed; field-theoretic extension partial.
Operational/observer-relative interpretation; substantially philosophical; phenomenologically equivalent to standard QM.
MERA articulates ground states; doesn't directly address measurement problem but provides information-theoretic structure.
Information-theoretic axiomatic derivations of QM structure; partially address why-this-formalism question but not the measurement-event question directly.

if_real_implies.condition: If the measurement problem is resolved by an objective stochastic modification of unitary Schrödinger evolution producing genuine wavefunction collapse — as in the GRW, CSL, and Diosi-Penrose families — with collapse rate, localization length, and noise structure fixed by empirically-constrained parameters.

nodes.label: Matter-AM
nodes.full_name: Matter-Antimatter Asymmetry / Baryogenesis
nodes.description: Within-architecture frontier within QFT, with cosmological spillover to cosmological-models. The observed baryon asymmetry $\\eta = n_B / n_\\gamma \\approx 6 \\times 10^{-10}$ requires Sakharov's three conditions (baryon number violation, C and CP violation, departure from thermal equilibrium) to be satisfied. The Standard Model satisfies all three in principle but quantitatively insufficient — SM electroweak baryogenesis with SM CP violation produces asymmetry many orders of magnitude below observation. The frontier is structurally apparatus- mechanism-failure: the SM apparatus produces some asymmetry but extrapolates to insufficient magnitude.

architecture_level_edges.description: QFT's SM apparatus partially satisfies Sakharov conditions but quantitatively produces asymmetry well below observation.

regime_content_loci.description: Electroweak phase transition is the SM mechanism producing baryogenesis; partial discharge.
Empirical locus: $\eta_B$ measured via BBN and CMB.

candidate_targeting.annotation: Heavy right-handed neutrino decays produce lepton asymmetry, converted to baryon asymmetry via sphaleron processes. Substantial structural articulation; constrained by neutrino-mass observations.
Beyond-SM models (e.g., MSSM with light stop, two-Higgs-doublet) can enhance EW baryogenesis. Constrained by LHC.
Scalar-field condensates carrying baryon number decay to produce asymmetry. SUSY-motivated.
Heavy GUT-scale particles decay producing asymmetry. Constrained by inflationary dilution.

nodes.label: DM
nodes.full_name: Dark Matter (particle identity)
nodes.description: Within-architecture frontier within QFT (with possibility of new architecture if dark matter requires apparatus beyond standard QFT). The cosmological models' dark-matter loose end articulated from the empirical side (gravitational evidence is robust: galaxy rotation curves, lensing, large-scale structure, CMB); the frontier here is on the particle-physics side — what specific particle (or modification) sustains the gravitational signal. Structural-reason: apparatus-mechanism-failure (the SM apparatus doesn't produce a dark-matter candidate; new physics required) with the possibility that new physics could constitute a new architecture if dark matter has structurally novel content.

architecture_level_edges.description: QFT's SM apparatus does not produce a dark-matter candidate; new physics required within or beyond QFT.

regime_content_loci.description: Empirical locus: galaxy rotation curves, gravitational lensing produce gravitational signature requiring dark matter (or modified gravity).

candidate_targeting.annotation: Direct-detection experiments (LUX, XENON, PandaX) have ruled out canonical WIMP parameter space. Remaining parameter space narrows.
QCD axion (PQ) and ALPs (axion-like particles) provide candidates with motivation. Searches: ADMX, IAXO, ABRACADABRA. Connected to strong-cp-problem.
Right-handed neutrinos at keV scale could be DM; constrained by structure formation and X-ray observations.
PBH mass windows constrained by microlensing, CMB, gravitational waves; some windows remain.
Galaxy-scale phenomenology partial; cosmological-scale (CMB acoustic structure) struggles.
Addresses small-scale structure puzzles (core-cusp, missing satellites).

nodes.label: NS regularity
nodes.full_name: Navier–Stokes Regularity (Clay Millennium Problem)
nodes.description: Clay Millennium Problem: do smooth solutions to the 3D incompressible Navier–Stokes equations exist globally for all smooth initial data, or can singularities form in finite time? The Navier–Stokes apparatus is precisely defined (Newtonian mechanics + viscous stress + incompressibility) and the equations sustain enormous empirical content (turbulence, fluid engineering, atmospheric dynamics) — but global regularity remains open as of 2024. Distinct from intractability of generic computations: the question is about analytic structure of the equations themselves.

architecture_level_edges.description: Navier-Stokes regularity is internal to classical fluid dynamics, which the map hosts under stat-mech for cross-scale articulations.

regime_content_loci.description: Empirical locus where the question becomes most pressing — fully-developed turbulence may reflect near-singular dynamics.

candidate_targeting.annotation: Demonstrates blow-up in a closely-related averaged system; specific to averaged equations, doesn't settle the original problem.
Conditional regularity results showing what controlled quantities would imply smoothness.
Large-scale simulations have not exhibited finite-time singularities but resolve only finite range.

citations: Fefferman 2006 'Existence and smoothness of the Navier-Stokes equation' (Clay Mathematics Institute problem statement)
Tao 2016 'Finite-time blowup for an averaged three-dimensional Navier-Stokes equation' J Amer Math Soc 29:601
Constantin-Fefferman 1993 'Direction of vorticity and the problem of global regularity' Indiana Univ Math J 42:775
Beale-Kato-Majda 1984 (regularity criterion)
related_entries: stat-mech (hosting architecture under cross-scale articulation)
turbulence (empirical regime where the question is most pressing)
nodes.placement_rationale: Placed at stratum 4 (within-architecture frontier) because Navier-Stokes regularity is a question internal to a precisely-defined classical-fluid apparatus, not a cross-architecture gap. Classified solvability failure because the apparatus is fully articulated and the equations are precisely defined — what's missing is an analytic proof (or constructive blow-up) for smooth solutions in 3D. Solvability failure rather than apparatus-mechanism failure because the failure isn't in any specific mechanism within the apparatus but in solving the equations as written; solvability failure rather than reach termination because no apparatus boundary is being crossed — the apparatus simply hasn't been solved.

nodes.label: Topological phases
nodes.full_name: Classification of topological phases of matter
nodes.description: Classification of topological phases of gapped quantum matter. The free-fermion case is largely solved by the ten-fold way (Altland-Zirnbauer / Schnyder-Ryu-Furusaki-Ludwig / Kitaev) and its crystalline-symmetry extensions, which give complete K-theory classifications across dimensions. The broader problem — interacting topological phases, fractional / symmetry-enriched / symmetry-protected topological order in strongly-correlated systems, and the classification of topological order with anyonic excitations — remains open. The structural reason is reach-termination: the K-theory machinery that closes the free-fermion case reaches its applicable limit at interactions, where the classification can collapse (e.g. ℤ → ℤ₈ for class BDI in 1d under interactions; Fidkowski-Kitaev 2010) or be enriched in ways the free-fermion apparatus cannot capture. New classification machinery (cobordism for invertible phases, fusion categories for topological order, generalized cohomology theories) is partially developed and partially overlapping but not yet closure-grade for the full interacting domain.
nodes.closure_condition: Awaiting: (i) classification machinery for interacting fermionic SPT phases that subsumes Fidkowski-Kitaev-type collapses; (ii) a complete classification of (2+1)d topological order beyond the modular tensor category framework's current reach; (iii) classification of symmetry-enriched topological order across symmetry groups and dimensions; (iv) integration of crystalline-symmetry, magnetic-symmetry, and internal-symmetry classification programs into a single framework.

candidate_targeting.annotation: Tensor-network / matrix-product-state methods provide classification machinery for (1+1)d SPT phases (Chen-Gu-Wen 2011 and subsequent work); 2d projected entangled pair state (PEPS) classification is partially developed. Discharge is partial — significant content classified but not closure-grade.

if_real_implies.condition: If invertible interacting SPT phases in d spacetime dimensions with internal symmetry G are completely classified by the (Spin x BG)-bordism programme of Kapustin 2014 and Freed-Hopkins 2016 — the framework that already subsumes the Fidkowski-Kitaev Z->Z_8 collapse for 1d class BDI (Pin- bordism) and the Wang-Senthil Z_2->Z_16 refinement for 3+1d class AII (Pin+ bordism), but not yet completed across arbitrary internal-symmetry groups G.
condition_citations: Kapustin 2014 'Symmetry-protected topological phases, anomalies, and cobordisms: Beyond group cohomology' arXiv:1403.1467
Freed-Hopkins 2016 'Reflection positivity and invertible topological phases' arXiv:1604.06527 (published Commun Math Phys 374, 1021, 2021)
Wang-Senthil 2014 'Interacting fermionic topological insulators/superconductors in three dimensions' Phys Rev B 89:195124
Fidkowski-Kitaev 2010 'Effects of interactions on the topological classification of free fermion systems' Phys Rev B 81:134509

implications.description: An internal-symmetry-group axis (G in {Z_n, U(1), SU(N), discrete groups acting on fermions}) would be forced as an explicit third axis on freed-hopkins-cobordism, factored out from the current implicit packaging within the choice of tangential structure. The current FC enumerates five tangential structures (SO, Spin, Pin+, Pin-, Spin^c) that conflate spacetime-orientation data with specific internal Z_2 / U(1) symmetries; the resolution forces the general (Spin x BG) form with G as its own axis. Each (G, d) pair becomes a cell whose content is the bordism group Omega^{Spin x BG}_{d+1}.
derivation_citations: Freed-Hopkins 2016 arXiv:1604.06527 (the derivation that invertible interacting SPT in d spacetime dimensions with internal symmetry G is classified by the torsion in Omega^{Spin x BG}_{d+1})
Kapustin-Thorngren-Turzillo-Wang 2015 'Fermionic symmetry protected topological phases and cobordisms' JHEP 12:052 (independent derivation including specific Spin x BG examples)

if_real_implies.condition: If crystalline-symmetry-protected topological phases are completely classified by twisted equivariant cobordism / generalized homology with crystallographic space groups acting on the tangential structure (Shiozaki-Xiong-Gomi 2018), with the crystalline equivalence principle (Else-Thorngren 2019) reducing d-dim crystalline SPT under a point-group action to internal-symmetry SPT in lower dimensions.
condition_citations: Shiozaki-Xiong-Gomi 2018 'Generalized homology and Atiyah-Hirzebruch spectral sequence in crystalline symmetry protected topological phenomena' arXiv:1810.00801
Else-Thorngren 2019 'Crystallographic interactions and SPT phases' Phys Rev B 99:115116
Thorngren-Else 2018 'Gauging spatial symmetries and the classification of topological crystalline phases' Phys Rev X 8:011040

implications.description: A crystalline-space-group axis (the 230 crystallographic space groups, plus magnetic / Shubnikov extensions when time-reversal acts) would be forced as a further explicit axis on freed-hopkins-cobordism, beyond the internal-symmetry-G axis implied by the Kapustin-Freed-Hopkins resolution. Cells become triples (tangential-structure, internal-symmetry-G, crystalline-space-group); cell content is the twisted equivariant bordism group computed via the Atiyah-Hirzebruch spectral sequence of Shiozaki-Xiong-Gomi 2018. The crystalline-equivalence-principle of Else-Thorngren 2019 organizes how cells across this expanded axis relate.
derivation_citations: Shiozaki-Xiong-Gomi 2018 arXiv:1810.00801 (derivation of the twisted equivariant cobordism classification for crystalline SPT, including explicit Atiyah-Hirzebruch computations across the wallpaper and space groups)
Else-Thorngren 2019 Phys Rev B 99:115116 (crystalline equivalence principle as a structural map between crystalline cells and internal-symmetry cells)

if_real_implies.condition: If fracton phases (gapped phases with subextensive ground-state degeneracy and immobile or restricted-mobility excitations) genuinely form a class of topological phases outside the modular-tensor-category / TQFT classification framework, requiring foliated-TQFT or tensor-gauge-theory machinery for their classification rather than being limits or refinements of MTC-classified topological order.
condition_citations: Haah 2011 'Local stabilizer codes in three dimensions without string logical operators' Phys Rev A 83:042330 (Haah's cubic code — first definite Type-II fracton)
Vijay-Haah-Fu 2016 'Fracton topological order, generalized lattice gauge theory, and duality' Phys Rev B 94:235157 (X-cube model and the Type-I/Type-II distinction)
Chamon 2005 'Quantum glassiness in strongly correlated clean systems: an example of topological overprotection' Phys Rev Lett 94:040402

implications.description: A new FC organizing fracton phases would be forced, with axes (mobility-restriction-pattern [planon / lineon / fracton / type-I-vs-type-II] x ground-state-degeneracy-scaling-with-system-size x foliation-structure [number and orientation of foliations] x underlying-lattice-class). Cells are specific fracton models: X-cube (Vijay-Haah-Fu 2016), Haah's cubic code (Haah 2011), Chamon's model (Chamon 2005), checkerboard model (Vijay-Haah-Fu 2015), Type-II 'fractal' codes. The classification sits outside both modular-tensor-categories (anyons have free mobility; fractons do not) and freed-hopkins-cobordism (fractons are not invertible TQFTs; they have subextensive ground-state degeneracy).
derivation_citations: Pretko-Chen-You 2020 'Fracton phases of matter' Int J Mod Phys A 35:2030003 (review classifying fracton models by Type-I / Type-II and by mobility structure)
Nandkishore-Hermele 2019 'Fractons' Annu Rev Condens Matter Phys 10:295 (review establishing fractons as a distinct topological-order class)
Slagle-Aasen-Williamson 2019 'Foliated field theory and string-membrane-nets' SciPost Phys 6:043 (foliated-TQFT machinery for the Type-I subclass)

if_real_implies.condition: If (2+1)D symmetry-enriched topological (SET) phases — topological orders with anyonic excitations carrying an additional internal-symmetry action — are completely classified by the data (C, G, [rho], [w]) consisting of a modular tensor category C, a finite group G, a symmetry-localization class [rho], and a symmetry-fractionalization class [w] in H^2(G, A) (where A is the abelian-anyon subcategory), organized within the SymTFT framework.
condition_citations: Barkeshli-Bonderson-Cheng-Wang 2019 'Symmetry fractionalization, defects, and gauging of topological phases' Phys Rev B 100:115147
Etingof-Nikshych-Ostrik 2010 'Fusion categories and homotopy theory' Quantum Topol 1:209 (categorical-symmetry foundation)
Gaiotto-Kapustin-Seiberg-Willett 2015 'Generalized global symmetries' JHEP 02:172 (SymTFT antecedent)

implications.description: A new FC organizing (2+1)D SET phases would be forced, with axes (host-MTC C x internal-symmetry-group G x symmetry-localization-class [rho] x symmetry-fractionalization-class [w] in H^2(G, A) x extrinsic-defect-content). Cells are specific SET phases: G-enriched Z_2 toric code under various G-actions (Cheng-Gu-Senthil 2017 explicit lattice models); G-enriched Ising anyon model (rich landscape for G in {Z_2, Z_2 x Z_2, D_4}); fractional-quantum-Hall-state SET phases with magnetic-translation enrichment (Barkeshli-Manmana-Vishwanath 2014). The FC sits between modular-tensor-categories (its host-MTC axis) and generalized-symmetries (its SymTFT-bulk realisation of G); it does not subsume either.
derivation_citations: Barkeshli-Bonderson-Cheng-Wang 2019 Phys Rev B 100:115147 (the systematic derivation of the (C, G, [rho], [w]) classification with worked-out examples across G and C)
Bhardwaj-Schafer-Nameki 2024 'Lectures on generalized symmetries' Phys Rep 1051:1 (review showing SymTFT organization of the (C, G, [rho], [w]) data for (2+1)D SET phases)

citations: Senthil 2015, Annu. Rev. Condens. Matter Phys. 6, 299 (review of SPT phases)
Wen 2017, Rev. Mod. Phys. 89, 041004 (review of topological order)
Chiu, Teo, Schnyder, Ryu 2016, Rev. Mod. Phys. 88, 035005 (review of free-fermion classification)
nodes.placement_rationale: Within-architecture frontier hosted by QFT (specifically the condensed-matter / many-body QFT sub-domain). Stratum 4 placement reflects that the frontier is internal to QFT rather than cross-architecture. Added in v14 because no condensed-matter frontier existed in v13 and the ten-fold way needed a bears-on target.

nodes.label: Ten-fold way
nodes.full_name: Altland-Zirnbauer / Schnyder-Ryu-Furusaki-Ludwig / Kitaev classification of topological phases of free fermions
nodes.description: Classification of gapped free-fermion Hamiltonians by their discrete symmetries (time-reversal T, particle-hole C, chiral S = T·C) into ten symmetry classes (the Altland-Zirnbauer ten), each of which carries a topological invariant — trivial (0), ℤ, ℤ₂, or 2ℤ — at each spatial dimension d, with a period-eight (real classes) and period-two (complex classes) Bott pattern across dimensions. The structure was identified for random-matrix ensembles by Altland and Zirnbauer (1997), extended to topological phases in 3d by Schnyder-Ryu-Furusaki-Ludwig (2008), and completed across all dimensions with the Bott-clock structure made explicit by Kitaev (2009). The 'periodic table' framing is Kitaev's.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Gapped Hamiltonians of free (non-interacting) fermions in any spatial dimension d ≥ 0, with the three discrete symmetries T (anti-unitary, T² = ±1 or absent), C (anti-unitary particle-hole, C² = ±1 or absent), and S = T·C (unitary chiral, present iff both T and C present, or absent). Covers bulk topological invariants for crystalline systems in the absence of additional crystalline symmetries (with crystalline-symmetry extensions developed as a separate classification program). Excludes: interacting topological phases (where the classification can collapse or be enriched), fractional / symmetry-enriched topological order, topological order with anyonic excitations, gapless topological phases.

classification_axes.name: symmetry-class
classification_axes.description: The ten Altland-Zirnbauer symmetry classes, labeled by Cartan-class symbols. The class is determined by the triple (T², C², S-presence): A = (0,0,0); AIII = (0,0,1); AI = (+1,0,0); BDI = (+1,+1,1); D = (0,+1,0); DIII = (-1,+1,1); AII = (-1,0,0); CII = (-1,-1,1); C = (0,-1,0); CI = (+1,-1,1). Values listed in the canonical Bott-clock order used by Kitaev 2009 Table 1: complex classes (A, AIII) first, then the eight real classes in Bott-clock order starting from AI.

classification_axes.name: spatial-dimension
classification_axes.description: Spatial dimension of the gapped bulk. The classification extends to all d ≥ 0 with Bott-periodicity (period 8 for real classes, period 2 for complex classes); cells in this entry are populated for d ∈ {1, 2, 3} as the physically most-studied slice. Higher-dimensional content can be added without schema change.

cells.content: 0 (trivial)
cells.description: Class A in 1d: no nontrivial topological invariant.

cells.content: ℤ
cells.description: Class A in 2d: Chern-number-classified phases. The integer quantum Hall effect is the canonical realization.
realized_examples: integer quantum Hall effect (von Klitzing et al. 1980)
anomalous quantum Hall effect in magnetically-doped (Bi,Sb)₂Te₃ thin films (Chang et al. 2013)

citations: von Klitzing, Dorda, Pepper 1980, Phys. Rev. Lett. 45, 494 'New method for high-accuracy determination of the fine-structure constant based on quantized Hall resistance' (source-value: integer quantum Hall effect observed; Hall conductance quantized at σ_xy = n·e²/h with integer n at high B in 2DEG; ν=1 plateau gives σ_xy = 1·e²/h; the structural commitment recorded here is the quantization unit e²/h, the universal class-A 2D ℤ-classification signature; metrological-grade quantization precision better than 10⁻⁹ in best samples, anchoring the SI redefinition of the ohm)

Thouless, Kohmoto, Nightingale, den Nijs 1982, Phys. Rev. Lett. 49, 405 'Quantized Hall conductance in a two-dimensional periodic potential' (source-value: TKNN topological-invariant interpretation — each filled Bloch band contributes a TKNN integer (Chern number) to σ_xy in units of e²/h; the class-A 2D ℤ-classification IS the Chern number; T25 dimensionful-quantum-conductance reuse (16th-cluster anchor post-v55); T13 cell-direct; T10 uncertainty=null for universal-quantum-of-conductance value; retro-explanatory-only on the ten-fold-way side — the IQHE was discovered before the AZ classification framework subsumed it)

cells.content: 0 (trivial)
cells.description: Class A in 3d: trivial. The Bott pattern for complex classes alternates ℤ–0 with period 2.

cells.content: ℤ
cells.description: Class AIII (chiral unitary) in 1d: ℤ-classified by a winding number.
realized_examples: Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model (Su-Schrieffer-Heeger 1979) — theoretical paradigm; physical realizations in cold-atom and photonic lattices

cells.content: 0 (trivial)
ℤ
cells.description: Class AIII in 3d: ℤ-classified by a 3d winding number.

cells.content: 0 (trivial)
0 (trivial)
0 (trivial)
cells.description: Class AI (T² = +1, no particle-hole, no chiral) is trivial in d = 1, 2, 3. The first nontrivial entry occurs at d = 4 (ℤ).

cells.content: ℤ
cells.description: Class BDI (T² = +1, C² = +1, chiral) in 1d: ℤ-classified at the free-fermion level. The Kitaev chain (Kitaev 2001) is the canonical realization, with the winding number counting Majorana edge modes. Note: under interactions this classification collapses to ℤ₈ (Fidkowski-Kitaev 2010), a key example of the free-to-interacting reach-termination that motivates the open frontier.
realized_examples: Kitaev p-wave wire (Kitaev 2001) — theoretical paradigm

cells.content: 0 (trivial)
0 (trivial)
ℤ₂
cells.description: Class D (particle-hole, C² = +1, no time-reversal) in 1d: ℤ₂-classified, with the nontrivial sector hosting unpaired Majorana zero modes at boundaries. Predicted by Kitaev 2001 (as the spinless p-wave case of his model). Experimental claims of Majorana zero modes in semiconductor-superconductor heterostructures (Mourik et al. 2012 and follow-ups) have undergone significant revision, with notable retractions; the cell-level classification is theoretically uncontested but experimental confirmation has tension.
realized_examples: semiconductor nanowire / superconductor heterostructures — disputed (see Mourik et al. 2012, Albrecht et al. 2016, Zhang et al. retraction 2021)
vortex cores in topological-superconductor candidates (FeTe₀.₅₅Se₀.₄₅; Wang et al. 2018, also disputed)

citations: Mourik, Zuo, Frolov, Plissard, Bakkers, Kouwenhoven 2012, Science 336, 1003 arXiv:1204.2792 'Signatures of Majorana fermions in hybrid superconductor-semiconductor nanowire devices' (source-value: induced superconducting gap Δ ≈ 250 µeV in InSb nanowires proximity-coupled to NbTiN superconductor electrodes; zero-bias conductance peak observed at mid-gap consistent with Majorana zero-mode prediction; expected topological-phase entry condition E_Z > √(Δ²+µ²) requires magnetic field ≳ 0.15 T at this Δ scale; T13 cell-direct; T10 uncertainty=null for characteristic Mourik-setup value)

Kitaev 2001, Phys.-Usp. 44, 131 'Unpaired Majorana fermions in quantum wires' (source-value: theoretical prediction that the 1D class-D topological-superconductor phase hosts Majorana zero modes at wire endpoints; ℤ₂ classification of the cell)

Zhang et al. 2021, Nature 591, 354 (retraction); Frolov 2021, Nature 592, 350; Yu, Schneider, Khaymovich, Bardarson 2021, Nature Physics 17, 482 (source-value: status unconfirmed-tension — several high-profile zero-bias-peak Majorana claims subsequently challenged or retracted; the induced-gap value Δ ≈ 250 µeV remains literature-canonical as the characteristic Mourik-2012-setup scale, but the identification of zero-bias features as Majorana zero modes is contested; per cell[12] description and realized_examples 'disputed' annotation, the entry records the characteristic scale of the candidate-realization platform, not a confirmed Majorana observation)

cells.content: ℤ
cells.description: Class D in 2d: ℤ-classified topological superconductors. Sr₂RuO₄ was a long-standing candidate; the chiral p-wave interpretation is now considered disfavored by polarized-neutron-scattering experiments (Pustogow et al. 2019).
realized_examples: candidate p+ip topological superconductors (status revised)

citations: Maeno, Hashimoto, Yoshida, Nishizaki, Fujita, Bednorz, Lichtenberg 1994, Nature 372, 532 'Superconductivity in a layered perovskite without copper' (source-value: Sr₂RuO₄ superconducting transition T_c = 1.5 K in high-purity-limit single crystals; T13 cell-direct; T24 16th-use temperature-as-energy-scale: k_B × 1.5 K = 8.617333262 × 10⁻⁵ × 1.5 = 1.29 × 10⁻⁴ eV; T10 uncertainty=null for characteristic clean-crystal-limit value with sample-purity-dependence below 1.5 K)

Mackenzie, Maeno 2003, Rev. Mod. Phys. 75, 657 'The superconductivity of Sr₂RuO₄ and the physics of spin-triplet pairing' (source-value: canonical Sr₂RuO₄ review establishing T_c = 1.5 K and the historical spin-triplet chiral p_x+ip_y identification that motivated the class-D 2D ℤ-classification assignment on this cell)

Pustogow et al. 2019, Nature 574, 72 'Constraints on the superconducting order parameter in Sr₂RuO₄ from oxygen-17 NMR' (source-value: polarized-NMR Knight-shift measurements rule out the spin-triplet chiral p+ip order parameter; the class-D-2D identification of Sr₂RuO₄ via chiral p+ip is now disfavored, leaving the AZ class assignment of Sr₂RuO₄ unresolved between D, DIII, and possibly even-parity scenarios; per cell[13] description, the chiral p-wave interpretation 'is now considered disfavored'; the T_c value itself is uncontested and serves as the cell-level characteristic scale of the historically-leading candidate-realization; bidirectional cross-FC anchoring with topological-insulator-and-superconductor-materials cell[7] (sub-PR 9 v51) which records the same Sr₂RuO₄ T_c = 0.000129 eV under a DIII 3D class label — the cross-FC same-value-different-class-label pattern makes literature's class-uncertainty about Sr₂RuO₄ structurally visible; firewall-defensible by sub-PR 9 precedent on candidate-contested-evidence cells)

cells.content: 0 (trivial)
ℤ₂
cells.description: Class DIII (T² = -1, C² = +1, chiral) in 1d: ℤ₂-classified time-reversal-invariant topological superconductor wires.

cells.content: ℤ₂
ℤ
cells.description: Class DIII in 3d: ℤ-classified time-reversal-invariant topological superconductor. Superfluid ³He-B is the canonical realization.
realized_examples: superfluid ³He-B (Volovik 2003; Qi-Hughes-Raghu-Zhang 2009)

citations: Osheroff, Richardson, Lee 1972, Phys. Rev. Lett. 28, 885 'Evidence for a new phase of solid ³He' + Osheroff, Gully, Richardson, Lee 1972, Phys. Rev. Lett. 29, 920 (source-value: discovery of ³He superfluidity; T_c at melting pressure (~34 bar) ≈ 2.5 mK with the B-phase below 2.0 mK; the topological-superfluid interpretation of ³He-B as the canonical class-DIII 3D ℤ-classified realization was consolidated decades later)

Greywall 1986, Phys. Rev. B 33, 7520 'Specific heat of normal liquid ³He' + Greywall 1986, Phys. Rev. B 33, 7520 supplementary 'Thermodynamic properties of liquid and superfluid ³He' (source-value: precision thermometry establishes T_c at zero pressure (saturated vapor pressure) ≈ 0.929 mK; T13 cell-direct; T24 17th-use temperature-as-energy-scale: k_B × 0.93 mK = 8.617333262 × 10⁻⁵ × 9.29 × 10⁻⁴ = 8.0 × 10⁻⁸ eV; T10 uncertainty=null for characteristic zero-pressure value, pressure-dependence (T_c rises smoothly to ~2.5 mK at melting pressure) absorbed into the canonical-anchor framing)

Lett. 102, 187001 'Time-reversal-invariant topological superconductors and superfluids in two and three dimensions' (source-value: the class-DIII 3D ℤ-classified topological-superfluid identification of ³He-B with topologically-protected gapless Majorana surface bound states; the ³He-B B-phase exists below T_c at all pressures up to the AB transition and is the canonical realized example of the cell)

cells.content: 0 (trivial)
ℤ₂
cells.description: Class AII (T² = -1, no particle-hole, no chiral) in 2d: ℤ₂-classified 'quantum spin Hall' topological insulators. The Kane-Mele model (2005) and BHZ model (Bernevig-Hughes-Zhang 2006) predicted the phase; experimental confirmation came in HgTe/CdTe quantum wells (König et al. 2007).
realized_examples: HgTe/CdTe quantum wells (König et al. 2007)
InAs/GaSb double quantum wells (Knez et al. 2011)
monolayer WTe₂ (Wu et al. 2018)

citations: Bernevig, Hughes, Zhang 2006, Science 314, 1757 'Quantum Spin Hall Effect and Topological Phase Transition in HgTe Quantum Wells' (source-value: BHZ-model prediction that HgTe/(Hg,Cd)Te quantum wells undergo a band-inversion phase transition at critical thickness d_c ≈ 6.3 nm; above d_c the system is a class-AII 2D ℤ₂-classified quantum-spin-Hall topological insulator with helical edge states; below d_c the system is a conventional insulator; T13 cell-direct; T10 uncertainty=null for theoretically-predicted critical thickness with ~0.1 nm sample-to-sample variation absorbed into the canonical value)

König et al. 2007, Science 318, 766 arXiv:0710.0582 'Quantum Spin Hall Insulator State in HgTe Quantum Wells' (source-value: experimental confirmation that d > 6.3 nm samples exhibit the quantum-spin-Hall plateau of residual conductance ≈ 2e²/h independent of sample width while d < 6.3 nm samples behave as conventional insulators; the quantum phase transition at d_c = 6.3 nm independently confirmed via magnetic-field-induced insulator-to-metal transition; the cell-level entry records the structural-FC anchor at the d_c-realization level — class AII 2D ℤ₂ is realized at HgTe with this characteristic thickness)

Kane, Mele 2005, Phys. Rev. Lett. 95, 226801 'Z₂ topological order and the quantum spin Hall effect' (source-value: pre-BHZ prediction of the class-AII 2D ℤ₂ topological insulator phase; the abstract structural existence of the cell predicted before HgTe-specific material identification; bidirectional cross-FC anchoring with topological-insulator-and-superconductor-materials cell[0] (sub-PR 9 v51) which records the same d_c = 6.3 nm value at the phenomenon-FC level; cross-FC pattern signal (x) opened: same characteristic scale at two abstraction levels — phenomenon-FC records HgTe-the-material; structural-FC records class-AII-2D-realized-by-HgTe; bidirectionally anchors the cross-classification edge between tenfold-way and the topological-insulator-and-superconductor-materials phenomenon-FC, completing the second symmetric structural-side anchoring arc started in sub-PR 9)

cells.content: ℤ₂
cells.description: Class AII in 3d: ℤ₂-classified 3D topological insulators. Predicted independently by Fu-Kane-Mele (2007), Moore-Balents (2007), and Roy (2009); confirmed first in Bi₁₋ₓSbₓ (Hsieh et al. 2008) and Bi₂Se₃ / Bi₂Te₃ / Sb₂Te₃ (Xia et al. 2009, Chen et al. 2009).
realized_examples: Bi₁₋ₓSbₓ (Hsieh et al. 2008)
Bi₂Se₃, Bi₂Te₃, Sb₂Te₃ family (Xia et al. 2009; Chen et al. 2009)

citations: Zhang, Liu, Qi, Dai, Fang, Zhang 2009, Nature Phys. 5, 438 'Topological insulators in Bi₂Se₃, Bi₂Te₃ and Sb₂Te₃ with a single Dirac cone on the surface' (source-value: first-principles band-structure calculation predicts Bi₂Se₃ bulk band gap ≈ 0.3 eV with ℤ₂ invariants (1;000), placing it in class AII 3D ℤ₂-classified; bulk gap is larger than the energy scale of room temperature, making Bi₂Se₃ the most thermally-robust 3D TI; T13 cell-direct; T10 uncertainty=null for characteristic literature value with ~0.05 eV ARPES-vs-DFT variation absorbed into the canonical anchor)

Fu, Kane, Mele 2007, Phys. Rev. Lett. 98, 106803 + Moore, Balents 2007, Phys. Rev. B 75, 121306 + Roy 2009, Phys. Rev. closure_status.description: Complete within the stated domain (gapped free-fermion Hamiltonians, three discrete symmetries, no further crystalline symmetries). Closure was established by Kitaev (2009) via the K-theory / Bott-periodicity argument, confirming and extending the d = 3 cases worked out by Schnyder-Ryu-Furusaki-Ludwig (2008). The 'within-domain' qualifier is load-bearing: closure does not extend to (a) interacting systems (where Fidkowski-Kitaev 2010 showed the BDI-d1 ℤ collapses to ℤ₈), (b) crystalline-symmetry-protected phases (a separate classification program), or (c) gapless topological phases.

nodes.label: SM rep content
nodes.full_name: Standard Model gauge-group representation content
nodes.description: The Standard Model's particle content, specified as the representations carried by the SM matter fields, gauge bosons, and Higgs doublet under the gauge group G_SM = SU(3)_c × SU(2)_L × U(1)_Y, prior to electroweak symmetry breaking. The classification is the structural specification of which gauge-rep multiplets nature populates with fundamental fields, and at what multiplicity (three generations for fermions; one each for gauge bosons and the Higgs). Closed empirically with the Higgs discovery (2012); before that, the (1, 2, +1/2) scalar cell carried a predicted-but-unobserved status for 48 years. The classification is famously rep-theoretically constrained — the requirement of gauge-anomaly cancellation forces the hypercharge assignments across the matter content to satisfy non-trivial relations, which is what made charm (Glashow-Iliopoulos-Maiani 1970) and the existence of a third generation top-quark predictions rather than fits.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: The Standard Model gauge group SU(3)_c × SU(2)_L × U(1)_Y and the field content it constrains. Includes: quark and lepton multiplets across three generations; SU(3)_c, SU(2)_L, U(1)_Y gauge bosons; the Higgs scalar doublet. Excludes: right-handed neutrinos (not part of the strict SM; would be (1, 1, 0) singlets and are added in many SM extensions for neutrino mass); gravitational interactions; physics above the EW scale where new gauge structure may exist; the Yukawa coupling values themselves (the rep content fixes which Yukawa couplings exist; their numerical values are separately measured and constitute the flavor-puzzle frontier).
nodes.internal_structure: Each multiplet's gauge representation under G_SM = SU(3)_c × SU(2)_L × U(1)_Y, in the convention where U(1)_Y is normalized so that electric charge Q = T_3 + Y: Q_L → (3, 2, +1/6); u_R → (3, 1, +2/3); d_R → (3-bar, 1, -1/3) [or equivalently the conjugate (3, 1, -1/3) depending on convention]; L_L → (1, 2, -1/2); e_R → (1, 1, -1); gluons → (8, 1, 0); W → (1, 3, 0); B → (1, 1, 0); H → (1, 2, +1/2). The anomaly-cancellation constraint Tr[Y³] = 0 + Tr[Y · T_a²] = 0 + gauge-gravitational consistency is satisfied non-trivially across the matter content per generation; this constraint is what made GIM's prediction of charm and Glashow-Iliopoulos-Maiani's structural argument possible.

classification_axes.name: particle-multiplet
classification_axes.description: The nine SM multiplets, in the canonical order: left-handed quark doublet; right-handed up-quark singlet; right-handed down-quark singlet; left-handed lepton doublet; right-handed charged-lepton singlet; SU(3)_c gauge bosons (eight gluons); SU(2)_L gauge bosons (three W's); U(1)_Y gauge boson (one B); Higgs scalar doublet. Each multiplet carries a specific G_SM representation; the reps are recorded in the node's internal_structure field rather than as a separate axis because the SM's structural claim is that nature populates exactly these reps and no others (i.e. the rep is metadata about each multiplet, not a free axis along which the classification varies).
classification_axes.citation: Particle Data Group, Review of Particle Physics, latest revision (live document at pdg.lbl.gov); see also Weinberg 1996, The Quantum Theory of Fields Vol. II, §21.3

classification_axes.name: generation
classification_axes.description: Generation index for fermion multiplets (1, 2, 3); 'N/A' for multiplets that do not replicate over generations (the gauge bosons and the Higgs doublet). The SM's structural claim that gauge bosons and the Higgs are gauge-group-determined and do not replicate, while fermions appear in three identical-rep copies with hugely different Yukawa couplings, is captured by the asymmetry of which (multiplet, generation) combinations are real versus structurally-excluded.

closure_status.description: Complete as a specification of G_SM representations carried by the matter, gauge, and scalar fields of the strict Standard Model. Closure was empirically completed in 2012 with the Higgs discovery; the rep content has been the SM's stable structural specification since Glashow-Weinberg-Salam-GIM completed the gauge structure in the early 1970s. 'Within-domain' qualifier is load-bearing: closure does not extend to (i) whether G_SM is the correct organizing gauge structure at energies above the EW scale (GUT-scale extensions add new gauge content), (ii) why there are exactly three generations (number not predicted, only the consistency of any given number is), (iii) whether right-handed neutrinos exist (not included in the strict SM but added in see-saw and other extensions for neutrino mass), (iv) gravitational interactions.
