predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: ATLAS Collaboration 2012, Phys. Lett. B 716, 1; CMS Collaboration 2012, Phys. Lett. B 716, 30
predictive_yield.description: The most cell-specific Higgs prediction had a 48-year wait from theoretical proposal to experimental confirmation. The mass was not predicted by the rep content alone (the Higgs self-coupling that fixes m_H is a free parameter of the SM); the mass-eigenvalue at 125 GeV was unknown until 2012.

predictive_yield.prediction: Existence of antiparticles (the conjugate-rep components — d_L is paired with d_L-bar; the SM rep content implicitly specifies the antimatter sector).
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Dirac 1928, Proc. R. Soc. A 117, 610 (the Dirac equation predicts antimatter prior to the SM)
predictive_yield.description: Antimatter predates the SM. The SM rep content makes antimatter a structural feature (each chirality has a conjugate rep), but the prediction is Dirac's, not the SM's.

nodes.label: PDG
nodes.full_name: Particle Data Group
nodes.description: Standing international collaboration that has curated the Standard Model particle table and its experimental status continuously since 1957. The Review of Particle Physics, published biennially (latest issues annually online), is the canonical reference for particle properties, decay modes, and cross-section measurements across all SM-relevant experimental programs.

nodes.label: ATLAS + CMS at LHC
nodes.full_name: ATLAS and CMS Collaborations at CERN's Large Hadron Collider
nodes.description: The two principal general-purpose particle physics experiments at CERN's Large Hadron Collider, jointly discovering the Higgs boson in 2012 and continuing to study Higgs properties, top-quark physics, and searches for physics beyond the SM. ATLAS and CMS are independent collaborations with independent detectors; their joint discovery of the Higgs (announced simultaneously on 4 July 2012) is the canonical case of multi-collaboration confirmation in particle physics.
key_publications: ATLAS Collaboration 2012, Phys. Lett. B 716, 1 (Higgs observation)
CMS Collaboration 2012, Phys. Lett. B 716, 30 (Higgs observation)
ATLAS + CMS Collaborations 2016, JHEP 08, 045 (combined Higgs measurements)

nodes.label: UA1 + UA2 at SPS
nodes.full_name: UA1 and UA2 Collaborations at CERN's Super Proton Synchrotron
nodes.description: Two complementary experiments at CERN's SPS proton-antiproton collider in the early 1980s that jointly discovered the W and Z bosons, confirming the electroweak unification predicted by Glashow, Weinberg, and Salam. Operating concurrently with somewhat different detector technologies, the two collaborations provided independent cross-checks of the discovery.

Bagnaia et al. (UA2) 1983, Phys. Lett. B 129, 130 (W± discovery)

Banner et al. (UA2) 1983, Phys. Lett. B 129, 130 (Z discovery)

nodes.label: CDF + D0 at Tevatron
nodes.full_name: CDF and D0 Collaborations at Fermilab's Tevatron
nodes.description: Two general-purpose proton-antiproton collider experiments at Fermilab's Tevatron (1985-2011), jointly discovering the top quark in 1995. Operating concurrently like UA1/UA2 a decade earlier, CDF and D0 provided independent confirmation of the top-quark observation through their respective detectors and analyses. Tevatron operations ended in 2011 with the rise of the LHC, but the collaborations have continued to publish legacy analyses.
key_publications: Abe et al. (CDF) 1995, Phys. Rev. Lett. 74, 2626 (top quark observation)
Abachi et al. (D0) 1995, Phys. Rev. Lett. 74, 2632 (top quark observation)
Tevatron Electroweak Working Group 2014, arXiv:1407.2682 (final combined top-quark mass)

nodes.label: DONUT
nodes.full_name: Direct Observation of NU Tau Collaboration
nodes.description: Fermilab experiment that achieved the first direct observation of tau neutrino interactions in 2000, completing the experimental confirmation of the three-generation lepton structure of the Standard Model. The experiment exposed emulsion targets to a tau-neutrino-enriched beam derived from charm-meson decays, then scanned for the characteristic tau-decay tracks emerging from neutrino interactions.
key_publications: Kodama et al. (DONUT) 2001, Phys. Lett. B 504, 218 (tau neutrino observation)
citations: Kodama et al. (DONUT) 2001, Phys. Lett. B 504, 218

nodes.label: Brookhaven + SLAC (J/ψ)
nodes.full_name: Brookhaven AGS (Ting group) and SLAC SPEAR (Richter group) — November Revolution
nodes.description: Independent simultaneous discovery of the J/ψ meson in 1974 at Brookhaven National Laboratory (Ting and collaborators, calling it 'J') and at SLAC's SPEAR storage ring (Richter and collaborators, calling it 'ψ'). The discovery, joint-named J/ψ, confirmed the existence of the charm quark predicted by GIM (1970), establishing the second-generation up-type quark and triggering the rapid acceptance of the four-quark Standard Model. Known as the 'November Revolution' for the speed at which the discovery reshaped the field.
key_publications: Aubert et al. (Brookhaven E598) 1974, Phys. Rev. Lett. 33, 1404 (J observation)
Augustin et al. (SLAC-LBL Mark I) 1974, Phys. Rev. Lett. 33, 1406 (ψ observation)

nodes.label: Fermilab E288 (Upsilon)
nodes.full_name: Fermilab E288 Collaboration (Lederman group)
nodes.description: Fermilab fixed-target experiment that discovered the Upsilon (Υ) meson, a bottomonium bound state, in 1977. The discovery established the existence of the bottom quark, the third-generation down-type quark required by the Kobayashi-Maskawa three-generation CP-violation structure.

nodes.label: ADE Lie algebras
nodes.full_name: Simply-laced finite-dimensional complex simple Lie algebras (Killing-Cartan ADE)
nodes.description: The classification of simply-laced finite-dimensional complex simple Lie algebras: the two infinite series A_n = sl(n+1, ℂ) for n ≥ 1 and D_n = so(2n, ℂ) for n ≥ 4, plus the three exceptional algebras E_6, E_7, E_8. 'Simply-laced' means all roots have the same length, equivalently the Dynkin diagram has only single edges. The classification is the most famous and structurally productive instance of the ADE pattern in mathematics; the other ADE classifications in this project (finite SU(2) subgroups, Du Val singularities, SU(2)_k WZW modular invariants, representation-finite quivers) all correspond to the ADE Lie algebras through structured bijections, formalized in v15 as typed cross-classification edges.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Finite-dimensional complex simple Lie algebras with simply-laced root systems. Excludes: non-simply-laced simple Lie algebras (B_n, C_n, F_4, G_2); infinite-dimensional Lie algebras (affine and Kac-Moody); Lie superalgebras; Lie algebras over non-algebraically-closed fields; loop algebras.
nodes.internal_structure: Each Cartan type has a Dynkin diagram: A_n is a path of n nodes; D_n is a path of n-2 nodes with two extra nodes attached at one end (a 'fork'); E_6, E_7, E_8 are 'forks' with one short, one medium, and one long branch of total node-counts 6, 7, 8 respectively.

classification_axes.name: cartan-type
classification_axes.description: The five Cartan-type labels of the simply-laced finite-dimensional simple Lie algebras.

citations: Cartan, E. 1894 'Sur la structure des groupes de transformations finis et continus' thèse Paris (foundational classification theorem establishing that the simple complex Lie algebras consist of the four classical infinite series A_n = sl(n+1, ℂ) / B_n = so(2n+1, ℂ) / C_n = sp(2n, ℂ) / D_n = so(2n, ℂ) plus the five exceptional algebras G_2 / F_4 / E_6 / E_7 / E_8 distinguished by their Dynkin-diagram structure; the integer dim(E_6) = 78 is the dimension of the complex Lie algebra as a vector space and is fixed by the Killing-Cartan combinatorial identity dim(g) = rank + #roots = 6 + 72 = 78, established in this thèse and tabulated identically in every subsequent canonical reference; mathematically exact, T10 uncertainty=null + T13 cell-direct)

Humphreys, J.E. 1972 'Introduction to Lie Algebras and Representation Theory' Springer-Verlag Graduate Texts in Mathematics 9 (canonical Lie-algebra textbook; §11 classifies root systems through Cartan matrices and Dynkin diagrams; §12 establishes the existence and uniqueness of the corresponding simple Lie algebras; appendix tables 1-2 tabulate the exceptional-Lie-algebra invariants — for E_6: rank r = 6, dimension dim(E_6) = 78, Coxeter number h = 12, dual Coxeter number h^∨ = 12 (E-series is simply-laced so h = h^∨), number of roots #Φ = 72 with #Φ^+ = 36 positive roots, with the Killing-Cartan combinatorial identity dim(E_6) = rank + #Φ = 6 + 72 = 78 verified explicitly; the integer dim(E_6) = 78 is a Lie-algebra classification invariant established in the Killing-Cartan classification and recorded identically across the Lie-theory literature)

Fulton, W., Harris, J. 1991 'Representation Theory: A First Course' Springer-Verlag Graduate Texts in Mathematics 129 chapter 22 (rep-theoretic Lie-algebra treatment with explicit construction of the exceptional Lie algebras; §22.1-22.4 cover the E_6 construction via the Freudenthal magic-square framework, the realization as the automorphism group of the bi-octonion plane, and the fundamental representations; the dimension dim(E_6) = 78 is tabulated explicitly together with the smallest non-trivial faithful representation of dimension 27 — the standard 'cubic-surface' fundamental rep with the 27 lines on a smooth cubic surface as the canonical realization — and the two 27-dimensional representations are dual; standard reference for the exceptional-Lie-algebra invariants in rep-theoretic context)

Bourbaki, N. 1968 'Groupes et algèbres de Lie' chapitres IV, V, et VI Hermann (canonical French treatise on Lie algebras and root systems; planche V tabulates the E_6 root system with full Cartan-matrix data, the explicit 72 root coordinates in ℝ^6, the Coxeter number h = 12, the dual Coxeter number h^∨ = 12, the dimension dim(E_6) = 78, the order of the Weyl group |W(E_6)| = 51840, and the fundamental-representation degrees 27, 27, 78, 351, 351, 2925; English translation 'Lie Groups and Lie Algebras: Chapters 4-6' Springer 2002 retains the planche numbering; canonical reference for the standardized exceptional-Lie-algebra invariant tables across the international Lie-theory community)

nodes.label: ADE finite SU(2) subgroups
nodes.full_name: Finite subgroups of SU(2) (Klein 1884)
nodes.description: The classification of finite subgroups of SU(2) up to conjugation. Two infinite series (cyclic Z_n and binary dihedral BD_{4n}) plus three sporadic cases (binary tetrahedral 2T, binary octahedral 2O, binary icosahedral 2I). The McKay correspondence (McKay 1980) gives a bijection between these finite SU(2) subgroups and the ADE Dynkin diagrams.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Finite subgroups of SU(2), equivalently finite subgroups of the unit quaternions S³. Excludes: continuous SU(2) subgroups; finite subgroups of higher SU(N); finite subgroups of SO(3) directly.
nodes.internal_structure: Each finite SU(2) subgroup Γ acts on ℂ² by its defining representation. The McKay quiver of Γ is built by taking the irreducible representations of Γ as vertices and drawing edges according to the decomposition of (2-dim defining rep) ⊗ (irrep V_i) into irreps.

classification_axes.name: cartan-type
classification_axes.description: ADE labels via the McKay correspondence.
classification_axes.citation: McKay 1980, in Proc. Sympos. Pure Math. 37, 183

cells.content: Cyclic groups Z_{n+1} of order n+1; abelian
Binary dihedral BD_{4(n-2)} of order 4(n-2) for n ≥ 4; non-abelian
Binary tetrahedral 2T of order 24

citations: McKay, J. 1980 'Graphs, singularities, and finite groups' Proceedings of Symposia in Pure Mathematics 37:183-186 American Mathematical Society (foundational correspondence paper establishing the bijection between finite subgroups of SU(2) up to conjugation and the simply-laced extended Dynkin diagrams via the character-table extended-Dynkin construction; tabulates the five families of finite SU(2) subgroups — cyclic Z_n / binary dihedral BD_{4n} / binary tetrahedral 2T / binary octahedral 2O / binary icosahedral 2I — with explicit subgroup orders n / 4n / 24 / 48 / 120 paired with the Dynkin diagrams A_{n−1} / D_{n+2} / Ẽ_6 / Ẽ_7 / Ẽ_8 respectively; the binary tetrahedral group 2T of order 24 is the double cover SU(2) → SO(3) of the tetrahedral rotation group T = A_4 of order 12 and corresponds to the exceptional E_6 Dynkin diagram under the McKay bijection; the subgroup order |2T| = 24 is a mathematically exact existence-classification integer established in the McKay-correspondence enumeration; mathematically exact, T10 uncertainty=null + T13 cell-direct)

Coxeter, H.S.M., Moser, W.O.J. 1972 'Generators and Relations for Discrete Groups' Springer-Verlag Ergebnisse der Mathematik und ihrer Grenzgebiete vol. 14, 3rd edition (the later 4th edition Springer 1980 retains the same content); §6.5-6.7 establish the canonical Coxeter presentations of the polyhedral groups and their binary (SU(2)) double covers, including the binary tetrahedral group 2T = ⟨R, S | R^3 = S^3 = (RS)^2⟩ of order 24; the order |2T| = 24 is the double of |T| = 12 since the central element −I in SU(2) gives a Z_2 central extension of the rotation group T = A_4; canonical group-theory reference with full presentation tables for all five finite SU(2) subgroup families)

Klein, F. 1884 'Vorlesungen über das Ikosaeder und die Auflösung der Gleichungen vom fünften Grade' Teubner Leipzig (historical anchor: the original treatment of the polyhedral symmetry groups in the context of solving the quintic equation; chapters I-II catalogue the tetrahedral, octahedral, and icosahedral rotation groups T / O / I of orders 12 / 24 / 60, of which the tetrahedral group T = A_4 of order 12 has the binary cover 2T of order 24 as its preimage in SU(2) → SO(3); English translation 'Lectures on the Icosahedron and the Solution of Equations of the Fifth Degree' Dover 1956 retains the original numbering; the binary covers of the polyhedral groups underlie the modern McKay correspondence and the ADE classification of Du Val singularities)

Slodowy, P. 1980 'Simple Singularities and Simple Algebraic Groups' Springer Lecture Notes in Mathematics 815 §6 (canonical treatment connecting binary SU(2) subgroups to Du Val surface singularities via the McKay correspondence; §6.1 tabulates the five families of finite SU(2) subgroups with their canonical orders — cyclic |Z_{n+1}| = n+1, binary dihedral |BD_{4(n−2)}| = 4(n−2), binary tetrahedral |2T| = 24, binary octahedral |2O| = 48, binary icosahedral |2I| = 120 — paired with the corresponding ADE Dynkin diagrams A_n / D_n / E_6 / E_7 / E_8; for the E_6 case |2T| = 24, §6.2 gives the explicit character-table extended-Dynkin construction and the realization of the corresponding Du Val singularity ℂ²/2T as the polynomial equation x^4 + y^3 + z^2 = 0; standard reference for the ADE-correspondence in singularity-theory context)

Coxeter, H.S.M., Moser, W.O.J. 1972 'Generators and Relations for Discrete Groups' Springer-Verlag Ergebnisse der Mathematik vol. 14, 3rd edition; §6.5-6.7 establish the canonical Coxeter presentations of the polyhedral groups and their binary (SU(2)) double covers, including the binary icosahedral group 2I = ⟨R, S | R^3 = S^5 = (RS)^2⟩ of order 120; the order |2I| = 120 is the double of |I| = 60 since the central element −I in SU(2) gives a Z_2 central extension of the rotation group I = A_5; the binary icosahedral group is the largest of the finite SU(2) subgroups and is the unique perfect group of order 120 — it is the Schur double cover of A_5; canonical group-theory reference with full presentation tables for all five finite SU(2) subgroup families)

Klein, F. 1884 'Vorlesungen über das Ikosaeder und die Auflösung der Gleichungen vom fünften Grade' Teubner Leipzig (historical anchor: the original treatise on the icosahedral group in the context of solving the quintic equation by transcendental methods — the icosahedral group I = A_5 of order 60 is the largest polyhedral rotation group and the main subject of the book; the binary cover 2I of order 120 — Klein's 'icosahedral group' in its SU(2) representation as the symmetry group of the regular icosahedron in ℝ^3 lifted to its double cover — is the perfect group of order 120 corresponding under the McKay bijection to the exceptional E_8 Dynkin diagram; English translation 'Lectures on the Icosahedron and the Solution of Equations of the Fifth Degree' Dover 1956 retains the original numbering)

Slodowy, P. 1980 'Simple Singularities and Simple Algebraic Groups' Springer Lecture Notes in Mathematics 815 §6 (canonical treatment connecting binary SU(2) subgroups to Du Val surface singularities via the McKay correspondence; §6.1 tabulates the five families of finite SU(2) subgroups with their canonical orders including binary icosahedral |2I| = 120 — the largest finite SU(2) subgroup; for the E_8 case, §6.2 gives the explicit character-table extended-Dynkin construction with all 9 nodes of the affine Ẽ_8 diagram obtained from the 9 conjugacy classes (equivalently 9 irreducible representations) of 2I, and the realization of the corresponding Du Val singularity ℂ²/2I as the polynomial equation x^5 + y^3 + z^2 = 0 — the Kleinian E_8 singularity; standard reference for the ADE-correspondence in singularity-theory context)

closure_status.description: Complete. Klein (1884) classified the finite rotation groups of the sphere; McKay (1980) recognized the ADE correspondence.

citations: Klein 1884, Vorlesungen über das Ikosaeder und die Auflösung der Gleichungen vom fünften Grade
McKay 1980, in Proc. Sympos. Pure Math. 37, 183
Reid 1997, arXiv:alg-geom/9702016

nodes.label: ADE Du Val singularities
nodes.full_name: Du Val / Kleinian / rational double point surface singularities (ADE)
nodes.description: The classification of isolated rational double points on complex algebraic surfaces, equivalently quotient singularities ℂ²/Γ where Γ ⊂ SU(2) is finite. The resolution attaches a configuration of (-2)-curves whose intersection graph is the corresponding ADE Dynkin diagram.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Isolated rational double points on complex algebraic or analytic surfaces. Excludes: higher-dimensional singularities; non-rational singularities; non-quotient singularities.

classification_axes.name: cartan-type
classification_axes.description: ADE Cartan type.

cells.content: D_n: x²y + y^{n-1} + z² = 0 for n ≥ 4; ℂ²/BD_{4(n-2)}
E_6: x⁴ + y³ + z² = 0; ℂ²/2T

citations: Du Val, P. 1934 'On isolated singularities of surfaces which do not affect the conditions of adjunction' Proceedings of the Cambridge Philosophical Society 30:453-491 (foundational paper defining the singularities now called Du Val singularities; establishes the ADE classification of rational double points on complex algebraic surfaces by showing that the dual graph of the exceptional divisor in the minimal resolution is one of the ADE Dynkin diagrams; identifies the quotient construction ℂ²/Γ where Γ ⊂ SU(2) is one of the finite binary polyhedral groups — for the E-series specifically the binary tetrahedral 2T (E_6), binary octahedral 2O (E_7), binary icosahedral 2I (E_8) — and tabulates these groups as the load-bearing covering groups of the singularity link; historical origin of the ADE classification of surface singularities)

Slodowy, P. 1980 'Simple Singularities and Simple Algebraic Groups' Springer Lecture Notes in Mathematics 815 §6 (canonical treatment of the McKay correspondence and Brieskorn theorem with explicit tabulations of the binary polyhedral group orders |Γ| paired with the ADE singularity equation and Dynkin diagram type — §6.1 tabulates |2T| = 24 (E_6), |2O| = 48 (E_7), |2I| = 120 (E_8); establishes that the singularity ℂ²/Γ has link a spherical space form S³/Γ with fundamental group π_1^loc = Γ; standard reference for the ADE-correspondence in the singularity-theory context; already cited at the FC top-level on ade-du-val)

Brieskorn, E. 1971 'Singular elements of semi-simple algebraic groups' Actes du Congrès International des Mathématiciens (Nice 1970) volume 2:279-284 Gauthier-Villars Paris (foundational theorem connecting simple singularities to simple algebraic groups via the ADE classification; identifies the Du Val singularity of type X∈ADE with the singular fiber of the adjoint quotient map of the corresponding simple Lie group at a subregular nilpotent element; via this identification the local fundamental group of the singularity link coincides with the binary polyhedral group Γ ⊂ SU(2) of corresponding ADE type, giving |π_1^loc(E_6)| = |2T| = 24, |π_1^loc(E_7)| = |2O| = 48, |π_1^loc(E_8)| = |2I| = 120; cornerstone of the modern Brieskorn-Slodowy connection between singularity theory and Lie theory; already cited at the FC top-level on ade-du-val)

McKay, J. 1980 'Graphs, singularities, and finite groups' Proceedings of Symposia in Pure Mathematics 37:183-186 American Mathematical Society (foundational correspondence paper establishing the bijection between finite subgroups Γ ⊂ SU(2) up to conjugation and the ADE-type extended Dynkin diagrams via the McKay quiver / character-table extended-Dynkin construction; for the E-series Du Val singularities ℂ²/Γ, the McKay correspondence identifies Γ as 2T / 2O / 2I with orders 24 / 48 / 120 paired with E_6 / E_7 / E_8 respectively; same correspondence cited in sub-PR 37 on ade-su2-subgroups, making sub-PR 39 the direct parallel-anchor partner that records the same physical fact through the singularity-theory framing)

Reid, M. 1987 'Young person's guide to canonical singularities' Proceedings of Symposia in Pure Mathematics 46(1):345-414 American Mathematical Society (modern pedagogical reference for Du Val singularities; §3 tabulates the binary polyhedral group Γ ⊂ SU(2) paired with the singularity equation and the ADE Dynkin diagram type, giving |Γ| = 24 / 48 / 120 for E_6 / E_7 / E_8; standard accessible introduction to the ADE classification of rational double points and the Brieskorn-Slodowy connection)

cells.content: E_7: x³y + y³ + z² = 0; ℂ²/2O

cells.content: E_8: x⁵ + y³ + z² = 0; ℂ²/2I

nodes.label: ADE SU(2)_k modular invariants
nodes.full_name: Cappelli-Itzykson-Zuber classification of modular invariants of the SU(2)_k WZW model
nodes.description: The classification of modular-invariant partition functions for the SU(2) Wess-Zumino-Witten conformal field theory at level k. The A-series invariant exists at all levels; D-series at even levels; E-series at sporadic single levels (k = 10, 16, 28). v15 retrofit: level axis values now typed via axis_value_ranges (A, D) or single integers (E_6, E_7, E_8), replacing the v14 string-encoded workaround.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Modular-invariant partition functions for the SU(2)_k WZW model in 2D rational conformal field theory.

classification_axes.name: cartan-type
classification_axes.description: ADE Cartan type of the modular invariant.

classification_axes.name: level
classification_axes.description: WZW level k (positive integer). v15: cells specify level coverage via axis_values (single point) or axis_value_ranges (ranges).

cells.content: Diagonal modular invariant Z_A = Σ_{λ=0}^{k} |χ_λ|²; labeled A_{k+1}
cells.description: The diagonal modular invariant is the universal partition function present at every level. v15 retrofit: level coverage now typed as axis_value_range with kind=all.

cells.content: Z_2-orbifold modular invariant of the diagonal; labeled D_{k/2+1}
cells.description: The D-series invariants are Z_2 orbifolds of the A-series at even level. v15 retrofit: level coverage now typed as axis_value_range with kind=every-nth, n=2, offset=0, min=4.

cells.content: Exceptional modular invariant tied to su(2) ⊂ sp(4); exists only at level k = 10
cells.description: Sporadic E_6 modular invariant. v15 retrofit: level is now an integer (10), not a string.

citations: Cappelli, A., Itzykson, C., Zuber, J.B. 1987 'Modular invariant partition functions in two dimensions' Nucl. Phys. B 280 [FS18]:445-465 (foundational classification paper establishing E_6-type exceptional modular invariant of the su(2)_k WZW theory at the unique sporadic level k = 10; the invariant Z_{E_6} = |χ_0 + χ_6|² + |χ_3 + χ_7|² + |χ_4 + χ_{10}|² combines five of the eleven primary characters at level k = 10 (the A_1^(1) integrable weights λ = 0, 1, ..., 10) into three diagonal blocks; the existence-criterion k = 10 matches k = h^∨ − 2 with h^∨(E_6) = 12 the dual Coxeter number of the E_6 exceptional Lie algebra; encoded value 10 is the integer existence-criterion level — sharp / two-sided, not a bound; mathematically exact, T10 uncertainty=null + T13 cell-direct; the conformal-embedding interpretation in subsequent literature realizes this invariant as the k = 1 WZW theory on Sp(4) = Sp(2,ℂ) under conformal embedding into the diagonal SU(2))

Cappelli, A., Itzykson, C., Zuber, J.B. 1987 'The A-D-E classification of A_1^(1) and minimal conformal invariant theories' Commun. Math. Phys. 113:1-26 (companion proof paper presenting the detailed and complete demonstration of the A-D-E classification; establishes that the set of all modular-invariant sesquilinear forms Σ N_{λμ} χ_λ χ̄_μ in the characters of the affine A_1^(1) = su(2)_k Kac-Moody algebra with non-negative integer coefficients and normalized vacuum coefficient is exhaustively enumerated by the A-D-E Dynkin diagrams; canonical reference for the labeling convention adopted across the rational-CFT literature)

Di Francesco, P., Mathieu, P., Sénéchal, D. 1997 'Conformal Field Theory' Springer-Verlag chapter 17 (canonical textbook treatment of rational conformal field theories and the modular-invariant classification; §17.3-17.6 cover the explicit construction of the su(2)_k characters, the modular S and T matrices, the proof structure of the A-D-E classification, and the explicit partition-function forms of the exceptional E_6 / E_7 / E_8 invariants at their respective sporadic levels k = 10 / 16 / 28; treatment establishes the level-value as an existence-criterion integer fixed by the dual Coxeter number of the exceptional Dynkin diagram via k = h^∨ − 2)

Gannon, T. 2000 'The Cappelli-Itzykson-Zuber A-D-E classification' Reviews in Mathematical Physics 12:739-748 (modern review summarizing the A-D-E classification and providing a substantially simplified 'modern' proof using the rich mathematical structure ignored by the original argument; confirms the canonical exceptional-level assignments k = 10, 16, 28 for E_6, E_7, E_8 and discusses the deeper structural reasons (rank-level duality, conformal embeddings into Sp(4), G_2, F_4 at level 1) underlying these sporadic existence criteria)

cells.content: Exceptional modular invariant; exists only at level k = 16
cells.description: Sporadic E_7 modular invariant. v15 retrofit: level is now integer (16).

citations: Cappelli, A., Itzykson, C., Zuber, J.B. 1987 'Modular invariant partition functions in two dimensions' Nucl. Phys. B 280 [FS18]:445-465 (foundational classification paper establishing the E_7-type exceptional modular invariant of the su(2)_k WZW theory at the unique sporadic level k = 16; the invariant Z_{E_7} = |χ_0 + χ_{16}|² + |χ_4 + χ_{12}|² + |χ_6 + χ_{10}|² + |χ_8|² + (χ_8 (χ̄_2 + χ̄_{14}) + c.c.) combines characters at level k = 16 (the A_1^(1) integrable weights λ = 0, 1, ..., 16) into diagonal and off-diagonal blocks reflecting the D_{10}-type non-trivial automorphism; the existence-criterion k = 16 matches k = h^∨ − 2 with h^∨(E_7) = 18 the dual Coxeter number of the E_7 exceptional Lie algebra; encoded value 16 is the integer existence-criterion level — sharp / two-sided, not a bound; mathematically exact, T10 uncertainty=null + T13 cell-direct; the E_7 invariant is the only su(2)_k exceptional invariant whose construction is not realized by a conformal embedding into a higher-rank Lie group, making it the most structurally subtle of the three exceptionals)

Cappelli, A., Itzykson, C., Zuber, J.B. 1987 'The A-D-E classification of A_1^(1) and minimal conformal invariant theories' Commun. Math. Phys. 113:1-26 (companion proof paper presenting the detailed and complete demonstration of the A-D-E classification; establishes that the set of all modular-invariant sesquilinear forms Σ N_{λμ} χ_λ χ̄_μ in the characters of the affine A_1^(1) = su(2)_k Kac-Moody algebra with non-negative integer coefficients and normalized vacuum coefficient is exhaustively enumerated by the A-D-E Dynkin diagrams; canonical reference for the labeling convention adopted across the rational-CFT literature)

Di Francesco, P., Mathieu, P., Sénéchal, D. 1997 'Conformal Field Theory' Springer-Verlag chapter 17 (canonical textbook treatment of rational conformal field theories and the modular-invariant classification; §17.3-17.6 cover the explicit construction of the su(2)_k characters, the modular S and T matrices, the proof structure of the A-D-E classification, and the explicit partition-function forms of the exceptional E_6 / E_7 / E_8 invariants at their respective sporadic levels k = 10 / 16 / 28; treatment establishes the level-value as an existence-criterion integer fixed by the dual Coxeter number of the exceptional Dynkin diagram via k = h^∨ − 2)

Gannon, T. 2000 'The Cappelli-Itzykson-Zuber A-D-E classification' Reviews in Mathematical Physics 12:739-748 (modern review summarizing the A-D-E classification and providing a substantially simplified 'modern' proof using the rich mathematical structure ignored by the original argument; confirms the canonical exceptional-level assignments k = 10, 16, 28 for E_6, E_7, E_8 and discusses the deeper structural reasons (rank-level duality, conformal embeddings into Sp(4), G_2, F_4 at level 1) underlying these sporadic existence criteria)

cells.content: Exceptional modular invariant tied to su(2) ⊂ E_8 at level 1; exists only at level k = 28
cells.description: Sporadic E_8 modular invariant. v15 retrofit: level is now integer (28). The level k=28 corresponds to the dual Coxeter number of E_8 minus 2.

citations: Cappelli, A., Itzykson, C., Zuber, J.B. 1987 'Modular invariant partition functions in two dimensions' Nucl. Phys. B 280 [FS18]:445-465 (foundational classification paper establishing the E_8-type exceptional modular invariant of the su(2)_k WZW theory at the unique sporadic level k = 28; the invariant Z_{E_8} = |χ_0 + χ_{10} + χ_{18} + χ_{28}|² + |χ_6 + χ_{12} + χ_{16} + χ_{22}|² combines eight of the twenty-nine primary characters at level k = 28 (the A_1^(1) integrable weights λ = 0, 1, ..., 28) into two diagonal blocks of four characters each, matching the embedding pattern of su(2) ⊂ E_8 at level one; the existence-criterion k = 28 matches k = h^∨ − 2 with h^∨(E_8) = 30 the dual Coxeter number of the E_8 exceptional Lie algebra; encoded value 28 is the integer existence-criterion level — sharp / two-sided, not a bound; mathematically exact, T10 uncertainty=null + T13 cell-direct; the conformal embedding su(2)_{28} ⊂ (E_8)_1 explains the structure of this exceptional invariant as the branching pattern of the (E_8)_1 vacuum character into the su(2)_{28} primary characters)

Cappelli, A., Itzykson, C., Zuber, J.B. 1987 'The A-D-E classification of A_1^(1) and minimal conformal invariant theories' Commun. Math. Phys. 113:1-26 (companion proof paper presenting the detailed and complete demonstration of the A-D-E classification; establishes that the set of all modular-invariant sesquilinear forms Σ N_{λμ} χ_λ χ̄_μ in the characters of the affine A_1^(1) = su(2)_k Kac-Moody algebra with non-negative integer coefficients and normalized vacuum coefficient is exhaustively enumerated by the A-D-E Dynkin diagrams; canonical reference for the labeling convention adopted across the rational-CFT literature)

Di Francesco, P., Mathieu, P., Sénéchal, D. 1997 'Conformal Field Theory' Springer-Verlag chapter 17 (canonical textbook treatment of rational conformal field theories and the modular-invariant classification; §17.3-17.6 cover the explicit construction of the su(2)_k characters, the modular S and T matrices, the proof structure of the A-D-E classification, and the explicit partition-function forms of the exceptional E_6 / E_7 / E_8 invariants at their respective sporadic levels k = 10 / 16 / 28; treatment establishes the level-value as an existence-criterion integer fixed by the dual Coxeter number of the exceptional Dynkin diagram via k = h^∨ − 2)

Gannon, T. 2000 'The Cappelli-Itzykson-Zuber A-D-E classification' Reviews in Mathematical Physics 12:739-748 (modern review summarizing the A-D-E classification and providing a substantially simplified 'modern' proof using the rich mathematical structure ignored by the original argument; confirms the canonical exceptional-level assignments k = 10, 16, 28 for E_6, E_7, E_8 and discusses the deeper structural reasons (rank-level duality, conformal embeddings into Sp(4), G_2, F_4 at level 1) underlying these sporadic existence criteria)

closure_status.description: Complete for SU(2)_k. CIZ 1987; Gannon 2000.

citations: Cappelli, Itzykson, Zuber 1987, Nucl. Phys. B 280, 445 and Commun. Math. Phys. 113, 1
Gannon 2000, Rev. Math. Phys. 12, 739
Di Francesco, Mathieu, Sénéchal 1997, Conformal Field Theory, Springer, Ch. 17

nodes.label: ADE quivers (Gabriel)
nodes.full_name: Connected representation-finite quivers (Gabriel's theorem)
nodes.description: Gabriel's theorem (1972): a connected quiver has finitely many indecomposable finite-dim representations iff its underlying graph is an ADE Dynkin diagram. The indecomposable representations are in bijection with positive roots of the corresponding ADE root system.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Connected finite quivers and their finite-dimensional representations over an algebraically closed field. Excludes: infinite quivers; quivers with relations; non-connected quivers.

classification_axes.name: cartan-type
classification_axes.description: ADE Cartan type; underlying graph of the quiver.

cells.content: A_n linear quiver, n vertices; n(n+1)/2 indecomposables (interval modules)
D_n quiver, n vertices for n ≥ 4; n(n-1) indecomposables
E_6 quiver; 36 indecomposables

citations: Gabriel, P. 1972 'Unzerlegbare Darstellungen I' Manuscripta Mathematica 6:71-103 Springer (foundational theorem establishing that a connected quiver has finitely many indecomposable finite-dimensional representations iff its underlying graph is a simply-laced Dynkin diagram of ADE type, and that the indecomposable representations are in bijection with the positive roots of the corresponding ADE root system — Gabriel's bijection; for the E_6 quiver this yields exactly 36 indecomposable representations corresponding to the 36 positive roots of E_6; the count |Φ^+(E_6)| = 36 is the canonical exact value tabulated in every modern quiver-representation-theory reference; published in German; cornerstone of the modern theory of quiver representations and the ADE classification of finite-representation-type algebras; mathematically exact, T10 uncertainty=null + T13 cell-direct)

Bernstein, I.N., Gelfand, I.M., Ponomarev, V.A. 1973 'Coxeter functors and Gabriel's theorem' Russian Mathematical Surveys 28(2):17-32 (alternative proof of Gabriel's theorem via reflection functors and Coxeter functors, establishing the bijection between indecomposable representations and positive roots in a more conceptual framework based on the action of the Weyl group on the representation category; tabulates the explicit indecomposable counts for ADE quivers including 36 for E_6 / 63 for E_7 / 120 for E_8 as positive-root cardinalities; the reflection-functor proof makes the Gabriel bijection structurally explicit by mapping indecomposables to positive roots through iterated reflections; canonical secondary reference for the Gabriel theorem and its modern conceptual underpinning)

Auslander, M., Reiten, I., Smalø, S.O. 1995 'Representation Theory of Artin Algebras' Cambridge Studies in Advanced Mathematics 36 Cambridge University Press chapter VIII (canonical textbook treatment of Auslander-Reiten quivers and ADE finite-representation type; chapter VIII covers Gabriel's theorem with full proof and the explicit construction of the Auslander-Reiten quivers for E_6 / E_7 / E_8 with all 36 / 63 / 120 indecomposables enumerated as positive-root labels; standard graduate-level reference for quiver representation theory in the modern Auslander-Reiten framework with full tables of indecomposable counts)

Assem, I., Simson, D., Skowroński, A. 2006 'Elements of the Representation Theory of Associative Algebras: Volume 1 — Techniques of Representation Theory' London Mathematical Society Student Texts 65 Cambridge University Press chapter VII (modern accessible textbook on quiver representation theory with full proof of Gabriel's theorem; chapter VII gives the explicit construction of the Auslander-Reiten quivers for E_6 / E_7 / E_8 with all 36 / 63 / 120 indecomposables enumerated as positive-root labels of the corresponding ADE root systems; detailed treatment of the reflection-functor proof structure; standard pedagogical reference)

cells.content: E_7 quiver; 63 indecomposables

citations: Gabriel, P. 1972 'Unzerlegbare Darstellungen I' Manuscripta Mathematica 6:71-103 Springer (foundational theorem establishing that a connected quiver has finitely many indecomposable finite-dimensional representations iff its underlying graph is a simply-laced Dynkin diagram of ADE type, and that the indecomposable representations are in bijection with the positive roots of the corresponding ADE root system — Gabriel's bijection; for the E_7 quiver this yields exactly 63 indecomposable representations corresponding to the 63 positive roots of E_7; the count |Φ^+(E_7)| = 63 is the canonical exact value tabulated in every modern quiver-representation-theory reference; published in German; cornerstone of the modern theory of quiver representations and the ADE classification of finite-representation-type algebras; mathematically exact, T10 uncertainty=null + T13 cell-direct)

Bernstein, I.N., Gelfand, I.M., Ponomarev, V.A. 1973 'Coxeter functors and Gabriel's theorem' Russian Mathematical Surveys 28(2):17-32 (alternative proof of Gabriel's theorem via reflection functors and Coxeter functors, establishing the bijection between indecomposable representations and positive roots in a more conceptual framework based on the action of the Weyl group on the representation category; tabulates the explicit indecomposable counts for ADE quivers including 36 for E_6 / 63 for E_7 / 120 for E_8 as positive-root cardinalities; the reflection-functor proof makes the Gabriel bijection structurally explicit by mapping indecomposables to positive roots through iterated reflections; canonical secondary reference for the Gabriel theorem and its modern conceptual underpinning)

Auslander, M., Reiten, I., Smalø, S.O. 1995 'Representation Theory of Artin Algebras' Cambridge Studies in Advanced Mathematics 36 Cambridge University Press chapter VIII (canonical textbook treatment of Auslander-Reiten quivers and ADE finite-representation type; chapter VIII covers Gabriel's theorem with full proof and the explicit construction of the Auslander-Reiten quivers for E_6 / E_7 / E_8 with all 36 / 63 / 120 indecomposables enumerated as positive-root labels; standard graduate-level reference for quiver representation theory in the modern Auslander-Reiten framework with full tables of indecomposable counts)

Assem, I., Simson, D., Skowroński, A. 2006 'Elements of the Representation Theory of Associative Algebras: Volume 1 — Techniques of Representation Theory' London Mathematical Society Student Texts 65 Cambridge University Press chapter VII (modern accessible textbook on quiver representation theory with full proof of Gabriel's theorem; chapter VII gives the explicit construction of the Auslander-Reiten quivers for E_6 / E_7 / E_8 with all 36 / 63 / 120 indecomposables enumerated as positive-root labels of the corresponding ADE root systems; detailed treatment of the reflection-functor proof structure; standard pedagogical reference)

nodes.label: Spacetime symmetry groups
nodes.full_name: Classification of spacetime symmetry groups used in physical theories
nodes.description: The classification of symmetry groups that act on spacetime in physical theories: the Galilean group of Newtonian spacetime, the Poincaré group of Minkowski spacetime, the conformal extension of Poincaré (or its 2D infinite-dimensional analog Virasoro), the (anti-)de Sitter groups of curved-spacetime cosmological backgrounds, and the diffeomorphism group of general relativity. Each is a Lie group (finite-dimensional in most cases; the 2D conformal case is the famous infinite-dimensional exception) whose representations classify particle content and field content in theories invariant under it. Wigner's 1939 classification of the unitary irreducible representations of the Poincaré group — by mass-squared (a Casimir) and spin/helicity — is the single most consequential application: it defines what a 'particle' is in relativistic quantum field theory.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Symmetry groups acting globally on a spacetime manifold (or locally, in the case of Diff), used as the symmetry group of a physical theory's action. Excludes: internal gauge groups (classified separately in the SM rep content entry); discrete spacetime symmetries (P, T, C); supersymmetry extensions.
nodes.internal_structure: The major spacetime symmetry groups are organized by which of the following operations they include: spatial rotations (always present); spatial translations (always present); time translations (always present); spatial boosts (Galilean: instantaneous; Lorentz: relativistic); spatial dilations (conformal only); special conformal transformations (conformal only); cosmological-constant-induced curvature (dS/AdS only); arbitrary smooth diffeomorphisms (Diff only). Most of the simple components of these groups are real forms of complex simple Lie algebras of ADE type — see the outgoing cross-classification edge to ade-lie-algebras.

classification_axes.name: symmetry-type
classification_axes.description: The six major spacetime symmetry families. Each label denotes a FAMILY of groups parameterized by spacetime dimension; cell content names the specific group and notes dimensional dependence.

cells.content: Gal(d) = SO(d-1) ⋉ (translations × boosts) ⋉ time-translation; non-relativistic; instantaneous boosts; central extensions (Bargmann) carry physical mass parameter
realized_examples: Newtonian mechanics
non-relativistic quantum mechanics (with Bargmann central extension)
non-relativistic field theories (cold atoms, condensed matter at low temperature)
cells.description: Galilean group: symmetry group of Newtonian spacetime. The central extension (Bargmann 1954) is required for projective representations in QM.

cells.content: ISO(d-1, 1) = SO(d-1, 1) ⋉ ℝ^d; Lorentz group SO(d-1, 1) ≃ Spin(d-1, 1)/Z_2; unitary irreps classified by Wigner 1939
realized_examples: special relativity
relativistic quantum mechanics
the Standard Model (Poincaré-invariant at energies below the Planck scale)
all of relativistic QFT in flat spacetime
cells.description: Poincaré group: symmetry group of Minkowski spacetime. Wigner's 1939 classification of unitary irreps by (mass², spin/helicity) defines the notion of 'particle' in relativistic QFT.

cells.content: For d ≥ 3: SO(d, 2) ≃ Conf(d-1, 1); finite-dimensional. For d = 2: infinite-dimensional Virasoro × Virasoro with central charge c
realized_examples: conformal field theories at critical points
AdS/CFT (the CFT side)
2D worldsheet of string theory (Virasoro constraints)
cells.description: Conformal group: extension of Poincaré by spatial dilations and special conformal transformations. The d = 2 case is qualitatively different — infinite-dimensional Virasoro algebra.

cells.content: SO(d, 1) acting on d-dimensional de Sitter spacetime (positive cosmological constant); finite-dimensional; no positive-energy unitary irreps
realized_examples: our observed accelerating universe (approximately dS in the far future under ΛCDM)
dS-invariant inflation models
the dS/CFT speculative correspondence
cells.description: de Sitter group: isometry group of de Sitter spacetime. The absence of a globally-defined positive-energy generator has major consequences for the swampland conjectures and dS thermodynamics (Gibbons-Hawking).

cells.content: SO(d-1, 2) acting on d-dimensional anti-de Sitter spacetime (negative cosmological constant); positive-energy unitary irreps exist; isomorphic to Conf(d-2, 1) (the AdS/CFT isomorphism at the group level)
realized_examples: AdS_5 × S^5 in Type IIB string theory
AdS/CFT-based holographic models
exact non-perturbative QFT-like theories with negative cc
cells.description: Anti-de Sitter group. The group-level isomorphism SO(d-1,2) ≃ Conf(d-2,1) is the structural origin of AdS/CFT.

cells.content: Diff(M): the full diffeomorphism group of a smooth manifold M; infinite-dimensional; the gauge group of general relativity
realized_examples: general relativity (all observationally-confirmed gravitational physics)
cells.description: General-coordinate (diffeomorphism) group. Infinite-dimensional; gauge of GR. Source of the 'problem of time' in canonical quantum gravity.

closure_status.description: Complete as a classification of the major spacetime symmetry families. Each family's mathematical structure is fully worked out. Does not extend to: (i) which family holds at the Planck scale (QG frontier); (ii) supersymmetric extensions; (iii) whether spacetime symmetry is fundamental or emergent.

predictive_yield.prediction: Existence and classification of particle types in relativistic QFT: each particle is a unitary irrep of Poincaré, labeled by (mass, spin) or (zero mass, helicity). Higher-spin massless particles (s > 2 in 4D) excluded in interacting theories.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Wigner 1939, Ann. Math. 40, 149
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Every observed elementary particle in the SM fits Wigner's classification.

predictive_yield.prediction: Galilean-invariant non-relativistic QM admits projective representations labeled by mass (Bargmann central extension); mass is a conserved superselection sector in NRQM.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Bargmann 1954, Ann. Math. 59, 1
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: The mass superselection rule is a routine feature of NRQM.

predictive_yield.prediction: 2D conformal field theories at criticality have infinite-dimensional Virasoro symmetry; c < 1 minimal models classified by (p, p') labels (ADE / minimal-model classification).
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Belavin, Polyakov, Zamolodchikov 1984, Nucl. Phys. B 241, 333
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: 2D Ising criticality, tricritical Ising, three-state Potts — matched to minimal-model predictions to high precision

nodes.label: Freed-Hopkins cobordism
nodes.full_name: Freed-Hopkins cobordism classification of invertible topological field theories and symmetry-protected phases
nodes.description: The classification of invertible topological field theories (and the gapped symmetry-protected topological phases they describe) by cobordism. Freed and Hopkins (2016, 2019) proposed that invertible TQFTs in d spacetime dimensions with tangential structure G (specifying orientation, spin, pin, or twisted variants thereof) are classified by [MTG, Σ^{d+1} IZ] — homotopy classes of maps from the Thom spectrum of G to a shift of the Anderson dual of the sphere spectrum. For most cases of physical interest the classification reduces to the torsion subgroup of the (d+1)-dimensional G-bordism group, Ω^G_{d+1}, often combined with its free part. The classification subsumes earlier classifications as special cases: free fermion topological phases (Kitaev's ten-fold way) are recovered for the appropriate tangential structures (Spin, Pin+, Pin-, etc.); bosonic SPT phases match the group-cohomology classification (Chen-Gu-Wen 2011, 2013) in low dimensions; and several Fidkowski-Kitaev-type interaction-induced collapses are computed structurally rather than discovered case-by-case.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Invertible ("deformation classes of") topological field theories and the SPT phases they describe, for matter with internal symmetries acting via a tangential-structure group G on spacetime. Covers fermionic and bosonic SPTs with discrete internal symmetries and U(1)-valued symmetries; covers crystalline-symmetry-protected phases via twisted tangential structures (a technical refinement). Excludes: non-invertible topological phases (true topological order with anyonic excitations — classified separately by modular tensor categories); gapless topological phases; phases with anomalous boundary states that are not fully captured by the cobordism invariant. Also excludes the cosmological-scale generalization to quantum gravity (anomaly cobordism for gravitational-with-internal-symmetry combinations is studied but the full classification is incomplete).
nodes.internal_structure: Each tangential structure G is a specific Lie group or topological group describing the geometric structure on spacetime: SO is orientation; Spin is the double cover of SO (required for fermions on orientable manifolds); Pin± are unoriented analogs of Spin (relevant when time-reversal symmetry is included); Spin^c is Spin × U(1) / Z_2 (relevant when charge conservation is included). The (d+1)-dimensional G-bordism group Ω^G_{d+1} contains the classification information for invertible phases in d spatial dimensions (or d+1 spacetime dimensions, depending on convention). Famous identities: Ω^{Pin-}_2 = Z_8 (Fidkowski-Kitaev's 1d BDI collapse); Ω^{Pin+}_4 = Z_16 (3+1d time-reversal-invariant fermionic SPT, refining the ten-fold way's class AII Z_2 entry to a Z_16 interacting classification). The classification's structure-group axis is canonically the `group` axis kind in the v14 schema (first use in Phase 2 entries).

classification_axes.name: tangential-structure
classification_axes.description: The five most-studied tangential structures in the Freed-Hopkins classification. SO: orientation (bosonic, oriented). Spin: orientation + spin structure (fermionic, no further symmetry). Pin+ / Pin-: unoriented spin structures (fermionic with time-reversal symmetry; the two signs distinguish T² = -1 from T² = +1). Spin^c: Spin structure twisted by U(1) (fermionic with charge conservation). Additional tangential structures exist (Spin × Z_2^F for fermion-parity-twisted theories; Spin × G for various internal symmetry groups G); these are not enumerated here. Notation follows Freed-Hopkins 2016 §1 and the standard references.
classification_axes.citation: Freed, Hopkins 2016, arXiv:1604.06527 (published 2019, Comm. Math. Phys. 374)

classification_axes.name: spacetime-dimension
classification_axes.description: Spacetime dimension d+1 in which the invertible field theory lives (equivalently: dimension d+1 of the closed manifold whose G-bordism class is the relevant classifying invariant). Conventions in the literature vary on whether the indexed dimension refers to the SPT's spatial dimension d or the field theory's spacetime dimension d+1; this entry uses spacetime dimension d, with cell content giving Ω^G_d for clarity. Cells populated for d ∈ {0, 1, 2, 3, 4}; higher dimensions extend the classification but are less physically relevant and are omitted for compactness.

cells.content: Ω^{SO}_0 = ℤ
cells.description: Oriented points with sign; the integer counts oriented zero-manifolds.
No invertible bosonic SPT phases in 1d.
No invertible bosonic SPT phases in 2d (within the strict-bosonic non-anomalous regime).
No invertible bosonic SPT phases in 3d (without further symmetry refinements).

cells.content: Ω^{SO}_4 = ℤ
cells.description: Classified by the signature of the oriented 4-manifold. The corresponding invertible TQFT is the gravitational theta-term (signature action).

cells.content: Ω^{Spin}_0 = ℤ
Ω^{Spin}_1 = ℤ₂
cells.description: The mod-2 invariant; corresponds to the choice of NS vs R sector in 1d fermionic systems.

cells.content: Ω^{Spin}_2 = ℤ₂
cells.description: Classified by the Arf invariant; the famous 2d Arf-Brown invariant of a quadratic refinement of the intersection form.

cells.content: Ω^{Spin}_3 = 0
Ω^{Spin}_4 = ℤ
cells.description: Classified by the signature (more precisely, by σ/16 — the Â-genus, an integer for Spin manifolds by Rokhlin's theorem).

cells.content: Ω^{Pin+}_0 = ℤ₂
Ω^{Pin+}_2 = ℤ₂
Ω^{Pin+}_3 = ℤ₂
Ω^{Pin+}_4 = ℤ₁₆
cells.description: The famous Z_16 classification of 3+1d topological insulators with time-reversal symmetry T² = -1. Refines the ten-fold-way class AII entry (Z_2 at the free-fermion level) to Z_16 in the interacting case. The Z_16 captures multi-particle interaction effects that the free-fermion classification cannot.
realized_examples: The general 3+1d topological insulator with T² = -1, when interactions are included. The free-fermion case is one of 16 distinct interacting phases.

predictions.prediction: Time-reversal-invariant 3+1d fermionic SPT phases form a Z_16 classification when interactions are included, refining the Z_2 free-fermion classification of the ten-fold way's class AII.
predictions.prediction_citation: Wang, Senthil 2014, Phys. Rev. B 89, 195124; Freed, Hopkins 2016
predictions.confirmation_citation: Theoretical confirmation via multiple independent calculations; experimental confirmation is partial (the higher-Z_16 phases require specific interaction-induced phases not yet definitively isolated experimentally).

citations: Kapustin, Thorngren, Turzillo, Wang 2015, JHEP 12:052 arXiv:1406.7329 'Fermionic Symmetry Protected Topological Phases and Cobordisms' (source-value: Ω^{Pin+}_4 ≅ ℤ_16 via Freed-Hopkins cobordism framework — 16 distinct interacting 3+1d fermionic SPT phases with T²=−1 collapse from the free-fermion ℤ classification; mathematically exact group order; encoded as dimensionless count |Ω^{Pin+}_4|=16 [T13 cell-direct, T10 uncertainty=null])

Wang, Senthil 2014, PRX 4:041031 arXiv:1401.1142 'Interacting Fermionic Topological Insulators/Superconductors in Three Dimensions' (physical interpretation: the ℤ → ℤ_16 reduction is realized via fermion-vortex condensation on the 3+1d topological-insulator surface)

cells.content: Ω^{Pin-}_0 = ℤ₂
Ω^{Pin-}_1 = ℤ₂
Ω^{Pin-}_2 = ℤ₈
cells.description: The Fidkowski-Kitaev classification: the Z classification of free fermion class BDI in 1d (ten-fold-way's BDI-d1 cell content) collapses to Z_8 when interactions are included. This is the canonical example of the free-to-interacting reach termination that motivated the topological-phases-classification frontier (added in v14.1).
realized_examples: 1d class BDI topological superconductors with strong interactions — the 8 distinct phases collapse from the free-fermion Z classification.

predictions.prediction: The free-fermion Z classification of 1d class BDI topological superconductors collapses to Z_8 under interactions; equivalently, eight free-fermion BDI phases that are distinct without interactions become continuously deformable into one trivial phase when interactions are turned on.
predictions.prediction_citation: Fidkowski, Kitaev 2010, Phys. Rev. B 81, 134509 (first explicit demonstration); Kitaev 2011 talk on classifications; Freed-Hopkins 2016 (cobordism interpretation)
predictions.confirmation_citation: Theoretical confirmation via multiple independent calculations (Turner-Pollmann-Berg 2011; etc.).

citations: Fidkowski, Kitaev 2010, Phys. Rev. B 81:134509 arXiv:1008.4138 'The effects of interactions on the topological classification of free fermion systems' (source-value: Ω^{Pin-}_2 ≅ ℤ_8 — eight distinct interacting 1+1d class BDI topological superconductor phases collapse from the free-fermion ℤ classification under symmetry-preserving four-fermion interactions; mathematically exact group order; encoded as dimensionless count |Ω^{Pin-}_2|=8 [T13 cell-direct, T10 uncertainty=null])

Kapustin, Thorngren, Turzillo, Wang 2015, JHEP 12:052 arXiv:1406.7329 'Fermionic Symmetry Protected Topological Phases and Cobordisms' (cobordism-framework interpretation: ℤ_8 identified as Ω^{Pin-}_2 at the Freed-Hopkins layer; consistent with the Fidkowski-Kitaev free-fermion calculation)

cells.content: Ω^{Spin^c}_0 = ℤ
Ω^{Spin^c}_1 = 0
Ω^{Spin^c}_2 = ℤ
cells.description: Classified by the first Chern number; corresponds to the integer quantum Hall effect's TKNN invariant (Thouless-Kohmoto-Nightingale-den Nijs 1982).
realized_examples: Integer quantum Hall effect (von Klitzing 1980; classified by Chern number)

citations: von Klitzing, Dorda, Pepper 1980, Phys. Rev. Lett. 45:494 'New method for high-accuracy determination of the fine-structure constant based on quantized Hall resistance' (source-value: integer quantum Hall plateau σ_xy = n·e²/h discovered with n=1 plateau yielding R_K = h/e² ≈ 25812.807 Ω; Hall conductance quantized exactly by topological invariant; now exact by 2019 SI redefinition; encoded as kind=coupling generator value 1.0 in units e²/h [T13 cell-direct, T25 dimensionful-quantum-conductance reuse, T10 uncertainty=null])

Thouless, Kohmoto, Nightingale, den Nijs 1982, Phys. Rev. Lett. 49:405 'Quantized Hall Conductance in a Two-Dimensional Periodic Potential' (TKNN invariant: the Hall conductance is the first Chern class of the Bloch bundle over the Brillouin zone — exactly the Ω^{Spin^c}_2 = ℤ classifying invariant in the cobordism layer)

cells.content: Ω^{Spin^c}_3 = 0
Ω^{Spin^c}_4 = ℤ²
cells.description: Rank-2 free part; classified jointly by the signature and the c_1²/2 invariant (Chern character integrals).

closure_status.description: Partial. The cobordism-classification framework is established and proven; the explicit cobordism groups for the five tangential structures and small dimensions above are computed and standard. What remains partial: (i) higher dimensions and more exotic tangential structures (twisted Spin × G for various G) are not all explicitly computed; (ii) the framework's extension to non-invertible topological phases (true topological order) requires the modular-tensor-category machinery and remains an active research area; (iii) cosmological-scale generalizations (anomaly cobordism for gravity combined with arbitrary symmetry) are partially developed but incomplete. The 'partial' status reflects that the classification is closure-grade for the cases enumerated here but does not extend to the full landscape of SPT and TQFT phases.

predictive_yield.prediction: The free-fermion Z classification of 1d BDI topological superconductors collapses to Z_8 under interactions.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Fidkowski, Kitaev 2010, Phys. Rev. B 81, 134509

predictive_yield.prediction: Time-reversal-invariant 3+1d fermionic SPT phases with T² = -1 form a Z_16 classification when interactions are included, refining the Z_2 free-fermion classification of class AII.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Wang, Senthil 2014, Phys. Rev. B 89, 195124; Kapustin-Thorngren-Turzillo-Wang 2015, JHEP 12, 052; Freed-Hopkins 2016
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Theoretical: established via multiple frameworks. Experimental: partial — the higher-Z_16 phases require interaction-induced phases not yet isolated experimentally.

predictive_yield.prediction: The integer quantum Hall effect's TKNN invariant (first Chern number) is the Ω^{Spin^c}_2 = Z cobordism invariant; this provides a structural derivation rather than a phenomenological one.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Freed-Hopkins 2016 (structural derivation)
predictive_yield.description: The IQHE was observed in 1980 (von Klitzing); the TKNN invariant interpretation came in 1982. The cobordism-theoretic re-derivation in Freed-Hopkins is retro-explanatory — it provides a uniform framework but did not predict the IQHE.

predictive_yield.prediction: The full classification of interacting fermionic SPT phases for arbitrary tangential structures in arbitrary dimensions reduces to computing cobordism groups Ω^G_{d+1}, a task that the algebraic-topology machinery (Adams spectral sequence, etc.) makes systematically computable.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Freed, Hopkins 2016
predictive_yield.description: Methodological prediction. Not unconfirmed in the sense of being challenged, but the prediction is about a research program's reach; many cases remain to be computed even where the framework is accepted as correct.

nodes.label: SU(5) GUT rep content
nodes.full_name: Georgi-Glashow SU(5) grand unified theory matter content
nodes.description:
The matter content classification under the Georgi-Glashow SU(5) GUT, which embeds the Standard Model gauge group SU(3)_c × SU(2)_L × U(1)_Y into SU(5) as a subgroup. Each Standard Model fermion generation is contained in two SU(5) representations: the 5̄ (conjugate fundamental, containing L_L and d_R^c) and the 10 (antisymmetric tensor, containing Q_L, u_R^c, and e_R^c). The gauge bosons of SU(5) decompose into the 12 SM gauge bosons (8 gluons + 3 W + 1 B) plus 12 additional 'leptoquark' X and Y bosons that mediate proton decay and unify quarks with leptons.

The classification's status is conjectural: the SU(5) rep content is a well-defined mathematical proposal, but its empirical confirmation requires proton decay at a rate consistent with SU(5)'s prediction (lifetime ~10^29 - 10^31 years, depending on the GUT-scale value), which is in tension with current experimental bounds from Super-Kamiokande (lifetime > 1.6 × 10^34 years for p → e+ π^0). Minimal SU(5) is ruled out; non-minimal variants (SUSY SU(5), missing-doublet SU(5), etc.) remain viable but conjectural.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Energies above the SU(5) unification scale (~10^15 - 10^16 GeV depending on the specific GUT model). Below this scale, the SU(5) symmetry is broken to the SM gauge group and the SU(5) rep content decomposes into SM multiplets via the conjectured embedding. Does not apply if SU(5) is not the correct unification group (alternative GUTs include SO(10), E_6, Pati-Salam, and supersymmetric variants of each).
nodes.internal_structure: Standard SU(5) embedding (per generation): each SM generation sits in 5̄ ⊕ 10 of SU(5). Decomposition: 5̄ → (1, 2, -1/2)_SM ⊕ (3̄, 1, +1/3)_SM = L_L ⊕ d_R^c. 10 → (3, 2, +1/6)_SM ⊕ (3̄, 1, -2/3)_SM ⊕ (1, 1, +1)_SM = Q_L ⊕ u_R^c ⊕ e_R^c. The right-handed neutrino is not part of the SU(5) matter content (it would be a singlet under SU(5)); minimal SU(5) is therefore neutrino-mass-silent. Gauge boson decomposition: 24 (adjoint) → (8, 1, 0) ⊕ (1, 3, 0) ⊕ (1, 1, 0) ⊕ (3, 2, -5/6) ⊕ (3̄, 2, +5/6). The last two are the leptoquark X and Y bosons mediating proton decay.

classification_axes.name: su5-rep
classification_axes.description: The three SU(5) representations populated in the Georgi-Glashow GUT: the 5̄ (conjugate fundamental) and 10 (antisymmetric tensor) for matter, and the 24 (adjoint) for gauge bosons. Higher-rank reps (45, 75, etc.) appear in non-minimal SU(5) extensions for Higgs sectors and additional matter.

classification_axes.name: generation
classification_axes.description: Generation index for matter content (5̄, 10); 'N/A' for the gauge-boson adjoint rep (which does not replicate over generations).

cells.content: 24 (adjoint of SU(5)): 12 SM gauge bosons (8 gluons + 3 W + 1 B) + 12 X/Y leptoquarks of charge ±4/3 and ±1/3 mediating proton decay

closure_status.description: Conjectural. The SU(5) rep content is well-defined mathematically (the 5̄ + 10 per generation embedding is unique up to the obvious freedoms), but its empirical confirmation depends on observing predictions specific to SU(5) — primarily proton decay. Minimal (non-supersymmetric) SU(5) is ruled out by current proton-decay non-observation; supersymmetric SU(5) and other variants remain viable but unconfirmed. The classification is therefore a structural proposal awaiting empirical settlement, not an established closure-within-domain.

predictive_yield.prediction: Proton decay via tree-level X/Y leptoquark exchange, with lifetime τ_p ~ M_GUT^4 / (g^4 m_p^5) ≈ 10^29 to 10^31 years for minimal SU(5).

predictive_yield.description: Minimal non-supersymmetric SU(5)'s proton-decay prediction is ruled out by Super-Kamiokande's lower bound. Supersymmetric SU(5) raises the GUT scale to ~10^16 GeV and pushes the proton lifetime to ~10^34 - 10^36 years, currently at the edge of experimental sensitivity.

nodes.label: SU(5) GUT program
nodes.full_name: SU(5) Grand Unified Theory program (Georgi-Glashow and successors)
nodes.description: The theoretical research program initiated by Georgi-Glashow (1974) proposing that the SM gauge group SU(3)_c × SU(2)_L × U(1)_Y is the low-energy remnant of an SU(5) grand unified gauge symmetry. The program has produced minimal (non-supersymmetric) SU(5), supersymmetric SU(5) (Dimopoulos-Raby-Wilczek 1981), various non-minimal variants (missing-doublet, flipped SU(5)), and serves as the canonical exemplar of grand unification. The program is the most-studied GUT and the structural template for SO(10), E_6, and other GUT programs.
nodes.scope: BSM particle physics; gauge unification; proton decay; magnetic monopoles; baryogenesis from GUT-scale physics.

candidate_targeting.annotation: SU(5) GUT does not solve the EW-GUT hierarchy (this requires SUSY or other mechanism layered on top).

citations: Georgi, Glashow 1974, Phys. Rev. Lett. 32, 438
Langacker 1981, Phys. Rep. 72, 185 (review of grand unification at the time)
Mohapatra 2003, Unification and Supersymmetry, Springer (textbook review)
nodes.placement_rationale: Stratum 2b (candidate-foundational architecture). Sits alongside other unification / candidate-foundational programs (AdS/CFT, tensor networks, NCG, causal sets). Distinct from those in that SU(5) GUT targets a specific BSM extension rather than a general structural reformulation; included here to demonstrate the v15.3 mechanism for unification programs.

nodes.label: CFT bootstrap exclusion regions
nodes.full_name: Conformal-bootstrap exclusion regions for unitary conformal field theories
nodes.description: The classification of unitary conformal field theories (CFTs) by the allowed regions in operator-dimension / OPE-coefficient parameter space cut out by the conformal-bootstrap equations. The bootstrap takes as input only conformal symmetry (associativity of the operator-product expansion plus crossing symmetry of four-point functions) together with unitarity (positivity of OPE coefficient squares) and outputs rigorous bounds on what operator spectra are compatible with these axioms in a given spacetime dimension and global-symmetry sector. The modern numerical bootstrap programme (Rattazzi-Rychkov-Tonni-Vichi 2008; El-Showk-Paulos-Poland-Rychkov-Simmons-Duffin-Vichi 2012) has produced (i) the highest-precision determination of 3D Ising critical exponents on record, (ii) sharp bounds on 4D N=4 super-Yang-Mills, 6D (2,0) and 3D N≥4 superconformal theories, (iii) carving-out of the allowed parameter space for O(N) vector models, Gross-Neveu-Yukawa, and a growing list of other universality classes, and (iv) rigorous structural constraints on the central-charge ratios of any unitary CFT. Each (d, CFT-family) cell records the structural shape of the exclusion region — what the bootstrap has shown to be allowed, excluded, or pinned to an isolated point ('kink' or 'island') in its parameter space.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Unitary, Poincaré-invariant, conformally-invariant quantum field theories with a finite-dimensional operator subspace at each scaling dimension and a discrete spectrum of conformal primaries. Spacetime dimensions 2 ≤ d ≤ 6 (with analytic continuation between integers a routine practice). Excludes: non-unitary CFTs (logarithmic CFTs, percolation-type theories; the bootstrap can be extended to these but the unitarity-based positivity machinery is absent); CFTs with continuous spectra (gauge theories on the conformal manifold without isolated primaries); theories with non-local operators that violate associativity of the conformal OPE; d > 6 (no interacting unitary CFTs exist for d > 6 by Nahm's theorem on superconformal algebras, and free-field bootstraps in higher d give no new bounds); d < 2 (a different conformal-algebra structure applies).
nodes.internal_structure: Each four-point function of identical scalar primaries ⟨φφφφ⟩ must satisfy the crossing equation in s/t-channel form: (Σ_{O} |λ_{φφO}|² g_{Δ_O,ℓ_O}(u,v)) = ((u/v)^{Δ_φ}) (Σ_{O} |λ_{φφO}|² g_{Δ_O,ℓ_O}(v,u)), where g_{Δ,ℓ}(u,v) is a conformal block (Dolan-Osborn closed forms in even d; series expansions otherwise) and the sum runs over primaries O exchanged in φ × φ. Unitarity requires |λ|² ≥ 0 and Δ ≥ Δ_unitarity(ℓ, d). The bootstrap algorithm (semidefinite programming via Simmons-Duffin's sdpb code) searches for linear functionals α acting on the crossing equation that yield α(crossing) ≥ 0 on all unitary operator spectra; finding such a functional excludes any spectrum violating the assumed gaps. The exclusion regions are the loci in (Δ_lowest-scalar, Δ_stress-tensor, central charge c, OPE coefficients ...) where no excluding functional has been found. 'Islands' arise when assumed gaps plus crossing localize the allowed region to a small disconnected piece, as for the 3D Ising point (Δ_σ, Δ_ε) ≈ (0.5181489, 1.412625).

classification_axes.name: spacetime-dimension
classification_axes.description: Spacetime dimension d. Integer values name physical CFTs in standard d-dimensional Minkowski/Euclidean signature; non-integer values name the analytic continuation in d used by the Wilson-Fisher ε = 4 − d expansion (Wilson-Fisher 1972) and by the continuous-d bootstrap programme (Henriksson et al. 2022; El-Showk-Paulos-Rychkov 2014). The bootstrap admits this continuation because the conformal blocks and crossing equation depend on d analytically. Axis kind `continuous` because both integer d cells and range-of-d cells appear; this is the first use of the continuous axis kind in the project.

classification_axes.name: cft-family
classification_axes.description: Global-symmetry / CFT-family label for the bootstrap problem. `Ising-Z2` is the unique unitary CFT with Z_2 global symmetry and a single relevant Z_2-odd scalar above the unitarity bound. `O(N)-vector` is the family of O(N)-symmetric CFTs with a vector-rep scalar; N enters as a parameter inside the cell content. `Gross-Neveu-Yukawa` is the fermion-scalar family with chiral-Yukawa interaction. `minimal-models-Virasoro` is the d=2 series of (p,q) minimal models with c < 1 (Belavin-Polyakov-Zamolodchikov 1984). `free-scalar` is the Gaussian fixed point in any d. `max-SCFT` is the maximally supersymmetric SCFT in each dimension (3D N=8, 4D N=4, 6D (2,0)). `N=2-SCFT-4d` is the 4D N=2 superconformal family (chiral-algebra protected sector; Beem-Lemos-Liendo-Peelaers-Rastelli-van Rees 2015). `stress-tensor-only` is the bootstrap problem with no global symmetry assumed beyond conformal invariance, asking only about the stress-tensor four-point function (central-charge bounds, Hofman-Maldacena conformal-collider bounds).
classification_axes.citation: Poland, Rychkov, Vichi 2019, Rev. Mod. Phys. 91, 015002 (comprehensive review of the modern bootstrap programme)

cells.content: Belavin-Polyakov-Zamolodchikov minimal models M(p,q) with central charge c = 1 − 6(p−q)²/(pq); unitary subseries M(m+1,m) at c = 1 − 6/(m(m+1)), m ≥ 2, including Ising (m=3, c=1/2), tricritical Ising (m=4, c=7/10), 3-state Potts (m=5, c=4/5), …
realized_examples: 2D Ising critical point at T_c (Onsager 1944; bootstrap-recovered as the m=3 minimal model)
tricritical Ising point in dilute Ising magnet
3-state Potts model at criticality
Lee-Yang edge singularity (non-unitary; M(2,5))
cells.description: The d=2 bootstrap is exactly solvable for the discrete minimal-model series because the conformal algebra is the infinite-dimensional Virasoro algebra rather than the finite-dimensional so(d,2). The Virasoro null-state conditions plus modular invariance close the bootstrap completely (BPZ 1984; Friedan-Qiu-Shenker 1984 for the unitarity restriction). The exclusion region in the (Δ_σ, c) plane reduces to the discrete BPZ lattice. This cell is the canonical case where the bootstrap programme's input axioms (conformal invariance + unitarity + OPE associativity + crossing) directly produce the full classification without numerical methods.
citations: Belavin, Polyakov, Zamolodchikov 1984, Nucl. Phys. B 241, 333
Friedan, Qiu, Shenker 1984, Phys. Rev. Lett. 52, 1575

cells.content: Free compact boson at c = 1; T-dual moduli space parametrized by radius R; Gaussian fixed line
realized_examples: 2D classical XY model at zero magnetic field, low-T Kosterlitz-Thouless phase
free chiral boson (one chirality, c = 1/2 per chirality after pairing)
cells.description: The c = 1 line is the upper edge of the unitary minimal-model series and the most-studied non-minimal-model 2D CFT. The bootstrap recovers the entire family via U(1) current algebra plus T-duality.

citations: Belavin A A, Polyakov A M, Zamolodchikov A B 1984, Nucl. Phys. B 241:333 — 'Infinite conformal symmetry in two-dimensional quantum field theory' (BPZ; foundational construction of 2D CFTs from Virasoro symmetry, identifying the free compact boson and the c=1 line as the canonical c=1 unitary representation; source-value: c=1 EXACT for the c=1 representation of Virasoro algebra at the free-scalar / free-compact-boson fixed point).

Friedan D, Qiu Z, Shenker S 1984, Phys. Rev. Lett. 52:1575 — 'Conformal invariance, unitarity, and critical exponents in two dimensions' (establishes the c=1 boundary of the minimal-model unitary series M(m+1, m); c=1 is the limit point m→∞ corresponding to the free compact boson; canonical unitarity-classification proof that c=1 is the EXACT central charge of the free-scalar fixed point).

Di Francesco P, Mathieu P, Sénéchal D 1997, 'Conformal Field Theory' (Springer Graduate Texts in Contemporary Physics) §6.3 — canonical textbook treatment of the c=1 free-boson CFT; T10 uncertainty=null for the theoretically-exact central charge value.

cells.content: 2D Ising CFT: c = 1/2, Δ_σ = 1/8, Δ_ε = 1; exact spectrum from M(4,3) minimal model
realized_examples: Lattice Ising model at the Onsager critical temperature
Free Majorana fermion in 2D (equivalent by Jordan-Wigner)
cells.description: The 2D Ising cell coincides with the m=3 entry of cell-bs-d2-virasoro and is recorded separately to make the d=2 entry of the Ising-Z2 column explicit. The bootstrap-recovered exponents Δ_σ = 1/8 and Δ_ε = 1 match Onsager (1944) exactly because the algebra closes.

citations: Onsager L 1944, Phys. Rev. 65:117 — 'Crystal statistics. I. A two-dimensional model with an order-disorder transition' (exact closed-form solution of the 2D Ising model on the square lattice; the canonical 2D Ising universality class with η = 1/4 exact, from which Δ_σ = (d-2+η)/2 = 1/8 exact in d=2; landmark exactly-solvable statistical-mechanics result).

Belavin A A, Polyakov A M, Zamolodchikov A B 1984, Nucl. Phys. B 241:333 — 'Infinite conformal symmetry in two-dimensional quantum field theory' (identifies the 2D Ising universality class with the minimal model M(4,3) at c=1/2; the spin operator σ has conformal weight (h, hbar) = (1/16, 1/16) giving Δ_σ = 2·(1/16) = 1/8 EXACT; source-value: Δ_σ = 1/8 = 0.125 EXACT via the M(4,3) minimal-model identification).

Friedan D, Qiu Z, Shenker S 1984, Phys. Rev. Lett. 52:1575 — 'Conformal invariance, unitarity, and critical exponents in two dimensions' (Kac-formula unitarity proof of the M(m+1,m) minimal series including M(4,3) Ising; the exact-rational exponent set ν=1, η=1/4, Δ_σ=1/8, Δ_ε=1, β=1/8, γ=7/4, δ=15, α=0-with-log; T26 reuse: 1/8 = 0.125 exact rational encoded as decimal; T10 uncertainty=null because the value is mathematically exact via the M(4,3) closed-form derivation, not measured; T13 cell-direct first-instance on cft-bootstrap-exclusion-regions; BIDIRECTIONAL-ANCHOR-CLOSURE with cell-crit-Ising-2D-Onsager (real-critical-systems sub-PR 11 v53) which anchors the same physics via η = 1/4 = 0.25 across the established `specializes` cross-classification edge real-critical-systems → cft-bootstrap-exclusion-regions; relation η = 2Δ_σ - (d-2) = 2(1/8) - 0 = 1/4 in d=2).

cells.content: Isolated 'island' in (Δ_σ, Δ_ε) space; current best bootstrap values Δ_σ = 0.5181489(10), Δ_ε = 1.412625(10); central charge c_T/c_T^{free} = 0.9466930(11)

Curie point of uniaxial ferromagnets
Order-disorder transition in binary alloys
Lattice 3D Ising model at T_c (Monte Carlo confirmation to comparable precision)
