cells.content: Lowest scalar glueball: lattice QCD predicts mass ≈ 1500-1700 MeV with J^P^C = 0++. Mendeleev-style empty cell — predicted but not unambiguously identified. Candidate states f_0(1500) and f_0(1710) have significant glueball admixture but mix with conventional q-qbar states.
realized_examples: f_0(1500) — leading glueball candidate (disputed)
f_0(1710) — alternative glueball candidate (disputed)
Lattice prediction: m ≈ 1730 MeV (Morningstar-Peardon 1999), 1500 MeV (recent SU(3) lattice)
cells.description: The lowest scalar glueball is one of QCD's most famous Mendeleev-style predictions: lattice QCD predicts its existence and mass, but mixing with light scalar mesons makes unambiguous identification difficult. Disputed identification with f_0(1500) or f_0(1710). Active search at GlueX (JLab), LHCb, and lattice studies. Status: unconfirmed-tension.
citations: Morningstar, Peardon 1999, Phys. Rev. D 60, 034509 (lattice glueball spectrum)
Amsler, Close 1996, Phys. Rev. D 53, 295 (glueball-q-qbar mixing analysis)
Klempt, Zaitsev 2007, Phys. Rep. 454, 1 (glueball candidates review)

S. Navas et al. (Particle Data Group) 2024, Phys. Rev. D 110, 030001 'Review of Particle Physics' (source-value: f₀(1500) mass m = 1522 ± 25 MeV/c² from PDG Meson Listings 2024 average; total width Γ = 108 ± 33 MeV; J^P^C = 0++; the f₀(1500) is widely regarded as the leading observed candidate for the lattice-QCD-predicted scalar glueball, with the alternative candidate f₀(1710) at m = 1733 ± 8 MeV; the glueball-identification of f₀(1500) is contested — competing interpretations include (i) predominantly glueball, (ii) predominantly q-qbar with glueball mixing, (iii) higher-Fock-state of f₀(1370); T13 cell-direct; T10 uncertainty is the PDG quoted ±25 MeV — NOT null; contested-candidate-evidence inclusion precedent (sub-PR 9 first-use on TI/TSC cells [6], [7]; reapplied sub-PR 13 on tenfold-way cells [12], [13]): cells whose realized-example identification is literature-contested but whose characteristic scale is literature-anchored receive qs entries with the dispute explicit in source-value; split-attachment with FC-pred[4] which anchors the Morningstar-Peardon 1999 lattice prediction m(0++) = 1730 ± 94 MeV — cell-direct here records the leading observed candidate, FC-pred records the lattice prediction)

C. Amsler, F. E. Close 1996, Phys. Rev. D 53, 295 arXiv:hep-ph/9507326 'Is f₀(1500) a Scalar Glueball?' (source-value: foundational glueball-interpretation paper for f₀(1500), arguing that the OZI-suppressed decay-pattern of f₀(1500) into ππ, KK̄, ηη, ηη' is consistent with predominantly-glueball content; the contested-glueball identification of this cell)

W.-J. Lee, D. Weingarten 1999, Phys. Rev. D 61, 014015 arXiv:hep-lat/9910008 'Scalar Quarkonium Masses and Mixing with the Lightest Scalar Glueball' (source-value: lattice study of glueball-q-qbar mixing supporting f₀(1710) rather than f₀(1500) as predominantly-glueball — illustrative of the long-standing literature dispute over which observed scalar candidate is the glueball; the cell records the leading candidate f₀(1500) per the Amsler-Close interpretation and PDG convention)

cells.content: Light 1-+ hybrid meson: predicted by lattice QCD at ≈ 1.8-2.0 GeV. The 1-+ J^P^C is exotic (forbidden for pure q-qbar mesons), so observation of any 1-+ light meson is direct evidence of hybrid content. Candidates π_1(1400) and π_1(1600) exist with disputed interpretations; η_1(1855) discovered by BESIII 2022 is the cleanest candidate to date.
realized_examples: π_1(1400) — early candidate, contested
π_1(1600) — better-established candidate, hybrid interpretation favored but not universal
η_1(1855) — BESIII 2022 discovery, isoscalar hybrid candidate
cells.description: Hybrid mesons contain explicit gluonic excitation (q-qbar-g content) and can carry exotic J^P^C. The 1-+ light hybrid is the canonical lattice-QCD-predicted exotic-quantum-number state. Multiple experimental candidates exist but unambiguous identification of a hybrid meson remains an active question. Status: unconfirmed-tension / partial.
citations: Lacock, Michael, Boyle, Rowland (UKQCD) 1996, Phys. Lett. B 401, 308 (lattice 1-+ hybrid)
Adams et al. (E852) 1998, Phys. Rev. Lett. 81, 5760 (pi1(1600) candidate)
Ablikim et al. (BESIII) 2022, Phys. Rev. Lett. 129, 192002 (η_1(1855) discovery)

S. Navas et al. (Particle Data Group) 2024, Phys. Rev. D 110, 030001 'Review of Particle Physics' (source-value: π₁(1600) mass m = 1661 +15/-11 MeV/c² from PDG Meson Listings 2024 average; J^P^C = 1-+ which is the exotic combination forbidden for pure q-qbar mesons — observation of any 1-+ light meson is by itself direct evidence of non-q-qbar content (hybrid q-qbar-g, tetraquark, or other); total width Γ = 240 ± 50 MeV; the π₁(1600) is the better-established 1-+ candidate than the lighter contested π₁(1400); the hybrid-interpretation of π₁(1600) is favored by lattice and most quark-model analyses but is not universally accepted — competing interpretations include tetraquark or non-resonant rescattering; T13 cell-direct; contested-candidate-evidence convention (sub-PR 9 first-use, sub-PR 13 reuse): cells with literature-contested realization but literature-anchored characteristic scale receive qs entries with dispute explicit in source-value; split-attachment with FC-pred[7] which anchors the UKQCD 1996 lattice prediction m(1-+) = 2.0 ± 0.2 GeV — cell-direct here records the observed candidate at ~1.66 GeV, FC-pred records the lattice prediction at ~2.0 GeV with the discrepancy between observed candidate and lattice prediction itself a structural fact)

M. Aghasyan et al. (COMPASS Collaboration) 2018, Phys. Rev. D 98, 092003 arXiv:1802.05913 'Light isovector resonances in π- p → π-π-π+ p at 190 GeV/c' (source-value: COMPASS partial-wave analysis confirming π₁(1600) as a 1-+ exotic resonance with mass 1600 +110/-60 MeV — the leading post-2010 experimental anchor for the π₁(1600) hybrid interpretation)

cells.content: d*(2380) dibaryon candidate: a 6-quark state with quantum numbers I(J^P) = 0(3+), mass ≈ 2380 MeV, observed by WASA-at-COSY 2014. The first dibaryon resonance with credible experimental support. Status: partial — observed by one collaboration with consistent signal across multiple decay modes; awaits independent confirmation at higher statistics.
realized_examples: d*(2380) — WASA-at-COSY 2014, mass 2380 MeV, width 70 MeV
cells.description: Dibaryons (6-quark exotic states) are predicted by various quark-model and lattice-QCD calculations. The H-dibaryon (a J^P=0+ uuddss state) was famously predicted by Jaffe 1977 but never observed despite extensive searches. The d*(2380) is the first credible dibaryon candidate; interpretations include ΔΔ-bound-state, hexaquark, and others. Active confirmation effort at JPARC and other facilities. Status: partial.
citations: Bashkanov et al. (WASA-at-COSY) 2014, Phys. Lett. B 736, 327 (d*(2380))
Jaffe 1977, Phys. Rev. Lett. 38, 195 (H-dibaryon prediction — not confirmed)
Clement 2017, Prog. Part. Nucl. Phys. 93, 195 (dibaryon review)

P. Adlarson et al. (WASA-at-COSY Collaboration) 2014, Phys. Rev. Lett. 112, 202301 arXiv:1402.6844 'Evidence for a New Resonance from Polarized Neutron-Proton Scattering' (source-value: d*(2380) dibaryon candidate mass m = 2380 MeV/c² and total width Γ = 70 MeV/c² (±10 MeV/c² typical uncertainty on the mass from the combined WASA-at-COSY pn-scattering analyses 2009-2014); quantum numbers I(J^P) = 0(3+); first credible experimental support for a strong-interaction-narrow dibaryon resonance — the d*(2380) is interpreted as a 6-quark state (uuuddd hexaquark, dineutron-diproton mixed isospin), as a ΔΔ molecule, or as a more complex multiquark configuration; T13 cell-direct; status established by the WASA-at-COSY program but not yet independently confirmed by another collaboration at comparable precision)

S. Navas et al. (Particle Data Group) 2024, Phys. Rev. D 110, 030001 'Review of Particle Physics' (source-value: PDG 2024 Further States compilation entry d*(2380) listed as observed-but-unconfirmed dibaryon resonance candidate; the canonical experimental existence is established by the WASA-at-COSY program)

cells.content: T_cc+(3875): doubly-charm tetraquark with quark content ccūd̄, J^P=1+, observed by LHCb 2021. The first confirmed doubly-heavy tetraquark and an unambiguously exotic state.
realized_examples: T_cc+(3875) — LHCb 2021/2022, mass 3875 MeV, very narrow width
cells.description: Doubly-charm tetraquark with content ccūd̄ — manifestly exotic (a four-quark state with two charm quarks of the same flavor, which cannot occur in any q-qbar meson). Theoretically predicted with significant interest because doubly-heavy tetraquarks have been argued to be stable or near-stable (Karliner-Rosner 2017). LHCb confirmation in 2021 established the doubly-heavy-exotic sector.
citations: Aaij et al. (LHCb) 2022, Nat. Commun. 13, 3351 (Tcc+)
Karliner, Rosner 2017, Phys. Rev. Lett. 119, 202001 (prediction of stable Tcc)

R. Aaij et al. (LHCb Collaboration) 2022, Nature Physics 18, 751 arXiv:2109.01038 'Observation of an exotic narrow doubly charmed tetraquark' (source-value: discovery measurement m(T_cc+(3875)) determined via mass-difference δm_pole = -273 ± 61 keV/c² from D*+D⁰ threshold = 3875.10 MeV/c² giving absolute mass m(T_cc+) = 3874.83 ± 0.10 MeV/c² with the ±0.10 MeV combining the unitarized Breit-Wigner pole-determination uncertainty and D-meson threshold-input uncertainty; J^P = 1+, I = 0; total width Γ = 410 keV — strikingly narrow given proximity to threshold; quark content cc̄ūd̄ — first confirmed doubly-charm tetraquark and first hadron with two charm quarks and no antiquark of the same flavor; T13 cell-direct; split-attachment with FC-pred[3] which anchors the Karliner-Rosner 2017 prediction m(T_cc) = 3882 ± 12 MeV — cell-direct here records the observed mass 3874.83 ± 0.10 MeV, FC-pred records the prediction; the prediction lies 7 MeV above the observed value, well within the prediction's ±12 MeV uncertainty — Karliner-Rosner-prediction confirmed)

R. Aaij et al. (LHCb Collaboration) 2022, Nature Communications 13, 3351 arXiv:2109.01056 'Study of the doubly charmed tetraquark T_cc+' (source-value: companion LHCb paper performing the unitarized resonance-pole analysis that establishes the precision mass-difference quoted in arXiv:2109.01038; the two papers together establish T_cc+(3875) as a near-threshold isoscalar tetraquark)

predictions.prediction: Pre-discovery predictions of a doubly-charm ccu-d-bar tetraquark molecular state (Janc-Rosina 2004; Karliner-Rosner 2017) predicted very narrow width below the D*+D0 threshold; the narrow-width prediction discriminates between loosely-bound molecular interpretations (very narrow) and compact-tetraquark interpretations (broader).
predictions.prediction_citation: Janc, Rosina 2004, Few-Body Syst. 35, 175, arXiv:hep-ph/0405208 (ccu-d-bar molecular tetraquark prediction); Karliner, Rosner 2017, 'Discovery of doubly-charmed Xi_cc baryon implies a stable bb-u-d-bar tetraquark', Phys. Rev. Lett. 119, 202001, arXiv:1707.07666 (mass and stability predictions for ccu-d-bar near D*D threshold)
predictions.confirmation_citation: LHCb Collaboration: Aaij et al. 2022, 'Observation of an exotic narrow doubly charmed tetraquark', Nature Physics 18, 751, arXiv:2109.01038; companion paper 'Study of the doubly charmed tetraquark T_cc+', Nature Communications 13, 3351, arXiv:2109.01056 (Breit-Wigner width measurement Gamma_BW = 410 +/- 165 (stat) +/- 43 (syst) +18/-38 (model) keV, with unitarised pole-width analysis giving Gamma_pole = 48 +/- 2 +0/-14 keV consistent with molecular interpretation)
predictions.description: The T_cc+ tetraquark observed by LHCb in 2022 at mass M = 3874.83 MeV (273 +/- 61 keV below D*+D0 threshold) carries Breit-Wigner width Gamma_BW = 410 +/- 165 keV; the unitarised pole analysis yields the more physically meaningful Gamma_pole = 48 +/- 2 keV, in close agreement with pre-discovery molecular-tetraquark predictions in the ~50-65 keV range (e.g. Janc-Rosina, Yan-Pavon Valderrama 2022 at 49 +/- 16 keV LO, 58 +/- 7 keV NLO). The BW value is encoded here for cluster-consistency with the literature-most-cited number; the source-value parenthetical notes the BW-vs-pole interpretive distinction.

citations: LHCb Collaboration: Aaij et al. 2022, 'Observation of an exotic narrow doubly charmed tetraquark', Nature Physics 18, 751, arXiv:2109.01038 (source-value: 'Gamma = 410 +/- 165 (stat) +/- 43 (syst) +18/-38 keV' from Breit-Wigner fit; here T11 stat ⊕ syst quadrature combination sqrt(165^2 + 43^2) ≈ 170 keV with the +18/-38 keV model-threshold component absorbed into the symmetric uncertainty for cluster-consistency with the 4 width entries in sub-PR 15; pole-width unitarised analysis in the same paper gives Gamma_pole = 48 +/- 2 +0/-14 keV — the BW value 410 is the literature-most-cited number and is recorded here per sub-PR 14 precedent of using the literature-headline value)

cells.content: Predicted doubly-bottom tetraquark T_bb (bbū-d̄, J^P=1+) at mass ≈ 10.4 GeV. Predicted to be stable against strong decay (Karliner-Rosner 2017; Eichten-Quigg 2017). Mendeleev empty cell — strong theoretical prediction, no experimental observation as of 2026.
realized_examples: T_bb (bbū-d̄) — predicted at m ≈ 10.4 GeV, J^P = 1+
T_bbb-bar — predicted higher-mass extension
cells.description: The doubly-bottom analog of T_cc, predicted by heavy-quark-spin-symmetry arguments to lie below the BB* threshold and therefore be STABLE against strong decay (decaying only weakly with macroscopic lifetime). Discovery would be one of the cleanest confirmations of the doubly-heavy-tetraquark family. Active search at LHCb. Status: unconfirmed-not-yet-tested.
citations: Karliner, Rosner 2017, Phys. Rev. Lett. 119, 202001
Eichten, Quigg 2017, Phys. Rev. Lett. 119, 202002 (independent prediction)

M. Karliner, J. L. Rosner 2017, Phys. Rev. Lett. 119, 202001 arXiv:1707.07666 'Discovery of the Doubly Charmed Ξ_cc Baryon Implies a Stable bb̄ū d̄ Tetraquark' (source-value: predicted doubly-bottom tetraquark T_{bb} = bb̄ūd̄ with J^P = 1+ at mass m(T_{bb}) = 10389 ± 12 MeV/c² via diquark-antidiquark quark-model calculation grounded in the simultaneously-discovered LHCb 2017 Ξ_cc^++ doubly-charmed baryon; predicted to lie 215 MeV below the B⁻B̄⁰* open-bottom threshold (≈ 10605 MeV) and 170 MeV below the B⁻B̄⁰γ electromagnetic-decay threshold; therefore stable against strong and electromagnetic decays — would decay only via weak interaction with macroscopic lifetime, making T_{bb} the first predicted exotic hadron with this property; T13 cell-direct; T28 first-use multi-prediction-envelope encoding: cell-direct value=10436 MeV is the midpoint of two foundational simultaneous-publication literature centrals (KR 10389 MeV + EQ 10482 MeV, midpoint 10435.5 rounded to 10436), uncertainty=58 MeV is the half-width of the literature envelope bracketing KR's full ±12 band on the low edge (10436-58=10378, slightly below KR-low 10377) and reaching the EQ central on the high edge (10436+58=10494, slightly above EQ central; EQ does not quote an explicit uncertainty in PRL 119:202002); the cell-direct encoding mode is distinct from FC-pred[5] which anchors specifically the KR publication's 10389 ± 12 MeV value, while cell-direct encodes the cell's overall characteristic predicted mass scale spanning both canonical predictions; split-attachment with FC-pred[5])

E. J. Eichten, C. Quigg 2017, Phys. Rev. Lett. 119, 202002 arXiv:1707.09575 'Heavy-Quark Symmetry Implies Stable Heavy Tetraquark Mesons Q_iQ_j q̄_k q̄_l' (alternative-prediction: independent simultaneous prediction of doubly-bottom tetraquark T_{bb} stability via heavy-quark symmetry with finite-mass corrections; predicts mass m(T_{bb}) = 10482 MeV, 121 MeV below the open-bottom B⁻B̄⁰* threshold (≈ 10605 MeV); paper does not quote an explicit uncertainty on the 10482 MeV central, so this value is treated as essentially-exact in the T28 envelope construction with KR providing the ±12 MeV low-edge buffer; the two predictions Karliner-Rosner 10389 ± 12 MeV and Eichten-Quigg 10482 MeV were published back-to-back in PRL 119:202001 and 119:202002 as the foundational T_{bb}-prediction pair, with the ~93 MeV difference between centrals reflecting different model methodologies — diquark-antidiquark structure (KR) vs heavy-quark-symmetry projection with finite-mass corrections (EQ); both predict strong/EM stability, both predict weak-decay-only macroscopic-lifetime signatures, both establish T_{bb} as the cleanest Mendeleev-empty-cell target in doubly-heavy tetraquark phenomenology; contested-candidate-evidence inclusion precedent (sub-PRs 9 [TI/TSC cells], 13 [tenfold-way cells], cell-h-glueball-scalar f₀(1500)/f₀(1710) contestation, cell-h-hybrid-1mp π₁(1400)/π₁(1600) contestation) extended to the prediction surface: when multiple canonical literature predictions span a wider scale than any single prediction's own uncertainty band, the cell-direct encodes the envelope and documents the disagreement explicitly in citations)

S. Navas et al. (Particle Data Group) 2024, Phys. Rev. D 110, 030001 'Review of Particle Physics' (source-value: PDG 2024 'Exotic Hadrons' / 'Multiquark States' / 'Tetraquarks' compilation entries acknowledge T_{bb} as the predicted-but-unobserved doubly-bottom tetraquark with theoretical mass estimates in the 10.4-10.5 GeV range from the Karliner-Rosner and Eichten-Quigg 2017 foundational prediction pair; PDG 2024 also documents broader literature spreads from QCD-sum-rules methods (10035 ± 260 MeV, Agaev-Azizi-Sundu 2019) and various lattice-QCD calculations (typical range 10400-10550 MeV with binding 80-150 MeV below B⁻B̄⁰* threshold per Francis-Hudspith-Lewis-Maltman 2017 PRL 118:142001, Junnarkar-Mathur-Padmanath 2019 PRD 99:034507, Mathur-Padmanath-Lewis 2022, RBC/UKQCD 2026 arXiv:2603.12418); LHCb and Belle II upper-limit searches for T_{bb} → B⁻B̄⁰π and weakly-decaying channels are ongoing as of the 2024 review; the cell-direct envelope encoding 10436 ± 58 MeV is anchored on the foundational KR+EQ pair specifically since the cell content names those two predictions; broader lattice/sum-rule consensus on T_{bb} mass remains within the cell-direct envelope at the ±2σ level)

cells.content: structurally-excluded
cells.description: Structurally-excluded: glueballs (gluon-only content) cannot carry baryon number, electric charge, or heavy-flavor quantum numbers. The combination (glueball, charm-baryon-regime) is kinematically meaningless.

cells.content: structurally-excluded
cells.description: Structurally-excluded: a 3-quark baryon (qqq) must carry half-integer total spin (J = 1/2, 3/2, 5/2, ...) by spin-statistics. J^P = 0++ is impossible for any 3-quark configuration.

closure_status.description: Partial. The classification framework (mesons/baryons/exotic categories × J^P^C × mass-regime) is rigorously established. Confirmed cells span hundreds of states catalogued by the Particle Data Group. Substantial Mendeleev-style empty cells remain: (i) the lowest scalar glueball is predicted by lattice QCD but not unambiguously identified — disputed candidates f_0(1500)/f_0(1710); (ii) light hybrid mesons (1-+ J^P^C) have multiple disputed candidates; (iii) the doubly-bottom tetraquark T_bb is predicted but not observed; (iv) the H-dibaryon predicted by Jaffe 1977 was searched extensively and not confirmed; (v) many predicted bottomonium and charmonium higher-radial states await observation; (vi) doubly-bottom baryons (Ξ_bb, Ω_bb) predicted but not observed. The classification framework is closed (the schema covers all known/predicted hadronic content categories), but the specific-cell-realization data is partial — many predicted cells await empirical confirmation or refutation.

predictive_yield.prediction: Ω- baryon (sss, J^P=3/2+) predicted at mass ≈ 1685 MeV by Gell-Mann's SU(3) eightfold-way classification.
predictive_yield.description: Gell-Mann 1962 predicted the Ω- baryon as the missing member of the baryon decuplet, with specific mass ≈ 1685 MeV and quantum numbers I(J^P)=0(3/2+). Discovered at Brookhaven in 1964 at mass 1672 MeV — the cleanest Mendeleev-style hadronic prediction confirmation. The 1% mass-prediction accuracy established SU(3) flavor symmetry as a real organizing principle of hadrons.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Gell-Mann 1962, Phys. Rev. 125, 1067
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Barnes et al. 1964, Phys. Rev. Lett. 12, 204

citations: Gell-Mann 1962, Phys. Rev. 125, 1067 (eightfold-way mass formula prediction M(Ω-) ≈ 1685 MeV from decuplet equal-spacing; no explicit uncertainty stated)

predictive_yield.prediction: Charmonium spectrum (J/ψ, ψ(2S), χ_c states) at GeV-scale masses predicted by GIM mechanism + quark-model arguments.
predictive_yield.description: Glashow-Iliopoulos-Maiani 1970 introduced the charm quark to suppress flavor-changing neutral currents; subsequent quark-model calculations predicted a 1-- c-cbar state at ~3 GeV. J/ψ discovery in November 1974 confirmed this and triggered the 'November Revolution'. The full charmonium spectrum was subsequently mapped out, matching Cornell-potential predictions.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Glashow, Iliopoulos, Maiani 1970, Phys. Rev. D 2, 1285 (GIM mechanism predicting charm)
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Aubert et al. 1974, Phys. Rev. Lett. 33, 1404 (J/psi discovery)

predictive_yield.prediction: Pentaquark states with hidden-charm quark content at ~4.3-4.5 GeV.
predictive_yield.description: Various pentaquark predictions emerged from 2003 onwards; the diquark model (Maiani-Polosa-Riquer 2015) predicted hidden-charm pentaquark states in the 4.3-4.5 GeV range. LHCb discovery in 2015 (P_c(4380), P_c(4450)) and refined spectrum in 2019 (P_c(4312), P_c(4440), P_c(4457)) confirmed the hidden-charm pentaquark sector after decades of failed searches for light pentaquarks (notably the false Θ+ claim of 2003).
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Maiani, Polosa, Riquer 2015, Phys. Lett. B 749, 289
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Aaij et al. (LHCb) 2015, Phys. Rev. Lett. 115, 072001

citations: Maiani, Polosa, Riquer 2015, Phys. Lett. B 749, 289 (diquark-model interpretation of LHCb-observed P_c(4380) and P_c(4450); range 4.3-4.5 GeV encoded as midpoint 4.4 ± 0.1 GeV)

predictive_yield.prediction: Doubly-charm tetraquark T_cc+ at mass ≈ 3875 MeV, stable or near-stable.
predictive_yield.description: Karliner-Rosner 2017 and Eichten-Quigg 2017 (independent calculations) predicted the doubly-charm tetraquark T_cc+ (cc-ūd̄) just below the DD* threshold with J^P=1+. LHCb 2021/2022 confirmed T_cc+(3875) with mass and width matching predictions, establishing the doubly-heavy-tetraquark family.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Karliner, Rosner 2017, Phys. Rev. Lett. 119, 202001
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Aaij et al. (LHCb) 2022, Nat. Commun. 13, 3351

citations: Karliner, Rosner 2017, Phys. Rev. Lett. 119, 202001 (Table II: M(T(ccūd̄)) = 3882 ± 12 MeV, J^P=1+, 7 MeV above D⁰D⁺ threshold at 3875.09 MeV — KR2017 calibration-adjusted value to match observed Ξ_cc is 3876 MeV)

predictive_yield.prediction: Lowest scalar glueball at mass ≈ 1500-1700 MeV with J^P^C=0++.
predictive_yield.description: Lattice QCD predicts a scalar glueball at this mass range (Morningstar-Peardon 1999 quenched-lattice; more recent unquenched lattice studies). Candidates f_0(1500) and f_0(1710) have significant glueball-q-qbar mixing; no consensus has emerged on which is dominantly the glueball, and some authors argue the glueball is split across both. Active experimental search (GlueX, LHCb) and theoretical refinement (unquenched lattice) ongoing. Tension: prediction is sharp; identification is disputed.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Morningstar, Peardon 1999, Phys. Rev. D 60, 034509
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Klempt, Zaitsev 2007, Phys. Rep. 454, 1 (review of disputed identification)

citations: Morningstar, Peardon 1999, Phys. Rev. D 60, 034509 (m(0++) = 1730 ± 50 (stat) ± 80 (anisotropy/syst) MeV from anisotropic quenched lattice; total uncertainty 94 MeV by quadrature √(50² + 80²); note central value 1730 MeV sits 30 MeV above the prediction-text upper bound 1700 MeV — tension flagged in v43 changelog)

predictive_yield.prediction: Doubly-bottom tetraquark T_bb (bb-ūd̄, J^P=1+) at mass ≈ 10.4 GeV, stable against strong decay (decays only weakly).
predictive_yield.description: Karliner-Rosner 2017 and Eichten-Quigg 2017 predicted T_bb to lie below the BB* threshold and thus be STABLE against strong decay. Would decay only weakly with macroscopic lifetime — observation would be one of the cleanest exotic-hadron confirmations possible. No experimental detection as of 2026 despite searches at LHCb and Belle II. Mass and structure predictions are sharp; null result thus far is significant but not yet decisive.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Karliner, Rosner 2017, Phys. Rev. Lett. 119, 202001

citations: Karliner, Rosner 2017, Phys. Rev. Lett. 119, 202001 (Table I: M(T(bbūd̄)) = 10389 ± 12 MeV, J^P=1+, 215 MeV below B⁻B̄⁰ threshold and 170 MeV below B⁻B̄⁰γ threshold — only-weakly-decaying exotic hadron)

predictive_yield.prediction: H-dibaryon (uuddss, J^P=0+) bound state with mass below 2 m(Λ).
predictive_yield.description: Jaffe 1977 predicted an H-dibaryon stable against strong decay with mass below 2 m(Λ) ≈ 2230 MeV. Extensively searched in nuclear-physics experiments through the 1980s-2000s; no clear signal. Modern lattice-QCD calculations (with realistic quark masses) find the H-dibaryon is unbound or only weakly bound above 2 m(Λ). Status: falsified for the original prediction (deeply bound H below threshold); refined predictions of a near-threshold state remain open.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Jaffe 1977, Phys. Rev. Lett. 38, 195
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Inoue et al. (HAL QCD) 2012, Nucl. Phys. A 881, 28 (lattice study finding H near or above threshold)

predictive_yield.prediction: Light hybrid meson with exotic J^P^C=1-+ at mass ≈ 1.8-2.0 GeV.
predictive_yield.description: Lattice QCD predicts light 1-+ hybrid mesons at this mass. Candidates π_1(1400), π_1(1600), and most recently η_1(1855) (BESIII 2022) have been identified but hybrid interpretation remains contested. The exotic quantum numbers 1-+ rule out pure q-qbar interpretation; whether the candidates are hybrids, tetraquarks, or molecular states remains debated. Active analysis at GlueX (JLab) and BESIII.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Lacock, Michael, Boyle, Rowland (UKQCD) 1996, Phys. Lett. B 401, 308
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Ablikim et al. (BESIII) 2022, Phys. Rev. Lett. 129, 192002 (eta1(1855) discovery — interpretation contested)

citations: Lacock, Michael, Boyle, Rowland (UKQCD) 1996, Phys. Lett. B 401, 308 (lightest spin-exotic state is J^PC=1⁻⁺ with mass 2.0(2) GeV from quenched lattice with quark masses near m_s)

predictive_yield.prediction: Doubly-bottom baryon Ξ_bb at mass ≈ 10 GeV.
predictive_yield.description: Quark-model and lattice-QCD calculations predict doubly-bottom baryons at mass ~10 GeV (Ξ_bb at ~10.2 GeV, Ω_bb at ~10.3 GeV). Ξ_cc++ has been confirmed (LHCb 2017); the bb analogs remain unobserved despite searches. Production cross-section at LHC is small but within reach of HL-LHC luminosities. Expected to be observed within the next decade.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Karliner, Rosner 2014, Phys. Rev. D 90, 094007

citations: Karliner, Rosner 2014, Phys. Rev. D 90, 094007 (M(Ξ_bb) = 10162 ± 12 MeV for the J=1/2 ground state; J=3/2 hyperfine partner M(Ξ_bb*) = 10184 ± 12 MeV also given — candidate cell-level entry for Step 4.3)

nodes.label: Fractional quantum Hall states
nodes.full_name: Classification of fractional quantum Hall topological orders in 2D electron systems
nodes.description: Fractional quantum Hall (FQH) states are topologically-ordered quantum phases of 2D electrons in strong perpendicular magnetic fields. They occur at specific filling fractions ν = (electron density n) / (magnetic flux density B/Φ_0), with each state characterized by quantized Hall conductance σ_xy = ν·e²/h, fractionally-charged quasiparticles, anyonic exchange statistics, and topology-dependent ground-state degeneracy. The classification organizes hundreds of observed and predicted FQH states by their hierarchy / composite-fermion family, specific filling fraction, and statistics class (abelian / non-abelian / paired). Each FQH state corresponds (per the topological-order ↔ modular-tensor-category correspondence) to a specific MTC describing its anyon content. The classification is *phenomenological*: cells are specific physical phases observed in 2D electron gases (GaAs heterostructures, graphene, ZnO/MgZnO, and other 2DEG platforms). Predicted-but-unconfirmed cells include the Read-Rezayi parafermion state at ν=12/5 (partial evidence), specific identification of the 5/2 state (Pfaffian vs anti-Pfaffian vs PH-Pfaffian — all candidates, identification disputed), and Fibonacci-anyon-bearing states that would enable universal topological quantum computation.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Topologically-ordered phases of 2D electrons in strong magnetic fields. Includes: Laughlin states (ν=1/3, 1/5, 1/7, ...), Jain composite-fermion sequences (2/5, 3/7, 4/9, ...), Moore-Read Pfaffian and related paired states at half-filled higher Landau levels (5/2, 7/2), Read-Rezayi parafermion states (12/5), particle-hole conjugate states (2/3, 3/5), and hierarchical states (4/11, 5/13). Excludes: integer quantum Hall states (ν=1, 2, 3, ... — these are non-interacting topological phases described by tenfold-way class A, structurally distinct from FQH); fractional topological insulators (3D analogs, separate domain); fractional Chern insulators (zero-magnetic-field lattice analogs).
nodes.organizing_principle: Each FQH state is characterized by its (hierarchy-family, filling-fraction-ν, statistics-class) triple. The hierarchy-family encodes the construction method (Laughlin, composite-fermion-Jain, Moore-Read-Pfaffian, Read-Rezayi parafermion, particle-hole-conjugate, hierarchical). The filling-fraction ν is a specific rational determined by the electron-to-flux ratio. The statistics-class distinguishes abelian (Laughlin, Jain, hierarchical) from non-abelian (Ising-type at 5/2, Fibonacci-type at 12/5 Read-Rezayi) from paired (specific pairing patterns within the half-filled Landau level). Two FQH states have equivalent topological order iff their corresponding MTCs are equivalent — this provides the structural-to-phenomenon bridge.

classification_axes.name: hierarchy-family
classification_axes.description: Construction-family of the FQH state. Laughlin: ν = 1/m, m odd; fully-abelian; Laughlin wavefunction ψ ∝ Π(z_i - z_j)^m exp(-|z|²/4). Composite-fermion-Jain: ν = p/(2p±1); composite-fermion construction (Jain 1989). Moore-Read-Pfaffian: ν=5/2 with Pfaffian pair-wavefunction; non-abelian Ising anyons. Read-Rezayi-parafermion: ν=12/5; non-abelian Fibonacci-like anyons. particle-hole-conjugate: ν → 1-ν gives a related state (e.g., 2/3 is PH-conjugate of 1/3). hierarchical-bosonic: less-common bosonic-construction states. anti-Pfaffian and PH-Pfaffian: alternative pairing patterns at ν=5/2.

classification_axes.name: filling-fraction
classification_axes.description: Specific rational filling fraction ν of the FQH state. Observed FQH states occur at characteristic ν values: simple Laughlin fractions (1/3, 1/5), Jain CF sequences (m/(2m±1) for m=1,2,3,...), half-integer Landau-level states (5/2, 7/2), and exotic states (12/5, 4/11). The Hall conductance is σ_xy = ν · e²/h to extreme precision. Modeling ν as discrete-enum (rather than continuous dimension) reflects that FQH states are SHARP — only specific rational ν's correspond to incompressible phases.

classification_axes.name: statistics-class
classification_axes.description: Anyonic statistics of quasiparticles. abelian: quasiparticle exchanges give phase factors only (Laughlin, Jain, hierarchical states). non-abelian-Ising: SU(2)_2 anyons; quasiparticles span a degenerate Hilbert space; canonical example at Moore-Read 5/2. non-abelian-Fibonacci: Fibonacci anyons supporting universal TQC; predicted at Read-Rezayi 12/5. non-abelian-parafermion-general: Z_k parafermions (k>3) at higher Read-Rezayi states. paired-Pfaffian / paired-PH-Pfaffian / paired-anti-Pfaffian: three competing pairing patterns at ν=5/2 with different edge thermal Hall signatures — the experimental identification is the central open question at 5/2.

cells.content: ν=1/3 Laughlin state. Hall conductance σ_xy = (1/3)e²/h. Quasiparticles have fractional charge e/3 with abelian fractional statistics (exchange phase 2π/3). Ground-state degeneracy 3 on torus. The foundational FQH state — Tsui-Stormer-Gossard 1982 discovery; Laughlin 1983 theory.
realized_examples: ν=1/3 in GaAs heterostructures — original 1982 discovery (Tsui, Stormer, Gossard)
ν=1/3 in graphene (Du et al. 2009; Bolotin et al. 2009)
ν=1/3 in ZnO/MgZnO 2DEG
cells.description: The canonical FQH state. Maps to Z_3 abelian MTC (cell-mtc-r3-z3 in MTC entry). Quasiparticle charge e/3 was the first directly-measured fractional charge (Saminadayar et al. 1997 shot-noise measurement at ν=1/3 confirming e/3).
citations: Tsui, Stormer, Gossard 1982, Phys. Rev. Lett. 48, 1559
Laughlin 1983, Phys. Rev. Lett. 50, 1395 (Laughlin wavefunction)
Saminadayar, Glattli, Jin, Etienne 1997, Phys. Rev. Lett. 79, 2526 (e/3 fractional charge measurement)

Tsui, Stormer, Gossard 1982, Phys. Rev. Lett. 48, 1559 'Two-Dimensional Magnetotransport in the Extreme Quantum Limit' (source-value: σ_xy = (1/3)(e²/h) — the foundational FQH plateau discovery at filling fraction ν=1/3; the observed Hall plateau quantizes to the rational multiplier 1/3 of the conductance quantum e²/h to within experimental precision, breaking the integer-only quantization of the IQHE and motivating the Laughlin-state framework; T13 cell-direct, T25 reuse of dimensionful-quantum-conductance encoding established in sub-PR 9 (BHZ + InSb at integer multiplier 2) now extended to rational p/q multiplier 1/3 — opens T26 first-use mint: rational-fraction encoding for `value` field as decimal p/q with source-value parenthetical preserving the rational p/q form in text under v18 schema's additionalProperties:false on qs object; T10 uncertainty=null for the canonical literature quantization value with sample-to-sample mobility-limited residual at the percent level absorbed)

Laughlin 1983, Phys. Rev. Lett. 50, 1395 'Anomalous Quantum Hall Effect: An Incompressible Quantum Fluid with Fractionally Charged Excitations' (source-value: the Laughlin wavefunction theoretical construction predicts σ_xy = ν(e²/h) at ν=1/m incompressible states with fractionally-charged quasiparticle excitations; ν=1/3 is the canonical anchor confirming the construction)

cells.content: ν=1/5 Laughlin state. Quasiparticle charge e/5; abelian statistics with phase 2π/5. Ground-state degeneracy 5 on torus.
realized_examples: ν=1/5 in high-mobility GaAs (Pan et al. 2002)
cells.description: The next Laughlin state. Less stable than 1/3 (smaller gap); observed in highest-mobility samples. Maps to Z_5 abelian MTC.
citations: Pan et al. 2002, Phys. Rev. Lett. 88, 176802

Pan, Stormer, Tsui, Pfeiffer, Baldwin, West 2002, Phys. Rev. Lett. 88, 176802 'Transition from an electron solid to the sequence of fractional quantum Hall states at very low Landau level filling factor' (source-value: σ_xy = (1/5)(e²/h) Laughlin plateau confirmed at very low Landau level filling in high-mobility samples; the ν=1/5 state sits at the boundary between Laughlin-fluid and Wigner-crystal regimes; T13 cell-direct, T25 reuse, T26 rational-fraction encoding p/q=1/5; T10 uncertainty=null for characteristic literature plateau value)

cells.content: ν=2/5 Jain composite-fermion state. The first 'non-Laughlin' Jain state. Quasiparticle charge e/5. Abelian statistics.
realized_examples: ν=2/5 in GaAs (Willett et al. 1987)
cells.description: The ν=2/5 state was the first to be explained by the composite-fermion construction (Jain 1989). In CF picture, attaching 2 flux quanta to each electron transforms the FQH problem at ν=2/5 into an integer QH problem of composite fermions at filling 2. This explains the Jain sequence ν = p/(2p+1) systematically.
citations: Willett et al. 1987, Phys. Rev. Lett. 59, 1776
Jain 1989, Phys. Rev. Lett. 63, 199 (composite-fermion construction)

Willett, Eisenstein, Stormer, Tsui, Gossard, English 1987, Phys. Rev. Lett. 59, 1776 'Observation of an even-denominator quantum number in the fractional quantum Hall effect' (source-value: σ_xy = (2/5)(e²/h) Jain composite-fermion plateau — first 'non-Laughlin' fractional plateau, confirming the Jain hierarchy via the ν=p/(2p±1) sequence at p=2; T13 cell-direct, T25 reuse, T26 rational-fraction encoding p/q=2/5; T10 uncertainty=null)

Jain 1989, Phys. Rev. Lett. 63, 199 'Composite-fermion approach for the fractional quantum Hall effect' (source-value: composite-fermion construction predicts the full Jain sequence ν=p/(2p+1); ν=2/5 corresponds to composite-fermion filling p=2)

cells.content: ν=3/7 Jain state. CF filling 3.
realized_examples: ν=3/7 in GaAs (Pan et al. 2003)
cells.description: Higher Jain state. Cleanly observed in high-mobility GaAs. Confirms the systematic CF sequence prediction.
citations: Pan et al. 2003, Phys. Rev. Lett. 90, 016801

Willett, Eisenstein, Stormer, Tsui, Gossard, English 1987, Phys. Rev. Lett. 59, 1776 'Observation of an even-denominator quantum number in the fractional quantum Hall effect' (source-value: σ_xy = (3/7)(e²/h) Jain plateau, p=3 in the ν=p/(2p+1) sequence; observed in the Willett 1987 batch of FQH discoveries alongside ν=2/5 and ν=3/5; T13 cell-direct, T25 reuse, T26 rational-fraction encoding p/q=3/7; T10 uncertainty=null)
Jain 1989, Phys. Rev. Lett. 63, 199 (composite-fermion sequence prediction; ν=3/7 maps to CF filling p=3)

cells.content: ν=4/9 Jain state. CF filling 4. Approaching the half-filled Landau level limit.
realized_examples: ν=4/9 in GaAs (Pan et al. 2003)
cells.description: Even higher Jain state. The series ν=p/(2p+1) approaches ν=1/2 as p→∞.
citations: Pan et al. 2003, Phys. Rev. Lett. 90, 016801

Willett, Eisenstein, Stormer, Tsui, Gossard, English 1987, Phys. Rev. Lett. 59, 1776 (source-value: σ_xy = (4/9)(e²/h) Jain plateau, p=4 in the ν=p/(2p+1) sequence approaching the half-filled Landau level ν=1/2 limit; the closer p approaches infinity, the smaller the gap and the harder the plateau is to observe; T13 cell-direct, T25 reuse, T26 rational-fraction encoding p/q=4/9; T10 uncertainty=null)
Jain 1989, Phys. Rev. Lett. 63, 199 (composite-fermion construction with p=4)

cells.content: ν=2/3 particle-hole conjugate of ν=1/3. Same anyon content as 1/3 by PH symmetry within the LLL.
realized_examples: ν=2/3 in GaAs (early FQH experiments)
cells.description: PH-conjugate of the 1/3 Laughlin state. Has the same topological order (same MTC) but inverted edge structure (counter-propagating modes). One of the strongest non-Laughlin FQH plateaus.
citations: MacDonald 1990, Phys. Rev. Lett. 64, 220 (PH conjugation edge structure)

Stormer, Chang, Tsui, Hwang, Gossard, Wiegmann 1983, Phys. Rev. Lett. 50, 1953 'Fractional Quantization of the Hall Effect' (source-value: σ_xy = (2/3)(e²/h) plateau — the particle-hole conjugate of ν=1/3 within the LLL, sharing the same anyon content via PH symmetry; the ν=2/3 state hosts counter-propagating edge modes ν=1 downstream and ν=1/3 upstream which complicate transport-based characterization but the bulk σ_xy plateau is robust; T13 cell-direct, T25 reuse, T26 rational-fraction encoding p/q=2/3; T10 uncertainty=null)
Tsui, Stormer, Gossard 1982, Phys. Rev. Lett. 48, 1559 (the original FQH discovery paper also reports the ν=2/3 minimum alongside ν=1/3)

cells.content: ν=5/2 Moore-Read Pfaffian candidate. Half-filled second Landau level with predicted non-abelian Ising anyon quasiparticles. Pfaffian wavefunction Pf(1/(z_i - z_j)) times Laughlin factor. Universal TQC NOT supported by Ising anyons alone (requires supplementation).
realized_examples: ν=5/2 plateau in highest-mobility GaAs heterostructures (Willett et al. 1987)
Specific Pfaffian-state identification: contested (Pfaffian vs anti-Pfaffian vs PH-Pfaffian)
cells.description: The ν=5/2 plateau is firmly observed; the identification of WHICH non-abelian topological order it realizes (original Pfaffian, anti-Pfaffian, or PH-Pfaffian) remains contested as of 2026. Thermal Hall conductance measurements (Banerjee et al. 2018) suggest PH-Pfaffian is favored, but interpretation is disputed.
citations: Moore, Read 1991, Nucl. Phys. B 360, 362 (Pfaffian wavefunction)
Willett et al. 1987, Phys. Rev. Lett. 59, 1776 (5/2 discovery)
Banerjee et al. 2018, Nature 559, 205 (thermal Hall at 5/2)

Moore, Read 1991, Nucl. Phys. B 360, 362 'Nonabelions in the fractional quantum Hall effect' (source-value: chiral central charge c = 7/2 = 3.5 for the Moore-Read Pfaffian edge theory at ν=5/2 = 2 (filled lower LLs, c=2) + 1 (charged Bose mode of the half-filled second LL, c=1) + 1/2 (downstream chiral Majorana mode from p-wave pairing, c=1/2), summing to c_chiral = 7/2; the chiral central charge IS the discriminating quantitative claim distinguishing the three competing 5/2 candidate topological orders sharing the same σ_xy = (5/2)e²/h plateau; T13 cell-direct, T10 uncertainty=null for pure-theory prediction with the Banerjee 2018 thermal-Hall measurement adjudication recorded at FC-predictive_yield[3] companion via split-attachment maneuver)

Greiter, Wen, Wilczek 1991, Phys. Rev. Lett. 66, 3205 'Paired Hall State at Half Filling' (source-value: original construction of paired-electron Hall states from which the Moore-Read Pfaffian wavefunction emerges; the half-filled second LL c=1 charge mode together with downstream Majorana c=1/2 are the edge-theory components)

cells.content: ν=5/2 anti-Pfaffian alternative. Particle-hole conjugate of Moore-Read Pfaffian within the second Landau level. Different anyonic content (anti-Ising statistics) from original Pfaffian. One of three competing candidates for the 5/2 state.
realized_examples: Theoretical prediction (Levin-Halperin-Rosenow 2007; Lee-Ryu-Nayak-Fisher 2007)
Not unambiguously identified experimentally; competes with Pfaffian and PH-Pfaffian
cells.description: PH-conjugate of original Pfaffian. Predicted by Levin-Halperin-Rosenow and Lee-Ryu-Nayak-Fisher 2007. Distinguishing experimentally from Pfaffian and PH-Pfaffian requires edge thermal Hall measurements with specific signatures.
citations: Levin, Halperin, Rosenow 2007, Phys. Rev. Lett. 99, 236806
Lee, Ryu, Nayak, Fisher 2007, Phys. Rev. Lett. 99, 236807

Levin, Halperin, Rosenow 2007, Phys. Rev. Lett. 99, 236806 'Particle-Hole Symmetry and the Pfaffian State' (source-value: chiral central charge c = 3/2 = 1.5 for the anti-Pfaffian edge theory at ν=5/2 = 2 (filled lower LLs, c=2) + 1 (charged Bose mode, c=1) − 3/2 (counter-propagating upstream neutral modes contributing -3/2 with full thermal equilibration), summing to c_chiral = 3/2; particle-hole conjugate of the Moore-Read Pfaffian within the second Landau level shares the same σ_xy = (5/2)e²/h plateau but predicts a distinctly different thermal Hall conductance; T13 cell-direct, T10 uncertainty=null for pure-theory prediction)

Lee, Ryu, Nayak, Fisher 2007, Phys. Rev. Lett. 99, 236807 'Particle-Hole Symmetry and the ν=5/2 Quantum Hall State' (source-value: independent derivation of the anti-Pfaffian as the particle-hole conjugate of Moore-Read Pfaffian within the partially-filled second LL; the chiral central charge c=3/2 follows from the symmetry analysis and edge-mode counting)

cells.content: ν=5/2 PH-Pfaffian candidate. Particle-hole-symmetric pairing pattern proposed by Son 2015 in the composite-Dirac-fermion framework. Distinct anyon content from Pfaffian and anti-Pfaffian. Thermal Hall measurements (Banerjee 2018) initially appeared consistent with PH-Pfaffian; later analyses dispute this.
realized_examples: Theoretical prediction (Son 2015)
Possibly favored by thermal Hall data (Banerjee et al. 2018) but disputed
cells.description: The third major candidate at ν=5/2. Manifestly preserves particle-hole symmetry within the LLL. The thermal Hall conductance (Banerjee 2018) gave κ_xy ≈ 2.5(πk_B²T/6ℏ), consistent with PH-Pfaffian's predicted (5/2)·(πk_B²T/6ℏ) within experimental uncertainty. Interpretation contested by Simon et al. 2020 (mesoscopic effects) and Yan et al. 2022 (more recent measurements suggesting different conclusions).
citations: Son 2015, Phys. Rev. X 5, 031027 (composite Dirac fermion)
Banerjee et al. 2018, Nature 559, 205
Simon 2020 (interpretation challenges)

Son 2015, Phys. Rev. X 5, 031027 'Is the Composite Fermion a Dirac Particle?' (source-value: chiral central charge c = 5/2 = 2.5 for the PH-Pfaffian edge theory at ν=5/2 — the particle-hole-symmetric pairing pattern proposed within the composite-Dirac-fermion framework; c=5/2 sits between the Moore-Read Pfaffian (c=7/2) and anti-Pfaffian (c=3/2) predictions and is the unique PH-symmetric candidate at the 5/2 plateau; T13 cell-direct, T10 uncertainty=null for pure-theory prediction with the Banerjee 2018 thermal-Hall measurement of κ_xy = 2.5 κ₀ recorded as the matching experimental observation at FC-predictive_yield[3] via split-attachment maneuver — the cell-level qs records the theoretical c value of THIS specific candidate; the FC-pred qs records the measured value adjudicating between candidates)

Zucker, Feldman 2016, Phys. Rev. Lett. 117, 096802 'Stabilization of the Particle-Hole Pfaffian Order by Landau-Level Mixing and Impurities at ν=5/2' (source-value: argues that disorder and Landau-level mixing in real samples can stabilize the PH-Pfaffian over the pure Pfaffian and anti-Pfaffian — relevant context for the Banerjee 2018 interpretation match)

cells.content: ν=12/5 Read-Rezayi state with Fibonacci anyons. Would support UNIVERSAL topological quantum computation by braiding alone. The only proposed FQH state with Fibonacci anyons; observation would be a landmark.
realized_examples: ν=12/5 plateau observed in highest-mobility GaAs (Xia et al. 2004; Choi et al. 2008)
Identification as Read-Rezayi state: partial evidence (numerical match), not unambiguously confirmed
Particle-hole conjugate at ν=13/5 also observed
cells.description: The ν=12/5 plateau is observed but its identification as the Read-Rezayi Z_3 parafermion state (with Fibonacci anyons) remains partial. Numerical exact-diagonalization studies favor RR; experimental signatures (thermal Hall, interferometry) of Fibonacci anyons have not been definitively measured. Status: PARTIAL — phase observed, theoretical identification favored but not confirmed.
citations: Read, Rezayi 1999, Phys. Rev. B 59, 8084 (parafermion state)
Xia et al. 2004, Phys. Rev. Lett. 93, 176809 (12/5 observation)
Bonderson, Slingerland 2008, Phys. Rev. B 78, 125323 (RR identification analysis)

Xia, Stormer, Tsui, Pfeiffer, Baldwin, West, Pan 2004, Phys. Rev. Lett. 93, 176809 'Electron correlation in the second Landau level: A competition between many nearly degenerate quantum phases' (source-value: σ_xy = (12/5)(e²/h) plateau observed in the second Landau level alongside the 5/2 plateau; the 12/5 state is the most promising FQH candidate for hosting Fibonacci anyons under the Read-Rezayi Z₃ parafermion construction, the only proposed FQH state supporting universal topological quantum computation by braiding alone; T13 cell-direct, T25 reuse, T26 rational-fraction encoding p/q=12/5; T10 uncertainty=null)

Bonderson, Slingerland 2008, Phys. Rev. B 78, 125323 'Fractional quantum Hall hierarchy and the second Landau level' (source-value: numerical exact-diagonalization studies of the second Landau level favor the RR Z₃ identification at ν=12/5 over competing candidates, though experimental confirmation is not yet decisive)

cells.content: ν=7/2 Moore-Read Pfaffian analog. Half-filled third Landau level. Less studied than 5/2; the analog non-abelian Ising-anyon state in the third LL.
realized_examples: ν=7/2 plateau observed (less mobile samples)
cells.description: The 5/2 analog in the third Landau level. Weaker plateau; less detailed identification. Predicted to have similar topological content to 5/2 Pfaffian (or anti-Pfaffian / PH-Pfaffian).
citations: Pan et al. 1999, Phys. Rev. Lett. 83, 3530

Pan, Xia, Stormer, Tsui, Adams, Pfeiffer, Baldwin, West 1999, Phys. Rev. Lett. 83, 3530 'Exact Quantization of the Even-Denominator Fractional Quantum Hall State at ν = 5/2 Landau Level Filling Factor' (source-value: σ_xy = (7/2)(e²/h) plateau observed in the third Landau level as the analog of the ν=5/2 even-denominator state; less studied than the 5/2 plateau but believed to host the same Moore-Read-Pfaffian-class non-abelian Ising-anyon topological order; T13 cell-direct, T25 reuse, T26 rational-fraction encoding p/q=7/2 = 3.5 — exact half-integer multiple of e²/h; T10 uncertainty=null)

cells.content: ν=8/3 CF state. In the second Landau level. Particle-hole conjugate of 7/3 (also a Jain state).
realized_examples: ν=8/3 in GaAs second LL (Pan et al. 1999)
cells.description: Higher-LL CF state. Less standard CF physics due to second-LL form factor.
citations: Pan et al. 1999

Xia, Stormer, Tsui, Pfeiffer, Baldwin, West, Pan 2004, Phys. Rev. Lett. 93, 176809 (source-value: σ_xy = (8/3)(e²/h) plateau in the second Landau level; particle-hole conjugate of the ν=7/3 Jain state within the second LL; both ν=7/3 and ν=8/3 plateaus are well-formed and routinely observed in high-mobility 2DEG samples; T13 cell-direct, T25 reuse, T26 rational-fraction encoding p/q=8/3; T10 uncertainty=null)

cells.content: ν=4/11 unconventional state. Predicted via second-generation Jain hierarchy (composite-fermion-of-composite-fermion); observed at higher mobility. Partial identification.
realized_examples: ν=4/11 plateau observed (Pan et al. 2003) — robust but identification debated
cells.description: Among the less-conventional Jain states. The 4/11 is a 'second-generation' CF state where CFs themselves undergo FQH; alternative interpretations as hierarchical bosonic states or non-CF states remain debated. Status: partial.
citations: Pan et al. 2003, Phys. Rev. Lett. 90, 016801
Goldman, Banerjee 1995 (precision charge measurements)

Pan, Baldwin, West, Pfeiffer, Tsui 2003 (rev. 2015), arXiv:1412.7451 'Fractional Quantum Hall Effect at Landau Level Filling ν=4/11' (source-value: σ_xy = (4/11)(e²/h) plateau observed at 15 mK in high-mobility low-density sample; quantized Hall resistance R_xy within 1% of expected h/(4/11)e²; longitudinal R_xx exhibits activated minimum with thermal-activation energy gap ≈ 7 mK; second-generation composite-fermion-of-composite-fermion hierarchical state; T13 cell-direct, T25 reuse, T26 rational-fraction encoding p/q=4/11; T10 uncertainty=null for the literature quantization value with the 1% experimental scatter at fixed temperature absorbed into the 'characteristic literature value' framing rather than encoded as a formal error bar)
Pan, Stormer, Tsui, Pfeiffer, Baldwin, West 2003, Phys. Rev. Lett. 90, 016801 (earlier short report of the ν=4/11 incompressible state; second-generation Jain hierarchy interpretation)

cells.content: ν=7/5 Read-Rezayi Z_4 parafermion candidate. Particle-hole conjugate of 12/5 within the LLL. PREDICTED Fibonacci-like or Z_4-parafermion anyons. NOT confirmed.
realized_examples: ν=7/5 plateau weakly observed (less robust than 12/5)
Theoretical Read-Rezayi prediction not confirmed by experiment
cells.description: The PH-conjugate of 12/5. If 12/5 is RR Z_3 parafermion, 7/5 should be its PH-conjugate. Mendeleev empty cell: predicted topological structure not unambiguously identified.
citations: Read, Rezayi 1999, Phys. Rev. B 59, 8084

Xia, Stormer, Tsui, Pfeiffer, Baldwin, West, Pan 2004, Phys. Rev. Lett. 93, 176809 (source-value: σ_xy = (7/5)(e²/h) plateau observed in second Landau level; particle-hole conjugate of the ν=12/5 Read-Rezayi Z₃ parafermion state within the LLL; predicted to host Fibonacci-like or Z₄-parafermion anyons under the RR construction but the RR identification remains unconfirmed — the plateau itself is observed, the topological-order classification of the state is the open question; T13 cell-direct, T25 reuse, T26 rational-fraction encoding p/q=7/5; T10 uncertainty=null)

Read, Rezayi 1999, Phys. Rev. B 59, 8084 'Beyond paired quantum Hall states: parafermions and incompressible states in the first excited Landau level' (source-value: ν=7/5 included in the parafermion-state predictions as a candidate for non-abelian topological order)

cells.content: ν=3/8 bosonic hierarchy state. Less robust; observed in highest-mobility samples.
realized_examples: ν=3/8 weak plateau (Pan et al.)
cells.description: A non-standard FQH state at 3/8. The interpretation involves hierarchical condensation of quasiparticles atop a 1/2-or-near-half-filled CF state.
citations: Pan et al. 2003

Pan, Baldwin, West, Pfeiffer, Tsui 2003 (rev. 2015), arXiv:1412.7451 'Fractional Quantum Hall Effect at Landau Level Filling ν=4/11' (source-value: σ_xy = (3/8)(e²/h) developing plateau in the same neighborhood as ν=4/11 and ν=5/13 — a 'developing' minimum in R_xx with partial Hall plateau formation, observed in the highest-mobility samples but not yet a fully quantized plateau analogous to the 4/11 confirmation; the bosonic-hierarchy interpretation places this state in a different family than the Laughlin/Jain sequences; T13 cell-direct, T25 reuse, T26 rational-fraction encoding p/q=3/8 = 0.375 exact; T10 uncertainty=null for theoretical plateau value with observation status flagged as 'developing' in source-value rather than encoded as a precision error bar)

cells.content: Predicted Fibonacci-anyon state at unconventional ν. Mendeleev empty cell. Various theoretical proposals for FQH states supporting Fibonacci anyons beyond 12/5; observation would enable universal TQC.
realized_examples: No experimental observation of Fibonacci anyons at any ν as of 2026
Predicted at multiple specific ν values in various theoretical proposals
cells.description: The discovery of Fibonacci anyons in ANY 2DEG platform would be a landmark — enabling universal topological quantum computation by anyonic braiding alone. The 12/5 Read-Rezayi candidate (cell-fqh-12-5-rr) is the most-studied prediction; other proposals exist. Status: unconfirmed-not-yet-tested.
citations: Nayak, Simon, Stern, Freedman, Das Sarma 2008, Rev. Mod. Phys. 80, 1083 (Fibonacci TQC review)

cells.content: ν=1/9 Laughlin state. Very fragile (extremely small gap); observed only in highest-mobility specimens.
realized_examples: ν=1/9 weakly observed in cleanest samples
cells.description: Edge of the Laughlin series. Smaller gaps make this state difficult to access. Confirms the m=9 extension of the 1/m Laughlin series.
citations: Goldman, Su 1995

Goldman, Su 1995, Science 267, 1010 'Resonant Tunneling in the Quantum Hall Regime: Measurement of Fractional Charge' (source-value: σ_xy = (1/9)(e²/h) Laughlin plateau observed at very low Landau level filling in highest-mobility specimens; the ν=1/9 state has an extremely small gap (the Laughlin gap scales as e²/εℓ_B × m^(-α) with m=9 making it fragile) and is observed only at the lowest temperatures and highest 2DEG mobilities; this is the highest-m Laughlin state currently confirmed in 2DEGs; T13 cell-direct, T25 reuse, T26 rational-fraction encoding p/q=1/9; T10 uncertainty=null for the characteristic literature plateau value with sample-mobility-dependent observation status not encoded as a formal error bar)

cells.content: structurally-excluded
cells.description: Structurally-excluded: the ν=1/3 state is the Laughlin state, definitively abelian (Z_3 anyons). Non-abelian Ising statistics at ν=1/3 are incompatible with the Laughlin construction and have not been observed.

cells.content: structurally-excluded
cells.description: Structurally-excluded: non-abelian Ising anyons require pairing structure (Pfaffian / paired state). ν=2/3 is well-established as abelian (PH-conjugate of 1/3). No theoretical or experimental basis for non-abelian Ising statistics at 2/3.

closure_status.description: Partial. The classification framework (hierarchy-family × ν × statistics-class) is well-established. Laughlin states (1/m, m odd) and Jain composite-fermion sequences (p/(2p+1)) are completely classified and confirmed. Open: (i) specific identification of ν=5/2 among Pfaffian/anti-Pfaffian/PH-Pfaffian candidates — extensively studied but unresolved as of 2026; (ii) Read-Rezayi Z_3 parafermion identification at 12/5 — observed plateau, partial identification; (iii) Fibonacci anyon detection at any ν — predicted, never observed; (iv) higher Read-Rezayi Z_k states at unconventional ν; (v) classification at higher Landau levels (n≥3) where less data exists; (vi) FQH states in non-GaAs platforms (graphene, ZnO, WSe2 moire) where the classification may differ or expand.

predictive_yield.prediction: Quasiparticle fractional charge e/m at Laughlin ν=1/m states.
predictive_yield.description: Laughlin 1983 predicted that quasiparticles at ν=1/m carry fractional charge e/m. Confirmed by shot-noise measurements at ν=1/3 (Saminadayar et al. 1997, e/3) and ν=1/5 (Reznikov et al. 1999, e/5). One of the most direct confirmations of FQH theory.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Laughlin 1983, Phys. Rev. Lett. 50, 1395
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Saminadayar, Glattli, Jin, Etienne 1997, Phys. Rev. Lett. 79, 2526

citations: Saminadayar, Glattli, Jin, Etienne 1997, Phys. Rev. Lett. 79, 2526 'Observation of the e/3 Fractionally Charged Laughlin Quasiparticle' (source-value: shot-noise Fano factor F = q*/e ≈ 1/3 measured at ν=1/3 confirms the Laughlin-predicted quasiparticle charge q* = e/3; the Fano factor F is the dimensionless ratio of measured shot noise to the Poissonian charge-e Schottky value, so F = q*/e directly reads off the quasiparticle charge in units of the electron charge; T13 split-attachment maneuver applied at the FC-predictive_yield scope (the canonical confirmation point for the cross-Laughlin-family prediction); T26 first-use mint: rational-fraction encoding kind=ratio + value=p/q as decimal with source-value preserving the rational form — applied to the FC-pred-level encoding of the discrete confirmation point e/3 from the cross-family prediction q*=e/m which itself isn't a single value but a family of rational fractions; T10 uncertainty=null for the canonical literature confirmation value with measurement scatter at the few-percent level not encoded as a formal error bar)

Laughlin 1983, Phys. Rev. Lett. 50, 1395 'Anomalous Quantum Hall Effect: An Incompressible Quantum Fluid with Fractionally Charged Excitations' (source-value: Laughlin wavefunction construction predicts fractionally-charged quasiparticles q* = e/m at ν=1/m incompressible states; ν=1/3 → q*=e/3, ν=1/5 → q*=e/5; original prediction)

Reznikov, Picciotto, Griffiths, Heiblum, Umansky 1999, Nature 399, 238 'Observation of quasiparticles with one-fifth of an electron's charge' (source-value: independent confirmation of q*=e/5 quasiparticle charge at ν=2/5 Jain plateau via shot noise — extending the Saminadayar protocol to the first non-Laughlin Jain state; the prediction q*=e/(2p+1) for the Jain p/(2p+1) sequence is verified)

predictive_yield.prediction: Composite-fermion construction explains the Jain sequence ν=p/(2p±1).
predictive_yield.description: Jain 1989 predicted that flux attachment maps FQH at ν=p/(2p+1) to integer QH of composite fermions at filling p. Confirmed by observation of the full series ν=2/5, 3/7, 4/9, 5/11, ... and by detection of composite fermions via Shubnikov-de Haas oscillations near ν=1/2 (Du, Stormer 1993; Kang, Stormer 1993).
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Jain 1989, Phys. Rev. Lett. 63, 199
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Kang, Stormer, Pfeiffer, Baldwin, West 1993, Phys. Rev. Lett. 71, 3850 (composite-fermion direct detection)

predictive_yield.prediction: Anyonic exchange statistics directly measurable via interferometry.
predictive_yield.description: Predicted that braiding quasiparticles around each other yields fractional exchange phases (Laughlin 1983, theoretical). Direct interferometric measurement: Bartolomei et al. 2020 (Nature) at ν=1/3 measured fractional statistics phase 2π/3; Nakamura et al. 2020 (Nature Physics) independently confirmed. Established that FQH anyons are real, not just an effective description.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Laughlin 1983, Phys. Rev. Lett. 50, 1395
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Bartolomei et al. 2020, Science 368, 173; Nakamura et al. 2020, Nature Physics 16, 931

citations: Bartolomei, Kumar, Bisognin, Marguerite, Berroir, Bocquillon, Plaçais, Cavanna, Dong, Gennser, Jin, Fève 2020, Science 368, 173 'Fractional statistics in anyon collisions' (source-value: anyonic braiding (encircling) phase 2θ = 2π/3 ≈ 2.0944 radians directly measured at ν=1/3 via the anyon-collider Fano-factor signature; the measured autocorrelation agrees with the theoretical braiding-in-time model with 2θ = 2π/3 without any fitting parameters; T13 split-attachment maneuver applied at FC-pred scope; T10 uncertainty=null for the measurement-matches-theory-without-fitting case generalizing T10 to clean prediction-confirmation pairs without quoted precision error)

Nakamura, Liang, Gardner, Manfra 2020, Nat. Phys. 16, 931 'Direct observation of anyonic braiding statistics' (source-value: independent confirmation using an electronic Fabry-Perot interferometer at ν=1/3 with discrete phase slips of magnitude 2π/3 attributed to braiding around localized anyons; consistent with theoretical anyonic phase θ_anyon = 2π/3)

Arovas, Schrieffer, Wilczek 1984, Phys. Rev. Lett. 53, 722 'Fractional Statistics and the Quantum Hall Effect' (source-value: original prediction that ν=1/3 Laughlin quasiparticles obey fractional statistics with exchange phase π/3 — equivalently braiding phase 2π/3 — derived from the topological monodromy of the Laughlin wavefunction)

predictive_yield.prediction: Non-abelian Ising anyons exist at ν=5/2.
predictive_yield.description: Moore-Read 1991 predicted Ising-type non-abelian anyons at ν=5/2 (the Pfaffian state). The 5/2 plateau is firmly observed; whether its topological order is Moore-Read Pfaffian, anti-Pfaffian, or PH-Pfaffian remains contested. Thermal Hall measurement (Banerjee 2018) gave κ_xy ≈ 2.5(πk_B²T/6ℏ) — consistent with PH-Pfaffian — but interpretation is disputed (Simon 2020; Yan 2022). Status: tension.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Moore, Read 1991, Nucl. Phys. B 360, 362
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Banerjee et al. 2018, Nature 559, 205 (thermal Hall consistent with PH-Pfaffian; interpretation disputed)

citations: Banerjee, Heiblum, Umansky, Feldman, Oreg, Stern 2018, Nature 559, 205 'Observation of half-integer thermal Hall conductance' (source-value: thermal Hall conductance measured at ν=5/2 yields κ_xy = 2.5 κ₀T with κ₀ = π²k_B²/(3h), directly extracting the chiral central charge c = 5/2 = 2.5 of the edge theory; consistent with PH-Pfaffian prediction (c=5/2) and inconsistent at face value with Moore-Read Pfaffian (c=7/2) and anti-Pfaffian (c=3/2); the half-integer value 5/2 demonstrates the non-abelian nature of the 5/2 state per Kane-Fisher formalism; T13 split-attachment maneuver applied at FC-pred scope — the cells [6,7,8] hold the three competing theoretical c predictions, this FC-pred entry holds the adjudicating measurement; T10 uncertainty=null generalized to characteristic-literature-measurement value with experimental scatter at the few-percent level not quoted as a formal error bar)

Simon 2020, Phys. Rev. B 102, 165135 'Interpretation of thermal conductance of the ν=5/2 edge' (source-value: argues that the measured 2.5 κ₀ value may also be consistent with non-equilibrated anti-Pfaffian edges through partial thermal equilibration physics, calling the PH-Pfaffian interpretation into question — interpretation tension documented)
Yan, Wang, Lu 2022 (alternative interpretation analyses; further sample-dependent caveats on extracting topological-order identity from κ_xy alone in non-fully-equilibrated regimes)

predictive_yield.prediction: Read-Rezayi Z_3 parafermion state with Fibonacci anyons at ν=12/5.
predictive_yield.description: Read-Rezayi 1999 predicted the ν=12/5 state to be a Z_3 parafermion phase supporting Fibonacci anyons (which enable universal TQC). The 12/5 plateau is observed (Xia et al. 2004); numerical exact-diagonalization studies favor RR identification (Bonderson-Slingerland 2008); experimental confirmation via Fibonacci-anyon-specific signatures has NOT been achieved. Status: tension — strong theoretical support, no decisive experiment yet.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Read, Rezayi 1999, Phys. Rev. B 59, 8084
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Bonderson, Slingerland 2008, Phys. Rev. B 78, 125323 (numerical RR identification favored, not decisive)

citations: Read, Rezayi 1999, Phys. Rev. B 59, 8084 'Beyond paired quantum Hall states: parafermions and incompressible states in the first excited Landau level' (source-value: the predicted ν=12/5 Z₃ parafermion FQH state hosts Fibonacci anyons τ with the canonical Fibonacci fusion rule τ ⊗ τ = 1 ⊕ τ; the quantum dimension d_τ is the unique positive solution of d_τ² = 1 + d_τ, i.e. d_τ = (1+√5)/2 = φ ≈ 1.6180339887... — the golden ratio; this irrational quantum dimension is THE structural-fingerprint quantitative claim that defines the Fibonacci-anyon character of the predicted topological order at ν=12/5 RR-Z₃; T13 split-attachment maneuver applied at FC-pred scope — the cell [9] holds the σ_xy = (12/5)e²/h plateau observation, this FC-pred entry holds the predicted Fibonacci structural fingerprint d_τ = φ; pairs structurally with the MTC FC's Fibonacci cell content which already names d_τ = φ in prose but holds no qs entry yet — flagged as future MTC sub-PR pairing target; T10 uncertainty=null for pure-theory irrational structural constant)

Nayak, Simon, Stern, Freedman, Das Sarma 2008, Rev. Mod. Phys. 80, 1083 'Non-Abelian Anyons and Topological Quantum Computation' (source-value: comprehensive review establishing that Fibonacci anyons with quantum dimension φ are the unique non-abelian anyon species supporting universal topological quantum computation by braiding alone; observation of d_τ = φ in any FQH platform would be a landmark for the topological-quantum-computation program)

Bonderson, Slingerland 2008, Phys. Rev. B 78, 125323 'Fractional quantum Hall hierarchy and the second Landau level' (source-value: numerical exact-diagonalization studies of the second Landau level favor the RR Z₃ identification at ν=12/5 over competing candidates; identification not yet experimentally decisive)

predictive_yield.prediction: Universal topological quantum computation by braiding Fibonacci anyons alone.
predictive_yield.description: Predicted that Fibonacci anyons (if realized) support universal TQC by braiding alone (Nayak et al. 2008 review). The prediction is mathematically rigorous; experimental realization requires (i) detecting Fibonacci anyons in any FQH platform — not yet achieved; (ii) demonstrating braiding-based gate operations. Most-likely platform: ν=12/5 Read-Rezayi if confirmed. Status: not-yet-tested — both prerequisites pending.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Nayak, Simon, Stern, Freedman, Das Sarma 2008, Rev. Mod. Phys. 80, 1083

predictive_yield.prediction: Thermal Hall conductance quantization κ_xy = (c/2)(πk_B²T/6ℏ) where c is central charge of edge theory.
predictive_yield.description: Kane-Fisher 1997 predicted quantized thermal Hall conductance proportional to edge central charge. Confirmed at integer plateaus by Jezouin et al. 2013; at FQH plateaus including 1/3 by subsequent experiments (Banerjee 2017, 2018). At 5/2 the measured value tests which non-abelian candidate is realized.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Kane, Fisher 1997, Phys. Rev. B 55, 15832
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Banerjee et al. 2017, Nature 545, 75 (thermal Hall at integer and 1/3)

citations: Banerjee, Heiblum, Rosenblatt, Oreg, Feldman, Stern, Umansky 2017, Nature 545, 75 'Observed quantization of anyonic heat flow' (source-value: thermal Hall conductance measured at ν=1/3 Laughlin plateau yields κ_xy = 1 · κ₀T with κ₀ = π²k_B²/(3h), confirming the chiral central charge c=1 of the single downstream charged edge mode of the Laughlin state; companion measurement to the Banerjee 2018 5/2 result; the ν=1/3 result is the canonical confirmation of the Kane-Fisher 1997 prediction κ_xy = (c/2)(πk_B²T/6ℏ) at the foundational FQH plateau; T13 split-attachment maneuver applied at FC-pred scope — the cell [0] holds the σ_xy = (1/3)e²/h plateau observation, this FC-pred entry holds the thermal-Hall confirmation of the chiral c=1 edge-theory prediction; T10 uncertainty=null for the canonical literature confirmation value with experimental scatter at the few-percent level not quoted as a formal error bar)

Kane, Fisher 1997, Phys. Rev. B 55, 15832 'Quantized Thermal Transport in the Fractional Quantum Hall Effect' (source-value: original prediction that thermal Hall conductance quantizes as κ_xy = (c_total/2)(πk_B²T/6ℏ) where c_total is the net chiral central charge of the edge theory; provides the framework for extracting topological-order information from heat-flow measurements that would later become decisive for the 5/2 candidate discrimination)

Jezouin, Parmentier, Anthore, Gennser, Cavanna, Jin, Pierre 2013, Science 342, 601 'Quantum limit of heat flow across a single electronic channel' (source-value: earlier confirmation of κ₀-quantized heat flow per chiral edge channel at integer QH plateaus — c=1 confirmed at ν=1 — establishing the metrology before extension to the FQH regime)

predictive_yield.prediction: Particle-hole symmetry constrains the 5/2 topological order — specifically forbidding pure Pfaffian or pure anti-Pfaffian if PH symmetry is exact.
predictive_yield.description: Son 2015 predicted that exact particle-hole symmetry within the LLL forbids the original Pfaffian and anti-Pfaffian, selecting PH-Pfaffian as the unique PH-symmetric candidate at 5/2. Experimental tests: thermal Hall favored PH-Pfaffian initially (Banerjee 2018), but interpretation disputed. Open question whether real samples have exact PH symmetry — Landau-level mixing breaks it weakly.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Son 2015, Phys. Rev. X 5, 031027
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Banerjee et al. 2018, Nature 559, 205 (interpretation tension)

related_entries: modular-tensor-categories (each FQH state corresponds to a specific MTC; THE central structural pairing)
generalized-symmetries / SymTFT (FQH states realize specific generalized-symmetry data; the 2+1D bulk Chern-Simons IS a SymTFT realization)
tenfold-way (does NOT classify FQH — only IQH; FQH states are strongly interacting and lie outside tenfold-way's free-fermion scope)
cft-bootstrap-exclusion-regions (FQH edge theories are CFTs; bootstrap constraints on these CFTs constrain FQH topological orders)
citations: Tsui, Stormer, Gossard 1982, Phys. Rev. Lett. 48, 1559 (FQH discovery)
Laughlin 1983, Phys. Rev. Lett. 50, 1395 (Laughlin wavefunction)
Jain 1989, Phys. Rev. Lett. 63, 199 (composite fermions)
Moore, Read 1991, Nucl. Phys. B 360, 362 (Pfaffian state)
Read, Rezayi 1999, Phys. Rev. B 59, 8084 (parafermion states)
Wen 1995, Adv. Phys. 44, 405 (topological order classification)
Nayak, Simon, Stern, Freedman, Das Sarma 2008, Rev. Mod. Phys. 80, 1083 (non-abelian anyons / TQC review)
Bartolomei et al. 2020, Science 368, 173 (anyonic statistics measurement)

nodes.label: Compact astrophysical objects
nodes.full_name: Classification of compact astrophysical objects — white dwarfs, neutron stars, black holes, and predicted exotic compact objects
nodes.description: Compact astrophysical objects are gravitationally-bound configurations of matter (or vacuum, in the case of black holes) at densities where general-relativistic effects are non-negligible to dominant. They occupy the end-state of stellar evolution (and a few non-stellar formation channels). The classification organizes confirmed individuals, confirmed populations, candidate individuals, and predicted-but-unobserved object classes by object-class × mass-regime × observational-status. Each compact object is a solution (or near-solution) to Einstein's field equations: white dwarfs and neutron stars are equilibrium solutions of TOV-type hydrostatic equations with quantum-statistical matter pressure; black holes are vacuum (or near-vacuum) solutions of Einstein's equations characterized by event horizons. The classification is phenomenological: cells correspond to specific physical objects (e.g., Sgr A*, PSR J0740+6620) or families of objects (stellar-mass merging BHs as catalogued by LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA). Mendeleev-style empty cells include the long-suspected intermediate-mass black hole population (10^2–10^5 M_sun; only partially populated by GW190521 and a small handful of candidates as of 2026), predicted-but-unobserved quark stars / strange-quark-matter compact objects, primordial black holes in specific mass windows (asteroid-mass, sublunar-mass, stellar-mass — some windows excluded as DM candidates, others remain open), and exotic-compact-object models such as boson stars and gravastars proposed as BH-mimickers but never observationally identified.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Stellar and post-stellar gravitationally-compact configurations where M/R is large enough that general-relativistic corrections matter (typically GM/(Rc²) ≳ 10^-4 for WDs, ~0.2 for NSs, ≃ 0.5 at BH horizons). Includes: white dwarfs, neutron stars (canonical and massive), magnetars, stellar-mass black holes, intermediate-mass black holes, supermassive black holes at galactic centers and AGN, post-merger remnants of compact binary coalescences, and predicted-but-unobserved exotic compact objects (quark stars, primordial BHs in various mass windows, boson stars). Excludes: ordinary main-sequence stars (insufficient compactness), brown dwarfs, supernova progenitors prior to collapse, galaxy-scale dark-matter halos, cosmological-scale black-hole populations as a class (the cosmological-population statistics belong in a cosmology-FC, not here; individual SMBHs do).
nodes.organizing_principle: Each compact astrophysical object is identified by its dominant physical mechanism of pressure support (electron-degeneracy → WD; nucleon-degeneracy + strong-force repulsion → NS; vacuum solution behind a horizon → BH; predicted alternatives → exotic-compact-object), together with its mass regime and its current observational status. Within a given (object-class, mass-regime) cell, individual objects are members of an empirically-defined population — e.g., the cell-compact-NS-massive cell contains PSR J0740+6620, PSR J0348+0432, and other ≳2 M_sun neutron stars constraining the dense-matter EOS at the upper end. The classification is constrained by GR (the Tolman-Oppenheimer-Volkoff equations for matter objects; the Kerr-Newman family for stationary axisymmetric vacuum solutions; the four laws of black-hole thermodynamics for horizons), by the matter EOS (which sets the maximum mass for WDs at the Chandrasekhar limit and for NSs at the TOV limit), and by formation-channel physics (which restricts what mass ranges are populated by what astrophysical processes). Two distinct organizing axes are in tension: a structural axis (the object IS a GR solution of a given type) and an observational axis (the object IS or IS NOT confirmed). The observational-status axis is essential because the population of unobserved-but-theoretically-predicted compact objects (PBHs, quark stars, exotic objects) is a major empty-cell zone driving experimental programs.

classification_axes.name: object-class
classification_axes.description: The structural class of the compact object. white-dwarf: electron-degeneracy-supported; final state of low/intermediate-mass stars (M_progenitor ≲ 8 M_sun). neutron-star: nucleon-degeneracy + strong-force-pressure supported; remnant of core-collapse supernovae. magnetar: subtype of NS with extreme magnetic fields B ~ 10^14-10^15 G (treated as a distinct class because its phenomenology — soft-gamma-repeater bursts, giant flares — is observationally distinct). stellar-mass-black-hole: M ~ 3-100 M_sun; formed in core-collapse of massive stars or by binary mergers. intermediate-mass-black-hole: M ~ 10^2-10^5 M_sun; sparsely populated cell, formation channels under debate. supermassive-black-hole: M ~ 10^6-10^10 M_sun; resides at galactic centers and powers AGN. primordial-black-hole: hypothesized BHs of cosmological origin from early-universe density fluctuations; mass not constrained to stellar values. quark-star: predicted strange-quark-matter compact object; never observationally confirmed. exotic-compact-object: catch-all for theoretical alternatives (boson stars, gravastars, fuzzballs, BH-mimickers) — predicted only.

classification_axes.name: mass-regime
classification_axes.description: Mass-scale regime. subplanetary: M ≲ 10^-15 M_sun (relevant only for PBHs from microscopic primordial collapse; mostly evaporated by now if formed). sublunar: 10^-15 - 10^-9 M_sun (asteroid-mass to lunar-mass PBH window — partial DM candidate). asteroid-mass: ~10^-12 M_sun region, narrow PBH-DM window. planetary: 10^-6 - 10^-3 M_sun (microlensing PBH searches). sub-solar: 0.1 - 1 M_sun (low-mass WDs; sub-solar PBH-LIGO searches). stellar: 1 - 30 M_sun (typical NSs, stellar-mass BHs, WDs). ultramassive-stellar: 30 - 150 M_sun (upper-mass-gap pair-instability region; partially populated by GW190521). intermediate: 100 - 10^5 M_sun (IMBH range; sparsely populated). supermassive: 10^6 - 10^9 M_sun (galactic-center SMBHs). ultramassive-supermassive: > 10^10 M_sun (the heaviest known SMBHs at AGN centers; few confirmed individuals).

classification_axes.name: observational-status
classification_axes.description: Empirical status of the object or population. confirmed-individual: a specific named object whose physical class is established (e.g., Sgr A*, PSR J0740+6620, Sirius B). confirmed-population: a statistical population is established but individual members are usually cataloged with parameters that have nontrivial uncertainty (e.g., LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA stellar-mass BH catalog). candidate-individual: a specific named object proposed to be of this class but identification disputed (e.g., specific IMBH candidates in dense stellar clusters). candidate-population: a population whose existence is suggested but not established (e.g., quark-star population; PBH population in some mass windows). predicted-only: theoretical prediction with no observational confirmation (e.g., boson-star, gravastar populations). excluded-as-DM-fraction: PBH mass windows excluded from being the dominant dark-matter component (~5σ exclusion or stronger).

cells.content: Canonical white dwarfs: M ≈ 0.6 M_sun, R ≈ R_Earth (~6000 km), supported by electron degeneracy pressure. Cooling timescales ~ Gyr. Mass-radius relation is well-measured for individuals including Sirius B (M ≈ 1.02 M_sun, R ≈ 0.0084 R_sun) and 40 Eridani B.
realized_examples: Sirius B — paradigmatic WD, M ≈ 1.02 M_sun, R ≈ 0.0084 R_sun (Bond et al. 2017 HST astrometric mass)
