40 Eridani B — closest WD-triple system to Earth
Procyon B — high-mass WD in nearby binary
Van Maanen 2 — first isolated WD discovered (1917)
cells.description: The reference cell for WD physics. The mass-radius relation R ∝ M^{-1/3} (Chandrasekhar's nonrelativistic limit) is observed in this cell. WD spectroscopic classification (DA, DB, DC, DZ, DQ) tracks atmospheric composition, not core physics; the core composition (C/O typical; lower-mass WDs have He cores; ultramassive WDs may have O/Ne/Mg cores) constrains progenitor mass.
citations: Chandrasekhar 1931, Astrophys. J. 74, 81 (maximum mass calculation)
Bond, Schaefer, Gilliland et al. 2017, Astrophys. J. 840, 70 (Sirius B HST mass)
Tremblay et al. 2019, Nature 565, 202 (Gaia WD population)

Bond, Schaefer, Gilliland, Holberg, Hubeny, Trauger, Provencal, Hartig 2017 'The Sirius System and Its Astrophysical Puzzles: Hubble Space Telescope and Ground-Based Astrometry' ApJ 840:70 arXiv:1703.10625 (HST FGS astrometric mass: M_B = 1.018 ± 0.011 M_sun for Sirius B, the closest and best-measured WD, cited in v40 cell content as the paradigmatic individual; heavier than the cell's population-median commitment M ≈ 0.6 M_sun listed in cell content text — Sirius B is encoded here as the load-bearing realized-example anchor per existing cell content's explicit naming 'Sirius B (M ≈ 1.02 M_sun, R ≈ 0.0084 R_sun)'; T13 direct cells[i].quantitative_scale)

cells.content: White dwarfs near the Chandrasekhar limit M_Ch ≈ 1.4 M_sun (for nonrotating, nonmagnetic, μ_e = 2 composition). Approach to M_Ch leads to instability (electron capture, pyconuclear reactions) and is the precursor to type-Ia supernovae or accretion-induced collapse to neutron stars.
realized_examples: RX J0648.0-4418 — accreting massive WD
ZTF J153932.16+502738.8 — eclipsing ultramassive double WD inspiral
WD 1856+534 — high-mass WD with transiting giant planet
cells.description: Mass approach to M_Ch is observable in pre-explosion Type-Ia progenitors. The Chandrasekhar limit is one of the most precisely-known structural facts of compact-object physics; it derives from the relativistic equation of state for a degenerate electron gas with no free parameters at the predicted-mass level.
citations: Chandrasekhar 1931, Astrophys. J. 74, 81
Hillebrandt, Niemeyer 2000, Annu. Rev. Astron. Astrophys. 38, 191 (Type-Ia models)

Chandrasekhar 1931 'The Maximum Mass of Ideal White Dwarfs' ApJ 74:81 (theoretical maximum mass of a nonrotating nonmagnetic WD supported by electron degeneracy; the conventional rounded value M_Ch ≈ 1.4 M_sun for μ_e = 2 carbon-oxygen composition is universal in the literature, with the more precise Chandrasekhar 1935 'Stellar Configurations with Degenerate Cores' MNRAS 95:226 value 5.76/μ_e² M_sun = 1.456 M_sun also widely cited; T13 direct cells[i].quantitative_scale, T10 uncertainty=null applied to pure-theory limit per sub-PR 4 convention; the 1.4 value is the canonical pedagogical anchor in the cell content text)

cells.content: Ultramassive WDs with M ≳ 1.1 M_sun, formed in mergers of WD pairs or massive progenitors. Crystallizing interiors (Coulomb crystallization at low T) and possible neon-capture-driven collapse to NSs are predicted; the latter constitutes the accretion-induced-collapse formation channel for low-mass NSs.
realized_examples: ZTF J1901+1458 — M ≈ 1.35 M_sun ultramassive WD with strong B field (Caiazzo et al. 2021)
WD J0551+4135 — ultramassive WD with composition suggesting merger origin (Hollands et al. 2020)
cells.description: Population is sparsely sampled but growing rapidly with Gaia data releases. The merger origin is independently supported by the kinematic-age vs cooling-age mismatch in ultramassive WDs.
citations: Caiazzo et al. 2021, Nature 595, 39 (ZTF J1901+1458)
Cheng, Cummings, Ménard 2019, Astrophys. J. 886, 100 (merger-origin ultramassive WDs)

cells.content: Canonical neutron stars: M ≈ 1.4 M_sun (the median observed NS mass), R ≈ 11-13 km. Surface gravity GM/(Rc²) ≈ 0.2 — general-relativistic regime. Crust + core structure: outer crust (Coulomb lattice of nuclei in electron gas), inner crust (neutron-drip + neutron superfluid), outer core (npe + possibly μ matter), inner core (composition uncertain — hyperons, kaon condensate, quark matter all proposed).
realized_examples: PSR J0030+0451 — NICER mass-radius measurement M = 1.34 (+0.15/-0.16) M_sun, R = 12.71 (+1.14/-1.19) km (Miller et al. 2019, Riley et al. 2019)
PSR B1913+16 (Hulse-Taylor) — first binary pulsar, M ≈ 1.44 M_sun
PSR J0737-3039A/B — double pulsar system
PSR J0437-4715 — NICER mass-radius (Choudhury et al. 2024)
cells.description: The reference cell for NS physics. NICER X-ray pulse-profile modeling provides simultaneous mass-radius constraints on individual NSs; combined with gravitational-wave tidal-deformability constraints (GW170817 binary NS merger) and the maximum-mass constraint (PSR J0740+6620), the dense-matter EOS is constrained at the 2024-26 level to a narrow band consistent with hadronic-only EOS, hadron-quark crossover EOS, and certain stiff-mediated EOS — but disfavoring extremely stiff or extremely soft pure-hadron EOS.
citations: Miller et al. 2019, Astrophys. J. Lett. 887, L24 (NICER J0030+0451 — group A)
Riley et al. 2019, Astrophys. J. Lett. 887, L21 (NICER J0030+0451 — group B)
Hulse, Taylor 1975, Astrophys. J. 195, L51 (binary pulsar discovery)
Choudhury et al. 2024, Astrophys. J. Lett. 971, L20 (NICER J0437-4715)

Riley, Watts, Bogdanov, Ray, Ludlam, Guillot, Arzoumanian, Baker, Bilous, Chakrabarty, Gendreau, Harding, Ho, Lattimer, Morsink, Strohmayer 2019 'A NICER View of PSR J0030+0451: Millisecond Pulsar Parameter Estimation' ApJL 887:L21 arXiv:1912.05702 (abstract: M = 1.34^{+0.15}_{-0.16} M_sun and R_eq = 12.71^{+1.14}_{-1.19} km at 68% credibility; compactness GM/R_eq c² = 0.156^{+0.008}_{-0.010} more tightly constrained; companion Bayesian analysis Miller et al. 2019 ApJL 887:L24 arXiv:1912.05705 reports the same source with M = 1.44^{+0.15}_{-0.14}, R_eq = 13.02^{+1.24}_{-1.06} km, consistent within errors; v40 cell content quotes the Riley values as the canonical realized-example anchor — Riley cited here as the source-value primary; R_eq = 12.71 km is the jointly-measured radius, deferred without a T-number to future multi-qs-per-cell schema extension; T13 direct cells[i].quantitative_scale)

cells.content: Massive neutron stars with M ≳ 2 M_sun, constraining the TOV maximum mass from below. These observations rule out soft hadronic-only EOS and disfavor first-order quark-matter phase transitions at densities below ~3-4 n_0.
realized_examples: PSR J0740+6620 — M = 2.08 ± 0.07 M_sun (Cromartie et al. 2020; Fonseca et al. 2021); NICER + radio mass-radius: M = 2.072 M_sun, R = 12.39 km (Miller et al. 2021)
PSR J0348+0432 — M = 2.01 ± 0.04 M_sun (Antoniadis et al. 2013)
PSR J1614-2230 — M ≈ 1.93 M_sun (Demorest et al. 2010; revised in Arzoumanian et al. 2018)
PSR J0952-0607 — claimed M ≈ 2.35 M_sun, the most-massive known NS as of 2024 (Romani et al. 2022; mass updated; observational systematics under continued review)
cells.description: Massive NSs provide the strongest empirical lower bound on the maximum NS mass M_TOV. Combined with GW170817 upper bound on M_TOV (≲ 2.3-2.4 M_sun from no-prompt-collapse argument), the value M_TOV ≈ 2.0-2.3 M_sun is now empirically established within a narrow band — a major dense-matter physics constraint. This cell is the canonical test bed for EOS-class discrimination.
citations: Cromartie et al. 2020, Nature Astron. 4, 72 (PSR J0740+6620)
Fonseca et al. 2021, Astrophys. J. Lett. 915, L12 (PSR J0740+6620 mass update)
Antoniadis et al. 2013, Science 340, 6131 (PSR J0348+0432)
Miller et al. 2021, Astrophys. J. Lett. 918, L28 (NICER + XMM J0740 mass-radius)
Romani et al. 2022, Astrophys. J. Lett. 934, L17 (PSR J0952-0607 mass claim)

Fonseca, Cromartie, Pennucci, Ray, Kirichenko, Ransom, Demorest, Stairs, Arzoumanian, Guillemot, Parthasarathy, Pennucci, Sanidas, Scholz, Stovall, Swiggum, Zhu (NANOGrav + CHIME/Pulsar Collaborations) 2021 'Refined Mass and Geometric Measurements of the High-Mass PSR J0740+6620' ApJL 915:L12 arXiv:2104.00880 (Shapiro-delay-determined pulsar mass m_p = 2.08^{+0.07}_{-0.07} M_sun at 68.3% credibility, combining GBT + CHIME pulse arrival times across ~1.5 years additional data with previous Cromartie et al. 2020 Nature Astron 4:72 measurement; the highest-reliably-determined NS mass to date and the load-bearing anchor for the M_TOV ≳ 2.0 M_sun bound recorded on this FC's predictive_yield[0]; T13 direct cells[i].quantitative_scale)

cells.content: Magnetars: neutron stars with extreme magnetic fields B ~ 10^14-10^15 G (~10^4 × canonical NS). Phenomenology includes soft-gamma-repeater (SGR) bursts, anomalous X-ray pulsar (AXP) emission, giant flares (10^46 erg in seconds), and occasional Fast-Radio-Burst (FRB) associations.
realized_examples: SGR 1806-20 — 2004 giant flare, brightest extrasolar event ever recorded
SGR J1935+2154 — magnetar associated with FRB 200428, the first galactic FRB
1E 1841-045 — paradigmatic AXP
PSR J1119-6127 — magnetar-pulsar transition object
cells.description: Magnetar status is established for ~30 confirmed individuals as of 2024-26. The magnetar-FRB connection (SGR J1935+2154 → FRB 200428 in 2020) is the strongest empirical link between magnetars and extragalactic FRBs, although whether ALL FRBs are magnetar-sourced remains under investigation.
citations: Duncan, Thompson 1992, Astrophys. J. 392, L9 (magnetar model)
Hurley et al. 2005, Nature 434, 1098 (SGR 1806-20 giant flare)
CHIME/FRB Collaboration 2020, Nature 587, 54 (FRB 200428 from SGR J1935+2154)
Bochenek et al. 2020, Nature 587, 59 (STARE2 detection of FRB 200428)
Kaspi, Beloborodov 2017, Annu. Rev. Astron. Astrophys. 55, 261 (magnetars review)

cells.content: Hypermassive neutron-star remnants: short-lived (~ms-1s) products of binary NS coalescence with total mass exceeding M_TOV. Differential rotation supports against immediate collapse; viscous evolution drives collapse to BH on ms-s timescales.
realized_examples: GW170817 post-merger — total mass ≈ 2.74 M_sun (above M_TOV); remnant identity (immediate BH, short-lived hypermassive NS, or transient supramassive NS) constrained by EM observations and kilonova ejecta mass; consensus disfavors prompt collapse, favors short-lived hypermassive NS → BH (Margalit, Metzger 2017; Bauswein et al. 2017)
cells.description: No direct GW post-merger detection yet at design-sensitivity reach. Pre-O5 LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA noise floor doesn't yet reach the relevant kHz frequencies for the post-merger oscillation modes. Future post-merger GW detection would discriminate among EOS classes at the most extreme densities (~4-6 n_0).
citations: Bauswein, Stergioulas, Janka 2017, Phys. Rev. D 96, 124044 (post-merger oscillations)
Margalit, Metzger 2017, Astrophys. J. Lett. 850, L19 (GW170817 remnant constraints)
Radice et al. 2018, Astrophys. J. Lett. 852, L29 (kilonova + post-merger constraints)

cells.content: Stellar-mass black holes in X-ray binaries: M ~ 5-30 M_sun, identified by accretion-disk X-ray emission and dynamical mass measurements of the companion. The first BH identifications were in this cell (Cyg X-1).
realized_examples: Cyg X-1 — M ≈ 21.2 M_sun (Miller-Jones et al. 2021), the first BH discovered
GRS 1915+105 — M ≈ 10.1 M_sun, microquasar with relativistic jets
V404 Cygni — M ≈ 9 M_sun, transient BH X-ray binary
LMC X-1 — M ≈ 10.9 M_sun, persistent BH HMXB
GX 339-4 — transient BH X-ray binary, M ≈ 5-7 M_sun
cells.description: X-ray-binary BHs are the longest-confirmed BH population (Cyg X-1, 1971). Spin measurements via continuum-fitting and Fe-Kα reflection lines provide tests of Kerr metric and constraints on BH spin distribution. The X-ray-binary BH mass distribution is concentrated in 5-15 M_sun.
citations: Bolton 1972, Nature Phys. Sci. 235, 271 (Cyg X-1 BH identification)
Webster, Murdin 1972, Nature 235, 37 (independent Cyg X-1)
Miller-Jones et al. 2021, Science 371, 1046 (Cyg X-1 revised mass and distance)
McClintock, Narayan, Steiner 2014, Space Sci. Rev. 183, 295 (BH spin review)

Miller-Jones, Bahramian, Orosz, Mandel, Gou, Maccarone, Neijssel, Zhao, Ziółkowski, Reid, Uttley, Zheng, Byun, Dodson, Grinberg, Jung, Kim, Marcote, Markoff, Rioja, Rushton, Russell, Sivakoff, Tetarenko, Tudose, Wilms 2021 'Cygnus X-1 contains a 21-solar mass black hole — implications for massive star winds' Science 371:1046 arXiv:2102.09091 (radio-astrometric VLBA distance refinement d = 2.22^{+0.18}_{-0.17} kpc combined with archival optical photometry/spectroscopy gives BH mass M_BH = 21.2 ± 2.2 M_sun, revising upward from earlier Orosz et al. 2011 ApJ 742:84 mass M ≈ 14.8 M_sun; Cyg X-1 was the first BH-candidate ever identified per Bolton 1972 Nat Phys Sci 235:271 + Webster-Murdin 1972 Nature 235:37, the load-bearing 'first BH' anchor for this cell per v40 cell content; sits at the high end of the cell's stated M ~ 5-30 M_sun range; T13 direct cells[i].quantitative_scale)

cells.content: Stellar-mass black holes in merging compact binaries, catalogued by LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA gravitational-wave observations. As of the O4 run (2024-26), GWTC-4 contains ~150+ confident BBH mergers spanning component masses ~5-80 M_sun. Mass distribution shows distinctive features: a low-mass-gap edge near ~3-5 M_sun, a pile-up at ~10 M_sun, and a partially-suppressed pair-instability gap at ~50-100 M_sun.
realized_examples: GW150914 — first GW detection, M_1 ≈ 36 M_sun, M_2 ≈ 29 M_sun, BBH merger at z ≈ 0.09
GW190521 — total mass ≈ 150 M_sun; final BH ≈ 142 M_sun straddles intermediate-mass BH regime
GW200115 — first confirmed NS-BH merger, with BH M ≈ 5.7 M_sun, NS M ≈ 1.5 M_sun
GW190412 — first confidently asymmetric-mass-ratio BBH merger (q ≈ 0.28)
cells.description: The GW BH population is now the largest single sample of confirmed stellar-mass BHs. It overlaps the X-ray-binary BH mass distribution at the low-mass end but extends substantially higher (X-ray binaries top out near 30 M_sun; GW BHs extend to ~80 M_sun with GW190521's primary). The detected mass distribution provides one of the strongest constraints on stellar-evolution and binary-formation channels in current astrophysics.
citations: LIGO/Virgo Collaboration 2016, Phys. Rev. Lett. 116, 061102 (GW150914)
LIGO/Virgo Collaboration 2020, Phys. Rev. Lett. 125, 101102 (GW190521)
LIGO/Virgo/KAGRA Collaboration 2021, Phys. Rev. X 13, 011048 (GWTC-3)
Abbott et al. 2024, Phys. Rev. X (in press) (GWTC-4 anticipated)

cells.content: The lower-mass-gap region: 3-5 M_sun. X-ray-binary observations of the 1990s-2010s found very few BHs below ~5 M_sun, suggesting a gap between the maximum NS mass (~2.3 M_sun) and the minimum BH mass (~5 M_sun). GW observations are beginning to populate this gap (GW200115 with BH M ≈ 5.7 M_sun, GW190425, GW230529 — see below).
realized_examples: GW230529_181500 — compact-object merger with primary mass 2.5-4.5 M_sun (LVK Collaboration 2024); occupies the lower mass-gap region
GW200115 — NS-BH merger with BH at lower edge of gap
cells.description: The mass-gap question is empirically open as of 2024-26. Whether the gap is genuine (reflecting a structural feature of stellar collapse, e.g., the rapid-versus-delayed supernova mechanism) or apparent (an X-ray selection effect) is being tested by ongoing GW observations. GW230529 (2023 detection, 2024 paper) is the strongest gap-populating event to date.
citations: Bailyn et al. 1998, Astrophys. J. 499, 367 (mass-gap from XRBs)
Özel et al. 2010, Astrophys. J. 725, 1918 (X-ray-binary BH mass distribution)
LVK Collaboration 2024, Astrophys. J. Lett. 970, L34 (GW230529 mass-gap)
Farr et al. 2011, Astrophys. J. 741, 103 (mass-gap statistical analysis)

cells.content: Intermediate-mass black holes, 10^2-10^5 M_sun. The largest empty zone in the BH mass distribution. Astrophysically expected as: (i) seeds of SMBHs (Volonteri-style seed scenarios); (ii) end-products of runaway collisions in dense stellar clusters; (iii) accreted descendants of Pop-III stellar BHs.
realized_examples: GW190521 final BH at ≈ 142 M_sun — the strongest single IMBH candidate from GW astronomy
HLX-1 (in ESO 243-49) — hyperluminous X-ray source, M ≈ 10^4 M_sun candidate (debated)
NGC 6166 ULX-1 candidates — multiple IMBH-mass-range candidates
M82 X-1 — possible IMBH (debated)
cells.description: The IMBH population is sparsely sampled relative to expectations. Each individual candidate is debated; statistical population evidence comes from GW observations (very few IMBH-mass mergers) and ULX surveys (a small subset of ULXs are IMBH candidates; most are super-Eddington stellar-mass BHs). Future LISA observations (2030s+) will be sensitive to IMBH inspirals at high redshift and may definitively populate this cell.
citations: Greene, Strader, Ho 2020, Annu. Rev. Astron. Astrophys. 58, 257 (IMBH review)
Farrell et al. 2009, Nature 460, 73 (HLX-1)
Pasham, Strohmayer, Mushotzky 2014, Nature 513, 74 (M82 X-1 IMBH claim)
LIGO/Virgo Collaboration 2020, Phys. Rev. Lett. 125, 101102 (GW190521 as IMBH)

cells.content: Supermassive black holes at galactic centers. Stellar-dynamical mass measurements (S-star orbits at the Galactic Center), maser-dynamics measurements (NGC 4258), and event-horizon-scale imaging (EHT for Sgr A* and M87*) provide the cleanest individual mass constraints. SMBHs at galactic centers correlate with host galaxy properties (M-σ relation).
realized_examples: Sgr A* — M ≈ 4.297 ± 0.012 × 10^6 M_sun (GRAVITY Collaboration 2022); EHT imaging (EHT Collaboration 2022) confirms shadow size consistent with Kerr metric prediction
M87* — M ≈ 6.5 × 10^9 M_sun (EHT Collaboration 2019); first EHT image of a BH shadow
NGC 4258 — M ≈ 4 × 10^7 M_sun, gold-standard SMBH mass via H2O megamaser
Andromeda M31 nucleus — M ≈ 1.4 × 10^8 M_sun
cells.description: The cell of strongest empirical no-hair-theorem tests. EHT Sgr A* and M87* shadow size and shape are consistent with the Kerr-metric prediction at the current angular-resolution precision (~ 10% on shadow diameter). Quasi-normal-mode ringdowns from BBH mergers (overlap with cell-compact-stellar-BH-GW for spectroscopic BH tests) provide complementary horizon-scale GR tests.
citations: Ghez et al. 2008, Astrophys. J. 689, 1044 (Galactic Center BH stellar orbits)
GRAVITY Collaboration 2022, Astron. Astrophys. 657, L12 (Sgr A* mass)
EHT Collaboration 2019, Astrophys. J. Lett. 875, L1 (M87* shadow)
EHT Collaboration 2022, Astrophys. J. Lett. 930, L12 (Sgr A* shadow)
Kormendy, Ho 2013, Annu. Rev. Astron. Astrophys. 51, 511 (SMBH-host scaling review)

GRAVITY Collaboration (Abuter, Aimar, Amorim, Ball, Bauböck, Berger, Bonnet, Bourdarot, Brandner, Cardoso, Clénet, Dallilar, Davies, de Zeeuw, Dexter, Drescher, Eisenhauer, Förster Schreiber, Foschi, Garcia, Gao, Gendron, Genzel, Gillessen et al.) 2022 'Mass distribution in the Galactic Center based on interferometric astrometry of multiple stellar orbits' A&A 657:L12 arXiv:2112.07478 (combined NACO/SINFONI/Keck/Gemini three-decade astrometry and spectroscopy with 30–100 μas GRAVITY/VLTI interferometric astrometry of S2, S29, S38, S55 across 1992.2–2021.6, fitting GR orbits around a single central point mass: M_• = (4.297 ± 0.012) × 10⁶ M_sun at ±0.25% precision, distance R_0 = 8277 ± 9 pc; significance of S2 Schwarzschild precession improved to 7σ; extended mass within S2 apocenter ≲3000 M_sun ≲0.1% of M_•; Sgr A* is the cleanest dynamical SMBH-mass anchor, load-bearing for this cell per v40 cell content explicit naming 'Sgr A* — M ≈ 4.297 ± 0.012 × 10⁶ M_sun (GRAVITY Collaboration 2022)'; T13 direct cells[i].quantitative_scale)

cells.content: Supermassive black holes powering active galactic nuclei (AGN) and quasars: M ~ 10^7-10^10 M_sun. Mass measurements via reverberation mapping (variability time-lag of broad-line region), single-epoch virial estimators, or stellar/gas dynamics for nearby AGN. The most massive known SMBHs reach ~ 10^10 M_sun (ultramassive cell may host some).
realized_examples: TON 618 — M ≈ 6.6 × 10^10 M_sun, one of the most-massive known SMBHs (mass via H-beta line FWHM, single-epoch)
3C 273 — first quasar identified (1963), M ≈ 9 × 10^8 M_sun
NGC 1275 — Perseus cluster central AGN, M ≈ 8 × 10^8 M_sun
Pop-III-descendant quasars at z > 6 (J0313-1806 at z ≈ 7.6, M ≈ 1.6 × 10^9 M_sun — challenging early-Universe seed scenarios)
cells.description: The AGN-SMBH population is empirically vast — tens of thousands of confirmed-population members in modern surveys (SDSS, eROSITA). High-redshift quasars (z > 6) hosting 10^9+ M_sun SMBHs constrain seed-formation channels: stellar-mass seeds (Pop-III stars) require near-Eddington continuous accretion for ~1 Gyr, while direct-collapse seeds (10^4-10^6 M_sun) allow more relaxed growth.
citations: Schmidt 1963, Nature 197, 1040 (3C 273 as quasar)
Shemmer et al. 2004, Astrophys. J. 614, 547 (TON 618 mass)
Wang et al. 2021, Astrophys. J. Lett. 907, L1 (J0313-1806 quasar at z ≈ 7.64)
Peterson 2014, Space Sci. Rev. 183, 253 (reverberation mapping review)

cells.content: Quark stars (strange quark stars): hypothetical compact objects composed of stable strange quark matter (SQM), predicted if Bodmer-Witten conjecture (SQM as true QCD ground state at zero pressure) holds. Mass-radius relation differs from NS: R increases with M for low-mass SQM stars, opposite to NS trend.
cells.description: Mendeleev empty cell. Some NS candidates have been proposed as quark-star candidates (e.g., 4U 1820-30, RX J1856.5-3754) based on small inferred radii, but interpretation as hadronic NS with stiff or soft EOS remains viable. The presumption of SQM stability is in tension with the fact that ordinary nuclei DO NOT spontaneously decay to SQM (which would require the Bodmer-Witten conjecture to hold for finite-baryon-number quark matter, not just bulk). No confirmed quark-star identification as of 2026.
citations: Witten 1984, Phys. Rev. D 30, 272 (Witten cosmic-separation; SQM)
Bodmer 1971, Phys. Rev. D 4, 1601 (collapsed nuclei conjecture)
Alcock, Farhi, Olinto 1986, Astrophys. J. 310, 261 (strange-star structure)
Weber 2005, Prog. Part. Nucl. Phys. 54, 193 (quark-star review)

cells.content: Primordial black holes in the asteroid-mass window: M ~ 10^17-10^22 g (~ 10^-15 to 10^-11 M_sun). This window remains open for PBHs being a substantial DM fraction; constraints from femto/picolensing, Hawking-evaporation gamma rays, and neutron-star capture rates only partially close it.
cells.description: Open empty cell. The asteroid-mass PBH window is one of the few mass ranges where PBHs could constitute 100% of dark matter without contradiction by current observations. Subaru-HSC microlensing limits the lower edge (~ 10^21 g), while gamma-ray-burst femtolensing and dynamical-disruption constraints limit the upper edge. Future GECCO, AMEGO-X gamma-ray missions can test the Hawking-evaporation signature.
citations: Carr, Kühnel, Sandstad 2016, Phys. Rev. D 94, 083504 (PBH constraints review)
Niikura et al. 2019, Nature Astron. 3, 524 (Subaru HSC PBH microlensing)
Green, Kavanagh 2021, J. Phys. G 48, 043001 (PBH DM review)
Carr, Kühnel 2020, Annu. Rev. Nucl. Part. Sci. 70, 355 (PBH review)

cells.content: Primordial black holes in the LIGO mass window: M ~ 1-100 M_sun. Once proposed as a substantial DM fraction (especially after GW150914), the window is now strongly excluded as ≳ 10% DM by combined microlensing (OGLE, EROS, MACHO), GW merger-rate statistics, CMB-accretion limits, and dwarf-galaxy survival constraints.
cells.description: Closed cell as a DM candidate: PBHs in 1-100 M_sun are ≲ 1% DM at the present empirical limit (Carr-Kühnel 2020; Green-Kavanagh 2021). They may still contribute as a small fraction of merging BBHs, but the LIGO-Virgo-KAGRA BBH population is now dominantly compatible with astrophysical (stellar-collapse + dynamical-cluster) origin, with PBH contribution ≲ 0.1%.
citations: Bird et al. 2016, Phys. Rev. Lett. 116, 201301 (PBH-BBH proposal)
Brandt 2016, Astrophys. J. Lett. 824, L31 (Eridanus II constraint)
Carr, Kühnel 2020, Annu. Rev. Nucl. Part. Sci. 70, 355 (PBH review)
Hütsi, Raidal, Vaskonen, Veermäe 2021, JCAP 03, 068 (BBH-rate PBH limits)

cells.content: Exotic compact objects: boson stars, gravastars, fuzzballs, dark stars, anisotropic stars, wormholes — proposed BH-mimickers that match BH external metric beyond the horizon scale but differ at horizon scale (no horizon, soft surface, etc.). Each model predicts a distinct GW-ringdown 'echo' signature or distinct shadow morphology potentially detectable at next-generation precision.
cells.description: Empty cell as a confirmed population. Some boson-star and gravastar models are ruled out (the inverse-T compactness configurations exceed observed BH-shadow morphology). However, dark-matter-aware exotic-compact-object models with weak self-interaction remain viable as a low-fraction admixture in the merging-compact-object population. GW-echo searches in LIGO-Virgo data (Abedi-Dykaar-Afshordi 2017+) have produced no confirmed detections as of 2024-26.
citations: Cardoso, Pani 2019, Living Rev. Relativ. 22, 4 (exotic compact objects review)
Liebling, Palenzuela 2017, Living Rev. Relativ. 20, 5 (boson stars)
Mazur, Mottola 2004, Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. 101, 9545 (gravastars)
Abedi, Dykaar, Afshordi 2017, Phys. Rev. D 96, 082004 (GW echo searches)

closure_status.description: Partial. The structural classification (object-class × mass-regime × observational-status) is well-established for the major populations: WDs, NSs, magnetars, stellar-mass BHs (XRB and GW), and SMBHs at galactic centers and AGN. Open: (i) the IMBH population is sparsely sampled — confirmation of a continuous population in the 10^2-10^5 M_sun range awaits LISA observations and improved EHT/ULX surveys; (ii) the lower-mass-gap (3-5 M_sun) is being populated by GW observations but its structural meaning (genuine gap vs selection effect) remains open; (iii) quark-star identification has no confirmed individual; (iv) primordial-BH presence at any DM fraction is partially constrained — some mass windows (asteroid-mass) remain open as substantial DM fractions, others (LIGO-mass, supernova-mass) are excluded; (v) exotic-compact-object populations remain pure predictions with no confirmed individuals; (vi) the dense-matter EOS at NS-core densities (4-6 n_0) is partially constrained but classification of high-density-matter phases (hadron-quark crossover, hyperon-onset, kaon-condensate, color-superconductivity) is itself partial-closure pending data from post-merger GW signals and additional NICER targets.

predictive_yield.prediction: Maximum neutron-star mass M_TOV ≳ 2.0 M_sun (lower bound from observation of massive pulsars)
predictive_yield.description: TOV-equation integration with any reasonable hadronic EOS predicts a maximum NS mass; the empirical lower bound from PSR J0740+6620 (2.08 M_sun, Cromartie et al. 2020; Fonseca et al. 2021) and PSR J0348+0432 (2.01 M_sun, Antoniadis et al. 2013) confirms M_TOV ≥ 2.0 M_sun, ruling out the softest hadronic EOS that fail to support such masses.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Oppenheimer, Volkoff 1939, Phys. Rev. 55, 374 (TOV equation)
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Cromartie et al. 2020, Nature Astron. 4, 72 (PSR J0740+6620 mass)

predictive_yield.prediction: Black-hole no-hair theorem: stationary axisymmetric BHs are described by the Kerr-Newman family with parameters (M, J, Q) only
predictive_yield.description: Israel-Carter-Hawking-Robinson uniqueness theorems predict that astrophysical BHs (Q ≈ 0 in practice) are fully described by (M, J). EHT shadow imaging of Sgr A* and M87* is consistent with Kerr-metric predictions at current angular-resolution precision (~10% on shadow diameter). BBH-ringdown spectroscopy (Isi et al. 2019, GW150914 first-overtone analysis; updated in O3+O4 data) probes consistency at the percent level; results are consistent with Kerr. Status `confirmed` AT CURRENT PRECISION; future-precision tests (LISA, next-generation EHT) will probe nearby modifications at the per-mille level. Caveats not falsifying the prediction but limiting its scope: the theorem assumes vacuum (matter near horizon could in principle add hair); scalar-tensor and dark-environmental modifications cannot yet be excluded at next-precision levels.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Israel 1967, Phys. Rev. 164, 1776; Carter 1971, Phys. Rev. Lett. 26, 331 (uniqueness theorems)
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Isi, Giesler, Farr, Scheel, Teukolsky 2019, Phys. Rev. Lett. 123, 111102 (BBH-ringdown no-hair test); EHT Collaboration 2022, Astrophys. J. Lett. 930, L12 (Sgr A* shadow consistent with Kerr)

predictive_yield.prediction: Sgr A* shadow diameter ≈ 51 ± 2 μas (Kerr-metric prediction given M and D)
predictive_yield.description: Given the GRAVITY-Collaboration mass M ≈ 4.297 × 10^6 M_sun and distance D ≈ 8.277 kpc, the Kerr-metric shadow has predicted angular diameter ≈ 51 μas. EHT Collaboration 2022 measured shadow diameter 51.8 ± 2.3 μas, in excellent agreement.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Bardeen 1973 (Kerr shadow formulation); Falcke, Melia, Agol 2000, Astrophys. J. Lett. 528, L13
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: EHT Collaboration 2022, Astrophys. J. Lett. 930, L12 (Sgr A* shadow)

predictive_yield.prediction: Binary neutron-star mergers produce kilonovae and r-process nucleosynthesis
predictive_yield.description: Lattimer-Schramm 1974 and Eichler-Livio-Piran-Schramm 1989 predicted that NS-NS mergers eject neutron-rich material that undergoes r-process nucleosynthesis and powers a kilonova transient. GW170817 + multi-messenger EM follow-up confirmed: kilonova spectrum (AT 2017gfo), r-process line identifications (strontium, Watson et al. 2019), and short-GRB association (GRB 170817A).
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Lattimer, Schramm 1974, Astrophys. J. 192, L145; Eichler, Livio, Piran, Schramm 1989, Nature 340, 126
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Abbott et al. (LVC + EM) 2017, Astrophys. J. Lett. 848, L12 (GW170817 multi-messenger); Watson et al. 2019, Nature 574, 497 (strontium r-process)

predictive_yield.prediction: Magnetars produce extragalactic Fast Radio Bursts
predictive_yield.description: Magnetar-flare models for FRBs (Popov-Postnov 2013; Lyubarsky 2014) predicted that magnetars can produce coherent radio bursts of the FRB type. SGR J1935+2154 produced FRB 200428, the first galactic FRB, with luminosity bridging the SGR-burst and extragalactic-FRB populations — confirming a magnetar source for at least a subpopulation of FRBs.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Popov, Postnov 2013, arXiv:1307.4924; Lyubarsky 2014, MNRAS 442, L9
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: CHIME/FRB Collaboration 2020, Nature 587, 54 (FRB 200428); Bochenek et al. 2020, Nature 587, 59

predictive_yield.prediction: Hawking radiation from astrophysical black holes
predictive_yield.description: Hawking 1974 predicted that BHs emit thermal radiation at T_H = ℏc³/(8πGMk_B). For stellar-mass BHs T_H ~ 10^-8 K — far below the CMB temperature, making the emission unobservable against the CMB background. For Sgr A* (M ≈ 4×10^6 M_sun) T_H ~ 10^-14 K. Astrophysical Hawking radiation is retro-explanatory: it features in the theoretical framework but no direct measurement is feasible with current or near-future technology. (Possible exception: PBH evaporation in the asteroid-mass window may produce gamma-ray emission detectable by GECCO/AMEGO-X — that would move PBH-related Hawking radiation toward confirmable, but no detection as of 2026.)
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Hawking 1974, Nature 248, 30; 1975, Commun. Math. Phys. 43, 199

predictive_yield.prediction: Primordial black holes constitute 100% of dark matter in the LIGO mass window (1-100 M_sun)
predictive_yield.description: Bird et al. 2016 proposed that the GW150914 BBH could be the first signal of a PBH-DM population in the LIGO mass range. Subsequent analyses combining microlensing (OGLE, EROS), GW merger-rate statistics, CMB-spectral-distortion constraints from accretion, and dwarf-galaxy dynamical-friction limits exclude PBHs as 100% DM in 1-100 M_sun at f_PBH ≲ 10^-3 at peak sensitivity. PBHs may still contribute as a small subdominant component of the BBH merger population.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Bird, Cholis, Muñoz, Ali-Haïmoud, Kamionkowski, Kovetz, Raccanelli, Riess 2016, Phys. Rev. Lett. 116, 201301
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Brandt 2016, Astrophys. J. Lett. 824, L31 (Eridanus II); Hütsi et al. 2021, JCAP 03, 068 (BBH-rate constraints); Carr, Kühnel 2020 review (combined)

predictive_yield.prediction: Intermediate-mass black hole population in 10^2-10^5 M_sun range
predictive_yield.description: Multiple formation channels predict IMBHs: Pop-III stellar collapse, runaway stellar collisions in dense clusters, direct-collapse seeds for SMBHs. As of 2024-26 the IMBH population is sparsely sampled: GW190521 final BH (~142 M_sun) is a confirmed individual at the lower end; ULX candidates (HLX-1, M82 X-1) remain disputed. Confirmation of a continuous IMBH population, with empirical mass function, awaits LISA observations (2030s+) of IMBH inspirals and improved EHT/ULX surveys.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Madau, Rees 2001, Astrophys. J. Lett. 551, L27 (Pop-III seeds); Portegies Zwart, McMillan 2002, Astrophys. J. 576, 899 (runaway-collision IMBH formation)

predictive_yield.prediction: Quark-star / strange-quark-matter compact-object population at NS masses
predictive_yield.description: If Bodmer-Witten SQM-stability conjecture holds, strange quark stars (or hybrid hadron-quark stars with a strange quark matter core) should exist as a parallel or replacement compact-object population. No confirmed individual identification. Candidates have been proposed (4U 1820-30, RX J1856.5-3754) based on inferred small radii but remain consistent with stiff-hadronic-EOS interpretations. NICER + GW170817 data are starting to discriminate among hadron-only, hybrid, and quark-only EOS classes but do not yet require a quark-matter component.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Bodmer 1971, Phys. Rev. D 4, 1601; Witten 1984, Phys. Rev. D 30, 272; Alcock, Farhi, Olinto 1986, Astrophys. J. 310, 261

predictive_yield.prediction: Black-hole-merger ringdown frequencies match Kerr quasi-normal-mode spectrum
predictive_yield.description: Chandrasekhar-Detweiler 1975 derived the quasi-normal-mode spectrum of Kerr BHs. BBH-merger ringdowns observed by LIGO-Virgo (starting with GW150914) match the predicted (l, m, n) mode frequencies for a Kerr final BH of inferred (M_f, χ_f). Recent overtone-resolved analyses (Isi et al. 2019; Capano et al. 2023) extract the n=1 overtone in addition to the dominant n=0 mode, providing two-mode no-hair tests consistent with Kerr at the few-percent level.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Chandrasekhar, Detweiler 1975, Proc. R. Soc. Lond. A 344, 441 (Kerr QNM spectrum)
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: LIGO/Virgo Collaboration 2016, Phys. Rev. Lett. 116, 221101 (GW150914 ringdown); Isi, Giesler, Farr, Scheel, Teukolsky 2019, Phys. Rev. Lett. 123, 111102 (overtone-resolved no-hair)

related_entries: spacetime-symmetry-groups (every compact object is a GR solution; the cell-stsym-poincare and cell-stsym-diff cells underlie the entire FC)

hadronic-states (NS interior composition derives from hadronic matter at high density; the connection is partial — NS interiors involve dense uniform-matter QCD phases distinct from the bound-state spectroscopy that hadronic-states organizes)
sm-rep-content (matter content of compact objects — electrons in WDs, neutrons / hyperons / quarks in NSs — derives from SM particle content)
cft-bootstrap-exclusion-regions (BH thermodynamics + microstate counting derive from holographic CFT in the ads-cft program; aspired for general astrophysical BHs)
bh-info-paradox (existing open-frontier node; semantically related but linked via narrative since cross-classification edges connect FCs only)

PLANNED FUTURE STRUCTURAL-FC: dense-matter-equation-of-state classification (EOS-FC) — would organize hadronic-only, hybrid hadron-quark, quark-only, and exotic EOS classes; compact-astrophysical-objects would specialize EOS-FC's mass-radius-stability cells. Deferred from v21 authoring per the EOS co-authoring decision (MAP_v21_findings.md §2)

PLANNED FUTURE PHENOMENON-FC: gravitational-wave-event catalog (would organize GW150914-style merger events as cells; multi-edge with compact-astrophysical-objects since each GW event IS a compact-object merger)
citations: Shapiro, Teukolsky 1983, Black Holes, White Dwarfs, and Neutron Stars (canonical textbook)
Glendenning 2000, Compact Stars (NS structure)
Lattimer, Prakash 2016, Phys. Rep. 621, 127 (NS observational constraints on EOS)
Frank, King, Raine 2002, Accretion Power in Astrophysics (BH accretion)
Carr, Kühnel 2020, Annu. Rev. Nucl. Part. Sci. 70, 355 (PBH review)
Cardoso, Pani 2019, Living Rev. Relativ. 22, 4 (exotic compact objects)
Greene, Strader, Ho 2020, Annu. Rev. Astron. Astrophys. 58, 257 (IMBH review)
Kaspi, Beloborodov 2017, Annu. Rev. Astron. Astrophys. 55, 261 (magnetar review)
LIGO/Virgo/KAGRA Collaboration 2021, Phys. Rev. X 13, 011048 (GWTC-3)
EHT Collaboration 2019, 2022 (M87* and Sgr A* shadows)

nodes.label: Topological insulator and superconductor materials
nodes.full_name: Classification of topological-insulator, topological-superconductor, topological-semimetal, and higher-order-topological materials — confirmed individuals and predicted candidates by symmetry class, dimensionality, and observational status
nodes.description: Topological insulators and superconductors are materials whose ground state has nontrivial band-topology in the Brillouin zone given a specified symmetry class. The bulk is gapped (or gapless in a protected way for semimetals); the boundary hosts robust gapless modes whose existence is guaranteed by bulk topological invariants via bulk-edge correspondence. The classification organizes confirmed individual materials, confirmed material families, candidate materials with contested evidence, and predicted-but-not-isolated cells. Each cell sits at a specific (symmetry-class, dimension, observational-status) coordinate. The symmetry classes are the tenfold-way (A, AIII, AI, BDI, D, DIII, AII, CII, C, CI) labeling combinations of time-reversal, particle-hole, and chiral (sublattice) symmetries, extended by crystalline-symmetry refinements (TCIs), magnetic-symmetry refinements (magnetic TIs / axion insulators), and higher-order refinements (HOTIs with corner / hinge modes). The classification is empirical at the cell level — cells are specific physical materials (Bi₂Se₃, HgTe, SnTe, MnBi₂Te₄, ...) with sharp experimental characterization (ARPES Fermi-arc imaging, STM tunneling spectra, transport quantization) — while the axis structure is derived from the formal tenfold-way / cobordism / SymTFT classifications that organize possible phases. Mendeleev-style empty cells include predicted intrinsic axion insulators with cleanly-realized θ = π without surface complications, fractional topological insulators (3D analog of FQHE — no material confirmed), higher-order topological superconductors in specific symmetry classes (predicted but uncleanly identified), and class CI / CII materials (no clean real-material candidates). The classification additionally records falsified claims — most notably the 2018 InSb/Al Majorana-zero-mode quantized-conductance claim, retracted in 2021 after the signatures were shown to be disorder-induced rather than topological.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Crystalline solid-state materials and engineered heterostructures whose low-energy electronic structure admits a topological classification, including: bulk crystalline TIs (Bi₂Se₃-family chalcogenides, SnTe family TCIs), 2D monolayer TIs (HgTe/CdTe quantum wells, 1T'-WTe₂, bismuthene), 3D magnetic TIs (MnBi₂Te₄ family), candidate intrinsic and proximity-induced topological superconductors (FeTe₀.₅₅Se₀.₄₅, CuₓBi₂Se₃, semiconductor-superconductor wires), topological semimetals (TaAs-family Weyl, Cd₃As₂-family Dirac, magnetic Weyl Co₃Sn₂S₂), higher-order TI candidates (Bi(111) hinges and corners), and engineered Majorana platforms. Excludes: (i) integer and fractional quantum Hall systems — those are a distinct phenomenon-FC (fractional-quantum-Hall-states) with their own structural pairing (MTC); (ii) photonic / acoustic / mechanical topological-band analogs — separable phenomenon-FC if authored later; (iii) gapless trivial metals without topological band features; (iv) interacting strongly-correlated systems where the noninteracting tenfold-way classification fails (Fidkowski-Kitaev 2010 showed BDI-d1 ℤ collapses to ℤ₈ with interactions — the classification of interacting topological matter is a separate, partially-developed effort).
nodes.organizing_principle: Each material is identified by (i) its tenfold-way symmetry class (which discrete symmetries — time-reversal T, particle-hole C, chiral S = TC — its mean-field Hamiltonian preserves, anti-preserves, or violates); (ii) its spatial dimension; (iii) the value of the topological invariant appropriate to (i, ii) — a ℤ or ℤ₂ class label depending on the row of the Bott-periodicity table. For materials beyond the strict tenfold-way (crystalline-symmetry-protected, magnetic, higher-order, gapless-protected), the symmetry-class axis carries refinement labels. The observational-status axis is essential because the field operates at the boundary between (a) materials whose topology is bulk-and-boundary confirmed (HgTe, Bi₂Se₃ family), (b) materials whose topology is bulk-confirmed but boundary modes are contested or missing (SmB₆ topological Kondo insulator), (c) candidate platforms whose evidence is debated (FeTe₀.₅₅Se₀.₄₅ for TSC, MnBi₂Te₄ for axion insulator), (d) predicted-but-not-identified phases (fractional TIs, intrinsic-bulk axion insulators), and (e) falsified claims (retracted 2018 Nature MZM signatures in InSb/Al). The classification is constrained from above by formal results — the tenfold-way table fixes which dimension-class combinations admit nontrivial topology in noninteracting systems; Freed-Hopkins cobordism extends this to interacting SPT phases; SymTFT extends to generalized-symmetry-protected variants — and from below by experimental quantization tests (quantized longitudinal conductance, ARPES Fermi-arc detection, STM zero-bias-peak signatures, magnetoelectric response).

classification_axes.name: symmetry-class
classification_axes.description: Symmetry class in the tenfold-way (Altland-Zirnbauer) classification, extended with refinement labels for phases beyond the strict noninteracting / on-site-symmetry classification. The first 10 values are the canonical tenfold-way classes specified by combinations of T (time-reversal, T² = ±1 or absent), C (particle-hole, C² = ±1 or absent), and S = TC (chiral, present iff T and C both present). The refinement labels denote: crystalline-mirror-protected (TCIs like SnTe where mirror symmetry promotes the classification), crystalline-rotation-protected (some HOTI classes), magnetic-AII (magnetic-symmetry-protected as in MnBi₂Te₄), higher-order-AII / higher-order-DIII (HOTI / HOTSC subclasses with corner or hinge modes), Weyl-semimetal-class-A (gapless protected Weyl points in nonmagnetic systems), Dirac-semimetal-class-A (gapless protected Dirac points).

classification_axes.name: dimensionality
classification_axes.description: Spatial dimension of the bulk material. higher-order-2D-corners: 2D system with 0D corner modes (codimension-2 boundary). higher-order-3D-hinges: 3D system with 1D hinge modes (codimension-2 boundary). higher-order-3D-corners: 3D system with 0D corner modes (codimension-3 boundary). HOTIs are a separate dimensional axis kind because their boundary mode lives at higher codimension than the canonical d-1 boundary.

classification_axes.name: observational-status
classification_axes.description: Empirical status of the material's topological classification. confirmed-bulk-and-boundary: both bulk topological invariant (via ARPES band structure + DFT calculation) and predicted boundary mode (via STM, transport, ARPES Fermi-arc) are observed and quantitatively consistent. confirmed-bulk-only: bulk topology established, boundary mode contested or absent (e.g., SmB₆ where Kondo bulk is established but surface state interpretation is debated). candidate-contested-evidence: signatures exist but interpretation is actively contested (FeTe₀.₅₅Se₀.₄₅ zero-bias peaks, Sr₂RuO₄ pairing symmetry post-2019-NMR, MZM platforms with non-quantized conductance peaks). predicted-only: theoretical prediction without confirmed material realization (fractional TIs, intrinsic-bulk axion insulators, class CI / CII candidates). falsified-or-retracted-claim: a specific published claim has been falsified (retraction, follow-up showing signal is non-topological). Distinguished from the structural-FC observational closure framework — here the cells are individual materials, not invariants — but operates analogously to the v21 phenomenon-FC observational-status axis.

cells.content: HgTe/CdTe quantum well above critical thickness ~6.3 nm
realized_examples: Molecular-beam-epitaxy-grown HgTe quantum wells (Bernevig-Hughes-Zhang theoretical prediction 2006; König et al. transport observation 2007). The quantum-spin-Hall phase with longitudinal conductance σ_xx = 2e²/h plateau is observed.
cells.description: First experimentally confirmed 2D topological insulator. BHZ band-inversion mechanism (s-p band inversion driven by strong spin-orbit coupling and the well-thickness tunability) places the system in class AII (time-reversal preserved, no particle-hole or chiral) with ℤ₂ invariant = 1.
citations: Bernevig, Hughes, Zhang 2006, Science 314, 1757 (BHZ prediction)
König et al. 2007, Science 318, 766 (transport confirmation)

Bernevig, Hughes, Zhang 2006, Science 314, 1757 'Quantum Spin Hall Effect and Topological Phase Transition in HgTe Quantum Wells' (source-value: d_c = 6.3 nm theoretical critical thickness for HgTe/CdTe band-inversion; T13 cell-direct, T10 uncertainty=null for theoretical-predicted-and-confirmed value with sample-to-sample d_c variation absorbed at the ~0.1 nm level not quoted as a precision error bar)

Koenig et al. 2007, Science 318, 766 arXiv:0710.0582 'Quantum Spin Hall Insulator State in HgTe Quantum Wells' (source-value: d > 6.3 nm samples exhibit the QSH plateau; d < 6.3 nm samples behave as conventional insulators; quantum phase transition at d_c = 6.3 nm independently confirmed by magnetic-field-induced insulator-to-metal transition)

cells.content: Bi₂Se₃, Bi₂Te₃, Sb₂Te₃ — the canonical 3D TI family
realized_examples: Bi₂Se₃ — single Dirac cone at Γ on the surface confirmed by ARPES (Xia, Hsieh 2009). Bi₂Te₃ and Sb₂Te₃ — analogous family members with larger bulk gap and stronger surface Dirac cones. STM imaging confirms surface state robustness to non-magnetic disorder.
cells.description: Canonical 3D class AII topological insulators. Strong spin-orbit coupling inverts the band gap at Γ; ℤ₂ invariant (ν_0; ν_1ν_2ν_3) = (1;000). Surface hosts a single Dirac cone with helical spin-momentum locking — directly observed by spin-resolved ARPES. The bulk insulating gap is ~0.3 eV (Bi₂Se₃) to ~0.15 eV (Bi₂Te₃), making these the most experimentally accessible 3D TIs.
citations: Zhang et al. 2009, Nature Phys. 5, 438 (theoretical prediction)
Xia et al. 2009, Nature Phys. 5, 398 (Bi₂Se₃ ARPES)
Hsieh et al. 2009, Nature 460, 1101 (spin-resolved ARPES)

Zhang, Liu, Qi, Dai, Fang, Zhang 2009, Nature Phys. 5, 438 'Topological insulators in Bi2Se3, Bi2Te3 and Sb2Te3 with a single Dirac cone on the surface' (source-value: Bi2Se3 bulk insulating gap ≈ 0.3 eV from first-principles band structure; Bi2Te3 ≈ 0.15 eV; canonical 3D class AII TI family with ℤ₂ invariant (1;000); T13 cell-direct, T10 uncertainty=null for characteristic literature value with ~0.05 eV ARPES-vs-DFT variation not quoted as formal error bar)

Xia et al. 2009, Nature Phys. 5, 398 'Observation of a large-gap topological-insulator class with a single Dirac cone on the surface' (source-value: ARPES confirms Bi2Se3 bulk gap ~0.3 eV with single Dirac cone at Γ on (111) surface)

cells.content: Monolayer 1T'-WTe₂
realized_examples: Monolayer 1T'-WTe₂ exfoliated from bulk crystals. Quantum spin Hall edge conductance observed up to ~100 K — substantially higher than HgTe. STM verifies edge state density.
cells.description: Second confirmed 2D TI platform after HgTe. Class AII, ℤ₂ = 1. Significant because the bulk gap (~50 meV) is robust to room-temperature thermal fluctuations and the material is air-stable when encapsulated, making it the most practically useful 2D TI to date.
citations: Qian et al. 2014, Science 346, 1344 (prediction)
Fei et al. 2017, Nature Phys. 13, 677 (transport)
Wu et al. 2018, Science 359, 76 (edge state mapping)

Qian, Liu, Fu, Li 2014, Science 346, 1344 'Quantum spin Hall effect in two-dimensional transition metal dichalcogenides' (source-value: monolayer 1T'-WTe2 predicted bulk inverted gap ≈ 50 meV from first-principles; class AII 2D TI with ℤ₂ = 1; T13 cell-direct, T10 uncertainty=null for characteristic value)

Fei et al. 2017, Nature Phys. 13, 677 'Edge conduction in monolayer WTe2' (source-value: QSH edge conductance plateau persists up to ~100 K consistent with bulk gap ~50 meV scale; k_B × 100 K = 8.6 meV thermal-floor sets the lower bound on the gap consistent with edge-state robustness)

cells.content: Bismuthene — monolayer Bi(111) on SiC substrate
realized_examples: Bismuthene grown epitaxially on SiC(0001). Spin-orbit-induced gap of ~0.8 eV — the largest known 2D-TI gap. Edge states verified by ARPES and STM.
cells.description: Class AII 2D TI with the largest experimentally confirmed bulk gap (~0.8 eV), making it the most thermally robust 2D TI candidate. The Bi honeycomb lattice on SiC realizes the BHZ-type Hamiltonian via SiC substrate effects.
citations: Reis et al. 2017, Science 357, 287 (bismuthene observation)

Reis et al. 2017, Science 357, 287 'Bismuthene on a SiC substrate: a candidate for a high-temperature quantum spin Hall material' (source-value: monolayer Bi(111) on SiC(0001) substrate exhibits a spin-orbit-induced bulk gap ≈ 0.8 eV — the largest known 2D-TI gap — confirmed by STS gap-fitting and ARPES band-bottom extraction; edge states verified by both STM and ARPES; T13 cell-direct, T10 uncertainty=null for characteristic literature value with STS-vs-ARPES extractions varying by ~0.1 eV not quoted as a formal error bar)

cells.content: SmB₆ — topological Kondo insulator candidate
realized_examples: SmB₆ single crystals. Bulk Kondo insulating behavior confirmed; surface state interpretation contested — some experiments support a topological surface state (ARPES of in-gap states, surface conduction at low T), others interpret the in-gap features as non-topological.
cells.description: Candidate strongly-correlated topological insulator. The bulk gap is Kondo-derived (~5 meV), and the topological invariant should be ℤ₂ = 1 from the band-inversion driven by Sm f-electron hybridization with d-electron conduction bands. However, the topological-surface-state interpretation remains contested as of 2026 — alternative explanations include non-topological surface conductivity. Classified `confirmed-bulk-only` reflecting the asymmetry between bulk and boundary evidence.
citations: Dzero, Sun, Galitski, Coleman 2010, Phys. Rev. Lett. 104, 106408 (prediction)
Kim et al. 2014, Nature Mater. 13, 466 (surface transport)
Hlawenka et al. 2018, Nature Commun. 9, 517 (alternative interpretation)

Dzero, Sun, Galitski, Coleman 2010, Phys. Rev. Lett. 104, 106408 'Topological Kondo insulators' (source-value: SmB6 predicted as a strongly-correlated topological Kondo insulator with narrow Kondo-derived bulk gap on the order of meV from Sm f-electron / d-electron hybridization; ℤ₂ = 1 expected from band-inversion; T13 cell-direct, T10 uncertainty=null for characteristic value)

Kim et al. 2014, Nature Mater. 13, 466 'Surface Hall effect and nonlocal transport in SmB6: evidence for surface conduction' (source-value: transport activation analysis yields bulk Kondo gap ≈ 4-5 meV with surface conduction surviving to low T; topological-surface-state interpretation remains contested as of 2026 [Hlawenka 2018 alternative interpretation])

cells.content: SnTe and Pb₁₋ₓSnₓTe — topological crystalline insulators
realized_examples: SnTe single crystals (Tanaka et al. 2012 ARPES). Pb₁₋ₓSnₓTe alloy with x > 0.32 shows the band-inverted phase. Surface Dirac cones at L points of the bulk BZ confirmed; symmetry-broken splitting under uniaxial strain confirmed (Okada et al. 2013 STM).
cells.description: First experimentally confirmed topological crystalline insulator. Topology protected by mirror symmetry rather than time-reversal — so the surface Dirac cones are protected only on mirror-invariant surface terminations [(001), (110), (111)]. Demonstrates that crystalline symmetries promote the tenfold-way classification.
citations: Fu 2011, Phys. Rev. Lett. 106, 106802 (TCI proposal)
Hsieh, Lin, Liu, Duan, Bansil, Fu 2012, Nature Commun. 3, 982 (SnTe prediction)
Tanaka et al. 2012, Nature Phys. 8, 800 (ARPES confirmation)

Hsieh, Lin, Liu, Duan, Bansil, Fu 2012, Nature Commun. 3, 982 'Topological crystalline insulators in the SnTe material class' (source-value: Pb1-xSnxTe band-inversion alloy threshold x ≳ 0.32 at low T where the TCI phase emerges with mirror-symmetry-protected surface Dirac cones at L points; T13 cell-direct, T4 bound-direction lower (≥), cumulative T4 entry 32; T5/T10 uncertainty=null tied to bound-direction inequality)

Tanaka et al. 2012, Nature Phys. 8, 800 'Experimental realization of a topological crystalline insulator in SnTe' (source-value: ARPES confirms surface Dirac cones at L points in SnTe and in band-inverted Pb1-xSnxTe with x > 0.32 threshold)

cells.content: FeTe₀.₅₅Se₀.₄₅ — superconducting iron chalcogenide with claimed Majorana surface states
realized_examples: Single crystals of FeTe₀.₅₅Se₀.₄₅. Wang et al. 2018 reported zero-bias conductance peaks at vortex cores via STM, interpreted as Majorana zero modes. Subsequent experiments report inconsistent vortex-to-vortex peak behavior; alternative non-topological interpretations (Caroli-de Gennes-Matricon states, Yu-Shiba-Rusinov bound states from impurities) remain viable.
cells.description: Most-studied candidate for an intrinsic topological superconductor with Majorana zero modes at vortex cores. The bulk is a multi-band Fe-based superconductor (T_c ~ 14 K). Combined with band-inversion-driven topological surface states, the surface superconducting state would be effectively a 2D class D topological superconductor — vortex cores would host MZMs. The 2018 STM observations are striking but not definitive: only a subset of vortices show the zero-bias peak; the field dependence is unclear; and the impurity interpretation has not been ruled out.
citations: Wang et al. 2018, Science 362, 333 (vortex STM)
Zhang et al. 2018, Science 360, 182 (topological surface state)
Machida et al. 2019, Nature Mater. 18, 811 (vortex-dependence)

Wang et al. 2018, Science 362, 333 'Evidence for Majorana bound states in an iron-based superconductor' (source-value: FeTe0.55Se0.45 single crystals exhibit bulk superconductivity below T_c ≈ 14 K with claimed Majorana zero modes at vortex cores; T13 cell-direct, T24 first-use: temperature-as-energy-scale convention k_B T_c = 8.617333262 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K × 14 K = 1.21 meV; T10 uncertainty=null for characteristic literature T_c value with ~0.5 K sample-to-sample variation absorbed below the precision floor)

Yin et al. 2015, Nature Phys. 11, 543 'Observation of a robust zero-energy bound state in iron-based superconductor Fe(Te,Se)' (source-value: ARPES topological surface state combined with bulk T_c ≈ 14 K produces effective 2D class D topological surface superconductor)

cells.content: Sr₂RuO₄ — long-debated chiral p-wave superconductor candidate
realized_examples: Sr₂RuO₄ single crystals; long thought to be a p_x + ip_y triplet superconductor (Mackenzie-Maeno 2003). 2019 NMR Knight-shift measurements (Pustogow et al. 2019) showed the spin susceptibility drops in the superconducting state — inconsistent with the equal-spin-pairing triplet scenario. The pairing symmetry is now actively re-debated; the original chiral-p-wave claim is substantially weakened though not formally retracted.
cells.description: Once the leading candidate for an intrinsic topological superconductor with chiral edge modes and half-quantum vortices. The post-2019 NMR result is one of the cleanest empirical updates against a topological-superconductor claim in the literature. Listed here as `candidate-contested-evidence` rather than `falsified` because the pairing is plausibly still unconventional and possibly topological (alternative pairings like d-wave or s+id mixtures remain candidates), but the specific chiral p-wave + topological-SC scenario is substantially weakened.
citations: Mackenzie, Maeno 2003, Rev. Mod. Phys. 75, 657 (chiral p-wave proposal)
Pustogow et al. 2019, Nature 574, 72 (NMR Knight shift)
Ghosh, Shekhter, Jerzembeck et al. 2020, Nature Phys. 17, 199 (post-NMR ultrasound)

Maeno et al. 1994, Nature 372, 532 'Superconductivity in a layered perovskite without copper' (source-value: Sr2RuO4 high-purity-limit superconducting T_c = 1.5 K from specific-heat and resistivity; T13 cell-direct, T24 second-use: k_B T_c = 0.129 meV via k_B = 8.617333262 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K; T10 uncertainty=null for characteristic literature value, sample-purity-dependence below 1.5 K resolved in clean-crystal limit)

Mackenzie, Maeno 2003, Rev. Mod. Phys. 75, 657 'The superconductivity of Sr2RuO4 and the physics of spin-triplet pairing' (source-value: review confirms T_c = 1.5 K canonical value; chiral p_x + ip_y triplet pairing claim post-2019 disconfirmed by NMR [Pustogow 2019] though T_c itself remains well-established; observational-status = candidate-contested-evidence reflects the pairing-symmetry uncertainty, not the T_c value)

cells.content: InSb/Al nanowire — the retracted 2018 Majorana zero-mode claim
realized_examples: Zhang et al. 2018 Nature paper claimed quantized 2e²/h conductance peaks attributable to Majorana zero modes in an InSb/Al hybrid nanowire. The paper was retracted in 2021 after re-analysis of the original data revealed that signatures consistent with MZMs were instead disorder-induced Andreev bound states, with the published quantization claim not supported by the underlying data.
cells.description: Most prominent retracted topological claim in condensed matter. Documents the falsified-claim cell-content: the prediction (1D class D topological superconductor with quantized MZM tunneling conductance) was specific, the original signature was clean-looking, and the falsification was definitive (data re-analysis showed the original claim was unsupported). This cell is recorded for the project's empirical-state map — it is a member of the small set of falsified specific predictions in the dataset (alongside proton decay tree-level GUT, H-dibaryon, and PBH-as-100%-DM-in-LIGO-window from prior FCs). Other MZM-platform searches (InAs/Al, InSb/Pb, etc.) continue with more careful protocols.
citations: Zhang et al. 2018, Nature 556, 74 (original claim)
Frolov 2021, Nature 592, 17 (commentary on disorder concerns)
Zhang et al. 2021, Nature 591, 354 (retraction)

cells.content: TaAs, NbAs, TaP, NbP — the canonical Weyl semimetal family
realized_examples: TaAs single crystals (Lv et al. 2015 ARPES; Xu et al. 2015 surface Fermi arcs). Surface Fermi-arc spectra confirmed; bulk Weyl-point band crossings confirmed via soft-X-ray ARPES at varying photon energies.
cells.description: First experimentally confirmed Weyl semimetals. Time-reversal preserved but inversion broken; the bulk has 12 pairs of Weyl points with definite chirality, connected by surface Fermi arcs (the hallmark Weyl-semimetal signature). Class A in the symmetry sense (no T-anticommuting symmetries enter the bulk band classification), but distinguished by being a gapless protected phase rather than a gapped TI — the symmetry-class axis uses the refinement label `Weyl-semimetal-class-A`.
citations: Wan, Turner, Vishwanath, Savrasov 2011, Phys. Rev. B 83, 205101 (theoretical setup for pyrochlore iridates)
Huang et al. 2015, Nature Commun. 6, 7373 (TaAs prediction)
Lv et al. 2015, Phys. Rev. X 5, 031013 (TaAs bulk ARPES)
Xu et al. 2015, Science 349, 613 (surface Fermi arcs)

Huang et al. 2015, Nature Commun. 6, 7373 'A Weyl Fermion semimetal with surface Fermi arcs in the transition metal monopnictide TaAs class' (source-value: TaAs, TaP, NbAs, NbP family predicted to possess 12 pairs = 24 total bulk Weyl points per single-crystal Brillouin zone, identified via first-principles calculations and Berry-curvature integration; T13 cell-direct, T15 first-use: integer count encoded as kind=dimensionless + value=24 with integer-typing not preserved in v18 schema, v19 kind-enum-expansion signal continuation; T10 uncertainty=null for exact integer count)

Weng et al. 2015, Phys. Rev. X 5, 011029 'Weyl semimetal phase in noncentrosymmetric transition-metal monopnictides' (source-value: independent first-principles confirmation of 12 Weyl-point pairs in TaAs family with explicit BZ locations and chiralities)

Lv et al. 2015, Phys. Rev. X 5, 031013 arXiv:1502.04684 'Experimental discovery of Weyl semimetal TaAs' (source-value: soft-X-ray ARPES directly observes bulk Weyl-point band-crossings in TaAs consistent with predicted 12-pair structure)

cells.content: Co₃Sn₂S₂ — magnetic Weyl semimetal
realized_examples: Co₃Sn₂S₂ single crystals. Below T_C ~ 175 K, ferromagnetic ordering breaks time-reversal. Anomalous Hall effect with large intrinsic contribution, surface Fermi arcs imaged by STM (Morali et al. 2019), are consistent with bulk magnetic Weyl points.
cells.description: Confirmed magnetic Weyl semimetal — Weyl points arise from time-reversal-breaking ferromagnetic order combined with strong spin-orbit coupling. Demonstrates that Weyl-semimetal classification extends naturally to magnetic systems. Important because the anomalous Hall conductivity has a topological contribution proportional to the separation of Weyl points in momentum space.
citations: Liu et al. 2018, Nature Phys. 14, 1125 (Co₃Sn₂S₂ Weyl prediction)
Morali et al. 2019, Science 365, 1286 (Fermi arc STM)
Liu et al. 2019, Science 365, 1282 (ARPES confirmation)

Liu et al. 2018, Nature Phys. 14, 1125 'Giant anomalous Hall effect in a ferromagnetic kagome-lattice semimetal' (source-value: Co3Sn2S2 shandite ferromagnetic Curie temperature T_C ≈ 175 K from magnetization and anomalous-Hall onset; below T_C the time-reversal-breaking magnetic order on the Co kagome lattice gives rise to bulk Weyl points; T13 cell-direct, T24 third-use: k_B T_C = 15.08 meV via k_B = 8.617333262 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K × 175 K; T10 uncertainty=null for characteristic literature value, literature range 175-177 K sample- and method-dependent)

Morali et al. 2019, Science 365, 1286 'Fermi-arc diversity on surface terminations of the magnetic Weyl semimetal Co3Sn2S2' (source-value: STM imaging confirms surface Fermi arcs consistent with bulk Weyl-point structure below T_C; ARPES T_C ≈ 177 K [Wang 2021])

cells.content: Cd₃As₂ — 3D Dirac semimetal
realized_examples: Cd₃As₂ single crystals. ARPES (Neupane et al. 2014) confirms two bulk 3D Dirac points along the k_z axis protected by C₄ rotational symmetry. Magnetoresistance studies show the predicted negative longitudinal magnetoresistance from the chiral anomaly.
cells.description: Confirmed 3D Dirac semimetal. Time-reversal and inversion both preserved — without the symmetry-breaking that would split a Dirac point into Weyl pairs. The Dirac points are protected by an additional crystalline rotational symmetry. Bulk gapless protected phase. Important because applying a magnetic field splits the Dirac point into Weyl pairs, allowing in-situ Dirac→Weyl phase transition observation.
citations: Wang, Weng, Wu, Dai, Fang 2013, Phys. Rev. B 88, 125427 (prediction)
Neupane et al. 2014, Nature Commun. 5, 3786 (ARPES)
Liu et al. 2014, Nature Mater. 13, 677 (transport)

Wang, Weng, Wu, Dai, Fang 2013, Phys. Rev. B 88, 125427 'Three-dimensional Dirac semimetal and quantum transport in Cd3As2' (source-value: Cd3As2 predicted as a 3D Dirac semimetal with 2 bulk Dirac points along the k_z axis protected by C4 rotational symmetry; each Dirac point is 4-fold-degenerate band-crossing splittable into 2 Weyl pairs by symmetry-breaking magnetic field; T13 cell-direct, T15 second-use: integer count encoded as kind=dimensionless + value=2; T10 uncertainty=null for exact integer count)

Neupane et al. 2014, Nature Commun. 5, 3786 'Observation of a three-dimensional topological Dirac semimetal phase in high-mobility Cd3As2' (source-value: ARPES confirms the predicted 2 bulk 3D Dirac points along k_z in Cd3As2)

cells.content: MnBi₂Te₄ and (MnBi₂Te₄)(Bi₂Te₃)ₙ family — intrinsic magnetic TI / axion insulator candidate
realized_examples: MnBi₂Te₄ single crystals — antiferromagnetic ordering below T_N ~ 25 K. Quantum anomalous Hall effect observed in thin flakes (Deng et al. 2020 Science). Axion-insulator response (θ = π) signatures reported but contested in terms of cleanliness of the bulk vs surface contributions.
cells.description: Leading candidate for an intrinsic magnetic topological insulator and axion insulator. The combined symmetry T × τ (time-reversal × half-translation) is preserved in the antiferromagnetic state, giving rise to an axion-coupling θ = π protected by this combined symmetry. Distinguishes from extrinsic magnetic TIs (magnetically doped Bi₂Se₃) where the magnetism is disorder-averaged. Listed `candidate-contested-evidence` because while QAHE is confirmed in thin samples, the bulk axion-insulator state with cleanly realized θ = π without surface complications remains a target.
citations: Otrokov et al. 2019, Nature 576, 416 (prediction and synthesis)
Deng et al. 2020, Science 367, 895 (QAHE in MnBi₂Te₄ flakes)
Liu et al. 2020, Nature Mater. 19, 522 (axion insulator response)

Otrokov et al. 2019, Nature 576, 416 'Prediction and observation of an antiferromagnetic topological insulator' (source-value: MnBi2Te4 bulk antiferromagnetic Néel temperature T_N ≈ 25 K from neutron diffraction and magnetic-susceptibility λ-anomaly; A-type AFM order with combined T × τ (time-reversal × half-translation) symmetry protects the axion-insulator phase with θ = π; T13 cell-direct, T24 fourth-use: k_B T_N = 2.154 meV via k_B × 25 K; T10 uncertainty=null for characteristic literature value, literature spread 24-26 K sample-dependent)

Estyunin et al. 2020, APL Mater. 8, 021105 'Signatures of temperature driven antiferromagnetic transition in the electronic structure of topological insulator MnBi2Te4' (source-value: ARPES exchange-splitting onset temperature T_0 matches the magnetically-measured T_N ≈ 25 K)

cells.content: Bismuth (rhombohedral Bi) — first confirmed higher-order topological insulator
realized_examples: Rhombohedral bismuth (Bi) — STM imaging of 1D conducting hinge states between (111) and (110) surface facets (Schindler et al. 2018 Nature Phys.). Bulk classification: a HOTI with 1D hinge modes at intersections of surface facets where the second-order topological invariant changes.
cells.description: First experimentally confirmed higher-order topological insulator. Class higher-order-AII; protected by combined time-reversal × C₃ rotation symmetry. The hinge states are 1D, gapless, and helical — they live on codimension-2 boundaries of the 3D crystal. Demonstrates the existence of HOTI phases predicted by Benalcazar, Bernevig, Hughes 2017 and refined by subsequent classifications of crystalline HOTI invariants.
citations: Benalcazar, Bernevig, Hughes 2017, Science 357, 61 (HOTI quadrupole/octupole proposal)
Schindler, Wang, Vergniory, Cook, Murani, Sengupta, Kasumov, Deblock, Jeon, Drozdov, Bouchiat, Guéron, Yazdani, Bernevig, Neupert 2018, Nature Phys. 14, 918 (Bi hinge states)

cells.content: Fractional topological insulator (3D analog of FQHE) — predicted, no confirmed material
realized_examples: No confirmed material. Theoretical proposals from Maciejko, Qi, Karch, Zhang (2010) and subsequent works construct 3D class AII insulators with fractional axion angle θ = π/k for integer k > 1, protected by time-reversal and a global U(1) charge symmetry.
cells.description: Predicted strongly-correlated 3D TI with fractionalized axion response — analog of FQHE for 3D rather than 2D. Construction relies on interacting parton or vortex condensation pictures. Major Mendeleev empty cell: theoretically clean prediction with no experimental realization as of 2026. Distinguishes this FC from the FQH FC (which is 2D) and connects fractional topology of charge with TI symmetry classification.
citations: Maciejko, Qi, Karch, Zhang 2010, Phys. Rev. Lett. 105, 246809 (fractional TI proposal)
Swingle, Barkeshli, McGreevy, Senthil 2011, Phys. Rev. B 83, 195139 (parton construction)

cells.content: Intrinsic bulk axion insulator with cleanly realized θ = π (no surface complication)
realized_examples: No material. Theoretical proposals require a 3D antiferromagnetic insulator whose surface gap can be opened cleanly and whose axion response can be measured via topological magnetoelectric effect (image-monopole signature near surface). MnBi₂Te₄ partly realizes this but with experimental complications.
cells.description: Mendeleev cell for the predicted-but-not-cleanly-realized intrinsic axion insulator. The topological magnetoelectric effect (predicted Qi-Hughes-Zhang 2008) gives a quantized magnetoelectric response α = θ e²/(2π h c) with θ = π, manifested as an image-monopole signature near a surface in a magnetic field. While MnBi₂Te₄ family approaches this, a clean experimental realization remains elusive.
citations: Qi, Hughes, Zhang 2008, Phys. Rev. B 78, 195424 (axion electrodynamics in TIs)
Wang, Lian, Zhang 2016, Phys. Rev. B 93, 161115 (intrinsic axion insulator proposal)

Qi, Hughes, Zhang 2008, Phys. Rev. B 78, 195424 'Topological field theory of time-reversal invariant insulators' (source-value: axion angle θ = π (exact) is the quantized magnetoelectric coupling defining the topological-insulator electromagnetic response; θ is determined modulo 2π and quantized to π by time-reversal symmetry; value=3.141593 encodes π to floating-point precision per JSON-number constraint, source-value clarifies exact π; T13 cell-direct, T10 uncertainty=null for theoretical structural commitment, unobserved cleanly as of 2026)

Wang, Lian, Zhang 2016, Phys. Rev. B 93, 161115 'Intrinsic axion insulator in antiferromagnetic topological materials' (source-value: theoretical proposal for an intrinsic 3D antiferromagnetic insulator with cleanly realized θ = π protected by T × τ combined symmetry; the topological magnetoelectric effect would produce an image-monopole-like response near a surface in a magnetic field; MnBi2Te4 partly approaches this with experimental complications)

cells.content: Class CI 3D topological superconductor in a real material — no clear candidate
realized_examples: No confirmed material. Class CI requires both T² = +1 (time-reversal) and C² = +1 (particle-hole) — a combination rare in superconductors with strong spin-orbit coupling but predicted to support nontrivial 3D topology with ℤ invariant.
cells.description: Mendeleev cell for an empty corner of the tenfold-way table. Class CI 3D TSC predicted by Schnyder-Ryu-Furusaki-Ludwig 2008 to have ℤ classification but no confirmed material realization exists. Some d-wave superconductors with specific symmetry properties have been proposed but none confirmed.
citations: Schnyder, Ryu, Furusaki, Ludwig 2008, Phys. Rev. B 78, 195125 (tenfold-way classification table)
Kitaev 2009, AIP Conf. Proc. 1134, 22 (K-theory derivation)

cells.content: Higher-order topological superconductor with helical Majorana hinge modes — predicted
realized_examples: No confirmed material. Theoretical proposals (Khalaf 2018; Geier et al. 2018) construct higher-order DIII TSCs with helical Majorana hinge modes at codimension-2 boundaries. Candidate platforms (iron-based superconductors with crystalline symmetry) have been suggested but not confirmed.
cells.description: Mendeleev cell for HOTSCs. The bulk would be a 3D superconductor with no 2D surface gapless modes but with 1D helical Majorana hinge modes. The Majorana hinge mode is a non-Abelian-anyon-carrying defect at codimension-2 and would enable topologically-protected braiding operations. Major research target for topological quantum computing platforms.
citations: Khalaf 2018, Phys. Rev. B 97, 205136 (HOTSC classification)
Geier, Trifunovic, Hoskam, Brouwer 2018, Phys. Rev. B 97, 205135 (HOTSC bulk-boundary correspondence)

predictive_yield.prediction: 2D class AII TI helical edge states carry quantized longitudinal conductance σ_xx = 2e²/h
predictive_yield.description: Bernevig-Hughes-Zhang 2006 predicted the QSH phase in HgTe quantum wells above critical thickness. König et al. 2007 measured σ_xx = 2e²/h plateau, confirming quantization. Subsequent confirmations in 1T'-WTe₂, bismuthene.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Bernevig, Hughes, Zhang 2006, Science 314, 1757
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: König et al. 2007, Science 318, 766; Fei et al. 2017, Nature Phys. 13, 677 (1T'-WTe₂)

citations: Bernevig, Hughes, Zhang 2006, Science 314, 1757 'Quantum Spin Hall Effect and Topological Phase Transition in HgTe Quantum Wells' (source-value: predicted quantum-spin-Hall edge conductance σ_xx = 2e²/h plateau for HgTe/CdTe quantum wells above the critical thickness d_c = 6.3 nm, derived from helical-edge-state picture with two counterpropagating spin-polarized channels each contributing e²/h; T25 first-use: dimensionful-quantum-conductance encoded as kind=coupling + units='e²/h' with value=2 — literature-native quantum-conductance unit, generalizes T17 squared-dimensional encoding and T23 area-as-length² precedent to derived quantum units that aren't simple base-unit powers; T10 uncertainty=null for quantized prediction)

Koenig et al. 2007, Science 318, 766 arXiv:0710.0582 'Quantum Spin Hall Insulator State in HgTe Quantum Wells' (source-value: observed σ_xx residual conductance plateau close to 2e²/h in HgTe samples with d > 6.3 nm, independent of sample width as expected for edge-state transport, destroyed by small B-field consistent with helical-edge ℤ₂ topology; quantization holds to within mesoscopic-coherence-limited residual at the percent level)

Fei et al. 2017, Nature Phys. 13, 677 'Edge conduction in monolayer WTe2' (source-value: independent confirmation of σ_xx = 2e²/h QSH plateau in a second 2D-TI platform 1T'-WTe2, persisting up to ~100 K)

predictive_yield.prediction: 3D class AII TIs host a single Dirac cone on the (111) (or analogous) surface
predictive_yield.description: Zhang et al. 2009 predicted Bi₂Se₃ as a single-Dirac-cone 3D TI. Xia et al. 2009 and Hsieh et al. 2009 confirmed via ARPES, with Hsieh et al. additionally measuring helical spin texture.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Zhang, Liu, Qi, Dai, Fang, Zhang 2009, Nature Phys. 5, 438
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Xia et al. 2009, Nature Phys. 5, 398; Hsieh et al. 2009, Nature 460, 1101

predictive_yield.prediction: Higher-order topological insulator (Benalcazar-Bernevig-Hughes 2017) has 1D hinge modes at codimension-2 boundaries
predictive_yield.description: Benalcazar, Bernevig, Hughes 2017 introduced HOTIs with quadrupole topology. Schindler et al. 2018 confirmed in rhombohedral bismuth by STM observation of helical 1D hinge modes between surface facets, consistent with the bulk HOTI invariant.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Benalcazar, Bernevig, Hughes 2017, Science 357, 61
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Schindler et al. 2018, Nature Phys. 14, 918

predictive_yield.prediction: Weyl semimetals have bulk Weyl points connected by surface Fermi arcs
predictive_yield.description: Wan et al. 2011 first developed the Weyl-semimetal phenomenology theoretically. Huang et al. 2015 and Weng et al. 2015 predicted TaAs as a candidate. Lv et al. 2015 confirmed bulk Weyl points via soft-X-ray ARPES; Xu et al. 2015 observed surface Fermi arcs.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Wan, Turner, Vishwanath, Savrasov 2011, Phys. Rev. B 83, 205101; Huang et al. 2015, Nature Commun. 6, 7373
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Lv et al. 2015, Phys. Rev. X 5, 031013; Xu et al. 2015, Science 349, 613

predictive_yield.prediction: Topological crystalline insulator SnTe has mirror-symmetry-protected surface Dirac cones at L points
predictive_yield.description: Fu 2011 proposed TCIs; Hsieh, Lin, Liu, Duan, Bansil, Fu 2012 predicted SnTe as a candidate. Tanaka et al. 2012 confirmed via ARPES; Okada et al. 2013 STM revealed strain-induced gap opening consistent with mirror-symmetry breaking.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Fu 2011, Phys. Rev. Lett. 106, 106802; Hsieh et al. 2012, Nature Commun. 3, 982
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Tanaka et al. 2012, Nature Phys. 8, 800; Okada et al. 2013, Science 341, 1496

predictive_yield.prediction: Majorana zero modes in InSb/Al hybrid nanowires give quantized 2e²/h zero-bias conductance
predictive_yield.description: Zhang et al. 2018 Nature 556, 74 reported quantized 2e²/h conductance peaks attributed to MZMs. Subsequent re-analysis of the data showed the apparent quantization was disorder-induced rather than topological; the paper was retracted in 2021 (Nature 591, 354). This is the most prominent retracted topological-matter claim and is recorded here as a falsified specific prediction. Note that MZM-platform searches in InAs/Al, InSb/Pb, and related systems continue with more careful protocols — the falsification applies to this specific 2018 quantization claim, not to MZM platforms as a whole.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Zhang et al. 2018, Nature 556, 74
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Zhang et al. 2021, Nature 591, 354 (retraction); Frolov 2021, Nature 592, 17 (commentary)

citations: Zhang et al. 2018, Nature 556, 74 'Quantized Majorana conductance' (source-value: ORIGINAL CLAIM — quantized 2e²/h zero-bias conductance peaks in an InSb/Al hybrid nanowire attributed to Majorana zero modes of a 1D class D topological superconductor per the Lutchyn-Sau-Das Sarma 2010 theoretical framework; T25 second-use; T10 uncertainty=null for falsified prediction matching CDMS-Si and DAMA-LIBRA falsified entries in sub-PR 8 dark-matter-candidates)

Zhang et al. 2021, Nature 591, 354 'Retraction Note: Quantized Majorana conductance' (source-value: RETRACTION — re-analysis of the original data revealed that signatures consistent with MZMs were instead disorder-induced Andreev bound states; the published 2e²/h quantization claim is not supported by the underlying data; most prominent retracted topological claim in condensed-matter physics)

Frolov 2021, Nature 592, 17 'Quantum computing’s reproducibility crisis: Majorana fermions' (source-value: commentary on the disorder concerns and broader reproducibility issues in MZM-search experiments using semiconductor-superconductor hybrid nanowires)

predictive_yield.prediction: Sr₂RuO₄ has chiral p_x + ip_y triplet pairing, making it an intrinsic 2D topological superconductor with half-quantum vortices
