nodes.label: Real critical systems
nodes.full_name: Classification of real experimental systems undergoing second-order phase transitions — universality classes, critical exponents, and bootstrap-tested critical theories
nodes.description: Real critical systems are physical systems exhibiting second-order phase transitions whose long-wavelength, near-critical behavior is described by a specific universality class — a class of critical theories sharing identical critical exponents independent of microscopic details. The classification organizes confirmed material/system realizations of each universality class, the cleanest precision measurements of critical exponents in those systems, the universality classes for which no clean material realization exists (theoretical-limit-only), and the current open tensions between bootstrap-predicted exponents and high-precision measurements. The universality-class axis encompasses the canonical Wilson-Fisher / Kadanoff/RG classes (3D Ising, 3D O(2) / XY, 3D O(3) / Heisenberg, 3D O(N≥4)), the Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless transition in 2D, percolation, self-avoiding walks in 3D (polymers), tricritical points, Lifshitz points, and surface-ordinary critical phenomena. Cells correspond to specific physical systems where critical exponents have been measured (CO₂ liquid-gas critical point, 4He λ-transition, Ni Curie point, ...) plus Mendeleev cells for universality classes without clean material realization (3D O(N≥4) absent at-physical-quark-masses, surface universality classes with limited realizations). The classification additionally documents the current open puzzle in critical phenomena: the ~5σ tension between bootstrap-precision (Chester et al. 2020) and 4He microgravity satellite measurement (Lipa et al. 1996, 2003) on the 3D O(2) correlation-length exponent ν — the '4He λ puzzle.'
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Real experimental systems exhibiting continuous (second-order) phase transitions, where the long-wavelength behavior is universal and the universality class is determined by symmetry and spatial dimension. Includes: liquid-gas critical points in fluids (water, CO₂, xenon, methane, ...), magnetic phase transitions (ferromagnetic / antiferromagnetic Curie / Néel points in Ni, EuO, RbMnF₃, FeF₂, ...), superfluid transitions (4He λ-transition, 3He A-B transitions), binary-fluid demixing (3-methylpentane + nitroethane, ...), polymer-in-solution scaling (SAW exponents), 2D thin-film superfluid transitions (BKT in 4He films), and percolation in materials systems. Excludes: first-order transitions (no critical exponents), quantum critical points (separate phenomenon-FC if authored — they involve T = 0 transitions with different scaling and may pair with the bootstrap differently), and finite-temperature confinement / chiral transitions in QCD (touched on indirectly via the theoretical 3D O(4) cell but the full QCD-phase-diagram structure is a separate hadronic-sector phenomenon-FC). The classification covers thermal critical points where the order parameter is a slow degree of freedom; systems with quenched disorder (random-field / spin-glass) require modified universality classes and are not included.
nodes.organizing_principle: Each cell corresponds to a specific physical system at its critical point, identified by (i) the universality class — the equivalence class of critical theories sharing identical exponents, which for second-order transitions corresponds to a specific 3D conformal field theory (the Wilson-Fisher fixed point of an O(N) model, the BKT free-boson critical line, the percolation universality, etc.); (ii) the system class — what kind of physical system realizes it (liquid-gas fluid, ferromagnet, superfluid, ...); (iii) the observational status — how precisely have the critical exponents been measured and whether they match the bootstrap prediction. The classification is constrained from above by the bootstrap exclusion regions in cft-bootstrap-exclusion-regions, which pin the allowed critical-exponent values to high precision (3D Ising ν = 0.6299709... at 10⁻⁷ precision; 3D O(2) ν = 0.6719... at 10⁻⁴ precision via 2020 bootstrap; etc.), and from below by experimental measurements that probe these exponents directly. The 'universality' principle — that diverse microscopic systems can flow under RG to the same critical fixed point and therefore share identical critical exponents — is the structural fact that this FC organizes empirically: 3D Ising universality appears in liquid-gas critical points AND in uniaxial Heisenberg antiferromagnets despite the radically different microscopic Hamiltonians.

classification_axes.name: universality-class
classification_axes.description: The Wilson-Fisher / Kadanoff RG universality class characterizing the critical theory. 3D-Ising: scalar real order parameter, Z_2 symmetry, 3D (the most-measured class; bootstrap-precision ν = 0.6299709...). 3D-O2-XY: complex / 2-component real order parameter, O(2) symmetry, 3D (4He λ-transition lives here). 3D-O3-Heisenberg: 3-component vector order parameter, O(3), 3D (isotropic ferromagnets / antiferromagnets). 3D-O4-or-higher: only theoretically realized — relevant to chiral phase transition in massless N_f = 2 QCD (theoretical limit). 2D-Ising: Onsager-solved exactly, ν = 1, η = 1/4, exact via Onsager. 2D-XY-BKT: topological transition with logarithmic exponents and universal superfluid-density jump ρ_s(T_BKT⁻)/k_B T_BKT = 2/π. 3D-percolation: cluster-mass scaling. 3D-SAW-polymer: self-avoiding-walk scaling for polymers in good solvent (ν ≈ 0.588). tricritical: meeting point of first- and second-order transitions (3He-4He mixtures). Lifshitz: a triple-point of phases where the second-order transition meets a modulated phase. surface-ordinary / surface-extraordinary: critical-exponent variations at surfaces and interfaces of bulk-critical systems.

classification_axes.name: system-class
classification_axes.description: The kind of physical system realizing the universality class. liquid-gas-fluid: classical fluids at their critical point (water, CO₂, xenon, ...). ferromagnet / antiferromagnet: magnetic transitions at Curie / Néel temperatures in materials (Ni, EuO, RbMnF₃, FeF₂, MnF₂). superfluid: 4He at the λ-point, superfluid 3He A-B transition. binary-fluid-mixture: critical demixing of two miscible fluids near consolute temperature. polymer-solution: SAW exponents in dilute polymers (light-scattering / SANS measurements). thin-film: 2D BKT realized in thin 4He films or superconducting thin films. mixture-tricritical-system: 3He-4He mixtures at the tricritical point. lattice-model-theoretical-only: a class realized only in lattice models / abstract field theories, not in any clean experimental system (e.g., 3D O(N≥4) at physical quark masses).

classification_axes.name: observational-status
classification_axes.description: Match between bootstrap-predicted critical exponents and experimental measurement. precision-confirmed: high-precision agreement between bootstrap exponents and measurement (3D Ising in liquid-gas critical points, 3D Heisenberg in isotropic ferromagnets at the few-percent level). confirmed-with-bootstrap-tension: measurement is precision-confirmed at the universality-class level but there is a current quantitative tension with the bootstrap exponent at high precision (the 3D O(2) / 4He λ-puzzle). qualitatively-confirmed: universality class identified but exponents not measured to bootstrap-precision (3D percolation, 3D SAW polymers, surface critical phenomena). predicted-only: universality class predicted to exist but no clean material realization (3D O(N≥4) at physical conditions, some surface classes). theoretical-limit-only: universality class realized only in an unphysical limit (3D O(4) realized only in massless 2-flavor QCD limit, not at physical quark masses).

cells.content: Liquid-gas critical points in pure fluids — H₂O (T_c = 647 K), CO₂ (T_c = 304 K), Xe (T_c = 290 K)
realized_examples: High-pressure light-scattering and density measurements near the critical point of CO₂, Xe, H₂O, methane, ethane. Critical exponents (ν, η, γ) measured to few-percent precision and consistent with 3D-Ising bootstrap predictions: ν = 0.630, η = 0.036, γ = 1.24.
cells.description: Canonical realization of 3D-Ising universality in classical fluids. The fluid liquid-gas transition has a scalar order parameter (the density difference from critical density) with Z_2 (particle-hole) symmetry at the critical point. Multiple fluids share identical exponents within experimental uncertainty — the textbook demonstration of universality.
citations: Sengers, Levelt Sengers 1986, Annu. Rev. Phys. Chem. 37, 189 (critical-fluid review)
Wilcox, Estler 1971, Phys. Rev. Lett. 26, 813 (early Xe measurements)
Anisimov 2004, J. Phys. A 37, 2697 (review of fluid critical phenomena)

Span, Wagner 1996, J. Phys. Chem. Ref. Data 25, 1509 'A new equation of state for carbon dioxide covering the fluid region from the triple-point temperature to 1100 K at pressures up to 800 MPa' (IUPAC reference equation for CO₂ thermophysical properties; critical point at T_c = 304.1282 K, P_c = 7.3773 MPa, ρ_c = 467.6 kg/m³; the textbook canonical 3D-Ising liquid-gas universality reference; source-value: CO₂ T_c = 304.13 K; T24 reuse: k_B × 304.13 K = 0.02621 eV via k_B = 8.617333262 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K; T13 cell-direct, T10 uncertainty=null for the characteristic-temperature anchor whose load-bearing role is universality-class membership rather than precision-error reporting; T24 sixth-use post sub-PR 9's first-use)

cells.content: Uniaxial antiferromagnets FeF₂ (T_N = 78 K), MnF₂ (T_N = 67 K)
realized_examples: Neutron scattering measurements of order-parameter exponent β at the Néel transition. FeF₂ and MnF₂ are textbook uniaxial antiferromagnets — their order parameter is a 1-component spin staggered magnetization, giving Z_2 symmetry → 3D-Ising universality.
cells.description: Magnetic realization of 3D-Ising universality. The textbook demonstration that 3D-Ising exponents are independent of whether the system is a fluid or a magnet — universality crossing system-class boundaries.
citations: Hutchings, Schulhof, Guggenheim 1972, Phys. Rev. B 5, 154 (FeF₂ neutron scattering)
Belanger, Yoshizawa 1987, Phys. Rev. B 35, 4823 (MnF₂)

Belanger, Yoshizawa 1987, Phys. Rev. B 35, 4823 'Neutron scattering and the critical behavior of the three-dimensional Ising antiferromagnet FeF₂' (neutron-scattering measurement of the canonical 3D-Ising uniaxial antiferromagnet FeF₂ giving T_N = 78.4 K and critical exponents ν = 0.64(1), γ = 1.25(2) consistent with 3D-Ising universality; source-value: FeF₂ T_N = 78.4 K; T24 reuse: k_B × 78.4 K = 0.006760 eV; T13 cell-direct, T10 uncertainty=null for the characteristic-temperature anchor; T24 seventh-use)

Chirwa, Lundgren, Nordblad 1988 (J. Magn. Magn. Mater. — weak spontaneous magnetisation measurement of pure FeF₂ giving the critical exponent β = 0.325(2) consistent with 3D-Ising universality; representative β anchor on this cell, although the load-bearing cell-level qs here is T_N as the characteristic-temperature anchor)

cells.content: Binary-fluid critical demixing — 3-methylpentane + nitroethane and similar mixtures
realized_examples: Light-scattering and refractive-index measurements at the upper consolute critical point of binary miscible-fluid systems near the demixing transition. Order parameter is the concentration difference; symmetry is Z_2 (concentration-swap) → 3D Ising.
cells.description: Third independent realization of 3D-Ising universality (after liquid-gas and uniaxial magnets). Confirms universality across yet another microscopic context.
citations: Goldburg, Aronson 1986, Physica A 134, 1 (binary-fluid critical phenomena review)
Beysens, Bourgou, Calmettes 1982, Phys. Rev. A 26, 3589 (3MP+NE)

cells.content: 4He superfluid λ-transition at T_λ = 2.17 K — THE 4He λ-puzzle cell
realized_examples: Lipa et al. 1996, 2003 Phys. Rev. B microgravity experiments aboard space shuttle (and ground-based heat-capacity measurements at sub-μK precision near T_λ): measured ν = 0.6709 ± 0.0001 and α = -0.0127 ± 0.0003. Chester et al. 2020 Phys. Rev. D bootstrap for 3D O(2): ν = 0.6719(7), α = -0.0157(20). The two values differ by ~5σ — the current 'λ-puzzle' in critical phenomena.
cells.description: The most precisely-measured critical exponents in physics, and currently the most prominent quantitative theory-experiment tension. The 4He λ-transition has a complex order parameter (the superfluid wave function) with O(2) symmetry. The Lipa et al. specific-heat measurements achieve sub-microkelvin temperature precision (heat-capacity peak resolved over ~10⁻⁶ K). The 2020 bootstrap result tightened the predicted ν below the measured value at ~5σ. Possible resolutions: bootstrap-systematic underestimate (unlikely given internal cross-checks); residual gravitational rounding in the satellite experiment (Lipa et al. argue against); additional corrections-to-scaling that have been underestimated by either side. This is the cleanest open puzzle in modern critical phenomena.
citations: Lipa, Nissen, Stricker, Swanson, Chui 2003, Phys. Rev. B 68, 174518 (microgravity satellite)
Chester, Landry, Liu, Poland, Simmons-Duffin, Su, Vichi 2020, Phys. Rev. D 101, 084017 (3D O(2) bootstrap)
Kos, Poland, Simmons-Duffin, Vichi 2016, JHEP 08, 036 (early bootstrap for O(N))

Donnelly, Barenghi 1998, J. Phys. Chem. Ref. Data 27, 1217 'The Observed Properties of Liquid Helium at the Saturated Vapor Pressure' (IUPAC-style reference compilation of 4He thermophysical properties; lambda transition at saturated vapor pressure occurs at T_λ = 2.1768 K, which is an ITS-90 thermometric fixed point per Hwang-Khorana 1976 first-reproducibility 0.01 mK measurement; source-value: T_λ = 2.1768 K at SVP, the textbook canonical value; T24 FIFTH-USE: k_B × 2.1768 K = 1.876 × 10⁻⁴ eV via k_B = 8.617333262 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K, downstream-extending the characteristic-temperature cluster from sub-PR 9 into the foundational few-K critical-phenomena regime; T13 cell-direct, T10 uncertainty=null for the characteristic-temperature anchor)

Lipa, Nissen, Stricker, Swanson, Chui 2003, Phys. Rev. B 68, 174518 'Specific heat of liquid helium in zero gravity very near the lambda point' (Space-Shuttle Microgravity Mission satellite experiment performing the most-precise specific-heat measurement near T_λ; the same dataset's α = -0.0127(3) and derived ν = 0.6709(1) sit in 8σ tension and 5σ tension with bootstrap consensus respectively — those tensions are encoded at cft-bootstrap-exclusion-regions pred[1] from sub-PR 2 and real-critical-systems pred[1] this sub-PR; the cell-level qs here is T_λ as the characteristic-temperature anchor distinguishing this cell from other 3D O(2) realizations)

cells.content: Planar (easy-plane) magnetic Curie / Néel transitions — gadolinium intermetallics, BaCo(PO₃)₂
realized_examples: Neutron scattering of planar (XY-symmetry) ferromagnets / antiferromagnets near their Curie / Néel temperature. Exponents consistent with 3D O(2) universality at the few-percent level — less precise than 4He λ but qualitatively confirming universality.
cells.description: Magnetic-system realization of 3D-O(2)/XY universality. Less precision than 4He λ but provides a system-class cross-check on the universality assignment.
citations: Collins 1989, Magnetic Critical Scattering (Oxford monograph)

cells.content: Isotropic 3D Heisenberg ferromagnets / antiferromagnets — Ni (T_C = 627 K), EuO (T_C = 69 K), RbMnF₃ (T_N = 83 K)
realized_examples: Neutron scattering, magnetization, and specific-heat measurements of isotropic 3D ferromagnets and antiferromagnets give exponents consistent with 3D O(3) bootstrap: ν ≈ 0.7117, η ≈ 0.038, γ ≈ 1.396.
cells.description: Realization of 3D-O(3)/Heisenberg universality. Isotropic (rotationally invariant) magnets with 3-component order parameter. Bootstrap-precision agreement for the isotropic class confirms that universality is not specific to scalar (Ising) cases.
citations: Hohenemser, Rosov, Kleinhammes 1989, Hyperfine Interact. 49, 267 (Ni critical exponents)
Salamon 1973, Phys. Rev. B 8, 3354 (RbMnF₃)
Chester, Landry, Liu, Poland, Simmons-Duffin, Su, Vichi 2021, Phys. Rev. D 104, 105013 (3D O(3) bootstrap)

Hohenemser, Rosov, Kleinhammes 1989, Hyperfine Interactions 49, 267 'Critical phenomena studied via nuclear techniques' (canonical 3D Heisenberg ferromagnet Ni with Curie temperature T_C = 627.4 K determined from hyperfine-field decay across the transition; representative 3D-Heisenberg universality realization with measured ν ≈ 0.71; source-value: Ni T_C = 627 K — cell content also names EuO T_C = 69.3 K and RbMnF₃ T_N = 83 K as additional Heisenberg representatives; T24 reuse: k_B × 627.4 K = 0.05407 eV; T13 cell-direct, T10 uncertainty=null for the characteristic-temperature anchor; T24 eighth-use)

cells.content: BKT transition in 2D thin 4He superfluid films at T_BKT ≈ 1-2 K (film-dependent)
realized_examples: Bishop, Reppy 1978 Phys. Rev. Lett. 40, 1727 — torsional-oscillator measurements of thin 4He films revealing the universal superfluid-density jump ρ_s(T_BKT⁻)/(k_B T_BKT) = 2 m²/(π ℏ²), confirming Kosterlitz-Thouless prediction.
cells.description: Definitive observation of the Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless topological phase transition. The 4He thin-film case is the cleanest realization — the universal jump in superfluid density is independent of microscopic details, exactly as predicted by Kosterlitz-Thouless 1973.
citations: Kosterlitz, Thouless 1973, J. Phys. C 6, 1181 (BKT prediction)
Bishop, Reppy 1978, Phys. Rev. Lett. 40, 1727 (definitive observation)
Bishop, Reppy 1980, Phys. Rev. B 22, 5171 (extended analysis)

cells.content: 3He-4He mixture tricritical point at T_t ≈ 0.87 K, x_t ≈ 0.67
realized_examples: Specific-heat and concentration measurements of 3He-4He liquid mixtures near the tricritical point where the λ-transition line meets the first-order phase-separation line. Exponents show mean-field-like behavior with logarithmic corrections, as predicted by Riedel-Wegner theory (tricritical d_c = 3).
cells.description: Canonical example of a tricritical point — where the second-order superfluid λ-transition line meets the first-order phase-separation line of 3He-4He mixtures. Tricritical exponents (mean-field at the upper critical dimension d_c = 3, with logarithmic corrections) are distinct from the bulk 3D O(2) class.
citations: Riedel, Wegner 1972, Phys. Rev. Lett. 29, 349 (tricritical theory)
Goellner, Behringer, Meyer 1973, J. Low Temp. Phys. 13, 113 (3He-4He measurements)

Goellner, Behringer, Meyer 1973, J. Low Temp. Phys. 13, 113 'Thermodynamic measurements on liquid 3He-4He mixtures near the tricritical point' (specific-heat measurements of dilute 3He-4He mixtures locating the tricritical point at T_t ≈ 0.867 K and concentration x_t ≈ 0.67; representative tricritical universality-class realization confirming the Riedel-Wegner 1972 PRL 29, 349 prediction that d=3 is the upper critical dimension for tricritical phase transitions, giving mean-field exponents with logarithmic corrections; source-value: 3He-4He tricritical T_t = 0.867 K, x_t = 0.67; T24 reuse: k_B × 0.867 K = 7.47 × 10⁻⁵ eV; T13 cell-direct, T10 uncertainty=null for the characteristic-temperature anchor; T24 ninth-use; brings the characteristic-temperature cluster into the millikelvin few-K regime adjacent to the 4He λ-transition anchor at 2.18 K)

cells.content: Self-avoiding random walks for polymers in dilute good-solvent solution — R_g ~ N^ν with ν ≈ 0.588
realized_examples: Light-scattering measurements of radius of gyration vs degree of polymerization for polystyrene, PMMA, and other polymers in good solvent. ν ≈ 0.588, matching the bootstrap-equivalent 3D-SAW prediction (which corresponds to the 3D O(N→0) limit of the Wilson-Fisher series).
cells.description: Polymer scaling in 3D maps onto the n → 0 limit of the O(N) Wilson-Fisher series (de Gennes). The measured radius of gyration scaling exponent ν ≈ 0.588 matches the predicted value. The connection between polymer statistics and critical phenomena was one of the early demonstrations of universality's reach.
citations: de Gennes 1979, Scaling Concepts in Polymer Physics (Cornell)
Schäfer, Kappeler 1985, J. Phys. Paris 46, 1853 (polystyrene)
Belohorec, Nickel 1997, Guelph (SAW Monte Carlo to high precision)

cells.content: 3D percolation universality class — cluster-mass scaling exponent τ ≈ 2.189
realized_examples: Site / bond percolation on 3D lattices (cubic, BCC, FCC) — Monte Carlo simulations match analytic predictions for cluster-size exponents. Experimental realizations in sol-gel transitions, microemulsion conductivity, and some glass-formers show qualitative agreement with percolation exponents.
cells.description: Geometric / connectivity-universality class — percolation is a 'critical phenomenon' in the sense that cluster statistics show power-law divergence at the percolation threshold but the underlying transition is geometric, not energetic. Distinct universality class from Wilson-Fisher (no underlying field-theoretic order parameter in the Landau sense, though related to the s → 0 limit of Potts).
citations: Stauffer, Aharony 1994, Introduction to Percolation Theory (2nd ed.)
Lorenz, Ziff 1998, Phys. Rev. E 57, 230 (3D percolation Monte Carlo)

Lorenz, Ziff 1998, Phys. Rev. E 57, 230 'Precise determination of the bond percolation thresholds and finite-size scaling corrections for the sc, fcc, and bcc lattices' (Monte-Carlo determination of the Fisher cluster-mass exponent τ = 2.18906(8) for 3D bond percolation across simple-cubic, face-centered-cubic, and body-centered-cubic lattices via finite-size scaling; the dimensionless universal exponent of the 3D-percolation universality class governing the n(s) ∝ s^(-τ) cluster-size distribution at criticality; source-value: 3D-percolation τ = 2.189(2); T13 cell-direct convention)

Newman, Ziff 2000, Phys. Rev. E 64, 016706 'Fast Monte Carlo algorithm for site or bond percolation' arXiv:cond-mat/0005264 (improved Newman-Ziff algorithm independent-confirmation of τ ≈ 2.189 for 3D percolation across multiple lattice classes; canonical method reference for high-precision percolation exponents)

cells.content: Lifshitz points in modulated magnetic systems — MnP, NaV₂O₅ candidate observations
realized_examples: MnP phase diagram exhibits a candidate Lifshitz point where ferromagnetic, helical, and paramagnetic phases meet. Neutron-scattering measurements partly confirm Lifshitz-class exponents but the realization is less clean than Wilson-Fisher classes.
cells.description: Lifshitz point — a multicritical point where a second-order transition line meets a transition to a modulated phase. The universality class is distinct from Wilson-Fisher (anisotropic scaling) and has fewer clean experimental realizations.
citations: Hornreich, Luban, Shtrikman 1975, Phys. Rev. Lett. 35, 1678 (Lifshitz theory)
Becerra, Shapira, Oliveira Jr, Chang 1980, Phys. Rev. Lett. 44, 1692 (MnP candidate)

cells.content: Chiral phase transition in massless 2-flavor QCD — 3D O(4) universality (theoretical only)
realized_examples: Pisarski-Wilczek 1984 prediction: in the massless 2-flavor limit, the chiral SU(2)_L × SU(2)_R / SU(2)_V transition is in the 3D O(4) universality class (4-component order parameter = chiral condensate). Lattice QCD simulations at very small but nonzero quark mass show evidence consistent with O(4) scaling but interpretation requires extrapolation.
cells.description: Universality class realized only in an unphysical limit. Physical QCD has nonzero u, d quark masses which break the exact chiral symmetry and shift the transition from genuinely-critical to crossover. The cleanest 'theoretical-limit-only' cell in this FC — the universality assignment is theoretical but matters because lattice studies of QCD-near-the-chiral-limit test the O(4) prediction at quark masses approaching zero.
citations: Pisarski, Wilczek 1984, Phys. Rev. D 29, 338 (O(4) prediction)
Ejiri et al. 2009, Phys. Rev. D 80, 094505 (lattice QCD O(4) scaling tests)

cells.content: 2D Ising model — exactly solved (Onsager 1944), ν = 1, η = 1/4
realized_examples: Onsager 1944 exact solution gives exact critical exponents. Experimental realizations in thin magnetic films (limited cleanliness) and in lattice-model simulations match perfectly. The 2D Ising CFT has central charge c = 1/2 and is the chiral Ising MTC.
cells.description: Exactly-solved class. Distinct from 3D-Ising — the dimensionality matters. Connects to the Ising MTC (the boundary CFT of 2D class D topological superconductors realizes Ising-MTC anyon content; see topological-insulator-and-superconductor-materials cells).
citations: Onsager 1944, Phys. Rev. 65, 117 (exact solution)
Belavin, Polyakov, Zamolodchikov 1984, Nucl. Phys. B 241, 333 (2D CFT framework)

Onsager 1944, Phys. Rev. 65, 117 'Crystal statistics. I. A two-dimensional model with an order-disorder transition' (exact closed-form solution of the 2D Ising model on the square lattice with nearest-neighbor coupling, including the critical temperature k_B T_c / J = 2/ln(1+√2) ≈ 2.269 and the correlation-length exponent ν = 1; landmark exactly-solvable statistical-mechanics result; source-value: 2D Ising η = 1/4 exact via the closed-form derivation; T26 reuse: 1/4 = 0.25 exact rational encoded as decimal value with the 1/4 rational form preserved in source-value parenthetical; T10 uncertainty=null because the value is mathematically exact, not measured)

Yang 1952, Phys. Rev. 85, 808 'The Spontaneous Magnetization of a Two-Dimensional Ising Model' (closed-form derivation of the spontaneous-magnetisation critical exponent β = 1/8 exact and the order-parameter scaling for the 2D Ising universality class; together with Onsager 1944 establishes the full set of exact rational exponents ν = 1, η = 1/4, β = 1/8, γ = 7/4, α = 0 with log, δ = 15 — the canonical exact-rational exponent set in critical phenomena, queryable as a T26-reuse instance across the 2D universality-class cell)

cells.content: 3D O(N≥5) universality classes — no clean material realization
realized_examples: Theoretical bootstrap-precision exponents exist for 3D O(N) for any N (Chester et al. 2021, 2022). No clean material realization of N ≥ 5. Some candidates (deconfined-criticality multi-component order parameters) have been proposed but the universality identification is debated.
cells.description: Mendeleev empty cell. Bootstrap predicts exponents for 3D O(N) at any N (and the large-N limit is analytically tractable), but no real-system has been identified that cleanly realizes O(5), O(6), or higher. Some proposed deconfined-quantum-criticality scenarios may map to high-N Wilson-Fisher fixed points but the assignments remain contested.
citations: Chester, Landry, Liu, Poland, Simmons-Duffin, Su, Vichi 2021, Phys. Rev. D 104, 105013 (O(N) bootstrap survey)
Senthil, Vishwanath, Balents, Sachdev, Fisher 2004, Science 303, 1490 (deconfined quantum criticality)

cells.content: Ordinary surface critical exponents in bulk-critical magnets — neutron-reflectometry on Ni, EuO surfaces
realized_examples: Neutron reflectometry of magnetic films at the bulk Curie point reveals modified critical exponents at the surface — the 'ordinary' surface universality class (Diehl 1986).
cells.description: Surface critical phenomena class. At a surface of a bulk-critical system, the order parameter near the surface has its own critical exponents (e.g., β_1 for surface magnetization) that differ from bulk values. Three sub-classes: ordinary (surface less ordered than bulk), special, extraordinary (surface more ordered). Measurements are less precise than bulk-critical and the assignment requires careful surface preparation.
citations: Diehl 1986, Field-Theoretical Approach to Critical Behavior at Surfaces (Phase Transitions and Critical Phenomena vol. 10)

cells.content: Surface-extraordinary universality class — predicted, no clean material realization
realized_examples: Diehl 1986 predicted the 'extraordinary' surface universality where surface ordering exceeds bulk ordering at the bulk-critical point. No clean experimental realization confirmed; some recent quantum-Hall-edge realizations have been proposed.
cells.description: Mendeleev empty cell at the surface-criticality boundary. The class is well-defined theoretically and bootstrap-style analyses give critical exponents (recent work: Padayasi et al. 2022 surface bootstrap), but no clean material realization is confirmed.
citations: Diehl 1986 (theory)
Padayasi, Krishnan, Metlitski, Gruzberg, Meineri 2022, SciPost Phys. 12, 190 (surface bootstrap)

cells.content: Rare-earth Heisenberg ferromagnet EuS (T_C = 16.5 K), other Eu chalcogenides
realized_examples: Neutron-scattering and specific-heat measurements of EuS at its Curie point give ν and γ consistent with 3D-Heisenberg universality at the few-percent level. EuS is canonical because it has nearly-isotropic Heisenberg exchange and minimal lattice anisotropy.
cells.description: Second-system realization of 3D-Heisenberg universality (parallel to cell-crit-O3-Heisenberg-Ni-EuO-RbMnF3). Demonstrates universality between different Heisenberg ferromagnets.
citations: Als-Nielsen, Holmes, Larsen, Guggenheim 1975, Phys. Rev. B 12, 191 (EuS neutron scattering)

Pernet, Quézel, Convert 1974, AIP Conference Proceedings 18, 1110 (canonical EuS Curie temperature T_C = 16.6 K from neutron diffraction across the ferromagnetic ordering transition; rare-earth Heisenberg-ferromagnet realization complementing the high-T Ni anchor in cell[5] at the low-T end of the 3D-Heisenberg characteristic-temperature spread; representative Eu chalcogenide system also studied by Stierstadt 1975 and Tao-Lange-Schmitt-Rink); source-value: EuS T_C = 16.6 K; T24 reuse: k_B × 16.6 K = 0.001431 eV; T13 cell-direct, T10 uncertainty=null for the characteristic-temperature anchor; T24 tenth-use; second 3D-Heisenberg anchor in the cluster (cell[5] Ni T_C = 627 K at high-T; cell[16] EuS T_C = 16.6 K at low-T) — 3D-Heisenberg universality is now numerically anchored across a factor-of-40 temperature range, making intra-universality-class temperature variation queryable post-MCP-rebuild

cells.content: Isobutyric acid + water binary-fluid critical demixing
realized_examples: Light scattering and refractive-index measurements of isobutyric-acid + water mixtures near the upper consolute critical point. Exponents fit 3D-Ising precisely.
cells.description: Additional binary-fluid realization of 3D-Ising universality, complementing cell-crit-Ising-binary-fluid-3MP-NE. Multiple independent binary-fluid systems give consistent 3D-Ising exponents — strengthening the universality observation.
citations: Beysens, Bourgou 1979, Phys. Rev. A 19, 2407
Beysens, Bourgou, Calmettes 1982, Phys. Rev. A 26, 3589

Greer, Hocken 1972, J. Chem. Phys. 56, 5938 'Critical exponents and amplitudes for some binary liquid mixtures' (precision measurement of the binary-fluid critical demixing temperature for the isobutyric-acid + water system T_c = 26.12 ± 0.01 °C = 299.27 K; one of the cleanest 3D-Ising binary-fluid universality-class realizations with associated measured β ≈ 0.328 consistent with the universal value; source-value: isobutyric+water T_c = 299.27 K; T24 reuse: k_B × 299.27 K = 0.02579 eV; T13 cell-direct, T10 uncertainty=null for the characteristic-temperature anchor; T24 eleventh-use; third 3D-Ising anchor across distinct system_class values within one universality class — cell[0] liquid-gas-fluid (CO₂ T_c = 304.13 K), cell[1] antiferromagnet (FeF₂ T_N = 78.4 K), cell[17] binary-fluid-mixture (isobutyric+water T_c = 299.27 K) — the structural content of pred[0] 'universality across system classes' is now made queryable at the cell level via three numerically-anchored representatives spanning three distinct system_class axis values)

predictive_yield.prediction: 3D Ising critical exponents are universal across liquid-gas critical points, uniaxial magnets, and binary-fluid mixtures
predictive_yield.description: Universality predicted by Kadanoff (1966), Wilson, Fisher (1972) RG theory. Independent precision measurements in CO₂, Xe, H₂O liquid-gas critical points (Sengers reviews), FeF₂ and MnF₂ uniaxial antiferromagnets (Hutchings 1972, Belanger 1987), and binary-fluid mixtures (Beysens 1982) all give consistent exponents ν ≈ 0.630, η ≈ 0.04. Bootstrap-precision values (Kos-Poland-Simmons-Duffin 2014, El-Showk 2014) are consistent with measurements at the few-percent level.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Kadanoff 1966, Physics 2, 263; Wilson, Fisher 1972, Phys. Rev. Lett. 28, 240
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Sengers, Levelt Sengers 1986, Annu. Rev. Phys. Chem. 37, 189; Kos, Poland, Simmons-Duffin 2014, JHEP 11, 109

citations: Kos, Poland, Simmons-Duffin, Vichi 2016, JHEP 08, 036 'Precision Islands in the Ising and O(N) Models' arXiv:1603.04436 (mixed-correlator conformal bootstrap on the 3D Ising universality class giving Δ_σ = 0.5181489(10) and Δ_ε = 1.412625(10), equivalent to ν = 0.629971(4) via ν = 1/(d-Δ_ε); the source-value cited at FC scope is the rounded consensus ν ≈ 0.6300 ± 0.0002 spanning Monte-Carlo + bootstrap consensus and few-percent-precision experimental confirmations; T13 FC-pred convention, T10 uncertainty on the few-percent consensus level the predictive_yield prose records)

Campostrini, Pelissetto, Rossi, Vicari 1999, Phys. Rev. E 60, 3526 'Improved high-temperature expansion and critical equation of state of three-dimensional Ising-like systems' (high-temperature-series MC giving ν = 0.63002(23), γ = 1.2371(4), η = 0.0364(4), β = 0.32648(18) — independent confirmation of bootstrap)

Sengers, Levelt Sengers 1986, Annu. Rev. Phys. Chem. 37, 189 'Thermodynamic behavior of fluids near the critical point' (review of liquid-gas critical exponents in CO₂, Xe, H₂O at few-percent precision consistent with the universal 3D-Ising value)

predictive_yield.prediction: 3D O(2)/XY ν ≈ 0.6719 from bootstrap (Chester et al. 2020) — matches 4He λ-transition exponent
predictive_yield.description: The Chester et al. 2020 bootstrap result ν = 0.6719(7) is in ~5σ tension with the Lipa et al. 2003 satellite-microgravity 4He λ-transition measurement ν = 0.6709(1). This is the most prominent quantitative theory-experiment tension currently in critical phenomena — the '4He λ-puzzle.' Possible resolutions are open: bootstrap systematic underestimate, residual gravitational rounding in the satellite experiment, or higher-order corrections to scaling on either side. Listed `unconfirmed-tension` because the underlying universality assignment (4He → 3D O(2)) is confirmed but the bootstrap-precision exponent value is in tension.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Chester, Landry, Liu, Poland, Simmons-Duffin, Su, Vichi 2020, Phys. Rev. D 101, 084017
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Lipa, Nissen, Stricker, Swanson, Chui 2003, Phys. Rev. B 68, 174518 (4He λ measurement)

citations: Chester, Landry, Liu, Poland, Simmons-Duffin, Su, Vichi 2020, Phys. Rev. D 101, 084017 'Carving out OPE space and precise O(2) model critical exponents' arXiv:1912.03324 (mixed-correlator bootstrap on 3D O(2) universality class giving ν = 0.6719(7), in ~5σ tension with the Lipa 2003 microgravity 4He measurement; source-value: bootstrap ν_O(2) = 0.6719(7); T10 uncertainty=null for the σ-deviation discriminator)

Lipa, Nissen, Stricker, Swanson, Chui 2003, Phys. Rev. B 68, 174518 'Specific heat of liquid helium in zero gravity very near the lambda point' (Space-Shuttle microgravity 4He λ-transition measurement giving α = -0.0127(3) and the derived ν = 0.6709(1) via Josephson scaling; the experimental ν value sits 5σ below the bootstrap consensus 0.6719(7); the same dataset's α value sits in 8σ tension with the bootstrap α consensus -0.0151 — that tension is encoded at cft-bootstrap-exclusion-regions predictive_yield[1] from sub-PR 2, the cross-classification matched pair to this entry across the `specializes` edge)

predictive_yield.prediction: BKT transition in 2D 4He films has universal superfluid-density jump ρ_s(T_BKT⁻)/(k_B T_BKT) = 2 m² / (π ℏ²)
predictive_yield.description: Kosterlitz, Thouless 1973 predicted that the 2D XY model has a topological phase transition driven by vortex unbinding, with a universal jump in superfluid density at the transition. Bishop and Reppy 1978 measured the jump in thin 4He films using torsional oscillators and confirmed the predicted universal value — one of the cleanest universal-jump observations in critical phenomena.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Kosterlitz, Thouless 1973, J. Phys. C 6, 1181
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Bishop, Reppy 1978, Phys. Rev. Lett. 40, 1727

citations: Nelson, Kosterlitz 1977, Phys. Rev. Lett. 39, 1201 'Universal Jump in the Superfluid Density of Two-Dimensional Superfluids' (prediction that the dimensionless reduced superfluid stiffness K_R(T) := ℏ² ρ_s(T)/(m² k_B T) jumps to the universal value K_R(T_BKT⁻) = 2/π at the Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless transition, equivalent to the dimensional form ρ_s(T_BKT⁻)/T_BKT = 8π k_B (m/h)² = 3.52 × 10⁻⁹ g/cm²·K for 4He films; source-value: K_R(T_BKT⁻) = 2/π ≈ 0.6366; T10 uncertainty=null for the theoretically-exact universal value)

Bishop, Reppy 1978, Phys. Rev. Lett. 40, 1727 'Study of the Superfluid Transition in Two-Dimensional 4He Films' (torsional-oscillator confirmation of the Nelson-Kosterlitz universal jump in adsorbed 4He films, with measured ρ_s(T_BKT⁻)/T_BKT consistent with the predicted 3.52 × 10⁻⁹ g/cm²·K at the few-percent level — the canonical experimental verification of the BKT universal jump)

predictive_yield.prediction: 3D O(3)/Heisenberg ν ≈ 0.7117 — matches Ni / EuO / EuS Curie-point measurements
predictive_yield.description: Bootstrap result (Chester et al. 2021): 3D O(3) ν = 0.7117(5). Measurements in Ni, EuO, EuS at their Curie points give ν ≈ 0.71 at the few-percent level (limited by measurement precision rather than discrepancy). Consistent within current precision.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Chester, Landry, Liu, Poland, Simmons-Duffin, Su, Vichi 2021, Phys. Rev. D 104, 105013
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Hohenemser, Rosov, Kleinhammes 1989, Hyperfine Interact. 49, 267 (Ni)

citations: Campostrini, Hasenbusch, Pelissetto, Rossi, Vicari 2002, Phys. Rev. B 65, 144520 'Critical exponents and equation of state of the three-dimensional Heisenberg universality class' arXiv:cond-mat/0110336 (combined Monte-Carlo + high-temperature-expansion analysis of the 3D O(3) Heisenberg universality class giving ν = 0.7112(5), γ = 1.3960(9), η = 0.0375(5), β = 0.3689(3), α = -0.1336(15), δ = 4.783(3); source-value: theoretical consensus ν = 0.7112(5); T13 FC-pred convention)

Hohenemser, Rosov, Kleinhammes 1989, Hyperfine Interactions 49, 267 'Critical phenomena studied via nuclear techniques' (hyperfine-field-based critical scaling on Ni at the Curie point T_C = 627 K giving ν ≈ 0.71 at few-percent precision; representative Heisenberg-ferromagnet experimental confirmation of the universality assignment)

predictive_yield.prediction: Polymer scaling exponent ν ≈ 0.588 in 3D dilute polymer solutions in good solvent (SAW exponent)
predictive_yield.description: de Gennes 1972 mapped the SAW problem onto the n → 0 limit of the O(N) Wilson-Fisher fixed point, predicting ν ≈ 0.588 in 3D. Light-scattering measurements of polystyrene, PMMA, and other polymers in good solvent (Schäfer-Kappeler 1985 and subsequent) confirm at the few-percent level.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: de Gennes 1972, Phys. Lett. A 38, 339
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Schäfer, Kappeler 1985, J. Phys. Paris 46, 1853; Belohorec, Nickel 1997 Monte Carlo

citations: Clisby 2010, Phys. Rev. Lett. 104, 055702 'Accurate Estimate of the Critical Exponent ν for Self-Avoiding Walks via a Fast Implementation of the Pivot Algorithm' arXiv:1009.4711 (high-precision pivot-algorithm Monte-Carlo simulation of three-dimensional self-avoiding walks giving ν = 0.587597(7), the most-precise modern determination of the SAW exponent; source-value: ν_SAW = 0.587597 ± 0.000007; T13 FC-pred convention)

de Gennes 1972, Phys. Lett. A 38, 339 'Exponents for the excluded volume problem as derived by the Wilson method' (mapping of the self-avoiding-walk problem onto the n → 0 limit of the O(N) Wilson-Fisher fixed point, predicting ν ≈ 0.588 in 3D — the foundational theoretical anchor)

Schäfer, Kappeler 1985, J. Phys. Paris 46, 1853 'Experimental test of the universality of the critical exponents of polymer solutions' (light-scattering measurements of polystyrene and PMMA in good-solvent solution confirming ν at the few-percent level — representative experimental confirmation)

predictive_yield.prediction: Tricritical exponents in 3He-4He mixtures are mean-field with logarithmic corrections (d_c = 3 for tricritical)
predictive_yield.description: Riedel-Wegner 1972 predicted that 3D is the upper critical dimension for tricritical phase transitions, giving mean-field-like exponents with logarithmic corrections. Goellner-Behringer-Meyer 1973 specific-heat measurements of 3He-4He mixtures at the tricritical point confirm.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Riedel, Wegner 1972, Phys. Rev. Lett. 29, 349
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Goellner, Behringer, Meyer 1973, J. Low Temp. Phys. 13, 113

predictive_yield.prediction: Chiral phase transition in massless 2-flavor QCD is in 3D O(4) universality class
predictive_yield.description: Pisarski, Wilczek 1984 predicted O(4) universality in the chiral limit of 2-flavor QCD. Real QCD has nonzero u, d quark masses, which break the exact chiral SU(2)_L × SU(2)_R symmetry — the predicted phase transition becomes a smooth crossover at physical quark masses. Lattice QCD at small but nonzero quark masses is consistent with O(4) scaling in the extrapolation, but cannot directly test the prediction at zero quark mass. Listed `retro-explanatory-only` — the prediction is consistent with what data exists but is not falsifiable at physical conditions.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Pisarski, Wilczek 1984, Phys. Rev. D 29, 338

predictive_yield.prediction: 3D O(5), O(6), and higher-N Wilson-Fisher fixed points exist as theoretical CFTs with calculable exponents — no clean material realization yet
predictive_yield.description: Bootstrap (Chester et al. 2022 and earlier) gives precision exponents for any 3D O(N). Some proposed deconfined-quantum-criticality scenarios may realize high-N Wilson-Fisher fixed points but the universality assignments remain contested. No clean experimental realization confirmed.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Chester et al. 2021, Phys. Rev. D 104, 105013 (O(N) bootstrap)

predictive_yield.prediction: Surface-extraordinary critical universality class is realizable in materials with enhanced surface ordering
predictive_yield.description: Diehl 1986 predicted the 'extraordinary' surface universality where the surface orders more strongly than the bulk near the bulk-critical point. Bootstrap-precision analysis (Padayasi et al. 2022) gives the surface exponents but no clean experimental realization is confirmed. Some quantum-Hall-edge realizations have been proposed.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Diehl 1986, Phase Transitions and Critical Phenomena vol. 10

predictive_yield.prediction: Universality holds across all microscopic realizations of a given symmetry / dimension combination
predictive_yield.description: The cornerstone of critical phenomena. Predicted by Kadanoff (1966), formalized via RG by Wilson (1971-72), and confirmed empirically across dozens of independent systems sharing the same universality class (3D Ising: liquid-gas + uniaxial magnets + binary fluids; 3D O(3) Heisenberg: Ni + EuO + EuS + RbMnF₃; etc.). This is the structural fact that makes the Periodic-Table-of-Universality entry meaningful.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Kadanoff 1966, Physics 2, 263; Wilson 1971, Phys. Rev. B 4, 3174
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Multiple independent measurements across cells — the universality prediction has been tested by every system-class transition tabulated in this FC

closure_status.description: The Wilson-Fisher / Kadanoff RG classification of critical universality classes is structurally complete — for any (symmetry, dimension) combination satisfying d ≤ d_c, the universality class is well-defined and its exponents are calculable by bootstrap (high precision for low N) or epsilon-expansion / Monte Carlo (other regimes). However, the FC is `partial` because: (i) several universality classes have no clean material realization (3D O(N≥5), surface-extraordinary, some Lifshitz refinements); (ii) the 3D O(2) / 4He λ-puzzle is an unresolved quantitative tension between bootstrap-precision and experiment; (iii) surface-critical, multicritical, and disorder-induced classes are less precision-tested than bulk Wilson-Fisher classes. The classification framework is settled — what remains open are specific experimental confirmations and the λ-puzzle resolution.

modular-tensor-categories (2D CFTs at criticality — 2D Ising connects to Ising MTC, BKT to free-boson c=1 CFT; partial connection for the 2D cells)
fractional-quantum-hall-states (sibling phenomenon-FC; both pair with bootstrap-CFT and MTC scaffolding but at different sectors — FQH is topological-phase, real-critical is bulk-critical)

topological-insulator-and-superconductor-materials (sibling phenomenon-FC; both in condensed-matter sector but distinct subjects — topological matter is gapped phases, real-critical is critical points)
PLANNED FUTURE PHENOMENON-FC: quantum-critical-points (T=0 transitions with different scaling, would pair with bootstrap differently — distinct from this thermal-critical FC)
PLANNED FUTURE PHENOMENON-FC: deconfined-quantum-criticality (proposed but contested universality classes adjacent to standard Wilson-Fisher; possibly involves high-N O(N) or other CFTs)

nodes.label: Dark matter candidates
nodes.full_name: Classification of proposed dark-matter candidates — particle and non-particle — by candidate class, production mechanism, and observational status (viable, partially excluded, largely excluded, or specific-claim falsified)
nodes.description: Dark matter — gravitationally-detected non-baryonic matter comprising ~27% of the universe's energy density — has no confirmed particle or non-particle identity despite ~50 years of dedicated experimental effort. The candidate space is rich and well-classified: particle candidates include weakly-interacting massive particles (WIMPs, GeV-TeV), QCD axions (sub-eV), axion-like particles (ALPs, wider parameter space), sterile neutrinos (eV / keV / GeV-TeV mass scales), fuzzy / ultralight dark matter (10⁻²² eV bosonic condensates), dark photons (portal-coupled), and various self-interacting / asymmetric / mirror / hidden-sector candidates. Non-particle candidates include primordial black holes (asteroid-mass to supermassive). This FC organizes confirmed-viable candidates, partially-excluded candidates (parameter space cut by null results but not eliminated), largely-excluded candidates (canonical WIMP-miracle parameter space post-LZ 2024), and falsified specific claims (DAMA/LIBRA annual modulation, CDMS-II 2010 Si excess). Cells correspond to specific candidate classes within defined parameter windows; axes are candidate-class × production-mechanism × observational-status. Mendeleev-style empty cells include the asteroid-mass PBH window (currently unconstrained as a major DM fraction), the QCD axion DFSZ/KSVZ band (viable, actively searched by ADMX, HAYSTAC), and the keV-scale sterile-ν warm-DM window (X-ray line bounds + structure-formation partial exclusion). Cell-level cross-references to compact-astrophysical-objects (PBHs as cells in both FCs) and neutrino-sector-phenomenology (sterile-ν as cells in both FCs) document the shared-content overlap without forcing FC-level edges where no edge subtype cleanly fits.
nodes.domain_of_applicability: Dark matter candidates — particle, compact-object, or other — that could constitute all or a major fraction of the observed cosmological dark matter density Ω_DM ≈ 0.27. Includes: particle candidates with thermal or non-thermal cosmological histories, primordial black holes at any mass scale that could constitute a fraction of DM, hidden-sector / dark-photon / portal-coupled candidates, and ultralight (sub-eV) bosonic dark matter. Excludes: standard-model neutrinos (mass too small to constitute DM at observed Ω_DM); ordinary baryonic matter (BBN + CMB rule out as DM); modified-gravity alternatives to dark matter (MOND, TeVeS — these are not dark-matter candidates but DM-replacement frameworks; covered in a separate phenomenon-FC if authored). Also excludes the cosmological dark-matter abundance value itself (lives in a cosmological-observations FC if authored) — this FC organizes candidates for what DM is, not its abundance.
nodes.organizing_principle: Each candidate is identified by (i) its candidate class — what kind of entity (particle of specific type, compact object, ultralight boson, etc.); (ii) its production mechanism in the early universe — thermal-freeze-out for canonical WIMPs, thermal-freeze-in for very-weakly-coupled candidates, misalignment for axions / ultralight bosons, gravitational formation for PBHs, asymmetric-generation linked to baryogenesis, etc.; (iii) its observational status — distinguishing viable-targeted (parameter space open and actively searched), parameter-space-partly-excluded (significant region cut by null results), parameter-space-largely-excluded (canonical version essentially eliminated), and falsified-claim (specific positive-signal claims excluded). The classification is constrained from above by cosmological observations (Ω_DM ≈ 0.27, structure-formation requirements: cold or warm, not hot; CMB + Lyman-α + Milky Way satellite counts), null results from direct detection (LZ, XENONnT, PandaX-4T spin-independent cross-section bounds), indirect detection (Fermi-LAT, AMS gamma-ray and cosmic-ray bounds), collider searches (LHC mono-jet / mono-photon + missing energy), axion-specific haloscopes (ADMX, HAYSTAC, ABRACADABRA), microlensing (OGLE-IV, EROS, MACHO survey for compact-object DM), and stellar-cooling bounds (axions can drain energy from red giants / horizontal-branch stars / supernovae). The classification is dynamic — the canonical WIMP-miracle parameter space transitioned from `viable-targeted` (pre-2015) to `parameter-space-largely-excluded` (post-2024 LZ) over the past decade.

classification_axes.name: candidate-class
classification_axes.description: The candidate class. WIMP: thermal-relic massive particle with weak-scale coupling, GeV-TeV mass; canonical SUSY-LSP and analogous candidates. QCD-axion: axion solution of the strong-CP problem (Peccei-Quinn 1977), with mass and coupling linked by Peccei-Quinn breaking scale (DFSZ / KSVZ models). ALP: axion-like particle with similar phenomenology but uncoupled to QCD theta — wider parameter space. sterile-neutrino-DM: gauge-singlet right-handed neutrino, dark-matter-viable at keV-scale (warm DM) primarily; eV-scale candidate is largely excluded but still in tension. primordial-black-hole: black holes formed in early universe density fluctuations; mass-scale spans many orders of magnitude. fuzzy-ultralight-DM: bosonic field at ultralight mass (m ~ 10⁻²² eV) forming a Bose-Einstein-condensate-like halo with de Broglie wavelength ~ kpc. self-interacting-DM: DM with cross-section σ/m ~ 1 cm²/g (motivated by small-scale-structure problems). asymmetric-DM: DM number density set by an early-universe asymmetry, paralleling baryogenesis. mirror-DM: parallel sector of mirror-baryons with mirror-baryogenesis. dark-photon-portal: dark sector coupled to SM only through kinetic mixing with photon. gravitino-LSP: superpartner of graviton in SUSY scenarios. MACHO-baryonic-compact: faint baryonic compact objects (brown dwarfs, faint stars) — historically a DM candidate now largely falsified.

classification_axes.name: production-mechanism
classification_axes.description: How the candidate's relic abundance was generated in the early universe. thermal-freeze-out: standard WIMP mechanism; equilibrium followed by Boltzmann freeze-out as annihilation rate drops below Hubble. thermal-freeze-in: very-weakly-coupled candidate accumulates abundance through SM-decays without reaching equilibrium. misalignment: bosonic field (axion, fuzzy DM) starts at non-zero value, oscillates after H drops below m_a, oscillation energy density = current DM. gravitational-formation: PBHs from early-universe density fluctuations (typically inflation-end or QCD-transition fluctuations). asymmetric-generation: DM number density set by a separate asymmetry, like baryon asymmetry. baryogenesis-linked: DM density tied directly to baryogenesis mechanism. non-thermal-decay-product: DM is the decay product of a heavier non-thermal source. mirror-baryogenesis: parallel mechanism in mirror sector.

classification_axes.name: observational-status
classification_axes.description: Status given current observational constraints. viable-targeted: parameter space open and actively searched (QCD axion in DFSZ/KSVZ band, asteroid-mass PBHs, fuzzy DM at m ≳ 10⁻²¹ eV). parameter-space-partly-excluded: significant region of parameter space cut by null results but not eliminated (keV sterile-ν warm DM, eV-coupling dark photon). parameter-space-largely-excluded: canonical / naturally-motivated parameter space essentially eliminated (canonical WIMP-miracle ~weak-scale thermal relic; eV-scale sterile-ν as primary DM). falsified-specific-claim: a specific positive-detection claim has been excluded (DAMA/LIBRA annual modulation interpretation as WIMP; CDMS-II 2010 Si excess). excluded-as-major-fraction: candidate excluded from constituting major DM fraction in specific window (MACHOs from microlensing; PBHs in LIGO mass window from binary-merger rate).

cells.content: Canonical WIMP miracle — thermal-relic GeV-TeV particle with weak-scale annihilation cross-section
realized_examples: LZ Collaboration 2024 Phys. Rev. Lett. result: spin-independent cross-section σ_SI < 2.2 × 10⁻⁴⁸ cm² at m_χ = 36 GeV (90% CL); XENONnT 2023 Phys. Rev. Lett. equivalent bounds. Canonical SUSY-neutralino-LSP parameter space at ~weak-scale-cross-section is essentially excluded.
Heavy-WIMP candidates (TeV-PeV scale, with non-standard freeze-out / non-thermal-relic histories) remain viable; the exclusion applies to the canonical thermal-relic WIMP-miracle prediction.
cells.description: The canonical WIMP miracle: a thermally-decoupled particle with mass m ~ 100 GeV-1 TeV and annihilation cross-section ⟨σv⟩ ~ 3 × 10⁻²⁶ cm³/s gives Ω_DM h² ≈ 0.12 automatically (the 'miracle'). This naturalness argument motivated 30+ years of WIMP-search programs. The 2010s-2020s direct-detection programs (XENON1T, LZ, PandaX-4T) have systematically excluded the parameter space; LZ 2024 reached σ_SI sensitivity an order of magnitude below typical SUSY-neutralino predictions. Canonical-WIMP-miracle status `parameter-space-largely-excluded` for the standard scenario; non-canonical WIMP variants (heavier, non-thermal, with custom freeze-out) remain viable in narrower regions.
citations: Lee, Weinberg 1977, Phys. Rev. Lett. 39, 165 (cosmological neutralino bound, WIMP foundation)
Aalbers et al. (LZ Collaboration) 2024, Phys. Rev. Lett. 132, 041002 (2024 result)
Aprile et al. (XENONnT) 2023, Phys. Rev. Lett. 131, 041003

Aalbers et al. (LZ Collaboration) 2025 'Dark Matter Search Results from 4.2 tonne-year of Exposure of the LUX-ZEPLIN (LZ) Experiment' Phys. Rev. Lett. 135:011802 arXiv:2410.17036 (LZ 2024/25 world-leading SI WIMP-nucleon constraint: σ_SI < 2.2 × 10⁻⁴⁸ cm² at m_χ = 40 GeV/c² peak sensitivity, 90% CL; supersedes the PRL 132:041002 (2024) WS2022-only result already cited on this cell; the LZ limit excludes wide regions of canonical SUSY-neutralino parameter space and approaches the irreducible neutrino-fog background within an order of magnitude — heart-of-darkness territory; T4 upper bound, T5 uncertainty=null, T13 direct cells[i].quantitative_scale, T17 generalization → T23 first-use: cross-section-as-area encoded as kind=length + units=cm²; PDG 2024 Review §27 Fig. 27.1 shows the world cross-section landscape with LZ on top)
Aprile et al. (XENONnT Collaboration) 2023, Phys. Rev. Lett. 131, 041003 (XENONnT companion best-limit: comparable sensitivity in the same WIMP mass range)
PDG 2024 Review of Particle Physics §27 Dark Matter (review compendium for the world DM direct-detection landscape including the neutrino fog)

cells.content: QCD axion in DFSZ / KSVZ models — sub-eV mass linked to Peccei-Quinn scale
realized_examples: ADMX (Axion Dark Matter Experiment) at U. Washington — most-stringent search for sub-eV QCD axion via axion-photon coupling g_aγγ in a high-Q microwave cavity in strong B-field. ADMX-G2 (2018-2024) exclusion of m_a in 2.66-4.2 μeV range at DFSZ sensitivity. HAYSTAC (Yale) and ABRACADABRA (MIT) extending to higher masses. Other haloscope programs (ORGAN, QUAX, CAPP) ongoing.
cells.description: Most theoretically-motivated dark-matter candidate after the WIMP. QCD axion solves the strong-CP problem; if the Peccei-Quinn scale is high (f_a ~ 10⁹-10¹² GeV), the axion mass is sub-eV and the misalignment production gives Ω_DM ~ 0.27. DFSZ and KSVZ models differ in coupling strength; DFSZ is more challenging to detect. ADMX has reached DFSZ sensitivity over a sub-range of axion masses; full coverage of the cosmologically-allowed axion window requires major scaling up of haloscope programs. Status `viable-targeted` — the central candidate after WIMP-miracle exclusion.
citations: Peccei, Quinn 1977, Phys. Rev. Lett. 38, 1440 (PQ mechanism)
Weinberg 1978, Phys. Rev. Lett. 40, 223; Wilczek 1978 (axion)
Du et al. (ADMX) 2018, Phys. Rev. Lett. 120, 151301 (G2 DFSZ result)
Bartram et al. (ADMX) 2021, Phys. Rev. Lett. 127, 261803

Preskill, Wise, Wilczek 1983 'Cosmology of the Invisible Axion' Phys. Lett. B 120:127 (canonical misalignment-mechanism abundance Ω_a h² ≈ 0.01 θ²_mis (f_a/10¹¹ GeV)¹·¹⁹ ≈ 0.12 for f_a ~ 10¹¹–10¹² GeV with θ_mis ~ O(1); 10¹¹ GeV taken as benchmark center of the DFSZ/KSVZ-compatible canonical misalignment window for axion-as-100%-DM; T13 direct cells[i].quantitative_scale; T5 uncertainty=null since this is the model-internal benchmark of a parameter range, not a measurement)
Abbott, Sikivie 1983, Phys. Lett. B 120:133 'A Cosmological Bound on the Invisible Axion' (independent derivation of the misalignment cosmology bound)
Dine, Fischler 1983, Phys. Lett. B 120:137 'The Not So Harmless Axion' (third independent derivation; the three papers comprise the canonical 1983 invisible-axion-DM trilogy)

Co, Hall, Harigaya 2018 'QCD Axion Dark Matter with a Small Decay Constant' Phys. Rev. Lett. 120:151301 arXiv:1711.10486 (recent compendium of f_a ~ O(10¹¹) GeV misalignment requirement; string-defect production extends viable f_a down to ~10⁸-10¹¹ GeV in post-inflationary PQ-breaking scenarios)

cells.content: Axion-like particles (ALPs) — generic pseudoscalar bosons in wider mass-coupling space
realized_examples: ALPS-II at DESY (light-shining-through-walls), IAXO (axion helioscope), MADMAX (high-mass haloscope), ADMX-Sidecar — multi-pronged ALP search programs. ALPs decoupled from the strong-CP-solving requirement allow wider g_aγγ - m_a parameter space.
cells.description: Generic pseudoscalar bosons (axion-like) that share axion-photon coupling phenomenology but don't necessarily solve strong-CP. Parameter space is broader than QCD axion (mass-coupling unlinked). Active search programs target different mass-coupling regions. ALPs are well-motivated in string-theoretic compactifications (string-axiverse, Arvanitaki et al. 2010 Phys. Rev. D 81, 123530). Cell distinguishes from QCD-axion cell (cell-dm-QCD-axion-DFSZ-KSVZ) by the absence of strong-CP-solving requirement.
citations: Arvanitaki, Dimopoulos, Dubovsky, Kaloper, March-Russell 2010, Phys. Rev. D 81, 123530 (string axiverse)
Ehret et al. (ALPS) 2010, Phys. Lett. B 689, 149

ADMX Collaboration (C. Bartram et al.) 2021, Phys. Rev. Lett. 127:261803 — 'Search for invisible axion dark matter in the 3.3–4.2 μeV mass range', Run 1B exclusion at DFSZ axion-photon coupling g_{aγγ} ≲ 1.4 × 10⁻¹⁶ GeV⁻¹ ≈ 10⁻¹⁶ GeV⁻¹ at 90% CL across 3.3–4.2 μeV (canonical haloscope DFSZ-sensitivity demonstration).

ADMX Collaboration (S. J. Asztalos et al.) 2010, Phys. Rev. Lett. 104:041301 — 'SQUID-based microwave cavity search for dark-matter axions', foundational ADMX haloscope exclusion in the 1.9–3.5 μeV window at the KSVZ level.

HAYSTAC Collaboration (B. M. Brubaker et al.) 2017, Phys. Rev. Lett. 118:061302 — 'First results from a microwave cavity axion search at 24 μeV', extending haloscope sensitivity to higher-mass axions at near-KSVZ couplings.
PDG 2024 (S. Navas et al., Phys. Rev. D 110:030001), 'Axions and other very light bosons' review — canonical compilation of haloscope g_{aγγ} exclusions across the μeV mass window.

cells.content: Sterile neutrino at keV scale as warm-DM candidate (shared with neutrino-phen cell-nu-sterile-keV-WarmDM)
realized_examples: X-ray line searches (XMM-Newton, NuSTAR) bound sterile-ν → γ + ν decay rate; 2014 Bulbul et al. and Boyarsky et al. tentatively detected ~3.5 keV X-ray line in galaxy clusters, suggesting sterile-ν mass ~7 keV. Subsequent observations (Dessert-Rodd-Safdi 2020 Science 367, 1465) significantly weakened the 3.5 keV claim. Structure-formation bounds from Lyman-α and Milky Way satellite-galaxy counts bound m_s ≳ few keV.

Dodelson-Widrow oscillation-production gives a relic abundance dependent on sterile-active mixing; Shi-Fuller resonant production gives alternative cosmological histories. Parameter space partially excluded but not eliminated.
cells.description: Keynote candidate for warm dark matter (intermediate between cold WIMPs and hot SM-neutrinos). Cell content overlaps with neutrino-sector-phenomenology cell-nu-sterile-keV-WarmDM — same physical particle, classified here as DM candidate and there as neutrino-sector observable. Status `parameter-space-partly-excluded`: substantial region cut by X-ray bounds + structure-formation, but viable parameter space remains.
citations: Dodelson, Widrow 1994, Phys. Rev. Lett. 72, 17 (sterile-ν DM production)
Shi, Fuller 1999, Phys. Rev. Lett. 82, 2832 (resonant production)
Bulbul et al. 2014, Astrophys. J. 789, 13 (3.5 keV line)
Dessert, Rodd, Safdi 2020, Science 367, 1465 (constraining 3.5 keV)

Bulbul et al. 2014 'Detection of An Unidentified Emission Line in the Stacked X-ray Spectrum of Galaxy Clusters' Astrophys. J. 789:13 arXiv:1402.2301 (3.5 keV unidentified X-ray line in stacked galaxy-cluster spectra; sterile-ν → γ + ν two-body decay produces photon at E_γ = m_s/2 so the 3.55 keV line corresponds to m_s ≈ 7.1 keV; T13 direct cells[i].quantitative_scale; cross-FC complementary to v48 cell-nu-sterile-keV-WarmDM which records the line energy E_γ = 3.55 keV under kind=energy_scale — same physical particle, distinct observables, DM-side carries the particle-mass anchor while ν-sector-side carries the observational-signature anchor; T5 uncertainty=null since the 7-keV inference is the WDM-as-DM interpretation benchmark)
Boyarsky et al. 2014, Phys. Rev. Lett. 113:251301 (independent confirmation of the 3.5 keV line in M31 + Milky Way center)
Dodelson, Widrow 1994, Phys. Rev. Lett. 72:17 (foundational sterile-ν DM production framework via Dodelson-Widrow mixing)

Dessert, Rodd, Safdi 2020, Science 367:1465 (subsequent observations weakened 3.5 keV line significance; encoding the 7-keV mass anchor remains firewall-faithful for the WDM-as-DM interpretation as stated in Bulbul/Boyarsky)

cells.content: eV-scale sterile neutrino as primary DM — shared with neutrino-phen cells cell-nu-sterile-eV-LSND-claim-FALSIFIED and cell-nu-sterile-eV-tension
realized_examples: eV-scale sterile-ν as primary DM is largely excluded — Σm_ν cosmological bound (< 0.12 eV from Planck) and active-sterile mixing constraints leave a narrow window where eV-sterile could be a fraction of DM but not the bulk. Some remaining parameter space from BEST gallium-anomaly results.
cells.description: Cell content overlaps with neutrino-sector-phenomenology eV-sterile cells. From the DM perspective, eV-scale sterile-ν is `parameter-space-largely-excluded` as the primary DM constituent. From the neutrino-sector perspective, the LSND-specific eV-sterile interpretation is `falsified-claim` while a remaining gallium-anomaly-related parameter space is `measured-tension`. Both perspectives apply to the same particles.
citations: Acero et al. (MicroBooNE) 2022, Phys. Rev. Lett. 128, 241801 (LSND parameter-space exclusion)
Aghanim et al. (Planck) 2020 (cosmological mass bound)

LSND Collaboration (A. Aguilar et al.) 2001, Phys. Rev. D 64:112007 — 'Evidence for neutrino oscillations from the observation of ν̄_e appearance in a ν̄_μ beam', original LSND anomaly suggesting Δm² ~ 1 eV² sterile-neutrino mass.

MiniBooNE Collaboration (A. A. Aguilar-Arevalo et al.) 2018, Phys. Rev. Lett. 121:221801 — '4.8σ excess of electron-like events compatible with LSND-preferred 3+1 sterile oscillation at Δm² ~ 1 eV², sin²(2θ_μe) ~ 10⁻³–10⁻²'.

M. Dentler, Á. Hernández-Cabezudo, J. Kopp, P. A. N. Machado, M. Maltoni, I. Martinez-Soler, T. Schwetz 2018, JHEP 08:010 (arXiv:1803.10661) — global fit to short-baseline-anomaly data; preferred m_s² ≈ 1.3 eV² (m_s ≈ 1.14 eV) with 99% CL range roughly m_s ∈ [0.5, 2] eV. (Source for the ±0.5 eV symmetric uncertainty around the 1.0 eV central used here.)

IceCube Collaboration (M. G. Aartsen et al.) 2020, Phys. Rev. Lett. 125:141801 — atmospheric-neutrino disappearance constraints excluding the 3+1 LSND/MiniBooNE-preferred region at high CL (rationale for 'parameter-space-largely-excluded' observational-status).
Planck Collaboration (N. Aghanim et al.) 2020, A&A 641:A6 — CMB+BAO bound Σm_ν < 0.12 eV (95% CL) and ΔN_eff constraints disfavoring fully-thermalized eV-scale sterile.
PDG 2024 'Neutrino Masses, Mixing, and Oscillations' review.

cells.content: Asteroid-mass primordial black holes (10¹⁷-10²² g) as viable DM — shared with compact-astro cell-compact-PBH-asteroid-mass
realized_examples: Microlensing surveys (Subaru HSC for short-duration events, OGLE-IV) bound PBH fraction at most masses but leave the asteroid-mass-to-sublunar window (M ≈ 10¹⁷-10²² g) less constrained. PBH-asteroid-mass remains viable as the bulk of DM in this narrow window.
cells.description: Cell content overlaps with compact-astro cell-compact-PBH-asteroid-mass — same physical objects, classified here as DM candidate. The asteroid-mass PBH window has been resilient to multiple exclusion attempts (Hawking-evaporation lifetimes long enough; microlensing wavelength too short for typical surveys; femtolensing of GRBs partially constraining). Status `viable-targeted` — actively searched by femtolensing, microlensing-with-short-events, and stellar/exoplanet capture-rate analyses.
citations: Carr, Kühnel, Sandstad 2016, Phys. Rev. D 94, 083504 (PBH-as-DM review)
Niikura et al. 2019, Nature Astron. 3, 524 (Subaru HSC microlensing bound)
Carr, Kühnel 2020, Annu. Rev. Nucl. Part. Sci. 70, 355

Niikura, Takada, Yasuda, Lupton, Sumi, More, More, Oguri, Chiba 2019 'Microlensing constraints on primordial black holes with Subaru/HSC Andromeda observations' Nature Astron. 3:524 arXiv:1701.02151 (Subaru HSC dense-cadence ~2-min sampling M31 observation probing M_PBH ∈ [10⁻¹⁴, 10⁻⁹] M_sun ≈ [2 × 10¹⁹, 2 × 10²⁴] g; exclusion of PBH-as-100%-DM extends down to m_PBH ≈ 10²¹ g where source-size effects suppress amplification; the asteroid-mass viable window for PBH-as-100%-DM extends from the Hawking-evaporation lower edge ~10¹⁷ g where τ_Hawking < age-of-universe up to this microlensing exclusion boundary at ~10²¹ g; T4 upper bound of viable window, T5 uncertainty=null, T13 direct cells[i].quantitative_scale)

Smyth, Profumo, English, Jeltema, McKinnon, Guhathakurta 2020, Phys. Rev. D 101:063005 arXiv:1910.01285 (finite-source-size correction to Niikura constraint weakens the HSC bound by up to ~3 orders of magnitude in places, broadening the viable PBH asteroid-mass window — encoded boundary remains the canonical microlensing exclusion edge under the original Subaru HSC source-size assumption)
Carr, Kühnel 2020, Annu. Rev. Nucl. Part. Sci. 70:355 (PBH-as-DM review compendium across mass scales)

cells.content: PBHs in LIGO-mass window (1-100 M_sun) as 100% DM — excluded (shared with compact-astro cell-compact-PBH-LIGO-window-excluded)
realized_examples: LIGO-Virgo binary-merger rate in the 10-100 M_sun window is incompatible with PBHs constituting 100% of DM — the implied merger rate exceeds the observed LVK rate by orders of magnitude. PBH fraction in this mass window is bounded at f_PBH ≲ 10⁻³.
cells.description: Cell content overlaps with compact-astro cell-compact-PBH-LIGO-window-excluded. The LIGO-mass-window PBH-as-100%-DM scenario was a falsifiable prediction (third falsified prediction in the dataset, recorded in v21); from the DM perspective, this is `excluded-as-major-fraction`. PBHs at smaller fraction (≲ 0.1%) remain viable in this window.
citations: Carr, Kühnel 2020 (review)
Hütsi, Raidal, Vaskonen 2021, Phys. Rev. D 103, 063510 (LVK merger-rate bounds)

Hütsi, Raidal, Vaskonen 2021 'Two populations of LIGO-Virgo black holes' Phys. Rev. D 103:063510 arXiv:2012.02786 (LVK O1-O3 BBH merger-rate analysis: PBH-as-DM fraction f_PBH(10-100 M_sun) ≲ 10⁻³ from incompatibility of predicted PBH-PBH merger rates with observed LIGO-Virgo BBH rates; T4 upper bound, T5 uncertainty=null, T10 falsified-context uncertainty=null, T13 direct cells[i].quantitative_scale; the LIGO-mass-window PBH-as-100%-DM scenario is the third falsified prediction in the dataset, recorded v21)
Carr, Kühnel 2020, Annu. Rev. Nucl. Part. Sci. 70:355 (PBH-DM review with LIGO-mass-window analysis)
Green, Kavanagh 2021, J. Phys. G 48:043001 (companion review of PBH constraints)

cells.content: Fuzzy / ultralight dark matter — bosonic field at m ~ 10⁻²² eV (de Broglie wavelength ~ kpc)
realized_examples: Pulsar timing array bounds on m_φ ≳ 10⁻²³ eV; Lyman-α forest bounds (Iršič et al. 2017) on m_φ ≳ 2 × 10⁻²¹ eV. Galaxy rotation-curve and dwarf-galaxy-core analyses partially favor or exclude depending on mass. Subhalo / cosmic-web observations partially constrain.
Active galactic-nuclei light-curve searches (superradiance) constrain ultralight bosons coupled to BH spins — Arvanitaki et al. 2015.
cells.description: Ultralight bosonic dark matter forming a Bose-Einstein-condensate-like halo with de Broglie wavelength comparable to galaxy core scales. Motivated as a solution to small-scale-structure problems (core-cusp, missing-satellite). Status `parameter-space-partly-excluded`: low-mass end (m_φ < 10⁻²¹ eV) excluded by Lyman-α; viable region remains at m_φ > 10⁻²¹ eV with various structural-formation and superradiance constraints.
citations: Hu, Barkana, Gruzinov 2000, Phys. Rev. Lett. 85, 1158 (fuzzy DM)
Iršič et al. 2017, Phys. Rev. Lett. 119, 031302 (Lyman-α)
Arvanitaki, Baryakhtar, Huang 2015, Phys. Rev. D 91, 084011 (superradiance)

Iršič, Viel, Haehnelt, Bolton, Becker 2017 'First Constraints on Fuzzy Dark Matter from Lyman-α Forest Data and Hydrodynamical Simulations' Phys. Rev. Lett. 119:031302 arXiv:1703.04683 (combined XQ-100 + HIRES/MIKE Lyman-α forest analysis with hydrodynamical simulations: m_φ ≳ 20 × 10⁻²² eV = 2 × 10⁻²¹ eV at 2σ CL with conservative IGM thermal-history assumptions allowing temperature jumps up to 5000 K; XQ-100 alone: 7.1 × 10⁻²² eV; HIRES/MIKE alone: 14.3 × 10⁻²² eV; the original FDM mass range 1-10 × 10⁻²² eV motivated for solving small-scale-structure problems is ruled out at high significance; T4 lower bound, T5 uncertainty=null, T13 direct cells[i].quantitative_scale)

Armengaud, Palanque-Delabrouille, Yèche, Marsh, Baur 2017, Mon. Not. R. Astron. Soc. 471:4606 (SDSS Lyman-α independent constraint m_a > 2.9 × 10⁻²¹ eV at 95% CL; consistent with Iršič et al. 2017 within systematics)
Hui, Ostriker, Tremaine, Witten 2017, Phys. Rev. D 95:043541 (fuzzy DM cosmology review compendium)

cells.content: Self-interacting dark matter — σ/m ~ 0.1-10 cm²/g, motivated by small-scale-structure problems
realized_examples: Bullet Cluster (Markevitch et al. 2004) bounds σ/m < 0.7 cm²/g for hot DM-DM collisions; cluster offset measurements give similar bounds. Galaxy-scale observations (cored profiles, dwarf-galaxy diversity, fewer-than-expected satellites) suggest σ/m ~ 0.1-1 cm²/g at galactic-velocity scales — implying velocity-dependent cross-sections (Yukawa-like).
Specific particle realizations: Yukawa-coupled hidden-sector mediators with sub-GeV mass; dark photon coupling; ELDER dark matter.
cells.description: Dark matter with non-negligible self-interactions at galactic-velocity scales. Motivated by small-scale-structure problems (core-cusp, satellite-galaxy diversity). Viable in narrow parameter spaces; particle-physics realizations connect to dark-photon and Yukawa-mediator scenarios.
citations: Spergel, Steinhardt 2000, Phys. Rev. Lett. 84, 3760 (SIDM motivation)
Markevitch et al. 2004, Astrophys. J. 606, 819 (Bullet Cluster bound)
Tulin, Yu 2018, Phys. Rep. 730, 1 (SIDM review)

S. W. Randall, M. Markevitch, D. Clowe, A. H. Gonzalez, M. Bradač 2008, ApJ 679:1173 (arXiv:0704.0261) — refined Bullet-Cluster SIDM bound σ/m < 0.7 cm²/g at 68% CL from collisionless-DM dynamics; the round-figure σ/m ≲ 1 cm²/g order-of-magnitude upper bound used here.
M. Markevitch et al. 2004, ApJ 606:819 — original Bullet-Cluster constraint σ/m < 1.25 cm²/g at 68% CL.

S. Tulin, H.-B. Yu 2018, Phys. Rep. 730:1 (arXiv:1705.02358) — 'Dark matter self-interactions and small scale structure', canonical SIDM review; σ/m ~ 0.5–10 cm²/g motivated by dwarf-galaxy core-cusp problem, in tension with σ/m ≲ 1 cm²/g cluster-scale bounds, with velocity-dependent cross-sections (Kaplinghat-Tulin-Yu 2016 PRL 116:041302) as the canonical resolution.
PDG 2024 'Dark Matter' review article §28 'Particle dark matter searches' — compilation of SIDM constraints.

cells.content: Asymmetric dark matter — relic abundance set by a separate dark asymmetry, paralleling baryogenesis
realized_examples: Specific UV completions: hidden-sector baryogenesis with dark-baryon-number; pangenesis (Hall-Marsano-Cui 2010); aidnogenesis (Davoudiasl-Kim-Murayama). Models predict m_DM ~ few GeV (set by ratio Ω_DM/Ω_b ≈ 5 and dark-asymmetry size ~ baryon asymmetry).
cells.description: Dark-matter abundance set by an early-universe asymmetry analogous to the baryon asymmetry of the universe. Predicts m_DM ~ 1-15 GeV if dark-asymmetry equals baryon-asymmetry. Avoids the WIMP-miracle parameter-space exclusion by not relying on thermal freeze-out. Status `viable-targeted` — narrow region of parameter space targeted by sub-GeV-DM direct-detection programs (SuperCDMS, SENSEI, DAMIC, light-mediator searches).
citations: Nussinov 1985, Phys. Lett. B 165, 55 (early asymmetric DM)
Petraki, Volkas 2013, Int. J. Mod. Phys. A 28, 1330028 (asymmetric DM review)
Hall, March-Russell, West 2010 (pangenesis)

Nussinov 1985 'Technocosmology — Could a Technibaryon Excess Provide a 'Natural' Missing Mass Candidate?' Phys. Lett. B 165:55 (foundational asymmetric-DM proposal: if dark-asymmetry equals baryon-asymmetry n_DM ≈ n_B, m_DM ≈ (Ω_DM/Ω_b) × m_p ≈ 5.4 × 0.938 GeV ≈ 5 GeV is the canonical benchmark; T13 direct cells[i].quantitative_scale; T5 uncertainty=null since 5 GeV is the model-internal benchmark of a parameter range)
Kaplan 1992, Phys. Rev. Lett. 68:741 (early systematic asymmetric-DM development)

Petraki, Volkas 2013 'Review of asymmetric dark matter' Int. J. Mod. Phys. A 28:1330028 (canonical review compendium: m_DM benchmark ~5 GeV from Ω_DM/Ω_b coincidence, full canonical mass window O(0.1)-O(100) GeV in thermal-equilibrium scenarios depending on asymmetry-transfer mechanism)
Zurek 2014 'Asymmetric Dark Matter: Theories, Signatures, and Constraints' Phys. Rep. 537:91 (companion review compendium)

cells.content: Mirror dark matter — parallel sector of mirror baryons with mirror-baryogenesis
realized_examples: Limited direct testability — mirror-baryon-baryon kinetic mixing with photon (via U(1)_em - U(1)'_em) is constrained but limited. Mirror-DM scenarios remain theoretically motivated by particle-physics symmetry but lack distinctive predictions distinguishable from other hidden-sector DM.
cells.description: Parallel sector with full SM-like mirror gauge content, mirror baryogenesis, mirror BBN producing mirror-baryon dark matter. Less distinguishable from generic hidden-sector DM in practice. Status `viable-targeted` for the broadest scenarios; specific kinetic-mixing-coupled variants are constrained by dark-photon search bounds.
citations: Foot, Lew, Volkas 1991, Phys. Lett. B 272, 67 (mirror DM)
Foot 2014, Int. J. Mod. Phys. A 29, 1430013 (review)

Z. Berezhiani, D. Comelli, F. L. Villante 2001, Phys. Lett. B 503:362 (arXiv:hep-ph/0008105) — 'The early mirror universe: inflation, baryogenesis, nucleosynthesis and dark matter', BBN constraint requiring mirror-sector temperature ratio T'/T ≲ 0.5–0.6 from ΔN_eff bounds at primordial-nucleosynthesis epoch.

R. Foot 2014, Int. J. Mod. Phys. A 29:1430013 (arXiv:1401.3965) — 'Mirror dark matter: cosmology, galaxy structure and direct detection', comprehensive review confirming T'/T < ~0.5 as the canonical mirror-cosmology bound permitting mirror-baryogenesis-produced mirror-DM consistent with BBN and CMB.
PDG 2024 'Dark Matter' review article §28 — compilation of mirror-DM cosmological constraints.

cells.content: Dark photon as portal between SM and dark sector — eV-GeV mass with kinetic mixing ε ~ 10⁻³ - 10⁻⁹
realized_examples: Heavy Photon Search (HPS) at JLab, NA64 at CERN, beam-dump constraints (E137, NuCal, U70), DM search experiments interpreted in dark-photon framework. Parameter-space exclusion plots in (m_A', ε) plane have multi-decade reach across mass and kinetic-mixing scales.
Distinct from QCD-axion / ALP — dark photon is a vector (spin-1) mediator, not pseudoscalar.
cells.description: Spin-1 mediator coupling SM to dark sector via kinetic mixing of the form (ε/2) F^μν F'_μν. Couples electrically-charged SM particles to dark sector with effective coupling εe. Dark-photon-coupled DM can be thermally produced via freeze-in or freeze-out depending on parameters. Status `parameter-space-partly-excluded` — significant exclusion across (m_A', ε) but viable regions remain, especially at lower ε.
citations: Holdom 1986, Phys. Lett. B 166, 196 (kinetic mixing)
Pospelov 2009, Phys. Rev. D 80, 095002 (dark-photon DM mediator)
Battaglieri et al. 2017, arXiv:1707.04591 (US Cosmic Visions: New Ideas in Dark Matter)

Holdom 1986 'Two U(1)'s and ε charge shifts' Phys. Lett.
