Ahlers, G., Grossmann, S., Lohse, D. 2009 'Heat transfer and large scale dynamics in turbulent Rayleigh-Benard convection' Rev. Mod. Phys. 81:503-537 (comprehensive review of Rayleigh-Benard convection scaling; the asymptotic exponent 1/3 is the high-Ra theoretical canonical value, with empirical measurements in the band Nu ~ Ra^(0.28) to Nu ~ Ra^(0.32) at accessible Ra and progressive approach to 1/3 at higher Ra in modern experiments) [T13 cell-direct, T10 uncertainty=null, T26 rational-fraction encoding (1/3 encoded as decimal 0.3333333333333333 per established precedent); dimensionless-kind 52nd cell-direct anchor; bound_direction=two-sided per v19 spec - the literature names the asymptotic 1/3 as the theoretical canonical value, with empirical exponents at finite Ra clustering in the band 0.28-0.32; closes macroscopic-classical-phenomena cell [15] coverage gap; 8th and final entry of sub-PR 34 sweep]

predictive_yield.prediction: Kolmogorov K41 inertial-range energy spectrum E(k) ~ k^(-5/3) at high Reynolds numbers
predictive_yield.description: Kolmogorov 1941 dimensional prediction confirmed at Re_λ ~ 1000+ in DNS, laboratory, atmospheric, and oceanographic measurements. Spectral slope -1.67 with small multifractal intermittency corrections.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Kolmogorov 1941, Dokl. Akad. Nauk SSSR 30, 301
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Frisch 1995, Turbulence (Cambridge) summarizing decades of confirmation

predictive_yield.prediction: Pipe flow transition to turbulence at critical Reynolds number Re_c ~ 2300 (Reynolds 1883)
predictive_yield.description: Reynolds 1883 original observation refined by modern decay-vs-splitting analysis (Avila et al. 2011) → Re_c = 2040 ± 10.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Reynolds 1883, Phil. Trans. R. Soc. 174, 935
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Avila et al. 2011, Science 333, 192

predictive_yield.prediction: NIF inertial-confinement-fusion achieves Q > 1 ignition via hot-spot Lawson criterion
predictive_yield.description: Lindl 1995 ICF hot-spot ignition prediction confirmed by NIF December 5 2022 shot: Q = 1.54 (3.15 MJ from 2.05 MJ drive). First laboratory Q > 1 fusion.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Lawson 1957, Proc. Phys. Soc. B 70, 6 (Lawson criterion); Lindl 1995 hot-spot ICF
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Abu-Shawareb et al. (NIF) 2024, Phys. Rev. Lett. 132, 065102

predictive_yield.prediction: JET D-T plasma demonstrates substantial fusion energy yield in 5-second pulses
predictive_yield.description: JET 2021 DT campaign: 59 MJ fusion energy in 5-second pulse — > 2× the 1997 record. Validates predicted DT plasma performance scaling for ITER.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: ITER Physics Basis 2007 + JET DT predictions
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: JET Team 2022 DT 2021 results

predictive_yield.prediction: Fast magnetic reconnection rate ~ 0.1 c_A (Alfvén velocity) in collisionless plasma — Hall-reconnection / plasmoid-instability regime
predictive_yield.description: Petschek 1964 + Drake-Shay-Swisdak Hall-reconnection theory + plasmoid-instability theory (Loureiro et al. 2007) predicts fast reconnection rate. Confirmed in MMS spacecraft observations (Earth magnetotail) and MRX laboratory experiment.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Petschek 1964, NASA SP-50, 425; Drake, Shay 1998
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Burch et al. (MMS) 2016, Science 352, aaf2939

predictive_yield.prediction: Jarzynski equality ⟨e^(-βW)⟩ = e^(-βΔF) holds rigorously for arbitrary nonequilibrium protocols
predictive_yield.description: Jarzynski 1997 rigorous proof confirmed in single-molecule RNA-pulling experiments (Liphardt et al. 2002) and many subsequent verifications across systems.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Jarzynski 1997, Phys. Rev. Lett. 78, 2690
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Liphardt et al. 2002, Science 296, 1832

predictive_yield.prediction: Multifractal intermittency corrections to K41: structure-function scaling ζ_p deviates from p/3 at high p
predictive_yield.description: She-Lévêque multifractal model (1994) gives explicit form for ζ_p deviating from K41 prediction p/3. Confirmed in high-precision DNS + experimental velocity-structure-function measurements.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Frisch-Parisi multifractal model 1985; She, Lévêque 1994, Phys. Rev. Lett. 72, 336
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Many high-Re experimental + DNS confirmations

predictive_yield.prediction: Tokamak H-mode transition at sufficient heating power — characterized edge pedestal formation
predictive_yield.description: Wagner et al. ASDEX 1982 discovery of H-mode confinement regime confirmed in subsequent tokamaks (JET, DIII-D, EAST, etc.); standard operating mode underlying ITER design.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Wagner et al. 1982, Phys. Rev. Lett. 49, 1408
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Universal tokamak H-mode reproduction since 1982

predictive_yield.prediction: Vicsek-model self-propelled particles exhibit long-range orientational order in 2D — flocking transition
predictive_yield.description: Vicsek et al. 1995 model prediction confirmed in bacterial suspensions, colloidal active particles, microtubule-kinesin in vitro systems. Active matter breaks Mermin-Wagner equilibrium prohibition on 2D long-range order.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: Vicsek et al. 1995, Phys. Rev. Lett. 75, 1226
predictive_yield.confirmation_citation: Bricard et al. 2013, Nature 503, 95 (colloidal); many subsequent

predictive_yield.prediction: ITER will achieve fusion-power-gain Q ≥ 10 in burning-plasma regime
predictive_yield.description: ITER design target: 500 MW fusion from 50 MW heating in 400-second pulses. First plasma now targeted 2034+; DT operation ~2039+. The Q ≥ 10 prediction tests scaling of tokamak confinement + alpha-particle self-heating in burning plasma regime — not previously achieved.
predictive_yield.prediction_citation: ITER Physics Basis 2007, Nucl. Fusion 47

closure_status.description: Macroscopic-classical-physics observational programs span foundational scaling-law confirmations (Kolmogorov K41, Rayleigh-Bénard Nu-Ra, Reynolds-number transitions, Lawson criterion), recent transformative milestones (NIF Dec 2022 fusion ignition, JET 2021 DT plasma, MMS magnetic-reconnection diffusion-region direct observations, Parker Solar Probe near-Sun cascade measurements), and rigorous theoretical results (Jarzynski equality, Crooks fluctuation theorem). The classification is `partial` because: (i) ITER Q ≥ 10 burning-plasma demonstration awaits ~2039+; (ii) Navier-Stokes regularity remains open (Clay Millennium problem); (iii) ultimate-regime transition in Rayleigh-Bénard (Ra > 10^13) under active investigation; (iv) full classification of plasma-instability hierarchy in tokamaks ongoing; (v) active-matter / nonequilibrium phase transitions remain an open frontier. The sector spans broader phenomenology than any single FC can fully cover.

related_entries: electromagnetic-phenomena-across-energy-regimes (CELL-LEVEL OVERLAP: solar-flare reconnection produces EM emission across X-ray + EUV; tokamak diagnostics use EM probes)
compact-astrophysical-objects (CELL-LEVEL OVERLAP: MHD jet physics applies to AGN / microquasar jet emission; reconnection in pulsar wind nebulae)
cosmological-observations (large-scale baryonic plasma physics in cosmological contexts; Parker Solar Probe measurements provide stellar-wind benchmark)
PLANNED FUTURE PHENOMENON-FCs: turbulence-cascade-precision-data; tokamak-instability-classification-detailed; nonequilibrium-stat-mech-detailed — would deepen specific subdomains

Marchetti et al. 2013, Rev. Mod. Phys. 85, 1143 (active matter review)

nodes.label: DUNE
nodes.full_name: Deep Underground Neutrino Experiment
nodes.description: Long-baseline neutrino-oscillation experiment combining a high-intensity neutrino beam from Fermilab's Long-Baseline Neutrino Facility with four 17-kt liquid-argon time-projection chambers at the Sanford Underground Research Facility (SURF) in South Dakota, 1300 km from the source. Primary physics targets: determination of the leptonic CP-violating phase $\delta_{CP}$, resolution of the neutrino mass ordering, precision $\theta_{23}$ octant determination, nucleon-decay searches in the SUSY-favoured $p \to \bar\nu K^+$ channel where Hyper-Kamiokande has weaker sensitivity, and supernova-neutrino burst detection. Far-detector construction at SURF is underway; near-detector design is finalized; beam operations projected for the late 2020s.
key_publications: DUNE Collaboration 2020 'Deep Underground Neutrino Experiment (DUNE) Far Detector Technical Design Report Volume I: Introduction to DUNE' arXiv:2002.02967
DUNE Collaboration 2020 'DUNE Far Detector TDR Volume II: Physics, Strategy, and Operations' arXiv:2002.03005
Abi et al. (DUNE Collaboration) 2020 'Long-baseline neutrino oscillation physics potential of the DUNE experiment' EPJ C 80:978
Abi et al. (DUNE Collaboration) 2021 'Prospects for beyond the Standard Model physics searches at DUNE' EPJ C 81:322
citations: DUNE Collaboration 2020 'DUNE Far Detector TDR' arXiv:2002.02967 (Vol I), arXiv:2002.03005 (Vol II), arXiv:2002.02980 (Vol III), arXiv:2002.03010 (Vol IV)
Abi et al. 2020 'Long-baseline neutrino oscillation physics potential of the DUNE experiment' EPJ C 80:978

nodes.label: Hyper-Kamiokande
nodes.full_name: Hyper-Kamiokande
nodes.description: Next-generation 258-kt fiducial water-Cherenkov detector under construction in the Tochibora mine in Kamioka, Japan, with an upgraded J-PARC neutrino beam (1.3 MW) and a 295-km baseline from Tokai. Successor to Super-Kamiokande. Primary physics targets: leptonic CP-violation phase $\delta_{CP}$, mass-ordering determination via atmospheric neutrinos, proton-decay search in the $p \to e^+ \pi^0$ channel (projected partial lifetime sensitivity $\sim 10^{35}$ yr — the benchmark discriminator for minimal SU(5)-class grand unification), and high-statistics supernova-burst detection. Construction began 2020; first beam projected ~2027.
host_institutions: Institute for Cosmic Ray Research (ICRR), University of Tokyo
key_publications: Hyper-Kamiokande Proto-Collaboration 2018 'Hyper-Kamiokande Design Report' arXiv:1805.04163
Abe et al. (Hyper-Kamiokande Collaboration) 2018 'Physics potentials with the second Hyper-Kamiokande detector in Korea' PTEP 2018:063C01
Hyper-Kamiokande Collaboration 2022 'Snowmass Neutrino Frontier: Hyper-Kamiokande Contribution' arXiv:2203.02029
citations: Hyper-Kamiokande Proto-Collaboration 2018 'Hyper-Kamiokande Design Report' arXiv:1805.04163
Abe et al. 2018 'Physics potentials with the second Hyper-Kamiokande detector in Korea' PTEP 2018:063C01

nodes.label: JUNO
nodes.full_name: Jiangmen Underground Neutrino Observatory
nodes.description: 20-kt liquid-scintillator detector at 53 km baseline from the Yangjiang and Taishan reactor complexes in Guangdong, China, designed for sub-percent energy resolution on reactor antineutrinos. Primary physics target: resolution of the neutrino mass ordering at $\gtrsim 3\sigma$ via the interference of the solar ($\Delta m^2_{21}$) and atmospheric ($\Delta m^2_{31}$, $\Delta m^2_{32}$) oscillation frequencies in the survival probability — a measurement independent of beam-based experiments and matter-effect modelling. Secondary targets: precision $\sin^2 2\theta_{12}$, $\Delta m^2_{21}$, $|\Delta m^2_{31}|$; geo-neutrino flux; supernova and solar neutrinos; sterile-neutrino searches. Filling completed late 2024; physics operations underway.
host_institutions: Institute of High Energy Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (IHEP-CAS)
key_publications: An et al. (JUNO Collaboration) 2016 'Neutrino Physics with JUNO' J. Phys. G 43:030401
JUNO Collaboration 2022 'JUNO Physics and Detector' Progress in Particle and Nuclear Physics 123:103927
JUNO Collaboration 2024 'Real-time monitoring of the Sun's neutrinos with JUNO' Chin. Phys. C 48:043003

nodes.label: LIGO/Virgo/KAGRA + CE/ET
nodes.full_name: Ground-based gravitational-wave detector network (LIGO + Virgo + KAGRA, Cosmic Explorer, Einstein Telescope)
nodes.description: International ground-based interferometric gravitational-wave detector network covering the $\sim 10\,\mathrm{Hz}$–$10\,\mathrm{kHz}$ band. Current generation (Advanced LIGO at Hanford and Livingston, Advanced Virgo at Cascina, KAGRA in Kamioka) is deployed and accumulating compact-binary detections (>90 confident events through O3, ongoing O4 observation run). Next-generation upgrades — Cosmic Explorer (US, 40 km arms) and Einstein Telescope (Europe, triangular underground 10 km arms) — are in design and planned for the mid-2030s with order-of-magnitude strain-sensitivity improvement. Physics targets: black-hole ringdown spectroscopy (Kerr no-hair / area-theorem tests), neutron-star equation-of-state via tidal deformability, stochastic background searches, multi-messenger transients.

nodes.label: LISA
nodes.full_name: Laser Interferometer Space Antenna
nodes.description: Space-based gravitational-wave observatory consisting of three spacecraft in heliocentric orbit forming an equilateral triangle of 2.5-million-km arm length, sensitive to the $\sim 10^{-4}$–$10^{-1}\,\mathrm{Hz}$ band. ESA-led mission with NASA contribution, adopted by ESA in January 2024; launch projected for the mid-2030s. Physics targets inaccessible to ground-based detectors: massive-black-hole mergers ($10^4$–$10^7 M_\odot$) across cosmic time including high-redshift seed-formation tests, extreme-mass-ratio inspirals (EMRIs) as precision Kerr-geometry probes, stellar-origin BH binaries detected $\sim\!10^4$ yr before LIGO-band coalescence, Galactic compact-binary population, and potential stochastic backgrounds from cosmological sources (cosmic strings, first-order phase transitions, primordial gravitational waves from inflation).
key_publications: Amaro-Seoane et al. (LISA Consortium) 2017 'Laser Interferometer Space Antenna' arXiv:1702.00786
Amaro-Seoane et al. 2023 'Astrophysics with the Laser Interferometer Space Antenna' Living Rev. Relativ. 26:2
LISA Consortium 2024 'LISA Definition Study Report' arXiv:2402.07571 (ESA-SCI(2024)1)
Auclair et al. (LISA Cosmology Working Group) 2023 'Cosmology with the Laser Interferometer Space Antenna' Living Rev. Relativ. 26:5
citations: Amaro-Seoane et al. 2017 'Laser Interferometer Space Antenna' arXiv:1702.00786
LISA Consortium 2024 'LISA Definition Study Report' arXiv:2402.07571

nodes.label: CMB-S4 + LiteBIRD + SO
nodes.full_name: Stage-4 CMB programme (CMB-S4, LiteBIRD, Simons Observatory)
nodes.description: Coordinated programme of next-generation cosmic-microwave-background experiments designed to measure polarization B-modes at unprecedented sensitivity. Simons Observatory at Cerro Toco (Chile) is in deployment with first light in 2024. CMB-S4 is a ground-based US programme planned for the late 2020s with detector arrays at the South Pole and Cerro Toco; LiteBIRD is a JAXA-led satellite mission targeting a 2032 launch. Combined sensitivity targets: tensor-to-scalar ratio $r$ down to $\sim 10^{-3}$ (testing the canonical predictions of single-field slow-roll inflation), $N_\mathrm{eff}$ to ${\sim}0.03$ (probing relativistic species beyond the SM), $\sum m_\nu$ via lensing of the temperature and polarization spectra, primordial non-Gaussianity, and constraints on dark-matter–baryon scattering.
key_publications: Abazajian et al. (CMB-S4 Collaboration) 2016 'CMB-S4 Science Book, First Edition' arXiv:1610.02743
Abazajian et al. (CMB-S4 Collaboration) 2022 'Snowmass 2021 CMB-S4 White Paper' arXiv:2203.08024

Hazumi et al. (LiteBIRD Collaboration) 2022 'LiteBIRD satellite: JAXA's new strategic L-class mission for all-sky surveys of cosmic microwave background polarization' PTEP 2023:042F01
citations: Abazajian et al. 2016 'CMB-S4 Science Book' arXiv:1610.02743
Ade et al. 2019 'Simons Observatory: Science goals and forecasts' JCAP 02:056
Hazumi et al. 2022 'LiteBIRD satellite' PTEP 2023:042F01

nodes.label: Vera C. Rubin Observatory (LSST)
nodes.full_name: Vera C. Rubin Observatory — Legacy Survey of Space and Time (LSST)
nodes.description: 8.4-m wide-field optical survey telescope at Cerro Pachón, Chile, conducting the 10-year Legacy Survey of Space and Time over $\sim 18{,}000\,\mathrm{deg}^2$ of the southern sky in six bands. First light achieved 2024; survey operations began 2025. Primary cosmological targets: dark-energy equation-of-state ($w_0, w_a$) via weak-lensing tomography, galaxy clustering, photometric supernovae, and cluster abundance; dark-matter substructure via strong-lensing flux-ratio anomalies and stellar streams; transient and variable-source astrophysics; Solar System inventory. Each part of the sky is visited $\sim\!825$ times over the survey.
key_publications: LSST Science Collaboration 2009 'LSST Science Book, Version 2.0' arXiv:0912.0201
Ivezić et al. 2019 'LSST: From Science Drivers to Reference Design and Anticipated Data Products' ApJ 873:111
The LSST Dark Energy Science Collaboration 2018 'The LSST Dark Energy Science Collaboration (DESC) Science Requirements Document' arXiv:1809.01669
citations: LSST Science Collaboration 2009 'LSST Science Book, Version 2.0' arXiv:0912.0201
Ivezić et al. 2019 'LSST: From Science Drivers to Reference Design and Anticipated Data Products' ApJ 873:111

nodes.label: DESI
nodes.full_name: Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument
nodes.description: 5000-fibre multi-object spectrograph on the 4-m Mayall Telescope at Kitt Peak, Arizona, surveying $\sim 40$ million galaxy and quasar redshifts over a 5-year programme ($\sim 14{,}000\,\mathrm{deg}^2$). Operations began 2021. Primary cosmological targets: baryon-acoustic-oscillation (BAO) standard ruler at sub-percent precision across redshift bins from $z \sim 0.1$ to $z \sim 3.5$ (Lyman-$\alpha$ forest), redshift-space-distortion measurements of growth, and constraints on the dark-energy equation-of-state. The DESI Year-1 BAO release (2024) reports a preference for an evolving equation-of-state $(w_0, w_a)$ over a cosmological constant at ${\sim}2.6\sigma$ when combined with CMB and SNIa data — a current empirical tension with $\Lambda\mathrm{CDM}$.
key_publications: DESI Collaboration (Levi et al.) 2013 'The DESI Experiment, a whitepaper for Snowmass 2013' arXiv:1308.0847
DESI Collaboration 2016 'The DESI Experiment Part I: Science, Targeting, and Survey Design' arXiv:1611.00036

nodes.label: Axion haloscope network
nodes.full_name: Axion haloscope network (ADMX, HAYSTAC, MADMAX, ABRACADABRA, DMRadio)
nodes.description: Programme of cavity- and broadband-haloscope experiments searching for galactic-halo dark-matter axions across the $\sim\!\mathrm{neV}$–$\mathrm{meV}$ mass band, with sensitivity targets reaching the QCD-axion benchmark couplings (KSVZ and DFSZ predictions). ADMX at the University of Washington operates resonant microwave cavities in the few-$\mu$eV range and has reached KSVZ/DFSZ sensitivity. HAYSTAC at Yale extends to higher masses via squeezed-state readout. MADMAX (Munich/DESY) uses dielectric haloscopes for the $40$–$400\,\mu\mathrm{eV}$ range. ABRACADABRA at MIT and DMRadio at SLAC pursue broadband lumped-element approaches at masses below $\sim 1\,\mu\mathrm{eV}$. The programme directly tests the Peccei-Quinn solution to the strong-CP problem and contributes axion-as-dark-matter constraints.
host_institutions: Max Planck Institute for Physics / DESY (MADMAX)
key_publications: Du et al. (ADMX Collaboration) 2018 'A Search for Invisible Axion Dark Matter with the Axion Dark Matter Experiment' Phys. Rev. Lett. 120:151301
Backes et al. (HAYSTAC Collaboration) 2021 'A quantum-enhanced search for dark matter axions' Nature 590:238
Brun et al. (MADMAX Working Group) 2019 'A new experimental approach to probe QCD axion dark matter in the mass range above 40 μeV' EPJ C 79:186
Salemi et al. (ABRACADABRA-10cm) 2021 'Search for Low-Mass Axion Dark Matter with ABRACADABRA-10 cm' Phys. Rev. Lett. 127:081801
Brouwer et al. (DMRadio Collaboration) 2022 'Projected sensitivity of DMRadio-m3: A search for the QCD axion below 1 μeV' Phys. Rev. D 106:103008

nodes.label: Next-gen EDM searches
nodes.full_name: Next-generation electric-dipole-moment searches (nEDM/n2EDM at PSI, ACME, HfF⁺ at JILA)
nodes.description: Programme of next-generation searches for permanent electric dipole moments of the neutron and of electrons (probed via polar paramagnetic molecules), where any nonzero signal would establish T-violation (and via CPT, CP-violation) at levels far beyond CKM-induced predictions. n2EDM at PSI is the successor to the nEDM apparatus and targets neutron-EDM sensitivity below $10^{-27}\,e\cdot\mathrm{cm}$. ACME (Northwestern/Harvard/Yale) has set the leading electron-EDM bound using ThO; ACME-III aims for further reduction. The JILA HfF⁺ experiment (Cornell/JILA) provides an independent and competitive electron-EDM bound using trapped molecular ions. The combined programme bears on the strong-CP problem (via $\bar\theta$ constraints from neutron EDM) and the matter-antimatter asymmetry (via new sources of CP violation).
key_publications: Abel et al. (nEDM Collaboration) 2020 'Measurement of the Permanent Electric Dipole Moment of the Neutron' Phys. Rev. Lett. 124:081803
Ayres et al. (n2EDM Collaboration) 2021 'The design of the n2EDM experiment' EPJ C 81:512
Andreev et al. (ACME Collaboration) 2018 'Improved limit on the electric dipole moment of the electron' Nature 562:355
Roussy et al. 2023 'An improved bound on the electron's electric dipole moment' Science 381:46
citations: Abel et al. 2020 'Measurement of the Permanent Electric Dipole Moment of the Neutron' PRL 124:081803
Andreev et al. 2018 'Improved limit on the electric dipole moment of the electron' Nature 562:355
Roussy et al. 2023 'An improved bound on the electron's electric dipole moment' Science 381:46

nodes.label: Muon g-2 continuation
nodes.full_name: Muon g-2 continuation (Fermilab E989 final analysis + J-PARC E34)
nodes.description: Programme of follow-on measurements of the muon anomalous magnetic moment $a_\mu = (g_\mu - 2)/2$ aimed at resolving the muon-g-2 totality-approach. The Fermilab E989 experiment has measured $a_\mu$ in the storage-ring configuration inherited from BNL E821 across multiple physics runs (Run-1 published 2021, Run-2/3 published 2023, final combined Run-1–6 published 2025 at 127 ppb design-completion precision [Aguillard et al. 2025 PRL 135:101802, arXiv:2506.03069]). J-PARC E34 (under construction at the J-PARC Materials and Life Science Experimental Facility) uses an independent technique — a thermal ultra-cold muon source accelerated to 300 MeV and stored in a compact magnetic ring without electric focusing — which eliminates the dominant electric-field-correction systematic of the storage-ring method and is therefore a systematics-independent cross-check. Combined with continued progress on lattice-QCD HVP and on dispersive $e^+e^- \to$ hadrons inputs, the programme is the empirical path to resolving the current discrepancy between Theory Initiative dispersive HVP and BMW lattice HVP.
key_publications: Abi et al. (Muon g-2 Collaboration) 2021 'Measurement of the Positive Muon Anomalous Magnetic Moment to 0.46 ppm' Phys. Rev. Lett. 126:141801
Aguillard et al. (Muon g-2 Collaboration) 2023 'Measurement of the Positive Muon Anomalous Magnetic Moment to 0.20 ppm' Phys. Rev. Lett. 131:161802
Abe et al. (J-PARC E34 Collaboration) 2019 'A new approach for measuring the muon anomalous magnetic moment and electric dipole moment' PTEP 2019:053C02
citations: Aguillard et al. (Muon g-2 Collaboration) 2025 'Measurement of the positive muon anomalous magnetic moment to 127 ppb' Phys Rev Lett 135:101802 (arXiv:2506.03069)
Aguillard et al. 2023 'Measurement of the Positive Muon Anomalous Magnetic Moment to 0.20 ppm' PRL 131:161802

nodes.label: FCC
nodes.full_name: Future Circular Collider (FCC-ee and FCC-hh)
nodes.description: Proposed 91-km circular collider complex at CERN, comprising an initial high-luminosity electron-positron stage (FCC-ee, $\sqrt{s} \sim 90$–$365\,\mathrm{GeV}$) for precision electroweak, Higgs, top, and flavour measurements, followed by a 100-TeV proton-proton stage (FCC-hh) for direct production of physics beyond the Standard Model. Conceptual Design Reports published 2019; mid-term feasibility-study report submitted to CERN Council in 2024; investment decision targeted for 2027–28. Primary physics targets: hierarchy-problem at energy scales well beyond LHC (TeV-decade reach for composite-Higgs, SUSY, and Z' states), Higgs self-coupling $\lambda_3$ to $\sim\!5\%$, EW precision to $10^{-5}$, flavour-changing neutral currents, and direct searches for dark-matter mediators in the TeV-decade range. Projected first FCC-ee beam in the mid-2040s; FCC-hh follows in the 2070s.
key_publications: Abada et al. (FCC Collaboration) 2019 'FCC-ee: The Lepton Collider — Future Circular Collider Conceptual Design Report Volume 2' EPJ ST 228:261
Abada et al. (FCC Collaboration) 2019 'FCC-hh: The Hadron Collider — Future Circular Collider Conceptual Design Report Volume 3' EPJ ST 228:755
Abada et al. (FCC Collaboration) 2019 'HE-LHC: The High-Energy Large Hadron Collider — FCC CDR Volume 4' EPJ ST 228:1109
Mangano et al. 2017 'Physics at a 100 TeV pp collider: Standard Model processes' CERN-2017-003-M arXiv:1607.01831
citations: Abada et al. 2019 'FCC-ee Conceptual Design Report' EPJ ST 228:261
Abada et al. 2019 'FCC-hh Conceptual Design Report' EPJ ST 228:755

edges.description: The ten-fold way fully solves the free-fermion sub-domain of the topological-phases-classification frontier. The remaining open content of the frontier — interacting phases, fractional / symmetry-enriched topological order, classification beyond K-theory — lies outside the ten-fold way's domain of applicability. The bears-on is partially-solves rather than constrains because within the free-fermion sub-domain the classification is genuinely closure-grade.
citations: Fidkowski-Kitaev 2010, Phys. Rev. B 81, 134509 (ℤ → ℤ₈ collapse for interacting BDI-d1, demarcating the ten-fold way's reach)
Senthil 2015, Annu. Rev. Condens. Matter Phys. 6, 299 (review of interacting SPT phases beyond ten-fold-way reach)

edges.description: QFT (specifically the many-body / condensed-matter sub-domain) invokes the ten-fold way as the standard classification machinery for free-fermion topological phases. The classification is single-particle / quadratic-Hamiltonian level and uses QFT's BdG / single-particle formalism rather than QFT's interacting machinery; this is the same architectural reach-limit that the topological-phases-classification frontier records on the v13 side.
citations: Chiu, Teo, Schnyder, Ryu 2016, Rev. Mod. Phys. 88, 035005

edges.description: The Higgs cell (1, 2, +1/2) at gauge rep but with a scalar mass parameter that is quadratically sensitive to UV physics. The SM rep content is what makes the hierarchy problem visible — the Higgs is the only fundamental scalar in the SM, and the rep content provides no symmetry protecting its mass. The bears-on is structural-only: the rep content does not solve the hierarchy problem; it specifies the structural arena in which the problem appears.
citations: Susskind 1979, Phys. Rev. D 20, 2619 (formulation of the hierarchy problem)
't Hooft 1980, in Recent Developments in Gauge Theories, Plenum (naturalness criterion)

edges.description: The fermion sector of the rep content has three generations with identical gauge representations but vastly different Yukawa couplings (mass ratios spanning six orders of magnitude in the charged-lepton sector, more in the quark sector). The rep content's structural assertion that the three generations have identical reps is what makes the flavor puzzle a puzzle — it removes any gauge-theoretic explanation for the mass hierarchy, leaving the Yukawa values unexplained. Nature: structural-only.
citations: Particle Data Group, Review of Particle Physics (mass data)
Ramond 1999, Journeys Beyond the Standard Model, Westview (review of the flavor puzzle)

edges.description: The gluon cell (8, 1, 0) at SU(3)_c gauge representation admits a θ-term θ G G̃ in the QCD Lagrangian, which is gauge-invariant, renormalization-stable, and CP-violating. The rep content does not constrain θ to be small; experimentally |θ| < 10⁻¹⁰ from EDM bounds, with no SM structural reason. Nature: structural-only.
citations: 't Hooft 1976, Phys. Rev. Lett. 37, 8 (the θ-term)
Peccei, Quinn 1977, Phys. Rev. Lett. 38, 1440 (axion as candidate resolution; outside the rep content)

edges.description: The CKM matrix arises from the misalignment of Q_L Yukawa couplings to u_R and d_R across generations; with three generations the CKM matrix admits one physical CP-violating phase (Kobayashi-Maskawa 1973). This provides one of Sakharov's three baryogenesis conditions (CP violation). However: the magnitude of CKM CP violation is insufficient by ~10 orders of magnitude to generate the observed baryon asymmetry under electroweak baryogenesis scenarios, and the SM's electroweak phase transition is a crossover rather than first-order at the Higgs mass observed (125 GeV). Nature: partially-solves — provides one Sakharov ingredient, but insufficient overall.
citations: Sakharov 1967, JETP Lett. 5, 24 (three conditions for baryogenesis)
Kobayashi, Maskawa 1973 (CKM CP violation from three-generation rep content)
Kajantie, Laine, Rummukainen, Shaposhnikov 1996, Phys. Rev. Lett. 77, 2887 (EW crossover, not first-order at observed Higgs mass)

edges.description: The SM rep content includes no electrically-neutral, color-neutral, weakly-interacting, stable, cosmologically-abundant particle that could account for dark matter. Neutrinos are too light to give cold dark matter and too weakly-bound by structure formation; no other SM cell qualifies. Nature: constrains — the rep content rules out all SM particles as dark-matter candidates, forcing the dark-matter sector to lie outside the SM rep content. This is one of the strongest empirical arguments for physics beyond the SM.
citations: Planck Collaboration 2020, Astron. Astrophys. 641, A6 (Ω_DM constraint)
Bertone, Hooper, Silk 2005, Phys. Rep. 405, 279 (review of why SM cannot host DM)

edges.description: The SM rep content includes no graviton multiplet and the gauge group G_SM does not include a gravitational sector. The classification's domain of applicability explicitly excludes gravitational interactions. Nature: structural-only — the rep content does not bear on the QG frontier by providing content, only by explicitly delimiting its non-coverage. Included as a bears-on edge to record the delimiting relation, not a substantive constraint.

QFT (specifically the gauge-theory sub-domain at energies up to the EW scale) uses the SM rep content as its primary specification of matter, force, and scalar content. The rep content is the structural input QFT's gauge-theory machinery operates on; QFT's apparatus (gauge invariance, renormalization, anomaly cancellation, Yukawa couplings, EWSB via the Higgs mechanism) is the apparatus whose application to this specific rep content produces the empirically-tested SM. The uses relation is internal-to-QFT rather than cross-architecture.
citations: Weinberg 1996, The Quantum Theory of Fields, Vol. II
Peskin, Schroeder 1995, An Introduction to Quantum Field Theory

edges.description: PDG curates and maintains the SM rep content table at whole-classification scope; no cell_refs (the relation is to the classification overall, not to any specific cell). The Review of Particle Physics is the canonical empirical anchor for every cell's experimental status, masses, decay modes, and cross sections.
citations: Workman et al. (Particle Data Group) 2022, Prog. Theor. Exp. Phys. 2022, 083C01

edges.description: ATLAS and CMS jointly discovered the Higgs boson in 2012, establishing the Higgs cell (1, 2, +1/2) of the SM rep content empirically. The discovery completed the SM table after 48 years (Englert-Brout-Higgs 1964 → ATLAS+CMS 2012).
citations: ATLAS Collaboration 2012, Phys. Lett. B 716, 1
CMS Collaboration 2012, Phys. Lett. B 716, 30

edges.description: UA1 and UA2 jointly discovered the W± (1983) and Z (1983) bosons, establishing the SU(2)_L × U(1)_Y gauge sector of the SM rep content empirically. The discovered W and Z are the post-EWSB mass eigenstates; the cell_refs point to the pre-EWSB W and B cells of the rep content. The discharge_status is partial since the gauge structure is established, but the symmetry-breaking mechanism's full content was confirmed only with the Higgs discovery in 2012.
citations: Arnison et al. (UA1) 1983, Phys. Lett. B 122, 103
Bagnaia et al. (UA2) 1983, Phys. Lett. B 129, 130

edges.description: CDF and D0 jointly discovered the top quark in 1995, establishing the third-generation up-type quark cell of the SM rep content. The discovery confirmed the Kobayashi-Maskawa prediction (1973) of a third-generation up-type quark required by CKM CP-violation structure.
citations: Abe et al. (CDF) 1995, Phys. Rev. Lett. 74, 2626
Abachi et al. (D0) 1995, Phys. Rev. Lett. 74, 2632

edges.description: DONUT directly observed tau neutrino interactions in 2000, completing the empirical confirmation of the third-generation lepton doublet (ν_τ_L, τ_L). The tau lepton component of the cell had been established in 1975 (Perl et al. discovery of tau); DONUT's tau-neutrino observation closed the cell.
citations: Kodama et al. (DONUT) 2001, Phys. Lett. B 504, 218

edges.description: Joint Brookhaven (Ting) + SLAC (Richter) discovery of the J/ψ meson in 1974 established the existence of the charm quark, confirming the GIM prediction (1970) of a second-generation up-type quark required by the four-quark electroweak structure. The 'November Revolution' confirmation triggered rapid adoption of the four-flavor Standard Model.
citations: Aubert et al. 1974, Phys. Rev. Lett. 33, 1404
Augustin et al. 1974, Phys. Rev. Lett. 33, 1406

edges.description: Fermilab E288 (Lederman group) discovered the Upsilon (Υ), a bottomonium bound state, in 1977, establishing the existence of the bottom quark. The discovery confirmed the Kobayashi-Maskawa prediction (1973) of a third-generation down-type quark required by the CKM CP-violation structure. The companion top-quark prediction was confirmed 18 years later by CDF+D0.
citations: Herb et al. (E288) 1977, Phys. Rev. Lett. 39, 252

edges.description: QFT uses ADE Lie algebras extensively: as gauge groups (SU(N) = A_{N-1}; SO(2N) = D_N; E_6, E_7, E_8 GUT and string-theoretic gauge groups), as global symmetry algebras, and as the chiral algebra of 2D WZW conformal field theories.
citations: Goddard, Olive 1985, Int. J. Mod. Phys. A 1, 303

edges.description: McKay correspondence: each finite subgroup Γ ⊂ SU(2) has a McKay quiver built from tensor products of the defining 2-dim rep with all irreps of Γ; the McKay quiver is the affine ADE Dynkin diagram of the corresponding Lie algebra. The bijection runs symmetrically: given an ADE Lie algebra, the corresponding SU(2) subgroup is recovered as the binary polyhedral group of matching ADE type. Canonical direction (subgroups → Lie algebras) reflects McKay's original construction direction.
citations: McKay 1980, in Proc. Sympos. Pure Math. 37, 183

axis_mapping.description: McKay correspondence: the ADE Cartan type of a finite SU(2) subgroup matches the ADE Cartan type of the Lie algebra pointwise (A-series ↔ A-series, D-series ↔ D-series, E_6 ↔ E_6, E_7 ↔ E_7, E_8 ↔ E_8). The McKay quiver of the subgroup is the affine Dynkin diagram of the matching Lie algebra.

edges.description: Brieskorn-Grothendieck: resolution of a Du Val singularity attaches a configuration of (-2)-curves on a non-singular replacement; the intersection graph of these curves is the ADE Dynkin diagram. The Lie algebra structure (Dynkin diagram, root system) is read off the singularity. Per v15 spec convention, 'A derives-from B' means A's structure is read off B; here the Lie algebra (A) is derived from the singularity (B).
citations: Brieskorn 1966, Math. Ann. 166, 76
Brieskorn 1968, Actes Congr. Internat. Math. Nice 2, 279
Slodowy 1980, Simple Singularities and Simple Algebraic Groups (LNM 815)

edges.description: Quotient construction: each Du Val singularity is realized as ℂ²/Γ where Γ ⊂ SU(2) is the corresponding ADE finite subgroup; the singularity is derived from the group action. Direction: 'ade-du-val derives-from ade-su2-subgroups' since the singularity's structure is read off the group.
citations: Klein 1884
Slodowy 1980, LNM 815

edges.description: Gabriel's theorem: connected representation-finite quivers are exactly the ADE quivers (those with ADE underlying graph). Moreover, indecomposable representations of an ADE quiver are in bijection with positive roots of the corresponding ADE root system. The bijection is symmetric: given a Lie algebra, the corresponding quiver is the underlying Dynkin diagram with any orientation; given a representation-finite quiver, its underlying graph is the Dynkin diagram.
citations: Gabriel 1972, Manuscripta Mathematica 6, 71
Bernstein, Gelfand, Ponomarev 1973, Russian Math. Surveys 28, 17

axis_mapping.description: Gabriel's theorem: the ADE Cartan type of a representation-finite connected quiver matches the ADE Cartan type of the Lie algebra whose Dynkin diagram is the quiver's underlying graph; indecomposable representations are in bijection with positive roots of that Lie algebra.

edges.description: Cappelli-Itzykson-Zuber classification: each ADE Cartan type corresponds to a modular invariant of the SU(2)_k WZW model (with appropriate level matching). A-series at all levels; D-series at even levels; E_6/E_7/E_8 at sporadic levels 10, 16, 28. The bijection is between Cartan types and modular-invariant structural patterns; the level axis adds a parametric refinement.
citations: Cappelli, Itzykson, Zuber 1987, Nucl. Phys. B 280, 445
Cappelli, Itzykson, Zuber 1987, Commun. Math. Phys. 113, 1

axis_mapping.description: Cappelli-Itzykson-Zuber classification: the ADE Cartan type of an SU(2)_k WZW modular invariant matches the ADE Cartan type of the simply-laced Lie algebra whose Coxeter number equals k+2. The level axis on ade-modular-invariants has no counterpart axis on ade-lie-algebras and is therefore not mapped.

edges.description: Bridgeland-King-Reid: derived equivalence between coherent sheaves on the minimal resolution of a Du Val singularity and representations of the McKay quiver of the corresponding finite SU(2) subgroup. The equivalence is functorial and runs symmetrically; canonical direction reflects the original 2001 result's statement.
citations: Bridgeland, King, Reid 2001, J. Amer. Math. Soc. 14, 535
Kapranov, Vasserot 2000, Math. Ann. 316, 565

axis_mapping.description: Bridgeland-King-Reid / Kapranov-Vasserot equivalence: the ADE Cartan type of a Du Val singularity matches the ADE Cartan type of the McKay quiver of the corresponding finite SU(2) subgroup; the derived equivalence D^b(Coh(X̃)) ≃ D^b(Rep(Q)) is induced pointwise on Cartan types.

edges.description: McKay quiver: each finite SU(2) subgroup Γ has a canonical quiver (the McKay quiver) whose vertices are irreps of Γ and edges record the tensor decomposition of the defining 2-dim rep ⊗ V_i; the McKay quiver of a finite SU(2) subgroup is the affine ADE quiver. Distinct from the Gabriel-quiver case (ade-quivers) which uses finite ADE quivers; the McKay quiver is the affine version.
citations: McKay 1980, in Proc. Sympos. Pure Math. 37, 183

axis_mapping.description: McKay quiver construction: the ADE Cartan type of a finite SU(2) subgroup matches the ADE Cartan type of its McKay quiver. The McKay quiver is the affine ADE quiver of type T; the corresponding cell on ade-quivers (which records finite ADE quivers) shares the cartan-type label T.

edges.description: Derivable from the chain (ade-su2-subgroups → ade-lie-algebras via McKay; ade-lie-algebras → ade-modular-invariants via CIZ). The composite relation is real but not primitive; for visual purposes it can be omitted in favor of the two primary edges that compose to it.
Derivable from the chain (ade-du-val → ade-lie-algebras via resolution/Brieskorn; ade-lie-algebras → ade-modular-invariants via CIZ).
Derivable from the chain (ade-quivers → ade-lie-algebras via Gabriel; ade-lie-algebras → ade-modular-invariants via CIZ).

The QG frontier sits structurally between Poincaré (the symmetry of low-energy flat-spacetime physics) and Diff (the gauge group of GR). The QG frontier asks which spacetime symmetry holds at the Planck scale and how the two reconcile.

The cosmological-constant frontier is structurally the question of which constant-curvature spacetime nature realizes: dS, AdS, or Minkowski. The symmetry-groups classification names the three but does not predict which.
citations: Weinberg 1989, Rev. Mod. Phys. 61, 1

edges.description: AdS/CFT depends on the group-level isomorphism SO(d-1,2) ≃ Conf(d-2,1). The bears-on is structural-only: the classification names the isomorphism but does not solve the paradox.
citations: Maldacena 1998

edges.description: QFT (relativistic) is built on the Poincaré-invariance axiom; unitary irreps of Poincaré (Wigner 1939) classify the asymptotic particle content. Conformal field theory (cell-stsym-conformal) is a sub-domain of QFT using the conformal symmetry-type. v15 retrofit: cell_refs now structurally pin the relevant cells.
citations: Streater, Wightman 1964, PCT, Spin and Statistics, and All That, Benjamin
Haag 1992, Local Quantum Physics, Springer

edges.description: GR uses the general-coordinate (Diff) symmetry as its gauge group; the Einstein-Hilbert action is diffeomorphism-invariant. v15 retrofit: cell_refs pins the Diff cell.
citations: Wald 1984, General Relativity, University of Chicago Press

edges.description: Stat-mech uses Galilean invariance for non-relativistic systems and conformal invariance at critical points (BPZ 1984 and successors). v15 retrofit: cell_refs pin both cells.
citations: Cardy 1996, Scaling and Renormalization in Statistical Physics, Cambridge

edges.description: The simple components of the finite-dimensional spacetime symmetry groups are real forms of complex simple Lie algebras of ADE type. Lorentz so(3,1) ≃ sl(2,ℂ)_ℝ is a real form of A_1 × A_1; conformal so(2,4) is a real form of A_3 = D_3 = sl(4)/so(6); de Sitter so(4,1) and anti-de Sitter so(3,2) are real forms of D-type complex algebras. The relation does NOT apply to the Galilean cell (different algebraic structure), the 2D-conformal sub-case (Virasoro is infinite-dimensional, not ADE), or the Diff cell (also infinite-dimensional). cell_refs pins the source-side cells where the relation applies; this convention (cell_refs as source-side pins on cross-classification edges) is documented in v15.1 findings as an open spec clarification for v15.2.
citations: Helgason 2001, Differential Geometry, Lie Groups, and Symmetric Spaces (AMS) — real forms of complex Lie algebras
Hsiang 1975, Cohomology Theory of Topological Transformation Groups (Springer) — real-form structure for spacetime groups

edges.description: The Freed-Hopkins cobordism classification is the most comprehensive answer to the topological-phases-classification frontier currently available. It subsumes the ten-fold way's free-fermion content, captures interaction-induced collapses (the canonical Z → Z_8 for 1d BDI) and refinements (Z_2 → Z_16 for 3+1d Pin+), and provides the general framework for invertible-phase classification. Nature: partially-solves — the framework solves the invertible-phase sub-domain of the frontier but does not address non-invertible topological order (anyons, fractional phases), which remains the open part of the topological-phases frontier.

subclaim_examples.free-fermion-sub-domain: substantial (via reduction to K-theory / ten-fold way)

citations: Freed, Hopkins 2016
Senthil 2015, Annu. Rev. Condens. Matter Phys. 6, 299 (review of SPT classification and its open territory)

edges.description: Anomaly cobordism — the application of Freed-Hopkins-style cobordism classification to anomalies of quantum field theories — constrains which theories can be consistently coupled to gravity. The Witten anomaly (SU(2) global anomaly), the Bardeen-Zumino anomaly, and the cobordism interpretation of mixed gauge-gravitational anomalies all live in this framework. Nature: structural-only — the cobordism classification provides constraints on the QG landscape (anomaly-free combinations of gauge group + matter content) but does not solve the QG frontier itself. The relation to QG is via the anomaly side, not the gravitational dynamics side.
citations: Witten 1982, Phys. Lett. B 117, 324 (the SU(2) global anomaly)
García-Etxebarria, Montero 2019, JHEP 08, 003 (anomaly cobordism in QG and the swampland program)
Hsieh 2018, arXiv:1808.02881 (cobordism classification of SM-like anomalies)

edges.description: QFT uses the Freed-Hopkins classification at multiple levels: (i) as the framework for classifying invertible TQFTs in any dimension and tangential structure; (ii) as the framework for computing anomalies of fermionic gauge theories (anomaly cobordism); (iii) as the structural setting in which the relation between SPT phases and topological field theories is made precise. cell_refs pins the cells whose physical realizations are most prominent in QFT applications: the Arf invariant (Spin-d2), Fidkowski-Kitaev (Pin--d2), Z_16 (Pin+-d4), and IQHE/TKNN (Spin^c-d2).
citations: Freed, Hopkins 2016
Freed 2014, in 'Mathematical Foundations of Quantum Field Theory' (Stanford Lectures)

edges.description: Statistical mechanics (specifically, condensed-matter many-body physics) uses Freed-Hopkins to classify gapped SPT phases beyond the free-fermion regime. The framework is the standard tool for analyzing whether two ground states of a gapped Hamiltonian are continuously connected (in the same SPT phase) or topologically distinct. cell_refs pins the cells with the most-cited condensed-matter realizations.
citations: Senthil 2015, Annu. Rev. Condens. Matter Phys. 6, 299

edges.description: The ten-fold way is the free-fermion specialization of the Freed-Hopkins cobordism classification. Each ten-fold-way symmetry class corresponds to a specific tangential structure plus internal symmetry combination in the Freed-Hopkins framework (e.g., class AII ↔ Pin+; class BDI ↔ Pin-; class A ↔ Spin^c; etc.), and the ten-fold-way's per-(class, dim) topological invariant (0, Z, Z_2, 2Z) is the FREE part or the Z_2-torsion part of the corresponding cobordism group Ω^G_{d+1}. The full cobordism group is in general LARGER (Z_8 vs Z for BDI in 1d; Z_16 vs Z_2 for AII in 4d) because the cobordism classification includes interaction-induced refinements that the free-fermion ten-fold way cannot see. cell_refs pins all 30 ten-fold-way cells (the entire ten-fold-way classification is what specializes). First use of the `specializes` cross-classification subtype in the project; the relation is canonically the example used in v14.1 findings §10.1 to motivate v15's typed cross-classification edges.
citations: Kitaev 2009, AIP Conf. Proc. 1134, 22 (ten-fold way; K-theory framing)
Freed, Hopkins 2016 (cobordism framing; recovers ten-fold way as free-fermion specialization)
Fidkowski, Kitaev 2010 (canonical example of where ten-fold way and Freed-Hopkins differ: the Z → Z_8 collapse demarcates the specialization)

edges.description: If SU(5) is the correct GUT framework, the SM rep content specializes the SU(5) rep content: each SM multiplet (the cells of sm-rep-content) is contained in a specific SU(5) representation (the cells of su5-gut-rep-content) under the conjectured SU(5) → SM breaking. The 18 cell_refs pin the SM cells whose embedding into SU(5) reps is part of the conjectured connection (15 matter cells + 3 gauge boson cells; the Higgs cell-H-gNA is not pinned because the Higgs embedding in SU(5) requires non-minimal Higgs content (24 + 5 or similar) which is not part of minimal SU(5)'s structural content). Status: conjectured — the connection has specific shape (the 5̄ + 10 embedding) but is not empirically established (proton decay non-observation has falsified minimal non-SUSY SU(5); other variants remain viable but unconfirmed).
citations: Georgi, Glashow 1974
Buras, Ellis, Gaillard, Nanopoulos 1978
Super-Kamiokande Collaboration 2017 (current proton decay limits constraining the connection)

edges.description: The CFT bootstrap bears on the topological-phases-classification frontier through the bulk-boundary correspondence: gapped SPT phases in (d+1) dimensions are characterized by their (d-dimensional) boundary CFTs, and the bootstrap classifies (or bounds) those boundary CFTs. The relation is partially-solves rather than constrains because (i) for the boundary-CFT sub-problem, the bootstrap actually pins the universality class (e.g. 3D Ising bounds pin the symmetry-breaking critical point of a Z_2 SPT with explicitly broken Z_2), and (ii) for the broader topological-phases-classification frontier (interacting fermion phases, fractional topological order, anomaly classifications) the bootstrap is one of several tools, not the complete answer. cell_refs pins the cells whose boundary-CFT interpretation is most direct: 3D Ising as the boundary of 4D Z_2 SPT; the O(N) family as boundaries of 4D O(N) SPTs; the WF trajectory as the boundary-criticality interpolation.
citations: Senthil 2015, Annu. Rev. Condens. Matter Phys. 6, 299 (boundary CFT / SPT correspondence)
Chen, Lu, Vishwanath 2014, Nature Commun. 5, 3507 (boundary anomaly matching for SPT classification)

edges.description: Via AdS/CFT, bootstrap bounds on CFTs translate to bounds on gravitational theories living in AdS_{d+1}. Specifically: large-gap bootstrap bounds on the dimension of the lowest non-stress-tensor primary correspond to bounds on the curvature scale of the AdS bulk in units of the string scale; central-charge bounds correspond to entropy bounds; conformal-collider bounds correspond to causality bounds on bulk dispersion relations. The bears-on is structural-only because (i) the bounds apply to any CFT, holographic or not, so they constrain the existence/non-existence of QG theories with prescribed AdS properties rather than solving the QG frontier; (ii) AdS itself is not the canonical setting for gravity in our universe. Nature: structural-only.
citations: Heemskerk, Penedones, Polchinski, Sully 2009, JHEP 10, 079 (large-gap bootstrap / weakly-coupled bulk)
Maldacena, Shenker, Stanford 2016, JHEP 08, 106 (causality-bound chaos bound)
Camanho, Edelstein, Maldacena, Zhiboedov 2016, JHEP 02, 020 (bulk causality from CFT bootstrap)

edges.description: QFT uses the conformal-bootstrap classification as its principal non-perturbative tool for CFTs: (i) the bootstrap gives the highest-precision determination of critical exponents in 3D universality classes (Ising, O(N), GNY); (ii) it provides rigorous bounds on the spectrum of supersymmetric CFTs (4D N=4, N=2; 3D N=8; 6D (2,0)) where no Lagrangian or weak-coupling expansion is available; (iii) it provides universal stress-tensor-sector bounds (Hofman-Maldacena, central-charge ratios) that constrain every unitary CFT. cell_refs pins the cells whose results are most prominent in QFT applications.
citations: Poland, Rychkov, Vichi 2019, Rev. Mod. Phys. 91, 015002
Simmons-Duffin 2017, TASI lectures on the conformal bootstrap (arXiv:1602.07982)

edges.description: Statistical mechanics uses the conformal bootstrap to determine critical exponents of phase transitions in universality classes corresponding to scalar CFTs at criticality. The d=2 Ising and minimal-model cells underpin the exactly-solvable 2D critical phenomena (Onsager Ising; tricritical Ising; 3-state Potts; etc.). The d=3 Ising cell gives the universality class of liquid-gas critical points and uniaxial ferromagnets. The O(N)-vector cell gives the universality classes of XY (superfluid helium lambda-transition), Heisenberg ferromagnets, and chiral-symmetry-restoration transitions. The Wilson-Fisher trajectory connects these to dimensional-regularization renormalization-group calculations. cell_refs pins the cells of primary statistical-mechanical relevance.
citations: Henkel 1999, Conformal Invariance and Critical Phenomena, Springer (textbook synthesis)
Cardy 1996, Scaling and Renormalization in Statistical Physics, Cambridge

edges.description: The ADE SU(2)_k modular invariants are a specialization of the d=2 bootstrap classification: they are the bootstrap solutions for rational d=2 conformal field theories with su(2)_k current algebra (modular-invariant partition functions on the torus, equivalently consistent crossing-invariant OPE structures with finitely many primaries). The CIZ classification (Cappelli-Itzykson-Zuber 1987) is the complete bootstrap-style classification for this sub-domain. The relation is specializes (ade-modular-invariants is the sub-domain; cft-bootstrap-exclusion-regions is the more general framework). Status: partial — the relation is established for the SU(2)_k rational sub-case, but the d=2 minimal-models-Virasoro cell (cell-bs-d2-virasoro) is more general than ADE: it includes the diagonal minimal-model series at all (p,q), of which the SU(2)_k WZW models with ADE modular invariants are a specific extended-chiral-algebra extension. Status `partial` reflects that ADE pins one sub-region of the d=2 bootstrap exclusion regions, not the entire d=2 column. cell_refs pins all 5 ADE-modular-invariant cells, which are the source-side cells specializing into the broader d=2 bootstrap.
citations: Cappelli, Itzykson, Zuber 1987, Nucl. Phys. B 280, 445
Belavin, Polyakov, Zamolodchikov 1984, Nucl. Phys. B 241, 333 (the minimal-model bootstrap that subsumes ADE-rational specifically)
Gannon 2000, Rev. Math. Phys. 12, 739 (review of modular-invariant classification)

edges.description: The conformal-bootstrap exclusion regions derive from the spacetime-symmetry-groups classification: the input axioms of the bootstrap are precisely conformal invariance (the conformal group structure recorded in the conformal cells of spacetime-symmetry-groups) plus unitarity plus OPE associativity. The bootstrap's machinery (conformal blocks, crossing equations, unitarity bounds) is constructed from the irreducible-representation theory of the conformal group. In d=2 the relevant group is the infinite-dimensional Virasoro × Virasoro (the d=2 entry of spacetime-symmetry-groups); in d ≥ 3 it is the finite-dimensional so(d,2) (the higher-d conformal cells). Without the symmetry-group input, the bootstrap has no equations to solve. Subtype `derives-from` per the v15 spec §3.2 reading: the bootstrap is constructed from the conformal-symmetry-group structure. cell_refs pins the source-side cells (bootstrap cells); per v15.1 convention, cell_refs pins source-side cells (cross-classification edges read from source to target via the relation, here 'derives-from'). Every bootstrap cell derives from the conformal-group structure, hence the comprehensive cell_refs list.
citations: Mack 1977, Commun. Math. Phys. 53, 155 (conformal-algebra unitarity bounds in d ≥ 3)
Dolan, Osborn 2000, Nucl. Phys. B 599, 459 (conformal blocks as eigenfunctions of conformal Casimirs)
Belavin, Polyakov, Zamolodchikov 1984 (d=2 Virasoro case)

edges.description: AdS/CFT aspires to demonstrate that the bootstrap exclusion regions for holographic CFTs (large-N, large-gap CFTs with sparse low-twist spectrum) are determined by the cobordism classification of the bulk SymTFT / invertible field theory data. The bulk theory is a cobordism-classified invertible (anomaly-inflow) theory; the boundary CFT data, in particular the central charges and stress-tensor 3-point function ratios subject to Hofman-Maldacena bounds, should derive from the bulk's cobordism data via holographic renormalization. Currently the bulk-boundary correspondence is established at the level of anomaly matching (Witten 1998; Henningson-Skenderis 1998) but the precise derivation of bootstrap exclusion boundaries from bulk cobordism data is aspired-not-shown. The four cell_refs pin the canonical holographic CFT cells where this aspired derivation would land first: 4D N=4 SYM (dual to AdS_5 × S^5), 6D (2,0) (dual to AdS_7 × S^4), 3D N=8 ABJM (dual to AdS_4 × S^7), and the universal stress-tensor sector (where bulk Einstein gravity directly fixes the boundary central charges). Discharge: established once a specific bootstrap exclusion bound (e.g., the Hofman-Maldacena bound on a/c ratios, or central charge minimization) is derived from bulk cobordism data without holographic-renormalization shortcuts.
citations: Heemskerk, Penedones, Polchinski, Sully 2009, JHEP 10, 079
Maldacena, Shenker, Stanford 2015, JHEP 08, 106 (chaos bound from holography matching bootstrap)
Freed, Moore, Teleman 2022, arXiv:2209.07471 (topological symmetry in QFT)
Apruzzi et al. 2023, Commun. Math. Phys. 402, 895 (SymTFT from string theory)
Witten 1998, Adv. Theor. Math. Phys. 2, 253 (anti-de Sitter space and holography)

edges.description: NCG aspires to derive the SM rep content from the K-theoretic / cobordism classification of anomaly-free fermion contents in 4D. Both programs land in K-theory: Connes-Chamseddine spectral triples are K-theoretic objects (Connes 1995); Freed-Hopkins cobordism for invertible field theories is K-theory-flavored (Freed-Hopkins 2021); García-Etxebarria-Montero have shown the SM is anomaly-free in the cobordism-precise sense (no Dai-Freed anomalies). The aspired connection: the cobordism-K-theory classification of admissible anomaly-free 4D fermion families should coincide with the spectral-triple classification at the level of SM-like solutions, providing a K-theoretic explanation for why the SM has exactly its observed rep content (rather than this being an arbitrary input). The 15 cell_refs pin the 15 matter cells (3 generations × 5 left/right-handed multiplets) that constitute the rep content being explained. Discharge: established once one demonstrates that the cobordism-K-theory classification (with appropriate symmetry data) and the spectral-triple classification (with appropriate finite-dimensional algebra input) bijectively identify the SM matter content as their joint solution. Currently partial successes exist on both sides separately but not as a derivation.
citations: Connes 1995, J. Math. Phys. 36, 6194 (NCG and reality)
Chamseddine, Connes, Marcolli 2007, Adv. Theor. Math. Phys. 11, 991 (gravity and SM with neutrino mixing)
Freed, Hopkins 2021, Geom. Topol. 25, 1165 (reflection positivity and invertible phases)
García-Etxebarria, Montero 2019, JHEP 08, 003 (Dai-Freed anomalies in particle physics)
Davighi, Lohitsiri 2020, JHEP 05, 098 (anomaly interplay for SM)

edges.description: Top-down holography (AdS/CFT applied to string compactifications) aspires to embed the SM rep content as the low-energy spectrum of a specific holographic CFT compactified on an internal geometry, making SM rep content categorically equivalent to a subset of bootstrap-allowed CFT data with the appropriate symmetry-breaking pattern. Concrete realizations: F-theory GUTs (Beasley-Heckman-Vafa 2009), where SM-like spectra arise from D-brane configurations whose dual CFTs sit in the bootstrap-allowed region. The aspiration is that some bootstrap-allowed SCFT, when compactified on a specific geometric background, gives exactly the SM at low energies — making the SM a 'special point' in the moduli space of bootstrap-allowed SCFTs. The four cell_refs pin the canonical SCFTs from which string compactifications might descend to SM-like physics: 4D N=4 SYM, 4D N=2 SCFTs (most relevant for SM-like phenomenology), 3D N=8 (M-theory side), and 6D (2,0) (F-theory parent). Discharge: established once a specific bootstrap-allowed SCFT plus a specific compactification geometry is shown to give exactly the SM gauge group, fermion content, and Yukawa structure (or equivalently, the SM is shown to sit at a specific point in the moduli space of bootstrap-allowed holographic CFTs).
citations: Maldacena 1998, Adv. Theor. Math. Phys. 2, 231 (the large N limit of superconformal field theories)
Beasley, Heckman, Vafa 2009, JHEP 01, 058 (GUTs and exceptional branes in F-theory I)
Heckman 2010, Annu. Rev. Nucl. Part. Sci. 60, 237 (particle physics implications of F-theory)
Weigand 2018, PoS TASI2017, 016 (TASI lectures on F-theory)

edges.description: The ten-fold-way classification of topological phases (in dimensions 1, 2, 3 with the ten Altland-Zirnbauer symmetry classes) aspires to derive from boundary CFT bootstrap data via the bulk-boundary correspondence. A (d+1)-dimensional topological phase has a d-dimensional boundary CFT or gapless boundary mode whose spectrum is subject to bootstrap exclusion bounds; the topological invariants in the bulk (Z, Z_2, 2Z entries of the table) classify the inequivalent gapless boundaries, which in turn correspond to bootstrap-allowed CFT spectra. AdS/CFT pursues this via the AdS_3/CFT_2 dictionary applied to Chern-Simons-style bulk theories; the tensor-network program (Vidal MERA; Evenbly-White) pursues it via explicit lattice constructions of holographic-like correspondences. Aspired discharge: established once every non-trivial entry of the ten-fold-way table is matched to a specific bootstrap-allowed boundary CFT solution. The 9 cell_refs pin the canonical non-trivial entries (the topological phases with anomalous boundary modes most likely to admit a CFT bootstrap dual).
citations: Vidal 2007, Phys. Rev. Lett. 99, 220405 (entanglement renormalization, MERA)
Ryu, Schnyder, Furusaki, Ludwig 2010, New J. Phys. 12, 065010 (topological insulators and superconductors)
Kitaev 2009, AIP Conf. Proc. 1134, 22 (periodic table of topological insulators)
Witten 2016, Rev. Mod. Phys. 88, 035001 (three lectures on topological phases of matter)

edges.description: NCG aspires to derive the SM rep content jointly with spacetime symmetry data from a single underlying spectral triple, where the spectral triple consists of a continuum spin manifold (encoding spacetime symmetries — Poincaré in the continuum limit) tensored with a finite-dimensional algebra A_F (encoding internal gauge structure and matter content). In this aspirational picture, both the SM rep content and the spacetime symmetries are jointly determined: changing the spectral triple changes both at once, and the SM's specific structure is the unique (or nearly-unique) solution consistent with a small set of axioms (order-one condition, finiteness, KO-dimension 6 mod 8, etc.). Partial successes: gauge group U(1) × SU(2) × SU(3) is recovered; three fermion generations recovered (with right input); spectral action contains the Einstein-Hilbert term as the leading term (deriving continuum spacetime structure). Falsified partial: Higgs mass prediction ~170 GeV inconsistent with measured 125 GeV; modifications (Devastato-Lizzi-Martinetti 2014; Chamseddine-Connes-Mukhanov 2014) have been proposed. The 18 cell_refs pin all 15 matter cells plus the 3 gauge cells of sm-rep-content, since the NCG derivation aims to recover both simultaneously. Discharge: established once a single spectral triple is shown to derive SM gauge group, fermion content, Yukawa structure, AND continuum spacetime symmetries consistent with all measurements (including Higgs mass).
citations: Connes, Lott 1991, Nucl. Phys. Proc. Suppl. 18B, 29 (particle models and NCG)
Chamseddine, Connes 1997, Commun. Math. Phys. 186, 731 (spectral action principle)
Chamseddine, Connes, Marcolli 2007, Adv. Theor. Math. Phys. 11, 991 (gravity and SM with neutrino mixing)
Devastato, Lizzi, Martinetti 2014, JHEP 01, 042 (grand symmetry, spectral action, Higgs mass)
Chamseddine, Connes, Mukhanov 2014, Phys. Rev. Lett. 114, 091302 (quanta of geometry)

edges.description: Causal sets aspires to demonstrate that the classification of admissible continuum spacetime symmetries (Poincaré, diffeomorphism) and the cobordism-K-theory classification of admissible spacetime topologies are categorically equivalent at the discrete causal-set level: the same combinatorial data of an underlying ensemble of causal sets jointly determines both the continuum-limit Lorentz/Poincaré symmetry and the cobordism class of the recovered manifold. The Bombelli-Lee-Meyer-Sorkin program for discrete-to-continuum recovery aims to show that a 'manifoldlike' causal set has a unique continuum limit (up to small fluctuations) whose Lorentz invariance is emergent and whose topology is determined by the causal-set ensemble. The aspired connection makes precise what is currently a hope: that the symmetry-group classification and the cobordism classification are not independent inputs to spacetime physics but joint outputs of a single discrete classificatory framework. The 2 cell_refs pin the Poincaré and diffeomorphism cells (the canonical continuum-limit symmetries that causal sets aspires to recover, and which would be matched to specific cobordism classes). Discharge: established once a specific class of causal sets is shown to admit a continuum limit whose Lorentz symmetry group and cobordism class are jointly fixed by the ensemble structure (e.g., from the Bombelli-Henson-Sorkin manifoldlike criterion). Note: causal sets has the weakest aspired-edge defense among the four candidate-foundational programs because most of its aspirations land on open-frontiers (qg-frontier, cc-frontier) rather than on connections between existing formal-classifications — this edge captures the one substantive cross-classification aspiration but the case is more speculative than the AdS/CFT or NCG entries.
citations: Bombelli, Lee, Meyer, Sorkin 1987, Phys. Rev. Lett. 59, 521 (space-time as a causal set)
Henson 2009, in Approaches to Quantum Gravity, ed. Oriti (the causal set approach)
Surya 2019, Living Rev. Relativ. 22, 5 (the causal set approach to quantum gravity)
Dowker, Surya 2020, Class. Quantum Grav. 37, 053001 (manifoldlike causal sets)

edges.description: Top-down holography (F-theory / string compactifications) aspires to demonstrate that SM rep content and ten-fold-way topological-phase data are categorically-equivalent extractions of a single underlying string compactification: same geometry, different data extractor, structurally-paired content. F-theory GUTs (Beasley-Heckman-Vafa 2009) produce SM-like fermion spectra from intersecting D-brane configurations on Calabi-Yau geometries; the same geometries carry discrete higher-form / generalized symmetry data (Cordova-Dumitrescu-Intriligator 2019; García-Etxebarria-Heidenreich-Regalado 2022) classifying topological-phase content. AdS/CFT aspires to make the equivalence categorical: the moduli of admissible string compactifications would bijectively map to (SM-like rep content, ten-fold-way class) pairs that are jointly realizable. This is the one program-co-targeted shadow edge surfaced by the v15.6 pattern-finding pass: both ends were previously targeted by ads-cft-instance via separate aspired edges to bootstrap (cft-bootstrap-exclusion-regions ↔ sm-rep-content and tenfold-way → cft-bootstrap-exclusion-regions), but the direct sm-rep-content ↔ ten-fold-way connection was unauthored despite literature support. cell_refs pin the 15 SM matter cells (the source-side data that the categorical equivalence would jointly determine with topological-phase data). Discharge: established once a specific string compactification gives exactly the SM rep content and a specific ten-fold-way class as joint outputs, with the moduli labelling the pair forming a bijection or at least an explicit functor.
citations: Beasley, Heckman, Vafa 2009, JHEP 01, 058 (GUTs and exceptional branes in F-theory I)
Cordova, Dumitrescu, Intriligator 2019, JHEP 02, 184 (2-group symmetries)
García-Etxebarria, Heidenreich, Regalado 2022, JHEP 09, 130 (IIB flux non-commutativity and higher-form symmetries)
Heckman 2010, Annu. Rev. Nucl. Part. Sci. 60, 237 (particle physics implications of F-theory)
Apruzzi et al. 2023, Commun. Math. Phys. 402, 895 (SymTFT from string theory — geometric origin of higher-form symmetries)

edges.description: RG-info aspires that the cobordism classification of invertible (gapped) phases derives from the bootstrap classification of CFT fixed points via RG-flow continuation. In the Kapustin-Seiberg / Freed-Hopkins picture, every cobordism-classified invertible field theory is the RG-flow IR endpoint of some massive deformation of a UV bootstrap-allowed CFT; the cobordism classification is then the structure of trivial-flow endpoints emerging from bootstrap-allowed UV theories. The C-theorem (Zamolodchikov 1986) and a-theorem (Komargodski-Schwimmer 2011) establish information-monotonicity along RG flows in 2D and 4D respectively; the rg-info aspiration extends this monotonicity to a derivation: cobordism classes are RG-trivial fibres over the bootstrap UV moduli space. NOTE: this edge points opposite to the v15.5 edge edge-xc-bootstrap-derives-cobordism-aspired (which is targeted by ads-cft-instance and reads bootstrap-derives-from-cobordism via the bulk-boundary / SymTFT direction). The two aspirations are non-conflicting: AdS/CFT works in the bulk-boundary direction; RG-info works in the UV→IR direction. Authoring both as separate edges exercises the multi-edge-between-same-pair pattern flagged in v15.5 as previously unused. cell_refs pin 5 source-side cells: Ω^{Spin}_2 = ℤ₂ (2D fermionic invertible phases ↔ minimal-model bootstrap IR endpoints); Ω^{Spin}_4 = ℤ (4D Pontryagin-class invertible phase ↔ 4D bootstrap IR); Ω^{Spin^c}_2 = ℤ (IQHE-class ↔ 2D bootstrap chiral central charge); Ω^{Spin^c}_4 = ℤ² (4D spin^c invertible ↔ 4D bootstrap); Ω^{Pin+}_4 = ℤ₁₆ (Wang-Senthil anomaly ↔ 4D bootstrap with time-reversal). Discharge: established once a specific cobordism class is shown to be the RG-flow IR endpoint of a specific bootstrap-allowed UV CFT, with the RG flow respecting the c/a-theorem ordering.
citations: Zamolodchikov 1986, JETP Lett. 43, 730 (c-theorem)
Komargodski, Schwimmer 2011, JHEP 12, 099 (a-theorem in 4D)
Kapustin, Seiberg 2014, JHEP 04, 001 (higher-form symmetries and RG)
Freed, Hopkins 2021, Geom. Topol. 25, 1165 (reflection positivity and invertible phases as RG endpoints)
Apenko 2012, Physica A 391, 62 (information theory and RG)
Beny, Osborne 2015, New J. Phys. 17, 083005 (RG as quantum-information renormalization)

edges.description: Modular tensor categories specialize the bootstrap exclusion regions: MTCs are the algebraic data of rational 2D CFTs, which are the sub-domain of bootstrap-allowed CFTs satisfying rationality (finitely many primaries, modular-invariant partition function). Moore-Seiberg 1989 established the categorical equivalence between rational 2D CFTs and MTCs, making this specializes-relation rigorous: every MTC produces a rational CFT in the bootstrap-allowed region, and every rational CFT corresponds to an MTC. The 7 cell_refs pin representative MTCs (Fibonacci, Lee-Yang, Ising, SU(2)_2, tricritical-Ising, toric code, SU(2)_3) that occupy specific points within the d=2 bootstrap exclusion region. Note: a finer subtype `constrains` would more precisely capture the structural-causal relation (MTC modular S-matrix structurally constrains the partition function of any bulk-boundary partner CFT). Such an edge is distinct from this `specializes` edge: this one is about the rational sub-case as a SET; the constrains edge would be about how MTC structure NARROWS the form of any boundary CFT realized as MTC bulk-boundary partner. The `constrains` subtype does not exist in v15.3 schema; this distinct edge is documented in findings as a v16-pending authoring item rather than authored here with a misfit subtype.
citations: Moore, Seiberg 1989, Commun. Math. Phys. 123, 177
Bakalov, Kirillov 2001, AMS (textbook proof of categorical equivalence)
Cardy 1986, Nucl. Phys. B 270, 186 (operator content of two-dimensional conformally invariant theories)

edges.description: The ADE SU(2)_k modular invariants specialize the modular tensor categories: ADE-modular-invariants are exactly the SU(2)_k WZW MTCs (the A-series at each rank k+1, plus the D and exceptional modular extensions). Cappelli-Itzykson-Zuber 1987 classified the modular-invariant partition functions for SU(2)_k WZW into ADE families; in MTC language, these are the MTC structures supported on the SU(2)_k chiral algebra (A-series) plus the orbifold-and-extension constructions (D, E_6, E_7, E_8 series). This edge complements the existing v15.4 partial edge `ade-modular-invariants -> cft-bootstrap-exclusion-regions [partial/specializes]`: that edge says ADE-modular is part of the bootstrap region; this new edge says ADE-modular is part of the MTC sub-region within bootstrap. Together they document the chain: ade-modular-invariants ⊂ modular-tensor-categories ⊂ rational-CFT ⊂ cft-bootstrap-exclusion-regions. cell_refs pin the 5 ADE-modular cells (all of them); each corresponds to an SU(2)_k MTC at the appropriate k.
citations: Cappelli, Itzykson, Zuber 1987, Nucl. Phys. B 280, 445
Gannon 2000, Rev. Math. Phys. 12, 739 (review of modular-invariant classification)
Frohlich, Fuchs, Runkel, Schweigert 2006, Nucl. Phys. B 763, 354 (algebraic approach via Frobenius algebras in MTCs)

edges.description: Modular tensor categories specialize the cobordism classification of (2+1)D invertible / non-chiral topological phases. Status: partial — the specialization holds for the invertible (rank-1) and non-chiral (c=0) sub-case of MTC, where MTC data reduces to cobordism-classified anomaly inflow. Specifically: a non-chiral MTC has an associated 3D TQFT that is (in the trivial cobordism class) reducible to a finite group gauge theory, which has a cobordism-classified anomaly. The chiral central charge c (mod 8 for bosonic MTCs, mod 16 for fermionic) is itself cobordism data: Ω_4^{Pin+} = Z_16 captures the gravitational anomaly of fermionic chiral MTCs (Kitaev's 16-fold way). General chiral MTCs (Fibonacci, Ising, SU(2)_k at k ≥ 1) are NOT cobordism-classified — they carry non-invertible anyon content. So the specialization is partial: invertible / non-chiral MTC ⊂ cobordism; general MTC properly extends cobordism by adding non-invertible anyonic data. The 3 cell_refs pin the structurally-degenerate cases: rank-1 vacuum (literally cobordism-class 0), toric code (Z_2 gauge theory, cobordism-classified by group-cohomology), and doubled-Ising (non-chiral Drinfeld center, partially cobordism-overlapping).
citations: Kitaev 2006, Ann. Phys. 321, 2 (16-fold way, Pin+ cobordism)
Freed, Hopkins 2021, Geom. Topol. 25, 1165 (reflection positivity and invertible phases)
Kong, Wen 2014, arXiv:1405.5858 (braided fusion categories of topological order)

edges.description: Tensor-network aspires to demonstrate that the full classification of (2+1)D topological phases factorizes as the composition of ten-fold-way symmetry-protected data (the SPT factor) with modular-tensor-category intrinsic-topological-order data (the MTC factor): full-topological-phase = ten-fold-way × MTC. For (2+1)D this gives the Kitaev-Kong-Wen classification scheme (Lan-Kong-Wen 2017 elaborated it). The two classifications are complementary: ten-fold-way handles invertible / SPT phases (no anyons in the bulk; symmetry-protected gapless or topological boundary modes); MTC handles non-invertible / intrinsic topological order (anyons in the bulk; topological boundary modes are the boundary anyons). The aspiration is that every (2+1)D topological phase is precisely characterized by one ten-fold-way class plus one MTC, with the composition operation explicit. Tensor-network methods (Levin-Wen string-net constructions, PEPS) provide the most concrete framework where this factorization is being made precise. The 3 cell_refs pin canonical (2+1)D ten-fold-way cells whose composition with specific MTCs is the active research target.
citations: Lan, Kong, Wen 2017, Phys. Rev. B 95, 235140 (classification of (2+1)D topological orders)
Levin, Wen 2005, Phys. Rev. B 71, 045110 (string-net condensation)
Senthil 2015, Annu. Rev. Condens. Matter Phys. 6, 299 (review of SPT phases)
Chen, Gu, Liu, Wen 2013, Phys. Rev. B 87, 155114 (SPT phases with on-site symmetries)

edges.description: Modular tensor categories structurally constrain the form of any boundary CFT realized as MTC bulk-boundary partner — even before identifying the boundary as rational. The constraints are: (1) the partition function on the torus must transform under SL(2,Z) according to the MTC's modular S and T matrices; (2) the OPE coefficients must factor through the MTC's F-symbols (pentagon-axiom-consistent) and R-symbols (hexagon-axiom-consistent); (3) the chiral central charge c (mod 8 for bosonic MTCs, mod 16 for fermionic) must match the MTC's encoded chiral anomaly. These three constraints together fix the form (not just the set-membership) of any boundary CFT realized as a bulk-boundary partner of a given MTC. This is structurally distinct from the v15.8 specializes edge between the same pair (edge-xc-mtc-specializes-bootstrap): the specializes edge identifies WHICH boundary CFTs are MTC-realized (the rational sub-case as a subset); this constrains edge identifies HOW the MTC structure narrows the form of any candidate boundary CFT realized as MTC partner. The two edges target the same FC pair but capture different content — set-membership vs structural-causal restriction. Status: established. Moore-Seiberg axioms make the constraint rigorous; Cardy's modular-invariance constraint analysis (1986) gives the constructive form; the categorical-equivalence theorem (Moore-Seiberg 1989, refined Bakalov-Kirillov 2001) closes the structural-causal direction. The 7 cell_refs pin the same MTCs as the specializes edge — these are the canonical cases where both the subset and the structural-causal readings apply.
citations: Cardy 1986, Nucl. Phys. B 270, 186 (modular invariance constraints on the operator content)
Moore, Seiberg 1989, Commun. Math. Phys. 123, 177 (the rational CFT axioms — the structural-causal direction)
Bakalov, Kirillov 2001, AMS (textbook proof of MTC ↔ rational CFT categorical equivalence)
Fuchs, Runkel, Schweigert 2002, Nucl. Phys. B 624, 452 (TFT construction of RCFT correlators — explicit constructive form)

edges.description: Generalized symmetries specialize the cobordism classification: the SymTFT bulk associated to any symmetry S in d-dim is a (d+1)-dim invertible TFT, which is cobordism-classified (Freed-Hopkins 2021). Every cell of generalized-symmetries (a specific (d, p, structure-type) triple with realized examples) corresponds to a specific cobordism class in Ω_{d+1}^{SO × BG} (for invertible cases) or to a fusion-categorical extension thereof (for non-invertible cases). The specializes-relation: generalized-symmetries provides MORE structure than cobordism alone (it also tracks form-degree, structure-type, and the boundary QFT's symmetry data), so it specializes the cobordism classification to the symmetry-data context. cell_refs pin 4 cells whose SymTFT bulks are canonically cobordism-classified: 2D Z_2 (3D DW), 3D Z_2 (4D DW), 4D YM center (5D), 6D (2,0) (7D CS-like).
citations: Freed, Hopkins 2021, Geom. Topol. 25, 1165 (reflection positivity and invertible phases — the cobordism classification used in SymTFT bulk)
Freed, Moore, Teleman 2022, arXiv:2209.07471
Kapustin 2014, arXiv:1403.1467 (cobordism approach to SPT phases — early SymTFT-like analysis)

edges.description: Generalized-symmetry anomalies structurally constrain the bootstrap exclusion regions.
