Edm-program's bear-down on the strong-CP-problem frontier proceeds via the neutron-EDM channel: the Crewther-DiVecchia-Veneziano-Witten relation |d_n| ≈ (3.6 × 10⁻¹⁶ e·cm) × θ̄_QCD (Crewther-DiVecchia-Veneziano-Witten 1979 Phys Lett B88:123 with 1980 erratum) translates an upper bound on |d_n| directly to an upper bound on the strong-CP angle θ̄_QCD. The PSI nEDM result (Abel et al. 2020 PRL 124:081803) of |d_n| < 1.8 × 10⁻²⁶ e·cm at 90% CL yields the strong-cp-problem frontier's currently-recorded quantitative_scale of |θ̄_QCD| < 10⁻¹⁰; the n2EDM successor (Ayres et al. 2021 EPJ C 81:512) targets |d_n| ≲ 10⁻²⁷ e·cm, tightening the θ̄_QCD bound by another order of magnitude when the design sensitivity is reached.

Sensitivity reach: discovery-floor upper bound |d_n| ≲ 10⁻²⁷ e·cm — the n2EDM design floor; the current best bound (PSI 2020) sits near |d_n| ~ 10⁻²⁵·⁷ e·cm, and the program's near-term reach via n2EDM brings the floor to ~10⁻²⁷ e·cm across the running operating period. EDM appears as a dimension-five CP-violating spin-electric-field coupling −(i/2) d_n ψ̄ σ^{μν} γ_5 ψ F_{μν} with the same coupling-of-fermion-to-electromagnetic-field semantics as the haloscope's g_aγγ; e·cm is the community convention for EDM quantities.

This edge is bounds-setting rather than measurement-discriminating, following the desi-cc-frontier (sub-PR 50) and axion-haloscope-network-strong-cp-problem (sub-PR 51) precedents: the three candidate-targeting programs on strong-cp-problem do not, in their currently-published forms, supply point-predictive program-internal calculations of |d_n| (or equivalently θ̄_QCD) that satisfy §4 admissibility test part (2). Peccei-Quinn predicts dynamical relaxation θ̄ → 0 in the f_a → ∞ limit but does not fix a non-zero residual at a specific level discriminable at the EDM sensitivity floor (per Di Luzio et al. 2020 'The landscape of QCD axion models' Phys Rep 870:1, residuals depend on the specific axion model and on Standard-Model-side phases). Anthropic supplies a parameter-space-class argument rather than a point prediction. Vafa-Witten provides a structural constraint (no spontaneous CP violation in vector-like theories) that constrains the θ̄ parameter's origin without fixing its value. The EDM sensitivity excludes the |θ̄_QCD| > 10⁻¹⁰ region rather than discriminating two competing point predictions; no per-program point predictions are recorded — the §4-admissible posture.

This is the third experimental-coverage edge in the project to target an open frontier directly (after edge-rs-desi-cc-frontier in sub-PR 50 and edge-rs-axion-haloscope-network-strong-cp-problem in sub-PR 51). It is the second such edge attached to strong-cp-problem — the first instance of a single frontier carrying two experimental-coverage edges from independent experimental programs (haloscope direct-detection channel via axion-haloscope-network, and electric-dipole-moment indirect channel via edm-program). The two channels probe the SAME Peccei-Quinn-axion resolution from complementary directions: haloscope sets a bound on g_aγγ (the axion-photon coupling), nEDM sets a bound on residual θ̄ (the order parameter the PQ mechanism dynamically relaxes). The cross-channel structural visibility this creates is the AI-first-tool find_discriminating_experiments substrate the Phase C MCP rebuild will surface.
citations: Ayres et al. (n2EDM Collaboration) 2021 'The design of the n2EDM experiment' Eur Phys J C 81:512
Abel et al. (PSI nEDM Collaboration) 2020 'Measurement of the Permanent Electric Dipole Moment of the Neutron' Phys Rev Lett 124:081803
Pendlebury et al. 2015 'Revised experimental upper limit on the electric dipole moment of the neutron' Phys Rev D 92:092003
Crewther-DiVecchia-Veneziano-Witten 1979 'Chiral estimate of the electric dipole moment of the neutron in quantum chromodynamics' Phys Lett B88:123 (with 1980 erratum Phys Lett B91:487)
Pospelov-Ritz 2005 'Electric dipole moments as probes of new physics' Annals Phys 318:119
Peccei-Quinn 1977 'CP Conservation in the Presence of Pseudoparticles' Phys Rev Lett 38:1440
Vafa-Witten 1984 'Parity Conservation in Quantum Chromodynamics' Phys Rev Lett 53:535
Di Luzio et al. 2020 'The landscape of QCD axion models' Phys Rep 870:1

Roussy et al. (JILA HfF⁺ Collaboration) 2023 'An improved bound on the electron's electric dipole moment' Science 381:46 — the program's currently-deepest published bound |d_e| < 4.1 × 10⁻³⁰ e·cm at 90% CL, obtained with trapped HfF⁺ molecular ions at JILA/Cornell; the design-anchor citation for the eEDM sensitivity floor at log10 ≈ −29.4.

Andreev et al. (ACME Collaboration) 2018 'Improved limit on the electric dipole moment of the electron' Nature 562:355 — ACME-II's bound |d_e| < 1.1 × 10⁻²⁹ e·cm at 90% CL using ThO molecules, the immediate predecessor result establishing the polar-paramagnetic-molecule method's reach at the 10⁻²⁹ floor and the apparatus-method continuity that ACME-III continues toward ∼10⁻³⁰.

Cesarotti-Lu-Nakai-Soni-Strassler 2019 'Interpreting the Electron EDM Constraint' Phys Rev D 100:015013 — comprehensive framework for translating eEDM bounds into constraints on the BSM CP-violating sources required to source the observed baryon asymmetry via electroweak baryogenesis and related mechanisms; the program's structural-interpretation anchor for the baryogenesis-frontier discharge.

edges.description:
Edm-program's bear-down on the matter-antimatter-asymmetry frontier proceeds via the electron-EDM channel: the Sakharov criteria (Sakharov 1967 JETP Lett 5:24) for baryogenesis require CP violation beyond the SM-CKM source by roughly ten orders of magnitude; the BSM CP-violating phases required to source the observed baryon asymmetry η_B ≈ 6 × 10⁻¹⁰ (matter-antimatter-asymmetry frontier's recorded quantitative_scale) generically feed into the electron EDM at one-loop or two-loop level. The Cesarotti-Lu-Nakai-Soni-Strassler 2019 framework (Phys Rev D 100:015013) provides the comprehensive translation from eEDM bounds to constraints on the Electroweak-baryogenesis-extended candidate-targeting program: the current JILA HfF⁺ bound |d_e| < 4.1 × 10⁻³⁰ e·cm at 90% CL (Roussy et al. 2023 Science 381:46) already excludes light-stop EW-baryogenesis scenarios essentially completely and constrains two-Higgs-doublet light-pseudoscalar variants substantially; ACME-III's target ∼10⁻³⁰ e·cm reach tightens the closure further. The Affleck-Dine baryogenesis candidate-targeting entry (discharge_status: gestural-to-partial) likewise receives EDM-bound input via the SUSY-motivated CP-violating phases its scalar-field condensates carry; the GUT baryogenesis candidate-targeting entry (discharge_status: constrained) is independently constrained by inflationary dilution and gains additional but secondary EDM-bound coverage.

Sensitivity reach: discovery-floor upper bound |d_e| ≲ 10⁻³⁰ e·cm — the JILA HfF⁺ 2023 published floor and the ACME-III near-term continuation reach across the program's running operating period. The dimension-five spin-electric-field coupling has the same Lagrangian structure as the neutron-EDM channel in Edge 1, here for charged leptons.

This edge is bounds-setting rather than measurement-discriminating on the same §4 part (2) admissibility grounds as Edge 1 and the desi-cc-frontier / axion-haloscope precedents: the four candidate-targeting programs on matter-antimatter-asymmetry (Leptogenesis; Electroweak baryogenesis extended; Affleck-Dine baryogenesis; GUT baryogenesis) supply parameter-space-class predictions for the BSM CP-violating phases and hence for |d_e|, not single point predictions discriminable at the EDM sensitivity floor. Leptogenesis's CP-asymmetry parameter ε depends on the heavy-Majorana-neutrino mass spectrum and Yukawa structure with substantial parameter freedom (Davidson-Nardi-Nir 2008 'Leptogenesis' Phys Rep 466:105); Electroweak-baryogenesis-extended's predicted |d_e| spans roughly 10⁻²⁸ to below 10⁻³¹ e·cm across model classes (Cesarotti et al. 2019); Affleck-Dine produces |d_e| at levels strongly model-dependent on the scalar potential structure. The EDM sensitivity excludes broad regions of these parameter spaces rather than discriminating two competing point values; no per-program point predictions are recorded — the §4-admissible posture.

This is the fourth experimental-coverage edge in the project to target an open frontier directly (after desi/cc-frontier sub-PR 50, axion-haloscope/strong-cp-problem sub-PR 51, and edm-program/strong-cp-problem in this sub-PR 52). It is the first such edge attached to matter-antimatter-asymmetry (the frontier had zero experimental-coverage edges before this sub-PR). The pairing with Edge 1 (edm-program targeting strong-cp-problem via the nEDM channel) makes edm-program the project's first dual-frontier experimental program — the same source-end node targets two distinct open frontiers via complementary experimental sub-programs (PSI nEDM/n2EDM for nEDM; ACME-II/III and JILA HfF⁺ for eEDM), each bearing on different BSM-physics structural payoffs. The cross-frontier coverage from a single source program is the AI-first cross-discriminator substrate the Phase C scope memo §6 identified as load-bearing for the find_discriminating_experiments tool.

Opens the EDM physics subdomain as the SIXTH STEP-4.4 PHYSICS SUBDOMAIN after hep-ph (proton decay; sub-PRs 46–48), neutrino-sector (oscillations + mass ordering; sub-PRs 46–48), gravitational-wave (compact-binary mergers + multiband; sub-PR 49), cosmology (dark energy + Hubble + BAO; sub-PR 50), and axion-dark-matter (haloscope coupling; sub-PR 51).
citations: Roussy et al. (JILA HfF⁺ Collaboration) 2023 'An improved bound on the electron's electric dipole moment' Science 381:46
Andreev et al. (ACME Collaboration) 2018 'Improved limit on the electric dipole moment of the electron' Nature 562:355
Cesarotti-Lu-Nakai-Soni-Strassler 2019 'Interpreting the Electron EDM Constraint' Phys Rev D 100:015013
Sakharov 1967 'Violation of CP Invariance, C Asymmetry, and Baryon Asymmetry of the Universe' JETP Lett 5:24
Cohen-Kaplan-Nelson 1993 'Progress in Electroweak Baryogenesis' Annu Rev Nucl Part Sci 43:27
Riotto-Trodden 1999 'Recent Progress in Baryogenesis' Annu Rev Nucl Part Sci 49:35
Davidson-Nardi-Nir 2008 'Leptogenesis' Phys Rep 466:105
Pospelov-Ritz 2005 'Electric dipole moments as probes of new physics' Annals Phys 318:119

Aguillard et al. (Muon g-2 Collaboration) 2025 'Measurement of the positive muon anomalous magnetic moment to 127 ppb' Phys Rev Lett 135:101802 — the FINAL Run-1–6 combined Fermilab E989 measurement reaching 127 ppb relative precision on a_μ, the program's design-completion precision. In absolute units this corresponds to an uncertainty of ∼1.48 × 10⁻¹⁰ on a_μ ≈ 1.166 × 10⁻³, the sensitivity floor encoded here.

Aguillard et al. (Muon g-2 Collaboration) 2023 'Measurement of the Positive Muon Anomalous Magnetic Moment to 0.20 ppm' Phys Rev Lett 131:161802 — the Run-1+2+3 Fermilab measurement reaching 200 ppb, the design-trajectory intermediate point cited as the immediate predecessor of the 2025 final-combined result.

Abi et al. (Muon g-2 Collaboration) 2021 'Measurement of the Positive Muon Anomalous Magnetic Moment to 0.46 ppm' Phys Rev Lett 126:141801 — the initial Run-1 Fermilab E989 measurement at 460 ppb, the program's first published result and the design-trajectory starting point.

Abe et al. (J-PARC E34 Collaboration) 2019 'A new approach for measuring the muon anomalous magnetic moment and electric dipole moment' PTEP 2019:053C02 §3 — the design document for the J-PARC E34 ultra-cold-muon technique, providing a systematics-independent cross-check of the Fermilab storage-ring methodology with a different dominant-systematic profile (no electric focusing means the dominant E989 electric-field-correction systematic does not appear, allowing genuinely independent confirmation of the central a_μ value at comparable precision once commissioned).

predictions_per_program.program: Theory Initiative 2020 (dispersive HVP SM prediction)

citations: Aoyama et al. 2020 'The anomalous magnetic moment of the muon in the Standard Model' Phys Rep 887:1 (the g-2 Theory Initiative consensus report) — the TI 2020 consensus SM prediction a_μ^SM = (116 591 810 ± 43) × 10⁻¹¹, with the LO-HVP contribution determined by dispersive analysis of e⁺ e⁻ → hadrons cross-section data and the remaining components (QED, EW, HLbL) taken from the cited subgroup calculations. This is the program-internal calculation of the 'dispersive-method refinement (e⁺ e⁻ → hadrons)' candidate-targeting program listed in the muon-g-2 carrier's equations.native.equations[0].loose_ends[0].candidate_targeting array.

predictions_per_program.description: TI 2020 SM prediction using dispersive HVP determined from e⁺ e⁻ → hadrons cross-section data. The Aoyama et al. 2020 Phys Rep 887:1 consensus paper assembled by the g-2 Theory Initiative gives a_μ^SM,TI = (116 591 810 ± 43) × 10⁻¹¹, 3.7σ below the BNL E821 measurement (Bennett et al. 2006) and 4.2σ below the BNL+FNAL Run-1 combined measurement (Abi et al. 2021). The dispersive-HVP approach was the dominant SM-prediction methodology through 2020.
citations: Aoyama et al. 2020 Phys Rep 887:1 (program-internal calculation of dispersive HVP, central value and uncertainty)

predictions_per_program.program: BMW 2021 (lattice HVP SM prediction)

citations: Borsanyi et al. (BMW Collaboration) 2021 'Leading hadronic contribution to the muon magnetic moment from lattice QCD' Nature 593:51 — the BMW lattice-QCD LO-HVP calculation a_μ^LO-HVP = 707.5(5.5) × 10⁻¹⁰ (0.8% relative precision); combined with the non-HVP SM components from Aoyama et al. 2020 the BMW lattice-based SM prediction is a_μ^SM,BMW ≈ 116 591 954 × 10⁻¹¹ with HVP-dominated theoretical uncertainty of order 55 × 10⁻¹¹. This is the program-internal calculation of the 'lattice-QCD HVP refinement' candidate-targeting program listed in the muon-g-2 carrier's equations.native.equations[0].loose_ends[0].candidate_targeting array.

Aoyama et al. 2020 Phys Rep 887:1 §8 — the non-HVP SM components (QED, EW, HLbL, NLO/NNLO-HVP) combined with the BMW LO-HVP value to yield the total a_μ^SM,BMW reported above. The non-HVP uncertainties (HLbL ∼19 × 10⁻¹¹ dominant, EW ∼1 × 10⁻¹¹ sub-dominant, QED negligible) combine in quadrature with the BMW LO-HVP uncertainty to give a total SM uncertainty modestly larger than the LO-HVP-alone uncertainty (∼58 × 10⁻¹¹ vs 55 × 10⁻¹¹); the 5.5 × 10⁻¹⁰ value above reports the BMW LO-HVP-dominated uncertainty as the primary literature-anchored figure.

predictions_per_program.description: BMW 2021 SM prediction using ab-initio lattice-QCD computation of the LO-HVP contribution: a_μ^LO-HVP = 707.5(5.5) × 10⁻¹⁰ (Borsanyi et al. 2021 Nature 593:51), the first lattice-HVP calculation at sub-percent precision. Combined with non-HVP SM components from Aoyama 2020 the BMW lattice-based SM prediction a_μ^SM,BMW ≈ 116 591 954 × 10⁻¹¹ sits 1.5σ from the FNAL+BNL combined measurement, substantially narrowing the BSM interpretation space of the apparent 4.2σ TI–experiment tension.
citations: Borsanyi et al. 2021 Nature 593:51 (program-internal calculation of LO-HVP, dominant uncertainty component)
Aoyama et al. 2020 Phys Rep 887:1 §8 (non-HVP SM components combined with BMW LO-HVP to yield total SM prediction)

edges.description:
Muon-g-2-continuation's discrimination among the muon-g-2 totality-approach's candidate-targeting programs proceeds via precision measurement of the muon anomalous magnetic moment a_μ = (g_μ - 2)/2. The Fermilab E989 storage-ring experiment has reached 127 ppb relative precision at design completion (Aguillard et al. 2025 PRL 135:101802), corresponding to an absolute uncertainty of ∼1.48 × 10⁻¹⁰ on a_μ ≈ 1.166 × 10⁻³. J-PARC E34 (under construction at the J-PARC Materials and Life Science Experimental Facility) provides a systematics-independent cross-check via the ultra-cold muon storage-ring technique without electric focusing (Abe et al. 2019 PTEP 2019:053C02), eliminating the dominant electric-field-correction systematic of the storage-ring method.

Sensitivity reach: two-sided precision-measurement target on a_μ (a dimensionless number), absolute design-completion uncertainty ≈ 1.48 × 10⁻¹⁰ centered on the experimentally measured value (T33 mint, precision-measurement sensitivity). T33 is structurally distinct from T30 (discovery-floor sensitivity as used in axion-haloscope-network sub-PR 51 and edm-program sub-PR 52): the muon g-2 measurement does NOT exclude above or below a floor but instead PINS a_μ to a two-sided precision range centered on the experimentally measured central value. Future precision-measurement programs that report achieved-precision-of-measurement sensitivities (e.g., FCC-ee Higgs-coupling precision targets, future g-2 of the electron or tau) will inherit T33; the T30/T33 distinction makes 'discovery-floor sensitivity' and 'achieved-precision sensitivity' queryable as semantically separate categories rather than conflated under T30 with direction-of-bound as the only discriminator.

Per-program competing predictions — TWO entries, the project's first measurement-discriminating (rather than bounds-setting) experimental-coverage edge: §4 part (2) admissibility passes cleanly for both the Theory Initiative 2020 dispersive HVP SM prediction (Aoyama et al. 2020 Phys Rep 887:1; central value 116 591 810 × 10⁻¹¹ ± 43 × 10⁻¹¹) and the BMW 2021 lattice HVP SM prediction (Borsanyi et al. 2021 Nature 593:51; central value ≈ 116 591 954 × 10⁻¹¹ with HVP-dominated theoretical uncertainty ∼55 × 10⁻¹¹). Both are explicit program-internal calculations of the two competing candidate-targeting programs on the muon-g-2 carrier (dispersive-method refinement and lattice-QCD HVP refinement, both 'active investigation' in the carrier's loose-ends.candidate_targeting array). The TI–BMW gap in central value is ∼144 × 10⁻¹¹, roughly an order of magnitude larger than the design-completion sensitivity ∼14.8 × 10⁻¹¹ — the experiment discriminates between the two SM-side programs cleanly at design completion, and indeed the Aguillard 2025 measurement is consistent with BMW at ∼1σ while remaining inconsistent with TI 2020 at the original ∼4σ level.

The third candidate-targeting program on the carrier ('new-physics interpretations — dark photons, sleptons, etc.', currently discharge_status: gestural-to-constrained) fails §4 part (2) on the parameter-freedom test: BSM-loop predictions of Δa_μ depend on the specific BSM-model parameter values (mediator mass, coupling strength, dark-photon mixing, slepton spectrum, etc.) and supply no single point prediction at the experimental floor; the BSM-loop literature (Athron et al. 2021 JHEP 09:080; Lindner-Platscher-Queiroz 2018 Phys Rep 731:1) works in model-parameter space rather than yielding a discriminable point prediction of a_μ. This is the standard parameter-space-class pattern that axion-haloscope-network (sub-PR 51) and edm-program (sub-PR 52) handled by recording the entire edge as bounds-setting. The muon-g-2 case differs because the SM-side candidate programs DO supply discriminable point predictions; PPP is populated for the SM-side programs while the BSM-loop family is excluded from PPP and the exclusion is documented in the v90 changelog's firewall_self_check field as a §4(2) finding consistent with the precedent.

First PPP-populated experimental-coverage edge on a totality-approach carrier: all 8 prior PPP-populated edges (hyper-k, dune, juno targeting cells in sm-rep-content / su5-gut-rep-content / neutrino-sector-phenomenology; gw-ground-network, lisa targeting cells in compact-astrophysical-objects) target specific cells with structurally-discrete candidate-foundational program predictions per cell. The muon-g-2 carrier is itself a totality-approach (not a cell), and the SM-side candidate programs deliver point predictions for the same observable (a_μ) rather than per-cell predictions for distinct phenomena. This establishes the precedent for the totality-approach-with-competing-SM-predictions-on-the-same-observable pattern.

Post-2021 trajectory (recorded as a methodological observation, not in scope for this sub-PR): the post-2021 literature has substantially changed the empirical picture. Aliberti et al. 2025 Phys Rep 1143:1 (the Theory Initiative 2025 update) adopts the consolidated lattice-QCD HVP average and shifts the TI SM prediction upward into agreement with the BMW lattice value at the current level of precision, effectively reconciling the 2020 4.2σ tension. Boccaletti et al. 2024 (BMW hybrid) provides a refined LO-HVP value a_μ^LO-HVP = 715.1(2.5)(2.3)[3.4] × 10⁻¹⁰ with reduced uncertainty (×0.6 the original BMW 2021 figure). The muon-g-2 carrier's currently-recorded quantitative_scale (sigma_deviation 4.2σ, citing Abi 2021 + Aoyama 2020 + Borsanyi 2021) and if_real_implies.muon-g2-bsm-loop entry are frozen in the 2021 picture and lag current consensus; refreshing the carrier's representation is a separate authoring decision outside this experimental-coverage sub-PR's scope. This edge records the historical TI-vs-BMW discriminating landscape that the Fermilab E989 + J-PARC E34 experimental programs were designed to address — the structural fact captured by this edge (the experimental program's precision is sufficient to distinguish the two competing SM-side calculations at design completion) is independent of which of the two calculations the post-2021 literature has subsequently consolidated around.

citations: Abazajian et al. (CMB-S4 Collaboration) 2016 'CMB-S4 Science Book, First Edition' arXiv:1610.02743 §2 — design-completion sensitivity σ(r) ~ 5 × 10⁻⁴ targeting the canonical single-field slow-roll inflation prediction band; the r ~ 10⁻³ figure quoted in the cmb-s4 source-node description is the standard combined-program design-floor target at 95% CL from the ground-based CMB-S4 array.

Hazumi et al. (LiteBIRD Collaboration) 2022 'LiteBIRD satellite: JAXA's new strategic L-class mission for all-sky surveys of cosmic microwave background polarization' PTEP 2023:042F01 §2 — the JAXA-led satellite design specifies δr < 10⁻³ at 95% CL via full-sky B-mode polarization measurement, the complementary space-based arm of the combined CMB-S4 + LiteBIRD sensitivity at r ~ 10⁻³.

Abazajian et al. (CMB-S4 Collaboration) 2022 'Snowmass 2021 CMB-S4 White Paper' arXiv:2203.08024 — the Snowmass-era science-case update reaffirming the r ~ 10⁻³ design-completion floor as the threshold for distinguishing single-field slow-roll inflation models from null.

edges.description: Cmb-s4 + LiteBIRD + SO targets the primordial-gravitational-wave Mendeleev empty cell via degree-scale CMB B-mode polarization. The cell records the BICEP/Keck 2021 upper bound r < 0.036 and explicitly anticipates LiteBIRD and CMB-S4 reaching r ~ 10⁻³ sensitivity; this edge formalizes the empirical-discharge pathway. Sensitivity reach: discovery-floor upper bound r ≲ 10⁻³ — r = P_T / P_S is genuinely a ratio of primordial tensor to scalar power spectra at the pivot scale k = 0.05 Mpc⁻¹ (Lyth-Riotto 1999 Phys Rep 314:1, eq. 2.21–2.24), so a dimensionless-ratio encoding is structurally the correct discriminator on this edge even though the cell's existing quantitative bound uses a plain dimensionless encoding (a Step-4.3 legacy resolved at a future cell-refresh sub-PR; recorded as a methodological observation in the v91 changelog). This is the FIRST kind=ratio resolves-edge entry in the project and closes resolves-edge sensitivity-kind coverage to 8/8 of the dimensional and ratio-flavored kinds in the v18 enum (energy_scale remains the only kind absent from the resolves-edge surface — cmb-s4 does NOT directly measure the inflation energy scale V_inf; that is the derived Lyth-bound quantity V_inf^(1/4) ~ 10¹⁶ GeV × (r/0.1)^(1/4), not the cmb-s4 observable). This edge is bounds-setting rather than measurement-discriminating: §4 part (2) admissibility fails for every candidate-targeting program at this cell. Single-field slow-roll inflation produces r in a band parameterized by the inflaton potential shape — the simplest large-field m²φ² potential predicting r ~ 0.13–0.16 is already excluded by BICEP/Keck (Tristram et al. 2022 PRD 105:083524); the Starobinsky R² potential predicts r ~ 0.003–0.005 (Starobinsky 1980 Phys Lett B 91:99); hilltop and Higgs-inflation classes predict r ≲ 10⁻³. Each is a parameter-class family, not a discriminable point prediction at the cmb-s4 floor — the same structural pattern as axion-haloscope (sub-PR 51), edm-program (sub-PR 52), and the BSM-loop family for muon-g-2 (sub-PR 53), all of which were recorded as bounds-setting (rather than measurement-discriminating) on parameter-freedom grounds. What cmb-s4 reaching r ~ 10⁻³ does is RULE OUT the large-field and Starobinsky-adjacent classes (at non-detection) or confirm one of them (at detection), without per-program point-prediction discrimination at the floor itself.

citations: Abazajian et al. (CMB-S4 Collaboration) 2016 'CMB-S4 Science Book, First Edition' arXiv:1610.02743 §3 — design-completion sensitivity σ(f_NL^local) ~ 1 via combined CMB temperature + polarization + large-scale-structure cross-correlation; the design threshold for distinguishing multi-field inflation models (predicting f_NL^local ~ O(1)–O(10) in parameter classes) from single-field slow-roll (predicting f_NL^local ~ O(slow-roll) << 1 via Maldacena 2003 consistency relation).

Abazajian et al. (CMB-S4 Collaboration) 2022 'Snowmass 2021 CMB-S4 White Paper' arXiv:2203.08024 — the Snowmass-era science-case update reaffirming the σ(f_NL^local) ~ 1 design floor and the structural target of falsifying the single-field consistency relation indirectly via tightening upper bounds on multi-field-class candidates.

edges.description: Cmb-s4 + LiteBIRD + SO targets the primordial non-Gaussianity cell via three-point-function and bispectrum measurements of CMB temperature and polarization spectra, supplemented by large-scale-structure cross-correlation. The cell records the Planck 2018 bound |f_NL^local| < 5.0 (Akrami et al. 2020 Astron Astrophys 641:A9) and explicitly anticipates next-generation surveys reaching f_NL ~ O(1) sensitivity to distinguish multi-field from single-field inflation. Sensitivity reach: discovery-floor upper bound |f_NL^local| ≲ 1.0 — f_NL^local is the amplitude of the local-template bispectrum and is a dimensionless parameter rather than a ratio of physical scales; the dimensionless encoding matches both the cell-level recording and the structural semantics of the observable. Exclusion_only=true: §4 part (2) admissibility fails at this cell despite Maldacena's single-field consistency relation giving a CLEAN point prediction. Maldacena 2003 (JHEP 05:013) derives the single-field slow-roll prediction f_NL^local = (5/12)(1 − n_s) ≈ 0.02 in the squeezed limit, which is well BELOW the cmb-s4 σ(f_NL^local) ~ 1 floor — cmb-s4 hitting its floor does NOT discriminate single-field (it merely tightens the non-detection bound without reaching the predicted value). Multi-field inflation model classes — curvaton (Lyth-Wands 2002 Phys Lett B 524:5), modulated reheating (Dvali-Gruzinov-Zaldarriaga 2004 PRD 69:023505), Komatsu 2010 review (Class Quant Grav 27:124010) — predict f_NL^local ~ O(1)–O(10) in parameter-class families, failing §4(2) on parameter-freedom in the same pattern as axion DM model space (sub-PR 51) and BSM EDM models (sub-PR 52). The structural pattern is therefore: cmb-s4 rules out multi-field model classes (at non-detection) while remaining unable to confirm or discriminate the single-field point prediction sitting below the floor. PPP empty under the §4(2) firewall — single-field's prediction is not AT the floor, and multi-field's predictions are parameter classes.

citations: Abazajian et al. (CMB-S4 Collaboration) 2016 'CMB-S4 Science Book, First Edition' arXiv:1610.02743 §4 — design-completion sensitivity σ(Σm_ν) ~ 0.03 eV (30 meV) via gravitational-lensing reconstruction from CMB temperature and polarization spectra, combined with BAO and large-scale-structure measurements to break parameter degeneracies; the design threshold for ~3σ discrimination of the normal-hierarchy lower bound Σm_ν > 0.059 eV from null.

Ade et al. (Simons Observatory Collaboration) 2019 'The Simons Observatory: Science goals and forecasts' JCAP 02:056 §3 — the Simons Observatory forecast σ(Σm_ν) ~ 0.04 eV at design completion, combined with the LiteBIRD large-angle polarization optical-depth measurement to reach the σ ~ 0.03 eV combined-program floor.

edges.description: Cmb-s4 + LiteBIRD + SO targets the cosmological sum-of-neutrino-masses cell via CMB lensing reconstruction from temperature and polarization power spectra (the structure-suppression signature of massive neutrinos free-streaming during structure formation). The cell records the Planck 2018 + BAO + lensing bound Σm_ν < 0.12 eV (Aghanim et al. 2020 Astron Astrophys 641:A6) and explicitly carries an OVERLAP marker to neutrino-sector-phenomenology cell-nu-Sum-mass-Planck; this edge attaches to the cosmology-native cell per the gw-ground-network and DESI precedent (source-program's native-FC cell rather than the cross-FC mirror). Sensitivity reach: discovery-floor upper bound Σm_ν ≲ 0.03 eV — design-completion σ(Σm_ν) ~ 30 meV is sufficient for ~3σ discrimination of the normal-hierarchy oscillation-implied lower bound Σm_ν > 0.059 eV (Esteban-Gonzalez-Garcia-Maltoni-Schwetz-Zhou 2020 JHEP 09:178 NuFIT 5.2) from null. This edge is bounds-setting rather than measurement-discriminating: §4 part (2) admissibility fails at this cell on STRUCTURAL-DISCIPLINE grounds rather than parameter-freedom. The NH and IH lower bounds (NH: Σm_ν > 0.059 eV; IH: Σm_ν > 0.099 eV) derived from atmospheric and solar oscillation parameters are SM-neutrino-sector parameter constraints, not candidate-foundational program predictions in the §4(2) sense — the carrier cell does not host a candidate_targeting array (cells don't, by schema design — only frontiers and totality-approaches carry candidate_targeting per the v17 spec). The structural locus where NH/IH discrimination operates as a per-program prediction is the neutrino-sector-phenomenology FC cells, not this cosmology cell. cmb-s4 reaching σ(Σm_ν) ~ 30 meV therefore discriminates between hierarchy hypotheses observationally without discriminating between candidate-foundational programs — the §4(2) test fails on the locus where candidate-foundational program predictions live, not on the physics. PPP empty under the firewall.

citations: The LSST Dark Energy Science Collaboration (Mandelbaum, Eifler, Hložek, et al.) 2018 'The LSST Dark Energy Science Collaboration (DESC) Science Requirements Document' arXiv:1809.01669 (source-value: Y10 figure-of-merit FoM ≳ 500 in the (w_0, w_a) plane from joint 3×2pt (cosmic shear + galaxy clustering + galaxy-galaxy lensing) + photometric supernovae + cluster counts, corresponding to roughly σ(w_0) ≈ 0.01 and σ(w_a) ≈ 0.04 in the joint constraint at the joint-probe Y10 design floor; FoM definition §5 of the SRD). Represents an order-of-magnitude tightening over DESI Year-1 σ(w_0) ≈ 0.07, σ(w_a) ≈ 0.27.

Ivezić Ž. et al. (LSST Science Collaboration) 2019 'LSST: From Science Drivers to Reference Design and Anticipated Data Products' Astrophysical Journal 873:111 §13 (Dark energy) — design-completion 10-year survey area 18,000 sq deg, depth r ≈ 27.5 single-visit / ≈ 27.8 stacked, ~825 visits per field across ugrizy bands, supporting the 3×2pt analysis at the precision floor recorded here.

DESI Collaboration (Adame et al.) 2024 'DESI 2024 III: baryon acoustic oscillations from galaxies and quasars' arXiv:2404.03000 §6 — DESI Year-1 σ(w_0) ≈ 0.07 and σ(w_a) ≈ 0.27 joint constraint as the current-state comparison floor that Rubin Y10 supersedes; the ~10× tightening is the discriminator-relevant precision improvement.

predictions_per_program.program: ΛCDM (Einstein cosmological constant, w = −1 exactly)

citations: Aghanim N. et al. (Planck Collaboration) 2020 'Planck 2018 results. VI. Cosmological parameters' Astron. Astrophys. 641:A6 arXiv:1807.06209 §6.3 — base-ΛCDM parameter set fixes w_0 = −1 exactly and w_a = 0 by construction; the cosmological-constant term in the Einstein equations contributes equation-of-state w = −1 identically. The program-internal value is the structural fact of the ΛCDM model rather than a calculation: w_0 = −1, w_a = 0 are not fit parameters but structural inputs to the base-ΛCDM cosmology.

predictions_per_program.description: ΛCDM predicts w_0 = −1 exactly and w_a = 0 by construction; the cosmological constant is a constant. Rubin Y10 σ(w_0) ≈ 0.01 distinguishes this from quintessence-class predictions at ≳ several-σ if w_0 ≠ −1 at the percent level.
citations: Aghanim N. et al. (Planck Collaboration) 2020 Astron. Astrophys. 641:A6 §6.3 (base-ΛCDM structural definition with w_0 = −1, w_a = 0 by construction)

predictions_per_program.program: Quintessence (thawing-vs-freezing class, scalar-field dynamical dark energy)

citations: Caldwell R.R., Linder E.V. 2005 'The limits of quintessence' Phys. Rev. Lett. 95:141301 arXiv:astro-ph/0505494 — thawing vs freezing quintessence-class distinction: thawing models predict w_0 > −1 with w_a < 0 (the field begins frozen at w ≈ −1 and 'thaws' to evolve away as the universe expands); freezing models predict w_0 > −1 with w_a > 0 (the field is rolling and slows toward an attractor near w = −1). The characteristic value w_0 ≈ −0.95 ± 0.05 (with substantial model-class spread on the lower side toward w_0 ≈ −0.85) covers the thawing-class range across scalar-field-potential choices.

Linder E.V. 2003 'Exploring the expansion history of the universe' Phys. Rev. Lett. 90:091301 arXiv:astro-ph/0208512 — the canonical (w_0, w_a) parametrization w(a) = w_0 + w_a(1−a) used in DESC SRD §5 and in DESI Year-1 evolving-DE analyses; the parametrization is the joint linear two-parameter approximation to scalar-field-quintessence w(z) trajectories near z = 0.

predictions_per_program.description: Quintessence-class scalar-field models predict w_0 > −1 at the percent level in the (w_0, w_a) joint parameter space, with thawing-class predicting w_a < 0 and freezing-class predicting w_a > 0. Rubin Y10 σ(w_0) ≈ 0.01 distinguishes quintessence's w_0 ≠ −1 from ΛCDM's w_0 = −1 at the percent level — the program-internal class spread spans w_0 ∈ [−1.0, −0.85] depending on the scalar potential and initial conditions.
citations: Caldwell-Linder 2005 PRL 95:141301 (thawing/freezing class definitions and characteristic value ranges)
Linder 2003 PRL 90:091301 (the canonical w_0-w_a parametrization)

predictions_per_program.program: Modified gravity (f(R), DGP brane-world, late-time IR modification)

citations: Sotiriou T.P., Faraoni V. 2010 'f(R) theories of gravity' Rev. Mod. Phys. 82:451 arXiv:0805.1726 §IV.C — f(R) gravity background expansion can mimic ΛCDM at the percent level (w_0 ≈ −1 ± 0.05 across f(R) model-class) but predicts distinct growth-rate vs expansion-rate ratio splits in the 3×2pt framework. The discriminator from ΛCDM is NOT primarily w_0 (which is nearly degenerate) but the modified-gravity-distinct growth-index γ_eff = d ln δ/d ln a deviation from the GR value γ ≈ 0.55.

Abbott T.M.C. et al. (DES Collaboration) 2023 'Dark Energy Survey Year 3 results: Constraints on extensions to ΛCDM with weak lensing and galaxy clustering' Phys. Rev. D 107:083504 arXiv:2207.05766 §V — DES Y3 modified-gravity constraints in the joint (μ_0, Σ_0) phenomenological-MG parametrization: μ_0 = 0.04 ± 0.32, Σ_0 = 0.05 ± 0.08 at 68% CL. Rubin Y10 forecasts ~5–10× tightening on these growth-vs-expansion split parameters via the 3×2pt growth-rate measurement.

predictions_per_program.description: Modified-gravity models can mimic ΛCDM's w_0 ≈ −1 background expansion to the percent level but predict distinct growth-rate vs expansion-rate ratio splits in the 3×2pt framework. Rubin Y10 distinguishes modified-gravity from ΛCDM not primarily via w_0 (degenerate) but via the modified-gravity-distinct growth-index γ_eff deviation from the GR value γ ≈ 0.55; the (μ_0, Σ_0) phenomenological-MG parameters reach ~5–10× tighter constraints than DES Y3.
citations: Sotiriou-Faraoni 2010 RMP 82:451 §IV.C (f(R) gravity-class background w_0 ≈ −1 with growth-rate discriminator)
DES Collaboration (Abbott et al.) 2023 PRD 107:083504 §V (DES Y3 (μ_0, Σ_0) MG constraints; Rubin tightening factor)

edges.description: Rubin Y10 targets the cosmological-constant frontier via the joint 3×2pt (cosmic shear + galaxy clustering + galaxy-galaxy lensing) + photometric supernovae + cluster counts probe combination, reaching figure-of-merit FoM ≳ 500 in the (w_0, w_a) plane per DESC SRD §5 — equivalent to σ(w_0) ≈ 0.01 and σ(w_a) ≈ 0.04 at the joint-probe Y10 design floor. This is a ~10× tightening over DESI Year-1 σ(w_0) ≈ 0.07 / σ(w_a) ≈ 0.27 (Adame et al. 2024 DESI BAO Year-1) which already disfavors w(z) ≡ −1 ΛCDM at ≳ 2σ via the joint constraint. The Rubin Y10 floor is sufficient to distinguish ΛCDM (w_0 = −1 by construction) from quintessence-class scalar-field models (w_0 > −1 at the percent level in joint w_0–w_a space) at ≳ several-σ when w_0 deviates from −1 at the percent level. Modified-gravity models (f(R), DGP, late-time IR modification) reach Rubin Y10 via the distinct growth-vs-expansion-rate split in the 3×2pt framework — the (μ_0, Σ_0) phenomenological-MG parametrization tightens ~5–10× over DES Y3 (Abbott et al. 2023 PRD 107:083504). Sensitivity reach: dimensionless two-sided precision-measurement target σ(w_0) ≈ 0.01 around the ΛCDM reference value w_0 = −1 (T33 reuse from sub-PR 53 muon-g-2-continuation — precision-measurement sensitivity bounding deviation from a reference value in either direction). This edge is measurement-discriminating rather than bounds-setting; PPP populated with 3 candidate-foundational programs (ΛCDM, quintessence-class, modified-gravity) per §4 admissibility test part (2): each PPP entry cites the program-internal calculation deriving w_0 from program structure. The PARALLEL to desi's edge-rs-desi-cc-frontier (sigma_deviation 5σ discovery floor) is intentional — Rubin and DESI both target cc-frontier with DISTINCT methodologies (Rubin: WL + SNIa + clustering 3×2pt; DESI: BAO sound-horizon-at-drag-epoch) at distinct precision-floor encodings (Rubin: σ(w_0) ≈ 0.01 dimensionless precision; DESI: 5σ sigma_deviation discovery). Together they constitute the project's first instance of TWO independent experimental-program nodes targeting the same frontier with DIFFERENT sensitivity-encoding kinds — a parallel-resolves-edge pattern signal for the changelog. Second project-wide measurement-discriminating PPP-populated experimental-coverage edge after sub-PR 53 muon-g-2-continuation; first such edge on a cosmological frontier.

citations: The LSST Dark Energy Science Collaboration (Mandelbaum, Eifler, Hložek, et al.) 2018 'The LSST Dark Energy Science Collaboration (DESC) Science Requirements Document' arXiv:1809.01669 §5.2 (cosmic shear + 3×2pt structure-growth) — Y10 sub-percent precision on the cosmic-shear amplitude with σ(S_8) ~ 0.005 (S_8 ≡ σ_8 √(Ω_m/0.3)) achievable in the joint 3×2pt framework at design-completion 10-year coverage of 18,000 sq deg, ~37 sources arcmin⁻² effective number density.

Ivezić Ž. et al. (LSST Science Collaboration) 2019 'LSST: From Science Drivers to Reference Design and Anticipated Data Products' Astrophysical Journal 873:111 §3 (weak gravitational lensing) — design-completion shape-noise floor and source-density ~37 arcmin⁻² supporting the σ(S_8) ≈ 0.005 precision floor.

Abbott T.M.C. et al. (DES Collaboration) 2022 'Dark Energy Survey Year 3 results: Cosmological constraints from galaxy clustering and weak lensing' Phys. Rev. D 105:023520 arXiv:2105.13549 — DES Y3 σ(S_8) ≈ 0.017 current-state precision; Rubin Y10 ≈ 3–4× tighter, the discriminator-relevant improvement on the σ_8 / S_8 tension currently observed at 2–3σ between CMB-inferred (Planck) and WL-direct measurements.

edges.description: Rubin Y10 cosmic-shear measurement targets the σ_8 from weak-lensing cell via the joint 3×2pt (cosmic shear + galaxy clustering + galaxy-galaxy lensing) framework reaching σ(S_8) ≈ 0.005 sub-percent precision per DESC SRD §5.2 — a 3–4× tightening over DES Y3 σ(S_8) ≈ 0.017 (Abbott et al. 2022 PRD 105:023520). The cell records the current observed tension S_8 ≈ 0.77 (KiDS-1000, DES-Y3, HSC-Y3 average) vs Planck-CMB-inferred S_8 ≈ 0.83, at ~2–3σ significance. Rubin Y10 will either (a) confirm the tension at ≳ 5σ at the sub-percent precision floor, indicating new physics in the late-universe / dark-sector / structure-formation regime, or (b) resolve the tension via reduced WL-systematic floor (photo-z calibration, intrinsic-alignment modeling, baryonic feedback) at sub-percent significance — discriminating BETWEEN the two interpretations is exactly what the Rubin Y10 precision floor delivers. Sensitivity reach: dimensionless two-sided precision-measurement target σ(S_8) ≈ 0.005 around the Planck-CMB-inferred reference value (T33 reuse from sub-PR 53 muon-g-2-continuation and edge A this sub-PR — precision-measurement sensitivity bounding deviation from a reference value in either direction). This edge is bounds-setting rather than measurement-discriminating; no per-program point predictions are recorded. §4 admissibility test part (2) fails: the candidate-foundational programs for resolving the σ_8/S_8 tension split into (i) late-universe dark-sector new physics (interacting DE/DM, decaying DM, neutrino-mass effects) and (ii) systematic-on-WL-side resolution (improved photo-z calibration, intrinsic-alignment modeling, baryonic-feedback modeling). Class (ii) does not produce point predictions on σ_8 — it produces SYSTEMATIC-error revisions to the measured value. Class (i) produces point predictions in principle but the dataset's cosmological-observations.loose_ends.candidate_targeting array does not host explicit point-prediction PPP entries for these BSM programs at the sub-percent S_8 precision level (the candidate_targeting structure is at the carrier level, not the cell-level S_8 resolution level). Per the cmb-s4 sub-PR 54 precedent (Σm_ν, recorded as bounds-setting with structural-discipline §4(2) failure on STRUCTURAL-LOCUS grounds), this edge is also bounds-setting on this cell. The tension significance is the operationally-meaningful Rubin Y10 measurement outcome, not the BSM-vs-systematic class discrimination.

citations: Drlica-Wagner A. et al. 2019 'Probing the Fundamental Nature of Dark Matter with the Vera C. Rubin Observatory' arXiv:1902.01055 §III (warm dark matter and satellite census) — Rubin Y10 projects full Milky Way satellite-galaxy census detection at ≳5× the current Galactic-satellite count (DES + PS1 + classical), with the resulting subhalo mass-function (SHMF) constraint reaching thermal-relic warm-DM mass lower bound m_WDM ≳ 6 keV at 95% CL, a factor ~2 improvement on current Galactic-satellite-census + flux-ratio-anomaly bounds (~3 keV from Nadler et al. 2021 PRL 126:091101).

Nadler E.O. et al. 2021 'Milky Way Satellite Census. III. Constraints on Dark Matter Properties from Observations of Milky Way Satellite Galaxies' Phys. Rev. Lett. 126:091101 arXiv:2008.00022 — current pre-Rubin Galactic-satellite-census constraint m_WDM ≳ 6.5 keV (95% CL) from DES + PS1 + classical satellites + Milky Way potential model; Rubin Y10 supersedes via factor ~5 increase in satellite detection completeness plus deeper / fainter low-mass-subhalo coverage.

Newton O. et al. 2021 'Constraints on the properties of warm dark matter using the satellite galaxies of the Milky Way' JCAP 08:062 arXiv:2011.08865 — alternative-methodology WDM constraint at m_WDM ≳ 9 keV under different MW-potential / subhalo-mass-function assumptions; Rubin Y10 will tighten across the assumption space.

edges.description: Rubin Y10 targets warm-dark-matter mass via the full Milky Way satellite-galaxy census combined with stellar-stream gap-perturbation analysis and strong-lensing flux-ratio anomalies (Drlica-Wagner et al. 2019 §III). The satellite-galaxy detection efficiency at r ≈ 27.5 single-visit depth recovers a factor ~5 more satellites than DES + PS1 + classical surveys (mass function down to M_halo ~ 10⁶ M_sun), with the resulting subhalo mass function (SHMF) constraint projecting m_WDM ≳ 6 keV (95% CL) thermal-relic warm-DM mass lower bound. This tightens the current pre-Rubin Nadler et al. 2021 bound m_WDM ≳ 6.5 keV (cumulative Galactic satellites + Milky Way potential model) by ~2× in completeness-driven uncertainty. The targeted cell records sterile-neutrino warm-DM in the keV mass range, with the Bulbul et al. 2014 contested-X-ray-line 3.5 keV claim as a sub-claim — Rubin's m_WDM lower bound is a structurally distinct measurement-direction (cosmological-structure constraint on the WDM mass scale) from the X-ray-line direct-detection claim (positive-evidence claim contested by Dessert-Rodd-Safdi 2020 + XRISM 2025); both belong on this cell because the cell is parameterized by m_WDM ≈ keV-scale, the parameter space they probe in common. Sensitivity reach: lower bound m_WDM ≳ 6.0 keV from one-sided structure-formation constraint (small-scale-power suppression from WDM free-streaming would damp below the Rubin-observed satellite-population completeness floor). This edge is bounds-setting rather than measurement-discriminating; no per-program point predictions are recorded. §4 admissibility test part (2) fails on the same parameter-space-without-program-point-predictions grounds as sub-PR 51 (axion-haloscope-network), sub-PR 52 (edm-program) — the candidate-foundational programs for WDM (thermal-relic sterile-neutrino, Dodelson-Widrow sterile-neutrino, generic-keV-scale fermion DM) do not supply specific m_WDM point predictions discriminable at Rubin's Y10 satellite-census precision; they parameterize the m_WDM mass scale and the survey constrains the parameter from below.

citations: Drlica-Wagner A. et al. 2019 'Probing the Fundamental Nature of Dark Matter with the Vera C. Rubin Observatory' arXiv:1902.01055 §V (primordial black holes and macroscopic dark matter) — Rubin Y10 projects sensitivity to primordial-black-hole dark-matter fraction f_PBH at the 10⁻² level across the asteroid-mass-to-planet-mass window (~10¹⁷ g to ~10²⁵ g) via photometric monitoring of LSST-cadence stellar variability + microlensing-event detection of stars in the Milky Way bulge and disk over the 10-year survey; represents ~10× tightening over current Subaru/HSC M31 single-night-cadence bounds (Niikura et al. 2019 ~10⁻¹).

LSST Science Collaboration 2009 'LSST Science Book, Version 2.0' arXiv:0912.0201 §13 (transients and variables) — design-completion ~10⁹ stellar light-curves monitored at ~3-day median cadence supports microlensing-event detection across event-duration timescales (~minutes to ~years) covering the asteroid-mass-to-stellar-mass primordial-black-hole lens-mass window.

Niikura H. et al. 2019 'Microlensing constraints on primordial black holes with Subaru/HSC Andromeda observations' Nature Astronomy 3:524 arXiv:1701.02151 — current pre-Rubin best bound f_PBH ≲ 0.1 in the (10¹⁷–10²²) g asteroid-mass window from Subaru HSC M31 dense-cadence (~2-min sampling) observations; the cell's existing quantitative_scale anchor M_PBH ≲ 10²¹ g viability window.

edges.description: Rubin Y10 targets the primordial-black-hole asteroid-mass viability cell via photometric microlensing-event detection in the Milky Way bulge and disk: ~10⁹ stellar light-curves monitored across the 10-year survey at ~3-day median cadence covers microlensing event-durations corresponding to lens masses from ~10¹⁷ g (~minute-duration events) through ~10⁰ M_sun (~year-duration events), spanning the asteroid-mass-to-stellar-mass primordial-black-hole window. The Drlica-Wagner et al. 2019 §V projection at f_PBH ≲ 10⁻² in the asteroid-mass window represents a ~10× tightening on the current Subaru/HSC M31 single-cadence Niikura et al. 2019 bound f_PBH ≲ 0.1; combined with the existing cell anchor (M_PBH ≲ 10²¹ g asteroid-mass-window viability) from Subaru/HSC, Rubin Y10 sharpens the upper bound on the asteroid-mass-PBH-as-DM fraction. Sensitivity reach: dimensionless upper bound f_PBH ≲ 0.01 (asteroid-mass-window). This edge is bounds-setting rather than measurement-discriminating; no per-program point predictions are recorded. §4 admissibility test part (2) fails on parameter-space-without-program-point-predictions grounds as in edge C: candidate-foundational PBH-formation programs (inflation-driven density-perturbation-collapse, phase-transition-driven domain-wall-collapse, cosmic-string-loop collapse) do not produce point predictions on f_PBH at specific PBH-masses discriminable at Rubin's precision; they parameterize f_PBH(M_PBH) and the survey constrains the parameter from above. The bound applies across the (~10¹⁷ g, ~10²² g) asteroid-mass window with strongest constraint near 10²⁰ g.

citations: Stapf B., Selvaggi M., Mangano M., Helsens C., Cardini A. 2023 'Higgs self-coupling measurements at the FCC-hh' arXiv:2312.03513 — combined-channel (bbγγ + bbℓℓ+E_T^miss) precision on the Higgs self-coupling modifier κ_λ ≡ λ_HHH / λ_HHH^SM reaching σ(κ_λ) = 3.2–5.7% at 68% CL depending on systematic-uncertainty assumptions, at √s = 100 TeV with 30 ab⁻¹ integrated luminosity assuming all other Higgs couplings follow SM expectations. The bbγγ channel drives the precision; the bbℓℓ+E_T^miss channel alone reaches σ(κ_λ) ≈ 20%. Encoded value 0.05 is the central-systematic-assumption point in the 3.2–5.7% range.

Bernardi G., Brost E., Denegri D., et al. 2022 'FCC-ee: physics motivations' Eur. Phys. J. Plus 137:46 (also arXiv:2110.15084) §4 (Higgs sector) — FCC-ee single-Higgs-NLO indirect bound on δκ_λ via ZH cross-section at two energy points (240 + 350 GeV): σ(δκ_λ) = 42% (FCC-ee alone), reduced to 33% in combination with HL-LHC. The lepton-stage indirect measurement is complementary to the hadron-stage di-Higgs direct measurement; the full FCC program reaches σ(κ_λ) ≈ 3–5% via the FCC-hh stage.

Abada A. et al. (FCC Collaboration) 2019 'FCC-hh: The Hadron Collider — Future Circular Collider Conceptual Design Report Volume 3' Eur. Phys. J. ST 228:755 §3.2 (Higgs sector benchmarks) — FCC-hh CDR design-completion projection σ(κ_λ) ≈ 5% from di-Higgs production via gluon-gluon fusion, consistent with the more recent Stapf 2023 detailed-channel analysis.

predictions_per_program.program: Standard Model (NLO)

citations: Frederix R., Frixione S., Hirschi V., Maltoni F., Mattelaer O., Torrielli P., Vryonidou E., Zaro M. 2014 'Higgs pair production at the LHC with NLO and parton-shower effects' Phys. Lett. B 732:142 arXiv:1401.7340 — NLO QCD calculation of the di-Higgs production cross-section in the SM, with κ_λ ≡ λ_HHH/λ_HHH^SM = 1 by construction since the SM Higgs trilinear coupling λ_HHH = 3 m_H²/v is fixed by the Higgs mass and the electroweak vacuum expectation value. The 'predicted value' is the structural fact of the SM Higgs sector rather than a free parameter.

Maltoni F., Pagani D., Shivaji A., Zhao X. 2018 'Trilinear Higgs coupling determination via single-Higgs differential measurements at the LHC' Eur. Phys. J. C 78:937 arXiv:1806.07101 — single-Higgs NLO calculation establishing the same κ_λ = 1 SM-reference value for indirect single-Higgs measurements at lepton-collider precision.

predictions_per_program.description: The SM trilinear Higgs coupling is fixed by structural constraints (m_H = 125 GeV, v = 246 GeV) to λ_HHH = 3 m_H²/v ≈ 191 GeV at tree level; NLO corrections are at the few-percent level. The conventional Higgs-self-coupling modifier κ_λ = λ_HHH/λ_HHH^SM equals 1 by construction in the SM. FCC-hh σ(κ_λ) ≈ 5% precision distinguishes the SM value from BSM modifications at the percent level.
citations: Frederix et al. 2014 Phys. Lett. B 732:142 arXiv:1401.7340 (di-Higgs NLO QCD)
Maltoni et al. 2018 Eur. Phys. J. C 78:937 arXiv:1806.07101 (single-Higgs indirect κ_λ extraction)

predictions_per_program.program: Composite Higgs (pseudo-Nambu-Goldstone Higgs from strong sector)

citations: Gillioz M., Grober R., Grojean C., Muhlleitner M., Salvioni E. 2012 'Higgs Low-Energy Theorem (and its corrections) in Composite Models' JHEP 10:004 arXiv:1206.7120 — pseudo-Nambu-Goldstone-boson Higgs class predicts κ_λ = (1 - 2ξ)/√(1-ξ) at leading order in ξ ≡ v²/f², where f is the compositeness scale; for ξ = 0.1 (f ≈ 780 GeV, the upper-edge of currently-allowed compositeness from LHC EW-precision constraints), κ_λ ≈ 0.84; for ξ = 0.05 (f ≈ 1.1 TeV), κ_λ ≈ 0.93. The asymmetric uncertainty {low: 0.1, high: 0.05} on the encoded value 0.85 captures the canonical composite-Higgs benchmark range from current LHC-allowed ξ.

Contino R., Marzocca D., Pomarol A., Rattazzi R. 2011 'Implications of an improved sensitivity to Higgs couplings' JHEP 06:008 arXiv:1102.3866 — SILH (Strongly-Interacting Light Higgs) effective-Lagrangian formalism with explicit κ_λ derivation from the c̄_6 SILH operator coefficient, deriving the same ξ-dependent κ_λ deviation pattern.

predictions_per_program.description: Composite-Higgs models with the Higgs as a pseudo-Nambu-Goldstone boson of a spontaneously broken global symmetry in a strongly-coupled sector predict κ_λ < 1 with deviation set by ξ ≡ v²/f² where f is the compositeness scale. For ξ = 0.1 (f ≈ 780 GeV, near the current LHC-allowed boundary), κ_λ ≈ 0.84. FCC-hh σ(κ_λ) ≈ 5% precision distinguishes composite-Higgs benchmarks from SM at ≳ 3σ across the ξ ∈ [0.05, 0.15] canonical-allowed range.
citations: Gillioz et al. 2012 JHEP 10:004 (κ_λ in composite-Higgs class with explicit ξ-dependence)
Contino et al. 2011 JHEP 06:008 (SILH effective-Lagrangian derivation)

edges.description: FCC targets the hierarchy problem at the carrier level via precision measurement of the Higgs trilinear coupling modifier κ_λ ≡ λ_HHH/λ_HHH^SM. FCC-hh's combined-channel di-Higgs analysis (bbγγ + bbℓℓ+E_T^miss, 30 ab⁻¹ at √s = 100 TeV) reaches σ(κ_λ) = 3.2–5.7% at 68% CL depending on systematic-uncertainty assumptions (Stapf et al. 2023 arXiv:2312.03513); the FCC-ee lepton stage contributes complementary single-Higgs-NLO indirect bounds at σ(δκ_λ) ≈ 33–42% (Bernardi et al. 2022 EPJ Plus 137:46). The Higgs self-coupling is the load-bearing observable of the Higgs-potential shape, which discriminates between SM (κ_λ = 1 by construction) and composite-Higgs BSM scenarios (κ_λ < 1, deviation set by compositeness scale ξ ≡ v²/f²). Sensitivity reach: dimensionless precision-measurement target, two-sided ±0.05 on κ_λ around the SM reference value (T33 reuse from sub-PR 53 muon-g-2-continuation and sub-PR 56 rubin-lsst — precision-measurement sensitivity bounding deviation from a reference value in either direction). Exclusion_only=false; PPP populated with two programs (SM at κ_λ = 1; composite-Higgs at κ_λ ≈ 0.85 ± {0.10, 0.05}) per §4 admissibility test part (2): each PPP entry cites program-internal calculations. SUSY's κ_λ prediction depends on tan β + stop-mass spectrum + decoupling-limit assumptions and does not yield a single discriminating point prediction at FCC precision; SUSY is intentionally not in PPP. The hierarchy-problem frontier already carries if_real_implies entries for SUSY-at-TeV and composite-Higgs (v17 surface, see hierarchy-problem.if_real_implies); this edge's PPP composite-Higgs entry shares the same candidate-foundational-program identity as the if_real_implies composite-Higgs resolution, providing cross-surface internal consistency.

citations: Ellis S.A.R., Zheng B. 2015 'Reaching for Squarks and Gauginos at a 100 TeV p-p Collider' Phys. Rev. D 92:075034 arXiv:1506.02644 — at √s = 100 TeV with 3 ab⁻¹ integrated luminosity, squark discovery reach (5σ significance) extends to m_squark ≈ 32 TeV in non-compressed spectra (large gluino-LSP mass splitting) via squark-gluino associated production, and to m_squark ≈ 37 TeV in compressed spectra (small gluino-LSP mass splitting). The 30 TeV encoded value is the lower edge of this range as the conservative single-number reach across spectrum-compression assumptions.

Mangano M.L. (ed.) 2017 'Physics at the FCC-hh, a 100 TeV pp collider' CERN Yellow Report CERN-2017-003 arXiv:1710.06353 — FCC-hh BSM-reach summary across SUSY (squarks, gluinos, stops, electroweakinos), composite-Higgs (top partners, gauge resonances), and heavy-gauge-boson (Z′) channels; gluino discovery reach 5σ in MSSM benchmarks at ~12–14 TeV depending on luminosity, squark reach 30+ TeV in associated production. The collection-level summary anchors the program-wide 'tens of TeV' direct-discovery reach that distinguishes FCC-hh from LHC by an order of magnitude.

Abada A. et al. (FCC Collaboration) 2019 'FCC-hh: The Hadron Collider — Future Circular Collider Conceptual Design Report Volume 3' Eur. Phys. J. ST 228:755 §4–§6 (BSM searches) — FCC-hh CDR design-document BSM-reach projections across multiple BSM model classes; gluino reach 10–14 TeV (5σ discovery), squark reach 30+ TeV in associated production, stop reach 10–13 TeV (5σ discovery), all at 30 ab⁻¹.

edges.description: FCC-hh targets the hierarchy problem at the carrier level via direct production of strongly-interacting BSM particles (squarks, gluinos, stops, top partners, Z′) at √s = 100 TeV with 30 ab⁻¹. The discovery reach (5σ significance, conservative single-number across SUSY-spectrum-compression assumptions) extends to particle masses ~30 TeV in squark-gluino associated production (Ellis-Zheng 2015 PRD 92:075034), an order of magnitude beyond HL-LHC's ~3 TeV reach. Specific channel-level reaches: gluinos 10–14 TeV (5σ MSSM benchmark), squarks 30+ TeV (associated production), stops 10–13 TeV, electroweakinos 1.7–6 TeV depending on representation. Sensitivity reach: lower bound m_BSM ≳ 30 TeV from one-sided exclusion — if no signal is observed, the inferred lower bound on the BSM-particle mass is m_BSM > 30 TeV in the associated-production channel. Exclusion_only=true; PPP empty. §4 admissibility test part (2) fails on parameter-space-without-program-point-predictions grounds (sub-PR 51 axion-haloscope-network, sub-PR 52 edm-program, sub-PR 56 rubin-lsst edges B/C/D precedents): the candidate-foundational programs for the hierarchy problem (SUSY, composite Higgs, extra dimensions, anthropic landscape, cosmological relaxation) parameterize the BSM mass scale rather than supplying single discriminating point predictions of squark/gluino/stop masses at FCC precision. SUSY benchmark scenarios cover m_squark ∈ [TeV-LHC-edge, MPl-decoupled] with no single point prediction; composite-Higgs top partners are similarly parameter-space-distributed. The discriminating power lives in FCC-hh's sensitivity reach extending across the parameter space, not in pre-computed program-internal predicted values.

citations: Saito M., Sawada R., Terashi K., Asai S. 2019 'Discovery reach for wino and higgsino dark matter with a disappearing track signature at a 100 TeV pp collider' Eur. Phys. J. C 79:469 arXiv:1901.02987 — FCC-hh disappearing-track search for chargino-neutralino mass-degenerate pure-wino and pure-higgsino DM via the disappearing charged-wino-track signature (decay length ~6 cm for wino, ~7 mm for higgsino). At √s = 100 TeV with 30 ab⁻¹ and optimized inner-tracker layouts (pixel-hit-level 50 ps timing for pileup ⟨μ⟩ = 500), the 5σ discovery reach extends to m_χ ≈ 3 TeV (wino) and m_χ ≈ 1 TeV (higgsino), both reaching the thermal-relic-DM mass limits. The encoded value 3 TeV anchors the wino-DM thermal-mass coverage as the higher of the two thermal-WIMP-DM benchmarks.

Han T., Liu Z., Zhao L.-T., Zhu K. 2018 'Electroweak Higgsinos, Neutralinos, and Sleptons at the LHC and 100 TeV pp Colliders' Phys. Rev. D 97:055007 arXiv:1612.02387 — complementary FCC-hh missing-transverse-momentum + soft-track + dilepton searches for higgsino-DM extending coverage of the m_χ ~ 1 TeV thermal-higgsino region.

Low M., Wang L.-T. 2014 'Neutralino Dark Matter at 14 TeV and 100 TeV' JHEP 08:161 arXiv:1404.0682 — foundational 100 TeV collider study of neutralino-DM reach across wino, higgsino, and mixed-state mass spectra at the canonical thermal-relic benchmark masses.

predictions_per_program.program: Thermal pure-wino LSP (anomaly-mediated SUSY breaking)

citations: Hisano J., Matsumoto S., Nojiri M.M., Saito O. 2007 'Non-perturbative effect on dark matter annihilation and gamma ray signature from galactic center' Phys. Rev. D 71:063528 arXiv:hep-ph/0412403 — early Sommerfeld-corrected wino-DM relic-abundance calculation establishing m_wino^thermal ≈ 2.7–2.9 TeV including non-perturbative electroweak Sommerfeld enhancement of the (co)annihilation cross-section into W+W-.

Beneke M., Hellmann C., Ruiz-Femenia P. 2015 'Heavy neutralino relic abundance with Sommerfeld enhancements — a study of pMSSM scenarios' JHEP 03:162 arXiv:1411.6930 — refined NLO Sommerfeld-corrected thermal wino-DM mass calculation m_wino^thermal = 2.87 ± 0.04 TeV combining Coulomb + electroweak Sommerfeld effects, bound-state-formation corrections, and full NLO matching. The 2.9 TeV encoded benchmark with ±0.1 TeV uncertainty captures this precision and the spread across alternative Sommerfeld treatments (Cohen et al. 2013, Hryczuk-Iengo-Ullio 2011).

Cohen T., Lisanti M., Pierce A., Slatyer T.R. 2013 'Wino Dark Matter Under Siege' JCAP 10:061 arXiv:1307.4082 — independent thermal-wino-mass calculation m_wino^thermal ≈ 2.7–2.8 TeV cross-validating the Beneke et al. range; also articulates the indirect-detection tension that complements the collider reach.

predictions_per_program.description: Pure SU(2)_L triplet Majorana fermion (the SUSY wino, with Z_2-stabilized neutral component as DM candidate). Cosmological thermal-relic abundance Ω_χh² = 0.12 fixes m_χ via Sommerfeld-enhanced (co)annihilation into W⁺W⁻ pairs; canonical value m_wino^thermal = 2.87 ± 0.04 TeV (Beneke et al. 2015 JHEP 03:162), with spread ±0.1 TeV across alternative Sommerfeld-correction treatments. Anomaly-mediated SUSY breaking (AMSB) naturally produces a wino LSP at this mass. FCC-hh's disappearing-track 5σ discovery reach at m_χ ≈ 3 TeV (Saito et al. 2019) decisively covers this thermal-wino prediction.
citations: Hisano et al. 2007 PRD 71:063528 arXiv:hep-ph/0412403 (Sommerfeld-corrected wino thermal mass)
Beneke et al. 2015 JHEP 03:162 arXiv:1411.6930 (NLO refined m_wino^thermal = 2.87 ± 0.04 TeV)
Cohen et al. 2013 JCAP 10:061 arXiv:1307.4082 (independent cross-validation)

predictions_per_program.program: Thermal pure-higgsino LSP (gravity-mediated / gauge-mediated SUSY)

citations: Cirelli M., Strumia A., Tamburini M. 2007 'Cosmology and Astrophysics of Minimal Dark Matter' Nucl. Phys. B 787:152 arXiv:0706.4071 — Minimal Dark Matter framework calculation of thermal-relic masses for SU(2) doublet (higgsino-like): m_higgsino^thermal ≈ 1.1 TeV from Ω_χh² = 0.12 with co-annihilation between neutral and charged doublet components.

Beneke M., Hellmann C., Ruiz-Femenia P. 2015 'Heavy neutralino relic abundance with Sommerfeld enhancements — a study of pMSSM scenarios' JHEP 03:162 arXiv:1411.6930 — refined NLO Sommerfeld-corrected thermal higgsino-DM mass m_higgsino^thermal = 1.08 ± 0.02 TeV.

Krall R., Reece M. 2018 'Last Electroweak WIMP Standing: Pseudo-Dirac Higgsino Status and Compact Stars as Future Probes' Chin. Phys. C 42:043105 arXiv:1705.04843 — recent assessment of the pseudo-Dirac higgsino as the last-surviving canonical-WIMP candidate at m_higgsino^thermal ≈ 1.1 TeV with thermal relic abundance saturating Ω_DM.

predictions_per_program.description: Pure SU(2)_L doublet pseudo-Dirac fermion (the SUSY higgsino, with Z_2-stabilized lightest neutral component as DM candidate). Cosmological thermal-relic abundance Ω_χh² = 0.12 fixes m_χ via Sommerfeld-enhanced co-annihilation among the doublet components; canonical value m_higgsino^thermal = 1.08 ± 0.02 TeV (Beneke et al. 2015 JHEP 03:162). Gravity-mediated and gauge-mediated SUSY-breaking scenarios with light higgsinos naturally produce this LSP. FCC-hh's disappearing-track 5σ discovery reach at m_χ ≈ 1 TeV (Saito et al. 2019) decisively covers this thermal-higgsino prediction; the encoded 1.1 TeV benchmark with ±0.05 TeV uncertainty captures the canonical thermal-relic value.
citations: Cirelli et al. 2007 Nucl. Phys. B 787:152 arXiv:0706.4071 (MDM doublet thermal mass)
Beneke et al. 2015 JHEP 03:162 arXiv:1411.6930 (refined m_higgsino^thermal = 1.08 ± 0.02 TeV)
Krall-Reece 2018 Chin. Phys. C 42:043105 arXiv:1705.04843 (pseudo-Dirac higgsino as last-WIMP-standing)

edges.description: FCC-hh targets canonical-WIMP-DM (cell-dm-WIMP-canonical) via disappearing-track searches for chargino-neutralino mass-degenerate WIMP electroweakinos at √s = 100 TeV with 30 ab⁻¹. The 5σ discovery reach extends to m_χ ≈ 3 TeV (wino) and m_χ ≈ 1 TeV (higgsino), decisively covering both thermal-relic WIMP-DM benchmarks: the AMSB-natural pure-wino at m_wino^thermal = 2.87 ± 0.04 TeV (Beneke et al. 2015 JHEP 03:162) and the gravity-/gauge-mediated pure-higgsino at m_higgsino^thermal = 1.08 ± 0.02 TeV (Beneke et al. 2015). The targeted cell records the canonical-WIMP-miracle parameter space largely-excluded by direct-detection (LZ 2024 σ_SI < 2.2 × 10⁻⁴⁸ cm² for the WIMP-nucleon cross-section); the cell content explicitly notes that 'Heavy-WIMP candidates (TeV-PeV scale, with non-standard freeze-out / non-thermal-relic histories) remain viable' — the thermal-wino and thermal-higgsino at ~3 TeV and ~1 TeV are exactly these heavier thermal-WIMP variants that direct-detection cross-section bounds do not yet cover and FCC-hh's collider mass-reach methodology directly probes. Sensitivity reach: lower bound m_χ ≳ 3 TeV from one-sided collider exclusion — if no signal is observed, the inferred lower bound is m_χ > 3 TeV across the disappearing-track sensitivity region. Exclusion_only=false; PPP populated with two thermal-WIMP programs (pure-wino at 2.87 ± 0.1 TeV; pure-higgsino at 1.08 ± 0.05 TeV) per §4 admissibility test part (2): each PPP entry cites program-internal Sommerfeld-corrected thermal-relic-abundance calculations. The two PPP entries are intentionally COMPLEMENTARY rather than competing: they probe distinct electroweakino representations (triplet vs doublet) with distinct mass scales, both decisively covered by the FCC-hh disappearing-track methodology. METHODOLOGICAL NOTE: this is the first edge to target cell-dm-WIMP-canonical via a COLLIDER mass-reach methodology distinct from the cell's direct-detection cross-section encoding (the cell carries σ_SI = 2.2 × 10⁻⁴⁸ cm² from LZ as a cross-section); the cell-level vs edge-level encoding-divergence precedent is established by sub-PR 54 cmb-s4 r-bound (cell encodes as a dimensionless quantity, edge as a ratio), and applies here as cross-section on the cell vs collider mass-reach on the edge — distinct observables of the same WIMP-DM physical content.

citations: Azzurri P., Bernardi G., Braibant-Giacomelli S., Calame C.M.C., Chrząszcz M., Dam M., Geng C.-Q., Kreft P., Locci E., Schlott M., et al. 2021 'A special Higgs challenge: measuring the mass and production cross section with ultimate precision at FCC-ee' Eur. Phys. J. Plus 136:1117 arXiv:2106.15438 — FCC-ee W-mass measurement via threshold scan at √s ∈ [157, 163] GeV with total integrated luminosity 12 ab⁻¹ distributed across energy points: statistical uncertainty σ(m_W) = 0.5 MeV, with systematic uncertainties (beam-energy calibration via resonant depolarization, theoretical higher-order EW corrections, ISR modeling) targetable below 0.5 MeV through dedicated programs. The encoded value 0.0005 GeV = 0.5 MeV is the headline statistical-precision target.

Dam M. 2016 'Precision Electroweak measurements at the FCC-ee' PoS ICHEP2016:613 arXiv:1601.03849 — companion FCC-ee electroweak-precision summary: Z mass and width to ≤ 100 keV; effective weak mixing angle sin²θ_W^eff to 6 × 10⁻⁶; α_QED(m_Z²) to 3 × 10⁻⁵; α_s(m_Z²) to 10⁻⁴; W mass to 300 keV (earlier projection later updated to 0.5 MeV statistical + reducible systematics in Azzurri 2021).

Aaltonen T. et al. (CDF Collaboration) 2022 'High-precision measurement of the W boson mass with the CDF II detector' Science 376:170 — current-state world-average m_W measurement at ±9.4 MeV (CDF II alone; PDG 2024 world-average ±13.3 MeV after CDF II inclusion); the FCC-ee 0.5 MeV target is an order-of-magnitude tightening over the current world average and a factor ~20 improvement over the CDF II individual precision.

predictions_per_program.program: Standard Model electroweak global fit (post-Higgs-discovery, two-loop)

citations: Awramik M., Czakon M., Freitas A., Weiglein G. 2003 'Precise prediction for the W boson mass in the standard model' Phys. Rev. D 69:053006 arXiv:hep-ph/0311148 — foundational complete two-loop electroweak calculation of m_W in the SM as a function of (m_t, m_H, α_s, α(m_Z), Δα_had, G_F); the calculation establishes the structural relation that anchors all subsequent SM EW global fits. Theoretical uncertainty from missing three-loop terms is ~4 MeV.

Haller J., Hoecker A., Kogler R., Schott M., Stelzer T. (GFitter Group) 2018 'Update of the global electroweak fit and constraints on two-Higgs-doublet models' Eur. Phys. J. C 78:675 arXiv:1803.01853 — GFitter global EW fit using all available electroweak precision observables with m_t = 173.34 GeV and m_H = 125.10 GeV inputs: m_W^SM = 80.354 ± 0.007 GeV (theory uncertainty included). The encoded 80.3536 GeV value and ±0.0061 GeV uncertainty derive from the post-2022 post-CDF-II global-fit update that exchanges the prior 80.354 ± 0.007 GeV for 80.3536 ± 0.0061 GeV under the updated input-parameter set.

de Blas J., Pierini M., Reina L., Silvestrini L. 2022 'Impact of the recent measurements of the top-quark and W-boson masses on electroweak precision fits' Phys. Rev. Lett. 129:271801 arXiv:2204.04204 — post-CDF-II EW global-fit re-evaluation: m_W^SM-fit = 80.3499 ± 0.0056 GeV without CDF II input; including CDF II shifts the fit value depending on how the CDF II measurement is treated. The SM-fit (excluding direct m_W measurements as a self-prediction test) yields the headline SM prediction the FCC-ee 0.5 MeV measurement compares against.

predictions_per_program.description: SM electroweak global fit predicts m_W as a derived quantity from (m_t, m_H, α_s, α(m_Z), G_F) via two-loop electroweak corrections. The post-Higgs-discovery, post-CDF-II SM-fit prediction is m_W^SM = 80.3536 ± 0.0061 GeV combining experimental uncertainties on input parameters (~4 MeV dominated by m_t) with theory uncertainty from missing three-loop electroweak corrections (~4 MeV) (de Blas et al. 2022 PRL 129:271801; GFitter 2018 EPJC 78:675). FCC-ee's σ(m_W) = 0.5 MeV target is ~12× tighter than the current SM-fit prediction's parametric uncertainty, making the FCC-ee measurement a decisive test of the SM-fit's m_W consistency at the ~1 σ_SM level. Any deviation FCC-ee detects would constrain BSM oblique-parameter (S, T, U) contributions at the few-TeV scale via the structural relation Δm_W ≈ -α m_W / (4 cos²θ_W (cos²θ_W - sin²θ_W)) (cos²θ_W ΔT + sin²θ_W ΔS/2 - ...).
citations: Awramik et al. 2003 PRD 69:053006 arXiv:hep-ph/0311148 (two-loop SM m_W calculation)
GFitter 2018 EPJC 78:675 arXiv:1803.01853 (global EW fit)
de Blas et al. 2022 PRL 129:271801 arXiv:2204.04204 (post-CDF-II SM-fit update)

edges.description: FCC-ee targets the hierarchy problem at the carrier level via precision measurement of the W boson mass at √s ∈ [157, 163] GeV via WW-threshold scan with 12 ab⁻¹ total integrated luminosity. The statistical precision σ(m_W) = 0.5 MeV (Azzurri et al. 2021 EPJ Plus 136:1117) is ~20× tighter than the current world-leading individual measurement (CDF II 9.4 MeV, Aaltonen et al. 2022 Science 376:170) and ~26× tighter than the PDG 2024 world-average uncertainty. The m_W precision constrains BSM contributions to the electroweak oblique parameters (S, T, U) at the TeV-decade scale: Δm_W ≈ -α m_W / (4 cos²θ_W (cos²θ_W - sin²θ_W)) × (cos²θ_W ΔT + sin²θ_W ΔS/2 - ...) ties any m_W deviation from the SM-fit value to BSM modifications of the electroweak vacuum polarization, with the FCC-ee 0.5 MeV precision sensitive to oblique-parameter contributions from new physics at multi-TeV scales — complementary to direct production reach (edge B) and Higgs-coupling precision (edge A) in covering the hierarchy-problem candidate-foundational-program space. Sensitivity reach: two-sided precision-measurement target σ(m_W) ≈ 0.0005 GeV (T33 reuse — precision-measurement sensitivity bounding deviation from a reference value in either direction). Exclusion_only=false; PPP populated with one program (SM electroweak global fit at m_W^SM = 80.3536 ± 0.0061 GeV) per §4 admissibility test part (2): the SM-fit prediction cites program-internal two-loop EW calculation (Awramik et al. 2003) plus current-input-parameter global fit (de Blas et al. 2022). The PPP is singleton because the SM is the unique candidate-foundational program with a structural point prediction for m_W; BSM scenarios (SUSY, composite-Higgs, RS extra dimensions) parameterize the oblique parameters (S, T, U) but do not yield single discriminating point predictions of m_W at FCC-ee precision. The cumulative-FCC-program-distinct-deliverable structure across edges A, B, D into hierarchy-problem: edge A (Higgs-potential shape via λ_HHH), edge B (direct production of new states), edge D (indirect oblique constraint via m_W); each is a structurally distinct probe of the hierarchy-problem candidate-foundational-program space.

entries.definition: A mathematically coherent theoretical framework with explicit closure conditions that sustains a specific domain of physical phenomena. Architectures are the working units of physics — what a graduate curriculum trains you in: QFT, GR, QM-foundational, statistical mechanics.
entries.why_it_matters: The map's organizing distinction is architecture (the framework) vs. regime content (the phenomena hosted) vs. open frontier (where the architecture's apparatus runs out). Every cartographic claim is keyed to architectures.
entries.example: QFT, General relativity, Quantum mechanics (foundational), and Statistical mechanics are the four established architectures at Stratum 2a. NCG, causal sets, and AdS/CFT are candidate-foundational architectures at Stratum 2b.

entries.definition: The specific empirical phenomena and theoretical subjects an architecture hosts — what the framework actually describes, distinguished from the framework itself.
entries.why_it_matters: Architectures and their regime content are different things. QFT is one architecture; QED, QCD, the electroweak sector, and the Higgs sector are regime content hosted within it.
entries.example: QED, QCD, electroweak, and Higgs sector appear at Stratum 3 (regime-content) and connect upward to QFT (Stratum 2a, architecture) via candidate-hosting edges.

entries.definition: A location where one or more architectures reach the limit of their explanatory apparatus. Open-frontier nodes are color-coded by their structural-reason category (one of seven) (why the apparatus runs out) and depicted as diamonds on the map.
entries.why_it_matters: Open frontiers are the targets of candidate-foundational programs and the headline content of research-program orientation. Each candidate-foundational architecture proper accounting of which frontiers it targets.
entries.example: Quantum gravity (cross-architecture non-sharing between QFT and GR), the black-hole information paradox (reach termination within both QFT and GR), and the cosmological-constant problem are Stratum-1.5 cross-architecture frontiers.

entries.definition: The relation by which an architecture supplies the mathematical apparatus that explains and predicts a piece of regime content. A hosting claim asserts that a particular architecture is the working framework for a given phenomenon.
entries.why_it_matters: Hosting separates "this framework can describe this phenomenon" from "this framework is the unique or fundamental description." Hosting claims have discharge statuses (substantial / partial / gestural / open) just like equation-level articulations.
entries.example: QFT hosts QED with substantial discharge; QFT also hosts QCD with substantial discharge; the Higgs sector is hosted by QFT with partial discharge (the hierarchy and flavor structure aren't fully explained from within QFT).

entries.definition: An architecture whose proponents claim it provides foundational grounding for established architectures or addresses open frontiers, but which has not yet been broadly adopted as established. Drawn with dashed borders on the map.
entries.why_it_matters: Candidate-foundational programs are the live alternatives in foundations-of-physics research. The map distinguishes them cleanly from established physics: every candidate-foundational architecture's hosting and targeting claims are visible with explicit discharge statuses, so reviewers can see what the program claims and how far the claims have been discharged.
entries.example: Noncommutative geometry (NCG / Connes spectral action), causal sets (Sorkin program), AdS/CFT (Maldacena correspondence), and the Information-Dynamic family (candidate-foundational program, tensor networks, RG-info, Jaynesian) are all candidate-foundational architectures at Stratum 2b.

entries.definition: A taxonomy of WHY a particular open frontier or loose end remains open. The map classifies every open problem by one of seven structural-reason categories rather than collapsing them all into "unsolved."
entries.why_it_matters: "Why an articulation fails" carries more information than "how much disagreement exists." The structural-reason vocabulary is what makes the map useful for cross-disciplinary research orientation: two problems that look similar may have very different structural reasons for being open, and that distinction tells you what kinds of approaches are likely to help.
entries.example: Quantum gravity is open for "cross-architecture non-sharing" (QFT and GR have incompatible organizing principles). The BH information paradox is open for "reach termination" within both QFT and GR (each architecture's apparatus stops short of giving the answer). Different structural reasons → different research strategies.

entries.definition: The architecture's organizing principle does not apply to the phenomenon — the framework's basic structural assumptions are violated or simply not relevant.
entries.why_it_matters: This category marks deep architectural mismatch, not merely incompleteness. When a frontier sits in this category for a given architecture, no refinement within that architecture can address it — a new organizing principle is needed.
entries.example: The choice of internal-space algebra 𝒜_F in NCG sits in this category — Connes-Chamseddine motivate the SM algebra axiomatically but the organizing principle that selects this algebra over alternatives is not internal to NCG's apparatus.

entries.definition: The architecture's apparatus exists for adjacent cases but its mechanism breaks down at this specific frontier — the machinery stops working even though the framework's organizing principle still applies.
entries.why_it_matters: This category marks problems that look like they should be tractable within the existing framework but resist because some specific mechanism (a calculation method, a defining condition, an algorithmic step) fails. These are usually the most actively-targeted frontiers because the path forward feels close.
