entries.example: Causal-set quantum dynamics: the path-integral apparatus over causal sets is well-defined formally, but partition-function existence in 4D is not established — the dynamics apparatus has a mechanism failure.

entries.definition: The architecture's apparatus extends a finite distance from its starting conditions but terminates at a boundary the framework cannot cross from within. Beyond that boundary, the framework's predictions simply stop.
entries.why_it_matters: These frontiers mark genuine boundaries of validity. They cannot be addressed by refinement within the architecture; they require either embedding the architecture into a larger framework or replacing it.
entries.example: The Standard Model's flavor puzzle is viewed from within QFT: QFT has the apparatus to encode Yukawa values but no mechanism within QFT explains why those specific values rather than others.

entries.definition: Two mathematical languages or articulation traditions both purport to describe a domain but no rigorous translation between them has been established. Bridge failure operates at the language or equation level — between articulations within or across architectures.
entries.why_it_matters: This category is distinctive: the content is on both sides, but the translation isn't. Targeting a bridge failure means building a formally rigorous correspondence rather than discovering new physics. Bridge failure sits below cross-architecture non-sharing in granularity — bridge failures are between specific articulations; cross-architecture non-sharing is between whole organizing principles. A single cross-architecture-non-sharing frontier may decompose into multiple bridge failures at the equation level.
entries.example: The connection between information-theoretic articulations of gravity (Bekenstein-Hawking, Jacobson's derivation) and the tensor-equation content of GR is partial — Jacobson's derivation reaches Einstein equations at equilibrium horizons but the non-equilibrium / dynamical extension remains a bridge failure.

entries.definition: Two coherent architectures both apply (or both are required) in a regime but their organizing principles do not compose into a single shared framework. Cross-architecture non-sharing operates at the architecture level — between organizing principles themselves, not between specific articulations within them.
entries.why_it_matters: Cross-architecture non-sharing is distinguished from bridge failure by granularity. Bridge failure is about missing translations between specific articulations; Cross-architecture non-sharing is about missing shared organizing structure. A cross-architecture-non-sharing frontier may admit bridge failures at the equation level, but the deeper issue is that no single architecture's organizing principle can host both contributing architectures' content. Closure requires either a deeper architecture or a principled merger.
entries.example: Quantum gravity is the canonical cross-architecture non-sharing frontier: QFT's gauge-theoretic apparatus and GR's diffeomorphism- invariant geometric apparatus do not share an organizing principle. This is what every candidate-foundational program for QG must address.

entries.definition: The architecture is fine, the apparatus is fine, the bridges connect — but the equations resist analytic solution and computational discharge is partial. This category is distinct from other categories in that nothing is structurally missing: it's a computational or analytic obstruction within a fully-articulated framework.
entries.why_it_matters: Many of physics' most famous open problems live here. They are not telling you something is wrong with your theory; they are telling you the theory is correctly formulated and the math is hard. Closure here means either solving the equations (often a major mathematical achievement in itself) or discharging the content numerically with sufficient confidence — not finding new physics.
entries.example: Confinement-from-QCD: the Lagrangian is precise, the path-integral apparatus is well-established, lattice methods provide substantial numerical discharge — but an analytic proof of color confinement from first-principles QCD remains absent. Navier–Stokes regularity (Clay Millennium problem) and many-body localization in full generality have the same flavor.

entries.definition: The architecture has explicit structure and definite operational predictions, but admits multiple incompatible content-level readings that agree on what the framework computes while disagreeing on what it says about the world.
entries.why_it_matters: This category is distinct from organizing-structure failure: the organizing principle isn't silent or missing — it's compatible with several competing accounts of what the principle's content actually is. Closure means either an empirical discriminator emerging between readings, or a principled reason to select one over the others. Pure interpretive-underdetermination frontiers do not respond to mathematical refinement of the existing framework.
entries.example: The measurement problem in QM: Many-worlds, collapse models, decoherence-and-branching, Bohmian mechanics, QBism and other readings compute the same probabilities and predict the same Born-rule statistics but differ on whether wavefunctions are physical, whether collapse is fundamental, whether branching is observer-relative, whether hidden variables exist. The architecture is operationally consistent across all of them.

entries.definition: The community-acceptance status of an architecture or claim. Four values: ESTABLISHED (broadly accepted working framework), CANDIDATE-FOUNDATIONAL (proposed foundational program not yet established), GESTURED (suggestive but not formally articulated), CONTESTED (actively disputed).
entries.why_it_matters: Empirical status is community status — separate from discharge status (how well the math works) and structural-reason (why something is open). All three together specify a claim's location in the map's diagnostic space.
entries.example: QFT, GR, QM-foundational, stat mech are ESTABLISHED. NCG, causal-sets, AdS/CFT, the four Information-Dynamic instances are CANDIDATE-FOUNDATIONAL. Some quantum-foundational reconstructions are GESTURED.

entries.definition: A rating of how fully a specific articulation, hosting claim, or candidate-targeting has been mathematically realized. Five values: SUBSTANTIAL (extensively articulated and matched to content), PARTIAL (meaningful articulation with significant gaps), GESTURAL (structural suggestion without specific discharge), OPEN (not yet articulated), FAILED (attempted and refuted).
entries.why_it_matters: Discharge status is a separate axis from empirical status. An established architecture can have only partial discharge of one of its own articulations; a candidate-foundational program can have substantial discharge of one specific claim. Reading discharge statuses lets you see what is actually known vs. promised.
entries.example: QFT's candidate-foundational articulation in stat-mech is "partial" (the candidate-foundational articulates the equilibrium ensemble substantially but non-equilibrium remains partial). NCG's spectral action discharge of Einstein-Hilbert is "substantial-local."

entries.definition: Discharge status can be composite: different scopes (local / global / universal, or geometry / matter / universal) may have different statuses. A claim might be substantial-local but gestural-global, for instance.
entries.why_it_matters: Many candidate-foundational programs work well in restricted regimes but not universally; collapsing this into a single discharge value would hide the structure. Composite split-status makes the regime-specificity of partial successes legible.
entries.example: AdS/CFT's geometric articulation has composite split-status {local: substantial, global: partial, universal: gestural}: it discharges substantially within canonical AdS×S backgrounds (local), partially across less-symmetric setups (global), and only gestures at universality (de Sitter, FRW).

entries.definition: The self-consistency requirements that define when an architecture is mathematically self-sustaining — what must hold for the framework to be internally complete and not lean on external apparatus.
entries.why_it_matters: Every architecture has implicit or explicit closure conditions. Surfacing them is what makes "this is a working framework" auditable.
entries.example: QFT's closure condition involves renormalizability and unitarity; GR's involves general covariance and the Einstein equations being Lorentzian self-consistent; statistical mechanics' involves the MaxEnt + appropriate-ensemble choice for given constraints.

entries.definition: Four formal traditions in which physical content can be articulated. Each architecture's equations-layer shows how its content reads when translated into each language. The four are: (1) Information-theoretic — entropy, channels, MaxEnt, holographic info; (2) Geometric / fiber-bundle — manifolds, connections, curvature, spectral triples; (3) Category-theoretic — topoi, functors, †-compact categories; (4) Native — the architecture's own articulation tradition.
entries.why_it_matters: The multi-language unification framework is what makes the map function as a translation device between subfields. A particle physicist's gauge-theoretic native language and a quantum- information theorist's entropic native language describe related structures; the map's equations layer makes the translation explicit.
entries.example: GR's equations layer articulates Einstein equations in four languages: information-theoretic partial (Bekenstein-Hawking, Jacobson), geometric substantial (Levi-Civita, Riemann curvature), category-theoretic gestural (cohesive ∞-topos), and native substantial.

entries.definition: A scale-tier in the map's vertical axis. Strata are conceptual scales of generality, not temporal or spatial scales. The published-tradition view shows the strata: 1.5 (cross-architecture frontiers + totality approaches); 2a (established architectures); 2b (candidate-foundational architectures); 3 (regime content); 4 (within-architecture frontiers).
entries.why_it_matters: Stratum is the map's organizing axis: every node is placed at a specific stratum, and edges typically run between strata according to type. Reading vertical position tells you the scale of generality.
entries.example: QFT is at Stratum 2a (established architecture). QCD is at Stratum 3 (regime content hosted by QFT). The flavor puzzle is at Stratum 4 (within-QFT open frontier).

entries.definition: A specific open problem associated with one equation or articulation, classified by structural-reason category and scope, with optional candidate-targeting (which programs address it). Loose ends live inside equations rather than at the node level — they record what is unresolved about a specific articulation.
entries.why_it_matters: Loose ends are where the map's diagnostic content actually surfaces. Reading an architecture's loose ends tells you what is known not to work within that articulation. The equation-level granularity lets you see which specific articulations have which kinds of unfinished business.
entries.example: GR's information-theoretic articulation has a loose end with structural-reason "apparatus-mechanism-failure": extending the Jacobson derivation to non-equilibrium gravity is open. Candidate targeting: Padmanabhan equipartition (partial), Verlinde entropic gravity (contested).

entries.definition: The set of candidate-foundational programs that explicitly aim to address a particular open frontier or loose end, each with its own discharge status. Visible both at the node level (which open frontiers a candidate-foundational program targets) and at the equation level (which programs target a specific loose end).
entries.why_it_matters: Candidate targeting is what lets a reviewer answer "who claims to have addressed this problem, and how far have they gotten?" Without this layer, "frontier" and "candidate" become floating lists; with it, the relationship is explicit and auditable.
entries.example: Quantum gravity (frontier) is candidate-targeted by a candidate-foundational program (partial), NCG (partial-local for SM-coupling, gestural- universal), causal sets (gestural), and AdS/CFT (substantial within AdS×S backgrounds, gestural elsewhere).

entries.definition: A group of sibling candidate-foundational architectures sharing an organizing principle, depicted as a labeled bounding box on the map at Stratum 2b. The map shows four families: Information-Dynamic (4 instances), Geometric/Spectral (NCG), Discrete-Causal (causal sets), and Holographic (AdS/CFT).
entries.why_it_matters: Families make the candidate-foundational landscape comparable: you can read off whether a frontier has been targeted by one family or multiple, and whether competing approaches are siblings within a family or genuinely different organizing principles.
entries.example: The Information-Dynamic family contains candidate-foundational, tensor-network, RG-info, and Jaynesian — four instances sharing the organizing principle that physical content is articulated through information dynamics.
